topics.title: Cloud Computing - Introduction. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing - Introduction. Overview of Computing Paradigms - Grid Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Computing — Formal Academic Definition

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** (National Institute of Standards and Technology), which is the de-facto standard reference adopted by KTU 2024 Scheme:

> **Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

The **5 Essential Characteristics**, **3 Service Models**, and **4 Deployment Models** form the foundational triad of cloud architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cloud Computing is *not* just "using the internet." It is a **paradigm shift** in resource provisioning — moving from *owned, capital-intensive* infrastructure to *rented, utility-based* on-demand services.

### 1.1.1 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **On-Demand Self-Service** | User provisions compute/storage automatically without human interaction with the provider. |
| 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Capabilities are available over the network via standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST APIs). |
| 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Multi-tenant model — physical/virtual resources dynamically reassigned per demand. |
| 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities scale out/in quickly, often appearing as *infinite* to the user. |
| 5 | **Measured Service** | Cloud systems automatically meter storage, processing, bandwidth — **pay-per-use** billing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Electricity Grid Analogy**
> Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity grid** in your home. You do **not** buy a diesel generator. You just plug a device into the wall socket. The power company meters your usage, bills you monthly, and instantly scales supply when you switch on an air conditioner. Likewise, in the cloud, you do **not** buy servers. You "plug" your application into a cloud API (like AWS, Azure, GCP), and the provider meters CPU-hours, GB-storage, and GB-bandwidth.

---

## 1.2 Computing Paradigms — Formal Definition

A **Computing Paradigm** is the fundamental model that defines *how* computing resources (compute, storage, network) are organized, accessed, and delivered to end-users. The evolution traces a clear historical lineage:

$$\text{Mainframe} \rightarrow \text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Client-Server} \rightarrow \text{Cluster} \rightarrow \text{Grid} \rightarrow \text{Cloud} \rightarrow \text{Fog/Edge}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — Transportation Paradigms**
> Imagine transport evolution:
> - **Mainframe** = Building your own railway track.
> - **Personal Computer** = Buying a personal bicycle.
> - **Client-Server** = Taking a city bus (one big central server).
> - **Cluster** = A fleet of buses running together.
> - **Grid Computing** = Hitchhiking across cities, where you combine lifts from multiple independent drivers.
> - **Cloud Computing** = Ordering an Ola/Uber — instant, on-demand, metered, elastic.

---

## 1.3 Grid Computing — Formal Academic Definition

> **Grid Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that coordinates **disparate, geographically dispersed, and heterogeneous resources** (computers, databases, instruments, sensors) belonging to multiple administrative domains, to deliver a single unified computational goal — typically for solving large-scale, compute-intensive scientific or engineering problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Grid computing was popularized by projects like **SETI@home** and **BOINC** — where millions of idle PCs volunteered spare CPU cycles to analyze radio telescope data for extraterrestrial signals. The phrase *"the grid"* itself was coined by analogy with the **electric power grid**.

### 1.3.1 Conceptual Analogy — The Volunteer Fire Brigade

Think of Grid Computing like a **volunteer fire brigade**:

- Each volunteer (computer) lives in a **different house** (administrative domain).
- They have **different tools and schedules** (heterogeneity).
- When an alarm rings (a job is submitted), a **dispatcher** (the Grid Middleware — e.g., **Globus Toolkit**) assigns specific tasks to specific volunteers.
- Each volunteer **independently** fights their part of the fire.
- After the operation, everyone returns to their normal life.

The key difference from Cloud: **Grid is about *sharing* idle capacity for a *shared scientific goal***; **Cloud is about *renting* dedicated capacity for *commercial services***.

---

## 1.4 Why These Paradigms Matter in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module-1 specifically asks students to distinguish between computing paradigms because this differentiation is **the single most-tested concept** in Part-A questions (2-mark/3-mark slots) and frequently appears as a sub-part (7-mark) in 14-mark Part-B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Cloud Computing Works

Cloud computing operates on a **layered architectural stack**. We analyze each layer in engineering-grade depth:

### Layer 1: Physical Infrastructure (Hardware Layer)
- Comprises **data centers** with thousands of **rack-mounted x86/ARM servers**, **SAN/NAS storage arrays**, and **high-speed network switches** (typically **10 Gbps / 40 Gbps / 100 Gbps** fabric).
- Industry metric: Modern hyperscale data centers (AWS, Google) consume **>100 MW** of power.

### Layer 2: Virtualization Layer (Hypervisor)
- The **hypervisor** (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen) abstracts physical hardware into **Virtual Machines (VMs)**.
- Each VM runs its own guest OS, creating **logical isolation**.
- Key concepts: **Type-1 (bare-metal)** vs **Type-2 (hosted)** hypervisors.

### Layer 3: Resource Pooling & Orchestration
- Resources are aggregated into **resource pools** and dynamically allocated by orchestrators like **Kubernetes**, **OpenStack**, **VMware vCenter**.
- Implements the *resource pooling* and *rapid elasticity* characteristics.

### Layer 4: Service Delivery (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS)

| Service Model | What is Provided | Who Manages | KTU Example |
|---------------|------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **IaaS** (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) | Raw VMs, Storage, Networks | User manages OS, Middleware, Apps | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine |
| **PaaS** (Platform-as-a-Service) | Runtime, OS, Middleware, Dev Tools | User manages only Apps & Data | Google App Engine, Heroku |
| **SaaS** (Software-as-a-Service) | Ready-to-use Software | Provider manages everything | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Dropbox |

### Layer 5: User Access Layer
- Consumers access via **Web Portal**, **REST APIs**, **CLI**, or **SDKs**.
- Metering is performed via **usage meters** (CPU-hours, GB-months, API calls).

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — How Grid Computing Works

Grid computing relies on **middleware** as the central nervous system. The classical **Grid Architecture** has 5 layers (the OGSA — Open Grid Services Architecture):

1. **Fabric Layer** — Physical resources (compute nodes, storage, sensors).
2. **Connectivity Layer** — Communication protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, SSL).
3. **Resource Layer** — Manages individual resources (e.g., GRAM — Grid Resource Allocation Manager).
4. **Collective Layer** — Coordinates multiple resources (directory services — MDS — Monitoring and Discovery Service).
5. **Application Layer** — User-facing scientific/engineering applications.

### 2.2.1 Key Properties of Grid (the "Why" Behind Each)

- **Heterogeneity:** Resources differ in OS, hardware, network. Middleware normalizes this.
- **Geographical Distribution:** Resources span continents. Latency is a real constraint.
- **Virtual Organization (VO):** A temporary logical grouping of users/Institutions sharing resources for a common goal (e.g., CERN's LHC Grid).
- **No Central Ownership:** Unlike cloud, grid resources belong to multiple owners — *coordination*, not *control*, is the goal.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Metrics

While cloud/grid computing is not heavily numerical, KTU frequently tests the **quantitative SLA (Service Level Agreement) metrics**.

| Metric | Formula | Units | KTU Exam Relevance |
|--------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| **Availability ($A$)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | % (uptime) | **Very High** — often 99.9% ("three nines") or 99.99% ("four nines") |
| **Annual Downtime ($D$)** | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes/year | Critical for cloud SLA comparison |
| **Scalability Ratio ($S$)** | $S = \dfrac{C_{\max}}{C_{\text{base}}}$ | dimensionless | Higher = better elasticity |
| **Throughput ($T$)** | $T = \dfrac{N_{\text{jobs}}}{t_{\text{total}}}$ | jobs/second | Grid scheduling efficiency |
| **Cost per CPU-hour ($C$)** | $C = \dfrac{Total\, Cost}{Total\, CPU\text{-}hours}$ | ₹ or \$ / CPU-hr | Utility computing billing |

Where:
- $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $C_{\max}$ = Maximum provisioned capacity
- $C_{\text{base}}$ = Baseline capacity
- $N_{\text{jobs}}$ = Number of jobs completed
- $t_{\text{total}}$ = Total wall-clock time

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** AWS EC2 guarantees **99.99% availability** per SLA. Compute its allowable annual downtime:
> $$D = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525{,}600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$
> This is a *guaranteed* number — beyond which AWS issues service credits.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Paradigm Shift

| Driver | Grid Era (Pre-2010) | Cloud Era (Post-2010) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Goal** | Solve *one big scientific problem* | Deliver *many varied business services* |
| **Ownership** | Decentralized (multi-institution) | Centralized (single provider) |
| **Billing** | Mostly free / academic grants | Pay-per-use, utility pricing |
| **User Type** | Scientists, researchers | Enterprises, developers, end-consumers |
| **Virtualization** | Light / manual | Heavy / automated (Kubernetes) |
| **QoS SLA** | Best-effort | Contractually guaranteed |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Annual Downtime from Availability

This is a **high-yield KTU 2024 derivation** that frequently appears in Part-A questions and as part of larger Part-B problems.

### Problem Setup

A cloud provider offers an **SLA of 99.95% availability** for its database service. Calculate the maximum allowable **annual downtime in minutes**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters

$$A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$

Total minutes in a non-leap year:

$$T_{\text{year}} = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 60 \text{ min/h}$$

### Step 2 — Evaluate Total Minutes Per Year

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{year}} &= 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
&= 365 \times 1440 \\
&= 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Express Downtime as a Fraction

By definition, availability is the *uptime fraction* of total time:

$$A = \dfrac{T_{\text{up}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T_{\text{up}} = A \cdot T_{\text{total}}$$

Therefore the downtime fraction is the complement:

$$D_{\text{fraction}} = 1 - A$$

### Step 4 — Compute Annual Downtime

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{annual}} &= D_{\text{fraction}} \times T_{\text{year}} \\
&= (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 262.8 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Final Interpretation

> The provider's infrastructure can fail (planned + unplanned) for a **maximum of 262.8 minutes (≈ 4.38 hours)** per year before violating SLA. Anything beyond triggers service-credit refunds to customers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Pattern:** Always keep **4 decimal places** in availability. A common student mistake is writing 99.95 as 0.9995 incorrectly or omitting unit conversion from hours to minutes.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Computing Paradigm Comparison

The single most important **analytical tool** for KTU Module-1 is the paradigm-comparison matrix. Memorize this.

| Dimension | **Mainframe** | **Cluster** | **Grid** | **Cloud** |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Centralized monolithic | Loosely coupled LAN nodes | Geographically distributed, multi-domain | Virtualized data-center pool |
| **Resource Type** | Homogeneous | Mostly homogeneous | **Heterogeneous** | Virtualized (homogeneous interface) |
| **Ownership** | Single org | Single org | **Multiple orgs (VOs)** | Single provider |
| **Coupling** | Tight | Tight (LAN) | **Loose (WAN/Internet)** | Loose (Internet APIs) |
| **Virtualization** | None | Light | Light/None | **Heavy (VM/Containers)** |
| **Job Type** | Transaction processing | Parallel scientific | **Large-scale scientific** | Diverse — web, AI, storage |
| **Scalability** | Vertical only | Vertical + limited horizontal | Horizontal (cross-org) | **Massive horizontal (elastic)** |
| **Pricing Model** | Capital expenditure | Capital expenditure | Mostly free / grant | **Pay-per-use (OpEx)** |
| **Middleware** | OS scheduler | OS + job schedulers (e.g., SLURM) | **Globus Toolkit, BOINC** | **Kubernetes, OpenStack** |
| **SLA Guarantee** | Internal | Internal | Best-effort | **Contractual (99.9% to 99.99%)** |
| **Example** | IBM z16 | Linux Beowulf cluster | SETI@home, LHC@CERN | AWS, Azure, GCP |

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Cluster vs Grid Throughput Comparison

Suppose a university has **2 clusters** (Cluster-A: 100 nodes, Cluster-B: 80 nodes) and wants to combine them into a **Grid**. Given:
- Cluster-A: $T_A = 50$ jobs/sec
- Cluster-B: $T_B = 40$ jobs/sec
- Grid middleware overhead: $\eta = 5\%$ loss in throughput

### Step 1 — Compute Aggregate Grid Throughput (without overhead)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{raw}} &= T_A + T_B \\
&= 50 + 40 = 90 \text{ jobs/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Middleware Overhead Penalty

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{grid}} &= T_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - \eta) \\
&= 90 \times (1 - 0.05) \\
&= 90 \times 0.95 \\
&= 85.5 \text{ jobs/sec}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Engineering Insight

> A **5% middleware overhead** is realistic for **Globus Toolkit**. As a result, even though we combined two clusters (180 nodes total), the **effective throughput** is lower than the naive sum. This demonstrates a *key tradeoff* in grid design: **coordination cost vs aggregation benefit**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evolution of Computing Paradigms (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MAINFRAME 1960s"] --> nodeB["PERSONAL COMPUTER 1980s"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["CLIENT SERVER 1990s"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CLUSTER COMPUTING 1990s"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["GRID COMPUTING 2000s"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["CLOUD COMPUTING 2006 onwards"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING 2015 onwards"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["QUANTUM AND SERVERLESS 2020s"]
```

## 4.2 Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (NIST Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeU["CLOUD CONSUMER User via Web or API"]
    nodeSP["SERVICE PROVIDER Manages Cloud Services"]
    nodeAP["AUDITOR Conducts Independent Assessment"]
    nodeBR["BROKER Selects and Manages Cloud Services"]
    nodeCA["CARRIER Provides Network Connectivity"]
    nodeCS["CLOUD CONSUMER INFRASTRUCTURE"]
    nodePI["PHYSICAL RESOURCE LAYER Servers Storage Network"]
    nodeRA["RESOURCE ABSTRACTION Layer Virtualization and Pooling"]
    nodeSM["SERVICE LAYER IaaS PaaS SaaS"]
    nodeSA["SERVICE MANAGEMENT LAYER SLA Billing Security Monitoring"]
    nodePR["PRIVACY AND SECURITY LAYER Authentication Compliance Governance"]

    nodeU -.->|Uses| nodeSA
    nodeSA --> nodeSM
    nodeSM --> nodeCS
    nodeCS --> nodeRA
    nodeRA --> nodePI
    nodeSP --> nodeCS
    nodeBR -->|Connects| nodeU
    nodeBR -->|Selects| nodeSP
    nodeCA -->|Provides| nodeCS
    nodeAP -.->|Audits| nodeSA
```

## 4.3 Grid Computing Architecture (OGSA 5-Layer Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeAL["APPLICATION LAYER User Scientific Software"]
    nodeCL["COLLECTIVE LAYER Grid Schedulers and Directories"]
    nodeRL["RESOURCE LAYER Local Resource Managers"]
    nodeCN["CONNECTIVITY LAYER Network Protocols TCP IP HTTP SSL"]
    nodeFB["FABRIC LAYER Physical Computers Storage Sensors"]

    nodeAL --> nodeCL
    nodeCL --> nodeRL
    nodeRL --> nodeCN
    nodeCN --> nodeFB
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Cloud vs Grid Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeReq["USER REQUEST Web UI or API"]
    nodeAuth["AUTHENTICATION Layer"]
    nodeSched["SCHEDULER Cloud or Grid Middleware"]
    nodeResA["RESOURCE POOL A VM Cluster"]
    nodeResB["RESOURCE POOL B VM Cluster"]
    nodeResC["REMOTE RESOURCE C BOINC Volunteer"]
    nodeMeter["USAGE METER Billing"]
    nodeResp["RESPONSE BACK TO USER"]

    nodeReq --> nodeAuth
    nodeAuth --> nodeSched
    nodeSched -->|Cloud path| nodeResA
    nodeSched -->|Cloud path| nodeResB
    nodeSched -->|Grid path| nodeResC
    nodeResA --> nodeMeter
    nodeResB --> nodeMeter
    nodeResC --> nodeMeter
    nodeMeter --> nodeResp
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KTU):** Notice how the **Grid path** involves a *remote volunteer machine* (heterogeneous, autonomous), while the **Cloud path** allocates a *standardized VM* from a controlled pool. This is the *single visual distinction* examiners expect students to articulate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cloud Computing. List its five essential characteristics as per NIST. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. *(NIST SP 800-145)*

**Five Essential Characteristics (2 marks — 0.4 each):**

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Automatic provisioning without human intervention.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Available over standard networks via heterogeneous client platforms.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic reassignment of physical/virtual resources.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capabilities can be scaled out or in, often appearing unlimited.
5. **Measured Service** — Automatic metering of usage for billing transparency.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Grid Computing and Cloud Computing. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each for any 3 strong points):**

| Dimension | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Ownership** | Multiple organizations form a Virtual Organization (VO) | Single provider owns and operates |
| **Resource Type** | Heterogeneous, often idle donated machines | Homogeneous virtualized data center |
| **Virtualization** | Light or none | Heavy (VMs/Containers) |
| **Pricing** | Mostly free, academic | Pay-per-use utility model |
| **SLA** | Best-effort, no guarantee | Contractual SLA (e.g., 99.99%) |
| **Workload** | Scientific/technical batch jobs | Diverse — web, mobile, AI, storage |
| **Middleware** | Globus Toolkit, BOINC | OpenStack, Kubernetes, VMware |

---

## 5.2 Part-B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **a)** Define Computing Paradigm. Explain in detail the different types of computing paradigms with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*
> **b)** Explain the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture with a neat diagram. Discuss the roles of Cloud Consumer, Cloud Provider, Cloud Broker, Cloud Auditor, and Cloud Carrier. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A computing paradigm is a fundamental model that defines how computing resources are organized, accessed, and delivered.

**Different Paradigms (6 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Mainframe Computing** — Centralized, monolithic, time-sharing systems used for high-volume transaction processing. *Example: IBM z16 used in banking.*

2. **Personal Computing (PC)** — Standalone desktop computers with local processing and storage. *Example: Intel x86 PCs running Windows/Linux.*

3. **Client-Server Computing** — Centralized server delivering services to distributed clients over a network. *Example: Web servers, Email servers.*

4. **Cluster Computing** — A group of homogeneous tightly-coupled computers working as a single system. *Example: Beowulf cluster, Google search cluster.*

5. **Grid Computing** — Loosely-coupled, geographically distributed, heterogeneous resources across administrative domains coordinated via middleware. *Example: SETI@home, LHC Computing Grid.*

6. **Cloud Computing** — On-demand, elastic, metered, multi-tenant access to virtualized resources over the Internet. *Example: AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure.*

7. **(Bonus)** **Fog/Edge Computing** — Extends computation to the network edge closer to data sources. *Example: IoT gateways, CDNs.*

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**NIST Reference Architecture — 5 Actors (5 marks — 1 each):**

| Actor | Role |
|-------|------|
| **Cloud Consumer** | The end-user or organization that uses cloud services via web portal, CLI, or API. |
| **Cloud Provider** | Owns and manages the infrastructure, platforms, and applications offered to consumers. Responsible for SLA. |
| **Cloud Broker** | Intermediary that aggregates, integrates, and customizes cloud services from multiple providers for consumers. |
| **Cloud Auditor** | Independent party that evaluates cloud services for performance, security, and SLA compliance. |
| **Cloud Carrier** | The network provider (ISP) that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services between provider and consumer. |

**Layered Stack (2 marks):** Includes **Physical Resource Layer**, **Resource Abstraction Layer** (virtualization), **Service Layer** (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS), and **Service Management Layer** (SLA, billing, security).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **a)** What is Grid Computing? Explain the key characteristics and the OGSA (Open Grid Services Architecture) reference model with a diagram. *(7 marks)*
> **b)** Discuss the various deployment models and service models of cloud computing. Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with examples. *(7 marks)*

#### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** Grid Computing is a distributed computing paradigm that coordinates disparate, geographically dispersed, heterogeneous resources belonging to multiple administrative domains to deliver a unified computational goal.

**Key Characteristics (3 marks):**
1. **Resource sharing** across organizations via Virtual Organizations.
2. **Heterogeneity** of hardware, OS, network.
3. **Coordinated problem solving** without central ownership.
4. **Geographical distribution** of resources across WANs.
5. **Middleware-driven** scheduling (Globus Toolkit, BOINC).

**OGSA Architecture Layers (3 marks — 0.6 each):**
1. **Fabric Layer** — Physical resources (computers, storage, sensors).
2. **Connectivity Layer** — Communication (TCP/IP, HTTP, SSL).
3. **Resource Layer** — Manages individual resources (GRAM).
4. **Collective Layer** — Coordinates multiple resources (MDS — directory service).
5. **Application Layer** — User-facing scientific/engineering apps.

#### Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks)

**Deployment Models (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Public Cloud** — Open for general public use. *Example: AWS, Azure, GCP.*
2. **Private Cloud** — Operated solely for one organization. *Example: Government internal cloud.*
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by orchestration.
4. **Community Cloud** — Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., healthcare, banking).

**Service Models Comparison (4 marks):**

| Aspect | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|--------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Provides** | VMs, Storage, Network | Runtime, OS, Middleware, Tools | Ready-to-use application |
| **User manages** | Apps, Data, Runtime, OS, Middleware | Apps and Data only | Just usage |
| **Provider manages** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | Everything below runtime | Everything end-to-end |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, S3, Google Compute Engine | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Dropbox, Office 365 |
| **Target user** | System admins, DevOps | Application developers | End-users / business teams |
| **Flexibility** | High | Medium | Low |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1-3 marks):**
> 1. **Confusing "Grid" with "Cluster":** Many students describe a LAN Beowulf cluster as grid. **Grid = WAN + multi-domain + heterogeneous.** A cluster is *single-domain* and *LAN-based*.
> 2. **Forgetting the Provider-side responsibilities in SaaS:** SaaS is not just "software on the web" — the provider manages *all* layers including security, patching, scaling.
> 3. **Misnaming "Virtualization" as a Cloud characteristic:** Virtualization is the *enabling technology*, not a NIST characteristic. The five characteristics are: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
> 4. **Mixing up Broker and Auditor:** Broker *aggregates/integrates* services; Auditor *evaluates compliance*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if swapped.
> 5. **Skipping OGSA layer names:** Naming only "middleware" earns 0/3 on the OGSA part. Use exact layer names: Fabric, Connectivity, Resource, Collective, Application.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-frequency KTU 2024 Module-1):**

- [x] **Cloud Computing = utility computing** on-demand over the Internet.
- [x] **NIST 5 characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.
- [x] **NIST 3 service models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — user-managed vs provider-managed stack layers.
- [x] **NIST 4 deployment models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [x] **NIST 5 actors:** Consumer, Provider, Broker, Auditor, Carrier.
- [x] **Grid = multi-domain, heterogeneous, WAN-coordinated, best-effort** (no SLA).
- [x] **Cloud = single-provider, homogeneous-interface, elastic, metered** (with SLA).
- [x] **OGSA 5 layers:** Fabric → Connectivity → Resource → Collective → Application.
- [x] **Globus Toolkit** is the canonical grid middleware; **BOINC** is the volunteer-based middleware.
- [x] **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- [x] **Annual Downtime:** $D = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes/year.
- [x] **Cost metric:** $C = \dfrac{Total\, Cost}{Total\, CPU\text{-}hours}$ — used in cloud billing models.
- [x] **Virtualization = enabling technology**, NOT a NIST characteristic.
- [x] **Cluster ≠ Grid:** Cluster is *single-domain LAN*; Grid is *multi-domain WAN*.
- [x] **Evolution order:** Mainframe → PC → Client-Server → Cluster → Grid → Cloud → Fog/Edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Advice:** In the 14-mark questions, *always* draw a **neat labeled diagram** (e.g., OGSA layers, NIST actors). Examiners award **1-2 marks extra** for clean diagrams, even if textual explanation is partial.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cluster Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cluster Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm in which a group of loosely coupled, independently operational computers (referred to as **nodes**) work together closely so that, in many respects, they can be viewed as a **single unified computing system**. The constituent nodes collaborate through a high-speed **interconnection network** and are coordinated by a combination of **cluster middleware**, **operating system extensions**, and **protocols/services** to deliver combined computational power, storage capacity, and high availability far beyond what a single machine can provide.

The conceptual roots of cluster computing trace back to the 1960s with **IBM's mainframe channel-attached systems** and the pioneering **DEC VAXcluster** (1980s). Modern academic and industrial deployments adopt the **Beowulf architecture** (proposed by Thomas Sterling and Donald Becker at NASA, 1994) as the canonical design pattern for commodity-hardware Linux clusters.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Restaurant Brigade System**
> 
> Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen during dinner rush. **One single chef** working alone (a traditional standalone server) can only prepare, say, **10 dishes per hour** — no matter how skilled. Now imagine a **brigade of 8 chefs**, each handling a small specialized task (sauces, grilling, plating, garnishing), all communicating by calling out "behind!", "hot!", "corner!" through a strict protocol. 
> 
> Although **no single chef is extraordinary**, their **collective output** (e.g., 90 dishes per hour) transforms the kitchen into a **single high-performance system**. 
> - The **head chef** acts as the **master node** assigning tasks.
> - The **intercom system** acts as the **interconnect**.
> - The **standardized recipes** act as the **middleware**.
> - The **single bill presented to the customer** represents the **Single System Image (SSI)**.

This is exactly what cluster computing achieves: many **ordinary commodity machines** become **one extraordinary supercomputer** through coordination.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Terminology

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Node** | An individual compute unit (workstation/PC/server) in the cluster | Building block |
| **Head/Master Node** | Node responsible for task scheduling, monitoring, and resource allocation | Central coordinator |
| **Compute Node** | Worker node that executes the parallel tasks | Worker unit |
| **Interconnect** | Network fabric (Ethernet, InfiniBand, Myrinet) linking the nodes | Communication backbone |
| **Single System Image (SSI)** | Illusion presented to the user that the cluster is one machine | Key design goal |
| **Middleware** | Software layer providing services like RPC, process migration, load balancing | Glue layer |
| **Beowulf** | A class of Linux-based commodity clusters using COTS hardware | Reference architecture |
| **Cluster Node Density** | Number of nodes per rack unit, often measured as **nodes per RU** | Scalability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Cluster Performance Metric:** Throughput is usually expressed in **GFLOPs** ($10^9$ floating-point operations per second), **TFLOPs**, or **PFLOPs** for the aggregate cluster.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Cluster

A cluster operates through **five tightly coupled layers** of interaction. Each layer is mandatory; the absence of any one breaks the unified system illusion.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Layer (Physical Nodes)
- Each node is a complete, independent computer with its own **CPU**, **RAM**, **disk**, and **NIC** (Network Interface Card).
- Nodes are **commodity hardware** (COTS — Commercial Off-The-Shelf) in Beowulf clusters, keeping cost low.
- The cluster achieves its power through **scale-out** (more boxes) rather than **scale-up** (bigger box).

### Layer 2 — Network Interconnect Layer
- Provides low-latency, high-bandwidth communication between nodes.
- Common technologies: **Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps)**, **10/40/100 Gigabit Ethernet**, **InfiniBand** (up to ~200 Gbps HDR/NDR), **Myrinet**.
- Topology choices: **bus**, **star**, **fat-tree**, **mesh**, **torus**.

### Layer 3 — Operating System Layer
- Each node runs a full independent OS (e.g., **Linux** — CentOS, RHEL, Ubuntu) so nodes remain **loosely coupled** and **fault-tolerant**.
- Linux clusters form the dominant KTU-referenced example due to open-source toolchains.

### Layer 4 — Middleware / Cluster Software Layer
- Provides **Single System Image (SSI)**, **High Availability (HA)**, **Load Balancing**, **Parallel Programming Libraries** (MPI, OpenMP, PVM).
- Examples: **MOSIX**, **OpenPBS / Torque**, **SLURM**, **SGE**, **Condor**, **Hadoop YARN** (in big-data clusters).

### Layer 5 — Applications and User Access Layer
- Users submit jobs via **schedulers**; the middleware distributes the workload.
- Applications are typically **parallelized** using **MPI (Message Passing Interface)** or **MapReduce-style** paradigms.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why It Exists | How It Functions |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Hardware | Need physical compute units | Independent commodity PCs/workstations |
| Interconnect | Nodes must talk to each other | LAN switches, NICs, InfiniBand HCA cards |
| OS | Manage local resources per node | Linux, FreeBSD, or Windows |
| Middleware | Hide distributed complexity | Globus Toolkit, MOSIX, SLURM |
| Application | Solve real problems | Parallel scientific, commercial, data workloads |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A cluster is **NOT a grid**. A **grid** federates **administratively distinct, geographically dispersed** organizations. A **cluster** is a **single administrative domain**, typically co-located in one room/rack.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $$S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = number of nodes |
| 2 | **Theoretical Max Speedup Limit** | $$S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$$ | When parallel portion dominates |
| 3 | **Cluster Efficiency** | $$E = \dfrac{S}{N} \times 100\%$$ | $E \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ if $f < 1$ |
| 4 | **Aggregate Throughput** | $$T_{\text{cluster}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i$$ | $T_i$ = throughput of node $i$ |
| 5 | **Interconnect Latency-bound Time** | $$T_{\text{comm}} = T_{\text{startup}} + \dfrac{M}{B}$$ | $T_{\text{startup}}$ (seconds), $B$ (bytes/s), $M$ = message size |
| 6 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $$\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{node}}}{N}$$ | Cluster of $N$ nodes is *less* reliable than one node! |
| 7 | **Availability** | $$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 8 | **Network Bandwidth (Bisection)** | $$B_{\text{bisection}} = \min \text{(cut)} \sum \text{edges}$$ | Sum of bandwidth across minimal cut |
| 9 | **Per-Node Cost Ratio** | $$\text{Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of 1 supercomputer}}{\text{Cost of } N \text{ cluster nodes}}$$ | Used to justify Beowulf economics |
| 10 | **Parallel Fraction Estimation** | $$f = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$$ | $f = 1$ means fully parallelizable |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** $\vert S_{\max} \vert$ is **never infinite** for any $f < 1$. Even with **1 million nodes**, the speedup is capped at $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. This is the central insight of Amdahl's Law — examiners test this directly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cluster computing underpins:
- **Scientific HPC:** Weather forecasting (NCAR), protein folding (Folding@home is distributed; Folding uses BOINC clusters).
- **Web Infrastructure:** Google Search originally ran on commodity clusters (the famous **Google File System / MapReduce** paper, 2003–2004).
- **Render Farms:** Pixar/ILM animation rendering on Linux clusters.
- **Big Data:** Hadoop/Spark clusters on commodity hardware.
- **DNS, Email, Database Replication:** Standard HA cluster use case.

> [!TIP]
> The economic principle is **"scale-out with COTS"** — buy 1000 cheap $1000 boxes instead of one $10 million supercomputer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup Ceiling from Amdahl's Law (Full Step-By-Step)

We derive the maximum possible speedup of a cluster when running a workload with a serial fraction.

**Given:**
- Total execution time on a single node: $T_1$
- Parallelizable fraction of the work: $f$ (where $0 \le f \le 1$)
- Serial (non-parallelizable) fraction: $(1 - f)$
- Number of nodes: $N$

**Step 1 — Decompose the workload on a single node.**

The single-node execution time $T_1$ is split into two components:

$$T_1 = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}}$$

By definition of $f$:

$$T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1$$

$$T_{\text{parallel}} = f \cdot T_1$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the parallel portion across $N$ nodes.**

The serial portion **cannot** be parallelized; it still takes $(1-f) T_1$ regardless of $N$. The parallel portion, however, is split evenly (assuming balanced load) over $N$ processors, so each takes $\dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$ time.

The total execution time on $N$ nodes is therefore:

$$T_N = (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Define Speedup $S$ as the ratio.**

$$S(N) = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N} = \dfrac{T_1}{(1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $T_1$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**, the central KTU formula.

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check (KTU-favorite example).**

Suppose $f = 0.95$ (95% parallel, 5% serial):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.95} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05} \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}$$

So **even with infinite nodes**, the maximum achievable speedup is **20×**. Adding more hardware beyond the point where the serial portion dominates is **useless** — this is the famous "diminishing returns" curve examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cluster MTBF (Why Bigger Clusters Fail More Often)

**Given:** A single node has MTBF $= M$ hours. A cluster has $N$ identical nodes operating in parallel, **any one of which must be alive for the cluster to function** (i.e., the cluster fails if any single node fails).

**Step 1 — Compute the failure rate of one node.**

$$\lambda_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{1}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cluster failure rate (series system reliability model).**

For independent exponential failures, the cluster's failure rate is the sum:

$$\lambda_{\text{cluster}} = N \cdot \lambda_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{N}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to obtain cluster MTBF.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} &= \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{cluster}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{N/M} \\
&= \dfrac{M}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A cluster of **100 nodes** each with MTBF of **10,000 hours** has an aggregate MTBF of only **100 hours** — it fails, on average, **once every 4 days** if there is no redundancy. This is why **fault-tolerance middleware** (job checkpointing, process migration, redundant storage) is essential, not optional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Statement:** "Scaling out always **decreases** MTBF unless the system is designed with redundancy and HA middleware."

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cluster vs Other Paradigms

| Feature | Single Computer | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|---------|-----------------|---------|------|-------|
| **Node Count** | 1 | 2 – 10,000+ | 10,000+ (cross-org) | Elastic, virtualized |
| **Coupling** | Tight | Loose (LAN) | Very Loose (WAN) | Loose, virtual |
| **Admin Domain** | One | One | Many | One (provider) |
| **Location** | One rack | One room/datacenter | Geographically distributed | Datacenters worldwide |
| **Failure Model** | Single point | Node failures common | Network/coord failures | Datacenter failures |
| **Cost Model** | CAPEX-heavy | CAPEX (low) | Shared/collaborative | OPEX (pay-per-use) |
| **Programming** | Sequential | MPI, OpenMP, PVM | Globus, gLite | REST APIs, MapReduce |
| **Single System Image?** | Native | Partial-to-Full SSI | No | Virtualized abstraction |
| **Example** | Workstation | Beowulf cluster | SETI@home | AWS EC2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A **grid is a "cluster of clusters"**; a **cloud is a "commercialized, virtualized, elastic grid/cluster."** Confusing these three will cost you 7 marks in a 14-mark question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

### Q1. Define cluster computing. List any **four advantages** of clusters over traditional supercomputers.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cluster computing is the use of **multiple interconnected, independent computers** (nodes) coordinated via **middleware** to function as a **single unified resource** for **high-performance**, **load-balanced**, or **highly available** computing.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Cost-effectiveness:** Built from **commodity off-the-shelf (COTS)** hardware, costing a fraction of a traditional supercomputer.
2. **Scalability:** New nodes can be added incrementally to increase capacity.
3. **High Availability:** Failure of one node does not collapse the system; HA middleware (e.g., Heartbeat) reroutes work.
4. **Reusability:** Existing PCs/workstations can be repurposed into compute nodes.
5. *Bonus:* Open-source software stack (Linux + MPI + SLURM) eliminates licensing costs.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M + 4 advantages × 0.5M = 3M.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **High-Performance (HPC) clusters** and **High-Availability (HA) clusters**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Maximum computational throughput | Maximum uptime / minimal downtime |
| **Workload Type** | Tightly coupled parallel jobs (MPI) | Independent service requests |
| **Architecture** | Homogeneous compute nodes | Often active-passive or active-active |
| **Failure Response** | Checkpoint/restart job | Failover to standby node |
| **Example** | Beowulf for scientific simulation | Database with hot-standby replica |
| **Metric of Merit** | GFLOPS, speedup, efficiency | Availability % (e.g., 99.999%) |

> **Mark Distribution:** 3 differences × 1M = 3M.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **Explain the architecture of a Beowulf cluster with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each component.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Beowulf cluster** is a class of commodity-hardware Linux clusters pioneered at NASA in 1994. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Master Node (Head/Front-End):**
   - Controls the cluster, runs the job scheduler (e.g., **SLURM, Torque, PBS**).
   - Acts as the gateway for user logins and job submission.
   - Houses configuration files and distributes work to compute nodes.

2. **Compute Nodes (Workers):**
   - Pure worker units, typically diskless or with local scratch storage.
   - Boot via PXE (Preboot Execution Environment) from the master.
   - Run parallel jobs orchestrated by MPI/Message Passing libraries.

3. **Interconnect Network:**
   - Typically **Gigabit Ethernet**; can scale to **InfiniBand** for HPC.
   - Provides low-latency, high-bandwidth communication for MPI messages.
   - Often a dedicated private LAN segregated from external traffic.

4. **Middleware / Cluster Software:**
   - **SLURM / Torque** (scheduling), **MPI** (parallel communication), **NFS** (shared filesystem), **Heartbeat** (HA).
   - Provides the **Single System Image (SSI)** illusion.

5. **Shared Storage:**
   - **NFS, Lustre, or GlusterFS** mounted across nodes for shared /home and shared job data.
   - Critical because compute nodes are often diskless.

**Diagram (to be drawn in the exam):**

```
   [ Users ]
       |
   [ Master Node ]  <----- SSH / Job Submit -----
       |
   [ Switch / Interconnect ]
   |    |    |    |
  [N1] [N2] [N3] [N4]   <----- Compute Nodes (MPI)
       |
   [ Shared Storage: NFS / Lustre ]
```

> **Mark Split:** Master Node 1.5M + Compute Nodes 1.5M + Interconnect 1M + Middleware 1.5M + Shared Storage 1M + Diagram 0.5M = 7M.

---

#### (b) **Apply Amdahl's Law to a workload with parallel fraction $f = 0.90$ on (i) $N = 4$ nodes and (ii) $N = 64$ nodes. Comment on the result.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Amdahl's Law states:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$

Given $f = 0.90$, therefore $(1 - f) = 0.10$.

**Case (i): $N = 4$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.08
\end{aligned}$$

**Case (ii): $N = 64$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{64}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.0140625} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.1140625} \\
&\approx 8.77
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Theoretical Speedup:**

$$S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

**Comment:**
- Going from 4 to 64 nodes (a **16×** increase in hardware) only improved speedup from **3.08× to 8.77×** (a **2.85×** improvement).
- The remaining **10% serial portion** is the bottleneck.
- Adding nodes beyond ~64 yields negligible gains; the cluster hits the **10× speedup ceiling** dictated by Amdahl's Law.
- **Engineering implication:** To scale further, the **serial portion of the code must be reduced**, not the cluster size.

> **Mark Split:** Formula 1M + Case (i) 2M + Case (ii) 2M + Max speedup 1M + Comment 1M = 7M.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

#### (a) **Compare and contrast Cluster Computing and Grid Computing across 6 key parameters. State at least two application domains for each.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Cluster Computing | Grid Computing |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Geographic Spread** | Single datacenter/room | Geographically dispersed, multi-org |
| 2 | **Administrative Domain** | Single owner | Multiple independent organizations |
| 3 | **Coupling** | Tight (LAN) | Loose (WAN, Internet) |
| 4 | **Resources** | Homogeneous (often Linux) | Heterogeneous (different OS/hardware) |
| 5 | **Scheduling** | Centralized (SLURM) | Decentralized (Globus, Condor-G) |
| 6 | **Middleware** | MPI, OpenPBS, Beowulf | Globus Toolkit, gLite, BOINC |
| 7 | **Trust Model** | Trusted internal | Cross-organizational trust required |
| 8 | **Failure Handling** | Local node failures | Network/coordination failures |
| 9 | **Throughput Focus** | Tightly-coupled HPC | High-throughput, embarrassingly parallel |
| 10 | **Example** | Beowulf at NASA | SETI@home, EGEE Grid |

**Cluster Applications:** (i) Weather simulation (NCAR), (ii) Bio-informatics BLAST search.
**Grid Applications:** (i) SETI@home, (ii) CERN LHC data analysis (Worldwide LHC Computing Grid).

> **Mark Split:** 6 parameters × 0.5M = 3M + 2 domains × 0.5M per paradigm = 2M + explanation 2M = 7M.

---

#### (b) **Explain the working of MPI-based message passing in a cluster. Sketch a typical MPI program structure and describe three collective communication primitives.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**MPI (Message Passing Interface)** is the de-facto standard for writing parallel programs that run across cluster nodes. Each parallel process has its own memory space and exchanges data via explicit `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` calls.

**Typical Program Structure (to be written in the exam):**

```
1.  #include <mpi.h>
2.  int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
3.      int rank, size;
4.      MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);                // Initialize MPI
5.      MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);  // Get process ID
6.      MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);  // Get total processes
7.
8.      if (rank == 0) {
9.          // Master: send work
10.         MPI_Send(data, count, MPI_INT, 1, tag, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
11.     } else {
12.         // Worker: receive work
13.         MPI_Recv(data, count, MPI_INT, 0, tag, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
14.     }
15.
16.     // ... compute ...
17.
18.     MPI_Finalize();                        // Shutdown MPI
19. }
20. mpiexec -n 8 ./program                     // Launch 8 processes
```

> **Note (KTU non-CS-friendly):** Pseudo-code may be written on paper; the actual MPI launch command is `mpiexec -np 8 ./a.out`.

**Three Collective Communication Primitives:**

1. **`MPI_Bcast` (Broadcast):** Master sends the same data to all other processes.
   - Communication pattern: **1 → N**.
   - Use case: distributing configuration, initial problem data.

2. **`MPI_Reduce` / `MPI_Allreduce`:** Combines (sum, max, min, etc.) values from all processes into one (or all) result(s).
   - Communication pattern: **N → 1** (or **N → N**).
   - Use case: summing partial results across cluster nodes after a parallel phase.

3. **`MPI_Barrier`:** Synchronization point — all processes wait until every process reaches this call.
   - Use case: ensuring phase-by-phase execution (e.g., after parallel work, before global reduction).

> **Mark Split:** MPI structure 2M + 3 primitives × 1.5M = 4.5M + launch explanation 0.5M = 7M.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Cluster Computing Questions**
> 
> 1. **Defining cluster as "grid" or vice versa:** Loss of **2–3 marks** for wrong definition in (a) part.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1 - f$ term in Amdahl's Law:** Many students write $S = N/f$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$.
> 3. **Claiming "more nodes = better forever":** Demonstrates ignorance of Amdahl's ceiling; **deduct 1 mark** for the missing critical comment.
> 4. **Confusing MPI primitives:** `MPI_Send` (point-to-point) ≠ `MPI_Bcast` (collective). Examiners **will** deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark sub-parts asking for a "neat diagram," **0.5 to 1 mark** is reserved for the figure — never omit it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cluster = multiple independent computers acting as one**, coordinated by middleware over a LAN-speed interconnect.
- **Beowulf** is the canonical COTS Linux cluster reference architecture.
- **Single System Image (SSI)** is the central design goal — illusion of a unified system.
- **Three main cluster types:** HPC (performance), Load Balancing, High Availability.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$ — speedup is **bounded** by $1/(1-f)$.
- **MTBF of cluster = MTBF of single node / N** — bigger cluster, more frequent failures (without HA).
- **Cluster ≠ Grid ≠ Cloud:** Cluster is local/admin-single; Grid is multi-admin/WAN; Cloud is virtualized/pay-per-use.
- **MPI** is the standard programming model for tightly-coupled parallel cluster jobs.
- **Middleware components:** Scheduler (SLURM), Job Launcher (mpiexec), Filesystem (NFS/Lustre), HA (Heartbeat).
- **Standard reference metric:** GFLOPS, speedup, efficiency, availability %.
- **Interconnect choices:** Gigabit Ethernet (cost) vs InfiniBand (performance) — bandwidth and latency dictate application fit.
- **Famous real-world Beowulf example:** The **Stone Soupercomputer** (1997, NASA) — first Linux cluster to crack the TOP500 list.</mm:think>### Quick Navigation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cluster Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm in which a group of loosely coupled, independently operational computers (referred to as **nodes**) work together closely so that, in many respects, they can be viewed as a **single unified computing system**. The constituent nodes collaborate through a high-speed **interconnection network** and are coordinated by a combination of **cluster middleware**, **operating system extensions**, and **protocols/services** to deliver combined computational power, storage capacity, and high availability far beyond what a single machine can provide.

The conceptual roots of cluster computing trace back to the 1960s with **IBM's mainframe channel-attached systems** and the pioneering **DEC VAXcluster** (1980s). Modern academic and industrial deployments adopt the **Beowulf architecture** (proposed by Thomas Sterling and Donald Becker at NASA, 1994) as the canonical design pattern for commodity-hardware Linux clusters.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Restaurant Brigade System**
> 
> Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen during dinner rush. **One single chef** working alone (a traditional standalone server) can only prepare, say, **10 dishes per hour** — no matter how skilled. Now imagine a **brigade of 8 chefs**, each handling a small specialized task (sauces, grilling, plating, garnishing), all communicating by calling out "behind!", "hot!", "corner!" through a strict protocol. 
> 
> Although **no single chef is extraordinary**, their **collective output** (e.g., 90 dishes per hour) transforms the kitchen into a **single high-performance system**. 
> - The **head chef** acts as the **master node** assigning tasks.
> - The **intercom system** acts as the **interconnect**.
> - The **standardized recipes** act as the **middleware**.
> - The **single bill presented to the customer** represents the **Single System Image (SSI)**.

This is exactly what cluster computing achieves: many **ordinary commodity machines** become **one extraordinary supercomputer** through coordination.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Terminology

| Term | Definition | KTU Significance |
|------|------------|------------------|
| **Node** | An individual compute unit (workstation/PC/server) in the cluster | Building block |
| **Head/Master Node** | Node responsible for task scheduling, monitoring, and resource allocation | Central coordinator |
| **Compute Node** | Worker node that executes the parallel tasks | Worker unit |
| **Interconnect** | Network fabric (Ethernet, InfiniBand, Myrinet) linking the nodes | Communication backbone |
| **Single System Image (SSI)** | Illusion presented to the user that the cluster is one machine | Key design goal |
| **Middleware** | Software layer providing services like RPC, process migration, load balancing | Glue layer |
| **Beowulf** | A class of Linux-based commodity clusters using COTS hardware | Reference architecture |
| **Cluster Node Density** | Number of nodes per rack unit, often measured as **nodes per RU** | Scalability metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Cluster Performance Metric:** Throughput is usually expressed in **GFLOPs** ($10^9$ floating-point operations per second), **TFLOPs**, or **PFLOPs** for the aggregate cluster.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Cluster

A cluster operates through **five tightly coupled layers** of interaction. Each layer is mandatory; the absence of any one breaks the unified system illusion.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Layer (Physical Nodes)
- Each node is a complete, independent computer with its own **CPU**, **RAM**, **disk**, and **NIC** (Network Interface Card).
- Nodes are **commodity hardware** (COTS — Commercial Off-The-Shelf) in Beowulf clusters, keeping cost low.
- The cluster achieves its power through **scale-out** (more boxes) rather than **scale-up** (bigger box).

### Layer 2 — Network Interconnect Layer
- Provides low-latency, high-bandwidth communication between nodes.
- Common technologies: **Gigabit Ethernet (1 Gbps)**, **10/40/100 Gigabit Ethernet**, **InfiniBand** (up to ~200 Gbps HDR/NDR), **Myrinet**.
- Topology choices: **bus**, **star**, **fat-tree**, **mesh**, **torus**.

### Layer 3 — Operating System Layer
- Each node runs a full independent OS (e.g., **Linux** — CentOS, RHEL, Ubuntu) so nodes remain **loosely coupled** and **fault-tolerant**.
- Linux clusters form the dominant KTU-referenced example due to open-source toolchains.

### Layer 4 — Middleware / Cluster Software Layer
- Provides **Single System Image (SSI)**, **High Availability (HA)**, **Load Balancing**, **Parallel Programming Libraries** (MPI, OpenMP, PVM).
- Examples: **MOSIX**, **OpenPBS / Torque**, **SLURM**, **SGE**, **Condor**, **Hadoop YARN** (in big-data clusters).

### Layer 5 — Applications and User Access Layer
- Users submit jobs via **schedulers**; the middleware distributes the workload.
- Applications are typically **parallelized** using **MPI (Message Passing Interface)** or **MapReduce-style** paradigms.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why It Exists | How It Functions |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| Hardware | Need physical compute units | Independent commodity PCs/workstations |
| Interconnect | Nodes must talk to each other | LAN switches, NICs, InfiniBand HCA cards |
| OS | Manage local resources per node | Linux, FreeBSD, or Windows |
| Middleware | Hide distributed complexity | Globus Toolkit, MOSIX, SLURM |
| Application | Solve real problems | Parallel scientific, commercial, data workloads |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A cluster is **NOT a grid**. A **grid** federates **administratively distinct, geographically dispersed** organizations. A **cluster** is a **single administrative domain**, typically co-located in one room/rack.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Speedup (Amdahl's Law)** | $$S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $N$ = number of nodes |
| 2 | **Theoretical Max Speedup Limit** | $$S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1-f}$$ | When parallel portion dominates |
| 3 | **Cluster Efficiency** | $$E = \dfrac{S}{N} \times 100\%$$ | $E \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$ if $f < 1$ |
| 4 | **Aggregate Throughput** | $$T_{\text{cluster}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_i$$ | $T_i$ = throughput of node $i$ |
| 5 | **Interconnect Latency-bound Time** | $$T_{\text{comm}} = T_{\text{startup}} + \dfrac{M}{B}$$ | $T_{\text{startup}}$ (seconds), $B$ (bytes/s), $M$ = message size |
| 6 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $$\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_{\text{node}}}{N}$$ | Cluster of $N$ nodes is *less* reliable than one node! |
| 7 | **Availability** | $$A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$ | MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 8 | **Network Bandwidth (Bisection)** | $$B_{\text{bisection}} = \min \text{(cut)} \sum \text{edges}$$ | Sum of bandwidth across minimal cut |
| 9 | **Per-Node Cost Ratio** | $$\text{Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of 1 supercomputer}}{\text{Cost of } N \text{ cluster nodes}}$$ | Used to justify Beowulf economics |
| 10 | **Parallel Fraction Estimation** | $$f = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$$ | $f = 1$ means fully parallelizable |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** $\vert S_{\max} \vert$ is **never infinite** for any $f < 1$. Even with **1 million nodes**, the speedup is capped at $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. This is the central insight of Amdahl's Law — examiners test this directly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cluster computing underpins:
- **Scientific HPC:** Weather forecasting (NCAR), protein folding (Folding@home is distributed; Folding uses BOINC clusters).
- **Web Infrastructure:** Google Search originally ran on commodity clusters (the famous **Google File System / MapReduce** paper, 2003–2004).
- **Render Farms:** Pixar/ILM animation rendering on Linux clusters.
- **Big Data:** Hadoop/Spark clusters on commodity hardware.
- **DNS, Email, Database Replication:** Standard HA cluster use case.

> [!TIP]
> The economic principle is **"scale-out with COTS"** — buy 1000 cheap $1000 boxes instead of one $10 million supercomputer.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Speedup Ceiling from Amdahl's Law (Full Step-By-Step)

We derive the maximum possible speedup of a cluster when running a workload with a serial fraction.

**Given:**
- Total execution time on a single node: $T_1$
- Parallelizable fraction of the work: $f$ (where $0 \le f \le 1$)
- Serial (non-parallelizable) fraction: $(1 - f)$
- Number of nodes: $N$

**Step 1 — Decompose the workload on a single node.**

The single-node execution time $T_1$ is split into two components:

$$T_1 = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}}$$

By definition of $f$:

$$T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - f) \cdot T_1$$

$$T_{\text{parallel}} = f \cdot T_1$$

**Step 2 — Distribute the parallel portion across $N$ nodes.**

The serial portion **cannot** be parallelized; it still takes $(1-f) T_1$ regardless of $N$. The parallel portion, however, is split evenly (assuming balanced load) over $N$ processors, so each takes $\dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$ time.

The total execution time on $N$ nodes is therefore:

$$T_N = (1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}$$

**Step 3 — Define Speedup $S$ as the ratio.**

$$S(N) = \dfrac{T_1}{T_N} = \dfrac{T_1}{(1 - f) \cdot T_1 + \dfrac{f \cdot T_1}{N}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $T_1$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(N) &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Amdahl's Law**, the central KTU formula.

**Step 5 — Take the limit as $N \to \infty$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + 0} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - f}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check (KTU-favorite example).**

Suppose $f = 0.95$ (95% parallel, 5% serial):

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.95} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.05} \\
&= 20
\end{aligned}$$

So **even with infinite nodes**, the maximum achievable speedup is **20×**. Adding more hardware beyond the point where the serial portion dominates is **useless** — this is the famous "diminishing returns" curve examiners love to test.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cluster MTBF (Why Bigger Clusters Fail More Often)

**Given:** A single node has MTBF $= M$ hours. A cluster has $N$ identical nodes operating in parallel, **any one of which must be alive for the cluster to function** (i.e., the cluster fails if any single node fails).

**Step 1 — Compute the failure rate of one node.**

$$\lambda_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{1}{M}$$

**Step 2 — Compute cluster failure rate (series system reliability model).**

For independent exponential failures, the cluster's failure rate is the sum:

$$\lambda_{\text{cluster}} = N \cdot \lambda_{\text{node}} = \dfrac{N}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Invert to obtain cluster MTBF.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MTBF}_{\text{cluster}} &= \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{\text{cluster}}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{N/M} \\
&= \dfrac{M}{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A cluster of **100 nodes** each with MTBF of **10,000 hours** has an aggregate MTBF of only **100 hours** — it fails, on average, **once every 4 days** if there is no redundancy. This is why **fault-tolerance middleware** (job checkpointing, process migration, redundant storage) is essential, not optional.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Winning Statement:** "Scaling out always **decreases** MTBF unless the system is designed with redundancy and HA middleware."

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cluster vs Other Paradigms

| Feature | Single Computer | Cluster | Grid | Cloud |
|---------|-----------------|---------|------|-------|
| **Node Count** | 1 | 2 – 10,000+ | 10,000+ (cross-org) | Elastic, virtualized |
| **Coupling** | Tight | Loose (LAN) | Very Loose (WAN) | Loose, virtual |
| **Admin Domain** | One | One | Many | One (provider) |
| **Location** | One rack | One room/datacenter | Geographically distributed | Datacenters worldwide |
| **Failure Model** | Single point | Node failures common | Network/coord failures | Datacenter failures |
| **Cost Model** | CAPEX-heavy | CAPEX (low) | Shared/collaborative | OPEX (pay-per-use) |
| **Programming** | Sequential | MPI, OpenMP, PVM | Globus, gLite | REST APIs, MapReduce |
| **Single System Image?** | Native | Partial-to-Full SSI | No | Virtualized abstraction |
| **Example** | Workstation | Beowulf cluster | SETI@home | AWS EC2 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A **grid is a "cluster of clusters"**; a **cloud is a "commercialized, virtualized, elastic grid/cluster."** Confusing these three will cost you 7 marks in a 14-mark question.
