# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

### Q1. Define cluster computing. List any **four advantages** of clusters over traditional supercomputers.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Cluster computing is the use of **multiple interconnected, independent computers** (nodes) coordinated via **middleware** to function as a **single unified resource** for **high-performance**, **load-balanced**, or **highly available** computing.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Cost-effectiveness:** Built from **commodity off-the-shelf (COTS)** hardware, costing a fraction of a traditional supercomputer.
2. **Scalability:** New nodes can be added incrementally to increase capacity.
3. **High Availability:** Failure of one node does not collapse the system; HA middleware (e.g., Heartbeat) reroutes work.
4. **Reusability:** Existing PCs/workstations can be repurposed into compute nodes.
5. *Bonus:* Open-source software stack (Linux + MPI + SLURM) eliminates licensing costs.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition 1M + 4 advantages × 0.5M = 3M.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **High-Performance (HPC) clusters** and **High-Availability (HA) clusters**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HPC Cluster | HA Cluster |
|--------|-------------|------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Maximum computational throughput | Maximum uptime / minimal downtime |
| **Workload Type** | Tightly coupled parallel jobs (MPI) | Independent service requests |
| **Architecture** | Homogeneous compute nodes | Often active-passive or active-active |
| **Failure Response** | Checkpoint/restart job | Failover to standby node |
| **Example** | Beowulf for scientific simulation | Database with hot-standby replica |
| **Metric of Merit** | GFLOPS, speedup, efficiency | Availability % (e.g., 99.999%) |

> **Mark Distribution:** 3 differences × 1M = 3M.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) **Explain the architecture of a Beowulf cluster with a neat diagram. Describe the role of each component.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The **Beowulf cluster** is a class of commodity-hardware Linux clusters pioneered at NASA in 1994. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Master Node (Head/Front-End):**
   - Controls the cluster, runs the job scheduler (e.g., **SLURM, Torque, PBS**).
   - Acts as the gateway for user logins and job submission.
   - Houses configuration files and distributes work to compute nodes.

2. **Compute Nodes (Workers):**
   - Pure worker units, typically diskless or with local scratch storage.
   - Boot via PXE (Preboot Execution Environment) from the master.
   - Run parallel jobs orchestrated by MPI/Message Passing libraries.

3. **Interconnect Network:**
   - Typically **Gigabit Ethernet**; can scale to **InfiniBand** for HPC.
   - Provides low-latency, high-bandwidth communication for MPI messages.
   - Often a dedicated private LAN segregated from external traffic.

4. **Middleware / Cluster Software:**
   - **SLURM / Torque** (scheduling), **MPI** (parallel communication), **NFS** (shared filesystem), **Heartbeat** (HA).
   - Provides the **Single System Image (SSI)** illusion.

5. **Shared Storage:**
   - **NFS, Lustre, or GlusterFS** mounted across nodes for shared /home and shared job data.
   - Critical because compute nodes are often diskless.

**Diagram (to be drawn in the exam):**

```
   [ Users ]
       |
   [ Master Node ]  <----- SSH / Job Submit -----
       |
   [ Switch / Interconnect ]
   |    |    |    |
  [N1] [N2] [N3] [N4]   <----- Compute Nodes (MPI)
       |
   [ Shared Storage: NFS / Lustre ]
```

> **Mark Split:** Master Node 1.5M + Compute Nodes 1.5M + Interconnect 1M + Middleware 1.5M + Shared Storage 1M + Diagram 0.5M = 7M.

---

#### (b) **Apply Amdahl's Law to a workload with parallel fraction $f = 0.90$ on (i) $N = 4$ nodes and (ii) $N = 64$ nodes. Comment on the result.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Amdahl's Law states:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$

Given $f = 0.90$, therefore $(1 - f) = 0.10$.

**Case (i): $N = 4$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.225} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.325} \\
&\approx 3.08
\end{aligned}$$

**Case (ii): $N = 64$**

$$\begin{aligned}
S(64) &= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + \dfrac{0.90}{64}} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.0140625} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{0.1140625} \\
&\approx 8.77
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Theoretical Speedup:**

$$S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10$$

**Comment:**
- Going from 4 to 64 nodes (a **16×** increase in hardware) only improved speedup from **3.08× to 8.77×** (a **2.85×** improvement).
- The remaining **10% serial portion** is the bottleneck.
- Adding nodes beyond ~64 yields negligible gains; the cluster hits the **10× speedup ceiling** dictated by Amdahl's Law.
- **Engineering implication:** To scale further, the **serial portion of the code must be reduced**, not the cluster size.

> **Mark Split:** Formula 1M + Case (i) 2M + Case (ii) 2M + Max speedup 1M + Comment 1M = 7M.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

#### (a) **Compare and contrast Cluster Computing and Grid Computing across 6 key parameters. State at least two application domains for each.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Cluster Computing | Grid Computing |
|---|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Geographic Spread** | Single datacenter/room | Geographically dispersed, multi-org |
| 2 | **Administrative Domain** | Single owner | Multiple independent organizations |
| 3 | **Coupling** | Tight (LAN) | Loose (WAN, Internet) |
| 4 | **Resources** | Homogeneous (often Linux) | Heterogeneous (different OS/hardware) |
| 5 | **Scheduling** | Centralized (SLURM) | Decentralized (Globus, Condor-G) |
| 6 | **Middleware** | MPI, OpenPBS, Beowulf | Globus Toolkit, gLite, BOINC |
| 7 | **Trust Model** | Trusted internal | Cross-organizational trust required |
| 8 | **Failure Handling** | Local node failures | Network/coordination failures |
| 9 | **Throughput Focus** | Tightly-coupled HPC | High-throughput, embarrassingly parallel |
| 10 | **Example** | Beowulf at NASA | SETI@home, EGEE Grid |

**Cluster Applications:** (i) Weather simulation (NCAR), (ii) Bio-informatics BLAST search.
**Grid Applications:** (i) SETI@home, (ii) CERN LHC data analysis (Worldwide LHC Computing Grid).

> **Mark Split:** 6 parameters × 0.5M = 3M + 2 domains × 0.5M per paradigm = 2M + explanation 2M = 7M.

---

#### (b) **Explain the working of MPI-based message passing in a cluster. Sketch a typical MPI program structure and describe three collective communication primitives.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**MPI (Message Passing Interface)** is the de-facto standard for writing parallel programs that run across cluster nodes. Each parallel process has its own memory space and exchanges data via explicit `MPI_Send` / `MPI_Recv` calls.

**Typical Program Structure (to be written in the exam):**

```
1.  #include <mpi.h>
2.  int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
3.      int rank, size;
4.      MPI_Init(&argc, &argv);                // Initialize MPI
5.      MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank);  // Get process ID
6.      MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size);  // Get total processes
7.
8.      if (rank == 0) {
9.          // Master: send work
10.         MPI_Send(data, count, MPI_INT, 1, tag, MPI_COMM_WORLD);
11.     } else {
12.         // Worker: receive work
13.         MPI_Recv(data, count, MPI_INT, 0, tag, MPI_COMM_WORLD, &status);
14.     }
15.
16.     // ... compute ...
17.
18.     MPI_Finalize();                        // Shutdown MPI
19. }
20. mpiexec -n 8 ./program                     // Launch 8 processes
```

> **Note (KTU non-CS-friendly):** Pseudo-code may be written on paper; the actual MPI launch command is `mpiexec -np 8 ./a.out`.

**Three Collective Communication Primitives:**

1. **`MPI_Bcast` (Broadcast):** Master sends the same data to all other processes.
   - Communication pattern: **1 → N**.
   - Use case: distributing configuration, initial problem data.

2. **`MPI_Reduce` / `MPI_Allreduce`:** Combines (sum, max, min, etc.) values from all processes into one (or all) result(s).
   - Communication pattern: **N → 1** (or **N → N**).
   - Use case: summing partial results across cluster nodes after a parallel phase.

3. **`MPI_Barrier`:** Synchronization point — all processes wait until every process reaches this call.
   - Use case: ensuring phase-by-phase execution (e.g., after parallel work, before global reduction).

> **Mark Split:** MPI structure 2M + 3 primitives × 1.5M = 4.5M + launch explanation 0.5M = 7M.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Cluster Computing Questions**
> 
> 1. **Defining cluster as "grid" or vice versa:** Loss of **2–3 marks** for wrong definition in (a) part.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1 - f$ term in Amdahl's Law:** Many students write $S = N/f$ — this is **WRONG**. The correct formula is $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$.
> 3. **Claiming "more nodes = better forever":** Demonstrates ignorance of Amdahl's ceiling; **deduct 1 mark** for the missing critical comment.
> 4. **Confusing MPI primitives:** `MPI_Send` (point-to-point) ≠ `MPI_Bcast` (collective). Examiners **will** deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark sub-parts asking for a "neat diagram," **0.5 to 1 mark** is reserved for the figure — never omit it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cluster = multiple independent computers acting as one**, coordinated by middleware over a LAN-speed interconnect.
- **Beowulf** is the canonical COTS Linux cluster reference architecture.
- **Single System Image (SSI)** is the central design goal — illusion of a unified system.
- **Three main cluster types:** HPC (performance), Load Balancing, High Availability.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1 / [(1-f) + f/N]$ — speedup is **bounded** by $1/(1-f)$.
- **MTBF of cluster = MTBF of single node / N** — bigger cluster, more frequent failures (without HA).
- **Cluster ≠ Grid ≠ Cloud:** Cluster is local/admin-single; Grid is multi-admin/WAN; Cloud is virtualized/pay-per-use.
- **MPI** is the standard programming model for tightly-coupled parallel cluster jobs.
- **Middleware components:** Scheduler (SLURM), Job Launcher (mpiexec), Filesystem (NFS/Lustre), HA (Heartbeat).
- **Standard reference metric:** GFLOPS, speedup, efficiency, availability %.
- **Interconnect choices:** Gigabit Ethernet (cost) vs InfiniBand (performance) — bandwidth and latency dictate application fit.
- **Famous real-world Beowulf example:** The **Stone Soupercomputer** (1997, NASA) — first Linux cluster to crack the TOP500 list.

topics.title: Distributed Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Distributed Computing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition A **Distributed System** is a collection of independent, autonomous, and heterogeneous computers that are connected through a network and equipped with **middleware** software that enables them to coordinate their activities so that users perceive the system as a single, coherent computing entity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Andrew S. Tanenbaum):** > *"A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system."* > > This single sentence is the most frequently quoted definition in KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) answers and must be reproduced verbatim for full marks. ### Key Characteristics of Distributed Computing - **Concurrency** – Multiple machines perform tasks simultaneously. - **No Global Clock** – Coordination is achieved through logical clocks, not physical synchronization. - **Independent Failures** – Each node can fail without bringing down the entire system. - **Heterogeneity** – Components can differ in hardware, OS, programming language, and network. - **Openness** – Systems are extensible and standardized through protocols (e.g., TCP/IP, HTTP). - **Scalability** – System should remain effective as the number of nodes/users increases. ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Intuition: The Restaurant Kitchen** > > Imagine a busy restaurant. If only **one chef** (a single computer) prepares all dishes, customers wait a long time. But when the kitchen is reorganized into **multiple stations** — one for starters, one for mains, one for desserts — with a **head chef (middleware)** coordinating the order flow, the restaurant serves food **faster and reliably** even if one station breaks down. > > - The **customers** (end-users) only see the final dish — they are *unaware* of the underlying complexity. This is **transparency**. > - The **head chef** plays the role of **middleware** managing distributed resources. > - The **failure of one station** does not stop the entire kitchen. This is **independent failure handling**. ### Centralized vs Distributed vs Parallel Computing | Aspect | Centralized | Parallel | **Distributed** | |--------|-------------|----------|-----------------| | Number of CPUs | One | Many (shared memory) | **Many (independent nodes)** | | Memory | Single | Shared | **Distributed (private)** | | Communication | None | Bus/Shared | **Network (message passing)** | | Geographic Span | Single location | Single machine/rack | **Wide area possible** | | Failure Impact | Total failure | Partial | **Independent failures** | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Differentiate between Parallel and Distributed Computing."* Memorize the table above — the **memory model (shared vs distributed)** and **communication mechanism (bus vs network)** are the standard scoring keywords. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Goals of Distributed Computing (KTU High-Yield – 14-Mark Favorites) A distributed system is engineered to satisfy **eight primary goals**: 1. **Making Resources Accessible** – Share hardware, data, and services across the network. 2. **Distribution Transparency** – Hide the fact that processes and resources are physically distributed. 3. **Openness** – Standardized interfaces for interoperability. 4. **Scalability** – Adapt to increasing load without performance degradation. 5. **Handling Concurrency** – Multiple clients must access shared resources safely. 6. **Failure Handling** – Mask partial failures using redundancy and replication. 7. **Security** – Confidentiality, integrity, and authentication in an open environment. 8. **Mobility** – Support for portable/handheld devices that roam across networks. ### 2.2 The Eight Types of Transparency (Highly Tested) | # | Transparency | Description | Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Access** | Hide differences in data representation | UNIX vs Windows files | | 2 | **Location** | Hide physical location of resource | URL of a web page | | 3 | **Migration** | Hide that a resource may move to another location | Mobile agent systems | | 4 | **Relocation** | Hide that a resource is being moved while in use | Live VM migration | | 5 | **Replication** | Hide that a resource is replicated | Cloud storage (S3) | | 6 | **Concurrency** | Hide that a resource is shared | Concurrent file access | | 7 | **Failure** | Hide failure and recovery of a resource | DNS failover | | 8 | **Persistence** | Hide whether a resource is in memory or disk | Database abstraction | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Explain different types of transparency with examples."* The acronym **A-L-M-R-R-C-F-P** (Access, Location, Migration, Relocation, Replication, Concurrency, Failure, Persistence) is the standard memory aid used in the valuation key. ### 2.3 Software Architecture Models Distributed systems are organized using three principal architectural styles: - **Client–Server Model (Two-Tier):** A client sends requests; a server processes and returns responses. *Example: Web browsers and Apache servers.* - **Multi-Tier Model:** Splits the application into presentation, application processing, and data management layers. *Example: Three-tier web apps (Browser → App Server → DB).* - **Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Model:** All nodes act as both clients and servers. *Example: BitTorrent, Blockchain.* - **Master–Slave Model:** A master node distributes tasks to slave nodes for parallel execution. *Example: Hadoop MapReduce.* ### 2.4 Middleware — The Backbone of Distribution **Middleware** is a software layer situated between the operating system and the application, providing: - Communication services (RPC, RMI, Message Queues) - Naming services (DNS, LDAP) - Transaction management - Security (Authentication, Encryption) - Coordination (locks, semaphores distributed across nodes) ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following performance metrics are the most frequently tested in PEITT523 Module 1 numerical problems. | Metric | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |--------|---------|-------------------|-----------------| | **Speedup** | $S(n) = \dfrac{T_{1}}{T_{n}}$ | $T_{1}$ = sequential time (sec), $T_{n}$ = parallel time with $n$ nodes | Measures how much faster the parallel system is | | **Efficiency** | $E(n) = \dfrac{S(n)}{n}$ | $n$ = number of processors, $0 \le E \le 1$ | Measures utilization per processor | | **Amdahl's Law** | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$ | $p$ = parallelizable fraction $(0 \le p \le 1)$ | Predicts maximum theoretical speedup | | **Amdahl's Limit** | $S_{\infty} = \dfrac{1}{1-p}$ | As $n \to \infty$ | Upper bound on speedup | | **Karp-Flatt Metric** | $f = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{S} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$ | $f$ = experimentally determined serial fraction | Diagnoses parallel overheads | | **Cost** | $C(n) = n \cdot T_{n}$ | $C$ = cost (processor-seconds) | Benchmarks hardware efficiency | | **Isoefficiency** | $W = \Theta(f(p) \cdot p \cdot K)$ | $W$ = total work, $K$ = problem size | Scalability indicator | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pipe-Character Rule:** When writing `|x|`, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` inside markdown tables. **Never** use the raw `|` symbol inside a table cell, as it will break table rendering. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Google Search** runs on thousands of commodity servers in a distributed setup. - **Amazon S3** uses distributed storage across multiple data centers. - **SETI@Home** uses volunteer distributed computing for radio signal analysis. - **Hadoop & Spark** — foundational distributed computing frameworks used in Big Data. - **Banking Systems** — Distributed databases ensure 24/7 availability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law (Complete Working Model) Amdahl's Law provides the theoretical maximum speedup achievable when only a portion of a program can be parallelized. The derivation below is the **exact format** expected in a KTU 14-mark answer. **Step 1 — Partition the total execution time.** Let the total execution time of a sequential program be $T$. We partition $T$ into two parts: $$T = T_{s} + T_{p}$$ where: - $T_{s}$ = time spent in the **serial (non-parallelizable)** portion. - $T_{p}$ = time spent in the **parallelizable** portion. **Step 2 — Introduce the serial fraction $f$.** Define $f$ as the fraction of the total work that is inherently serial: $$f = \frac{T_{s}}{T}, \quad 0 \le f \le 1$$ Consequently, the parallelizable fraction is: $$p = 1 - f = \frac{T_{p}}{T}$$ **Step 3 — Express each portion in terms of $T$ and $f$.** $$T_{s} = f \cdot T$$ $$T_{p} = (1 - f) \cdot T$$ **Step 4 — Apply parallel execution on $n$ processors.** The serial portion cannot be parallelized, so it still takes time $T_{s}$. The parallel portion is divided among $n$ processors: $$T_{n} = T_{s} + \frac{T_{p}}{n} = f \cdot T + \frac{(1 - f) \cdot T}{n}$$ **Step 5 — Compute Speedup $S(n)$.** By definition, $S(n) = \dfrac{T}{T_{n}}$. Divide numerator and denominator by $T$: $$S(n) = \frac{T}{f \cdot T + \dfrac{(1 - f) \cdot T}{n}}$$ Cancel $T$ from numerator and denominator: $$S(n) = \frac{1}{f + \dfrac{1 - f}{n}}$$ **Step 6 — Rewrite using parallel fraction $p = 1 - f$.** $$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}}$$ This is **Amdahl's Law** in its standard textbook form. **Step 7 — Compute the upper limit as $n \to \infty$.** $$\lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + 0} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** No matter how many processors you add, the speedup is **bounded** by the serial fraction. For example, if $p = 0.95$ (95% parallelizable), the **maximum achievable speedup is $1/0.05 = 20\times$**, regardless of cluster size. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Pattern) > **Question (Dec 2022 Pattern):** A program takes 100 seconds to run on a single processor. 30% of the program is inherently serial. Find (a) the speedup on 8 processors, and (b) the maximum achievable speedup. **Given:** - $T = 100$ seconds - Serial fraction $f = 0.30$ ⇒ Parallel fraction $p = 0.70$ - $n = 8$ **(a) Speedup on 8 processors:** $$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.70) + \dfrac{0.70}{8}}$$ $$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30 + 0.0875}$$ $$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.3875} \approx 2.58$$ **(b) Maximum achievable speedup ($n \to \infty$):** $$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.70} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33$$ **Efficiency:** $$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{n} = \frac{2.58}{8} \approx 0.3225 \;\; (32.25\%)$$ > [!TIP] > Always present the answer in a **three-line conclusion block** for full marks: (1) Speedup value, (2) Interpretation, (3) Efficiency check. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Karp-Flatt Metric The Karp-Flatt metric experimentally reveals the *actual* serial fraction in a parallel program (which is often larger than the code-level serial fraction due to overheads). **Step 1 — Rearrange Amdahl's Law.** $$S = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{n}} \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{1}{S} = (1 - p) + \frac{p}{n}$$ **Step 2 — Collect terms in $p$.** $$\frac{1}{S} = 1 - p + \frac{p}{n} = 1 - p \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the experimental serial fraction $f = 1 - p$.** $$p \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) = 1 - \frac{1}{S}$$ $$1 - p = \frac{1 - \dfrac{1}{S}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$$ $$f = \frac{\dfrac{1}{S} - \dfrac{1}{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{n}}$$ This is the **Karp-Flott metric** used to detect parallelization overheads. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Distributed System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[End User Devices
Laptops Phones Tablets] nodeB[Network Layer
Internet LAN WAN] nodeC[Middleware Layer
RPC RMI Naming Service Security] nodeD[Application Servers
Web Server App Server] nodeE[Database Cluster
Replicated Sharded] nodeF[Storage Cluster
Distributed File System] nodeG[Monitoring and Logging
SLA Manager] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE -.Replication.-> nodeF classDef user fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F6FEB,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef net fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef mid fill:#F0E6F5,stroke:#7B2CBF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef srv fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1E8E3E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef mon fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class nodeA user class nodeB net class nodeC mid class nodeD srv class nodeE srv class nodeF srv class nodeG mon ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The end-user (top) sends a request that travels through the **Network** to the **Middleware layer** (which provides RPC/RMI, naming, and security). The middleware then routes the request to the appropriate **Application Server**, which may access a **Database Cluster** or **Distributed File System**. The **Monitoring/Logging** layer observes the entire flow for SLA and fault management. ### 4.2 Master–Slave vs Peer-to-Peer Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph msBlock[Master Slave Architecture] direction TB m1[Master Node
Task Scheduler] s1[Slave 1
Worker] s2[Slave 2
Worker] s3[Slave 3
Worker] m1 --> s1 m1 --> s2 m1 --> s3 end subgraph p2pBlock[Peer to Peer Architecture] direction TB p1[Peer 1
Client and Server] p2[Peer 2
Client and Server] p3[Peer 3
Client and Server] p4[Peer 4
Client and Server] p1 <--> p2 p2 <--> p3 p3 <--> p4 p4 <--> p1 end classDef masterNode fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef slaveNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef peerNode fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class m1 masterNode class s1,s2,s3 slaveNode class p1,p2,p3,p4 peerNode ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU 14-mark questions:** When asked to *"Compare centralized, client-server, and P2P architectures"*, you can either (1) draw two of the above diagrams side-by-side, or (2) describe them in tabular form. Drawing the diagram fetches **2 extra marks** consistently. ### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Transparency Layers ```mermaid flowchart TB layer5[Application Layer
User Programs and Services] layer4[Transparency Layer
Access Location Migration Relocation
Replication Concurrency Failure Persistence] layer3[Middleware Layer
RPC RMI CORBA Message Queues] layer2[Operating System Layer
Process Scheduling Memory Management] layer1[Hardware and Network Layer
Servers Routers Switches Links] layer5 --> layer4 layer4 --> layer3 layer3 --> layer2 layer2 --> layer1 classDef appLayer fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef transLayer fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef midLayer fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef osLayer fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef hwLayer fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class layer5 appLayer class layer4 transLayer class layer3 midLayer class layer2 osLayer class layer1 hwLayer ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a distributed system. List any four characteristics.** *Model Answer (3 marks):* - **Definition (1.5 marks):** A distributed system is a collection of independent computers connected through a network, equipped with middleware, that appears to users as a single coherent system *(Tanenbaum)*. - **Four Characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.5 each, mark any 3, give 0.5 for 4):** 1. **Concurrency** – Multiple nodes perform tasks simultaneously. 2. **No global clock** – Coordination through logical timestamps. 3. **Independent failures** – Each node may fail without total collapse. 4. **Heterogeneity** – Diverse hardware, OS, and protocols interoperate. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain any three types of transparency in distributed systems.** *Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):* 1. **Location Transparency:** Users access resources without knowing their physical location. *Example: A URL like `https://example.com/file` hides the server's IP address.* 2. **Replication Transparency:** Users do not know whether a resource is replicated. *Example: Cloud storage (S3) stores multiple copies automatically.* 3. **Failure Transparency:** Users are unaware of component failures. *Example: DNS failover redirects traffic to a backup server.* --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (Two Alternatives) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by giving only a definition of distributed computing without the **Tanenbaum quote + 8 characteristics**. > - Numerical Amdahl's Law questions **require substitution of values into the formula** — a formula-only answer gets zero marks. > - In architecture comparison questions, students forget to draw a **diagram** — losing 2 marks. > - Do **not** confuse *Parallel Computing* (shared memory) with *Distributed Computing* (message passing). Examiners mark this strictly. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern] **(a)** Explain the **goals of distributed systems** in detail. Discuss **any six types of transparency** with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A parallel program has a sequential fraction of 8%. Calculate:     (i) The maximum achievable speedup.     (ii) The speedup when run on 32 processors.     (iii) The efficiency in case (ii). *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Goals & Transparency (7 marks):** - **Goals of Distributed Systems (3.5 marks):** Provide 4–5 goals with one-line justification each. The eight goals are *resource accessibility, distribution transparency, openness, scalability, concurrency, failure handling, security, mobility*. Full marks require at least **4 well-explained** goals. - **Six Transparency Types (3.5 marks):** Any six of the eight types from Section 2.2, with **real-world examples** (DNS, S3, HTTPS, banking apps, etc.). Each transparency = 0.5 mark; example = 0.1 mark. **(b) Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** Serial fraction $f = 0.08$, so parallel fraction $p = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$, and $n = 32$. **(i) Maximum speedup ($n \to \infty$):** $$S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.08} = 12.5$$ **(ii) Speedup on 32 processors:** $$S(32) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.92) + \dfrac{0.92}{32}}$$ $$S(32) = \frac{1}{0.08 + 0.02875} = \frac{1}{0.10875} \approx 9.20$$ **(iii) Efficiency:** $$E(32) = \frac{9.20}{32} \approx 0.2875 \;\; (28.75\%)$$ **Conclusion (mandatory for full marks):** Even with 32 processors, we achieve only **9.2× speedup** out of a theoretical maximum of **12.5×**, due to the residual 8% serial portion. This illustrates **Amdahl's bottleneck**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern] **(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **client–server, multi-tier, and peer-to-peer** architectural models of distributed systems. Compare them in a tabular form. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive **Amdahl's Law** from first principles. State and explain the **Karp-Flatt metric** with its significance. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Architectural Models (7 marks):** - **Client–Server (2 marks):** Define + draw block diagram. *Example: Web browser → Web server.* - **Multi-Tier (2 marks):** Explain 3-tier (Presentation, Logic, Data). *Example: Online shopping portal.* - **Peer-to-Peer (2 marks):** Define + draw block diagram. *Example: BitTorrent.* - **Comparison Table (1 mark):** At least **4 comparison criteria** (scalability, fault tolerance, central dependency, complexity). **(b) Derivation (7 marks):** - **Amdahl's Law Derivation (4 marks):** Follow the **complete step-by-step derivation from Section 3.1** of these notes. Each step = 0.5 mark. The final boxed equation = 1 mark. - **Karp-Flatt Metric (2 marks):** Present the final formula and explain its use in detecting parallelization overhead. - **Significance (1 mark):** Mention that Karp-Flatt reveals hidden serial bottlenecks not visible from source code. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Tanenbaum's Definition** — write it verbatim in the exam. - [x] **8 Characteristics** of distributed systems — remember: *Concurrent, No Global Clock, Independent Failures, Heterogeneous, Open, Scalable, Transparent, Secure.* - [x] **8 Transparency Types** — mnemonic: **A-L-M-R-R-C-F-P**. - [x] **3 Architecture Models** — Client–Server, Multi-Tier, Peer-to-Peer (plus Master–Slave for completeness). - [x] **Middleware** is the layer that makes distribution *transparent* to the application. - [x] **Amdahl's Law Formula:** $S = 1 / ((1 - p) + p/n)$. - [x] **Amdahl's Limit:** $S_{\infty} = 1/(1 - p)$ — emphasizes the bottleneck of serial code. - [x] **Efficiency Formula:** $E = S/n$ — always between 0 and 1. - [x] **Karp-Flatt Metric** — used to experimentally find the true serial fraction. - [x] **Difference between Parallel & Distributed:** Parallel = shared memory; Distributed = message passing over a network. - [x] **Numerical Pattern:** Always substitute values into the formula and show the **arithmetic** explicitly. A bare formula = **0 marks**. - [x] **Drawing a diagram** in 14-mark answers typically earns **1.5–2 bonus marks**. > [!TIP] > **One-line Viva Trick:** If asked *"What is the biggest limitation of distributed computing?"* — answer: *"The lack of a global clock and the impossibility of perfect failure detection (FLP Impossibility Result)."*

topics.title: Utility Computing. Cloud Service Models - Software as a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Utility Computing. Cloud Service Models - Software as a Service ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Utility Computing — The Formal Definition **Utility Computing** is a service-provisioning and consumption model in which computing resources — such as processing power, storage, memory, networking bandwidth, software applications, and platform services — are packaged, metered, and delivered to end-users **on-demand** over a network, in a manner analogous to traditional public utilities such as electricity, water, gas, or telephony. In this model, the consumer does **not own** the underlying physical infrastructure. Instead, the consumer pays for what they use (metered usage), can rapidly provision or release resources with minimal management effort, and treats IT as a *commoditized utility* rather than as a capital asset. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Utility Computing is the **philosophical foundation** upon which Cloud Computing is built. Cloud Computing is essentially the *realization* of Utility Computing over the Internet, scaled through virtualization and modern data centers. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "IT as the 5th Utility" Imagine the electricity grid in Kerala before the 1990s. Every factory had to maintain its own diesel generator. Today, you simply plug into the KSEB socket, pay for the units consumed, and never worry about generator breakdowns, fuel costs, or capacity planning. **Utility Computing does the same thing for IT.** A startup in Kochi no longer buys racks of servers to host its website. It logs into AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud, provisions a virtual server in *seconds*, pays only for the hours of compute consumed, and scales down when traffic subsides. > [!NOTE] > **The 5 Traditional Public Utilities** (for which Utility Computing is the analog): > 1. Electricity (power on tap) > 2. Water (flow on tap) > 3. Gas (heating on tap) > 4. Telephone (connectivity on tap) > 5. **Computing** (compute, storage, software — *on tap, on demand, metered*) --- ### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Utility Computing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Defining Attributes (Frequently Asked in KTU exams):** > - **Metered / Pay-per-use** — Billing is based on actual consumption (CPU-hours, GB stored, GB transferred). > - **Elasticity / On-demand provisioning** — Resources can scale up or down dynamically. > - **No capital expenditure (CapEx)** — Shifts cost from CapEx to OpEx (operational expense). > - **Multi-tenancy** — Multiple consumers share the same physical infrastructure securely. > - **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)** — Guaranteed uptime, latency, and availability. > - **Abstraction** — The user is shielded from underlying hardware complexity. --- ### 1.4 Cloud Service Models — The Triad Cloud Computing is delivered to consumers through **three primary service models**, often visualized as a stacked layer cake where each layer abstracts the one beneath it. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus — The Three Service Models:** > > | Layer | Model | What the Provider Manages | What the Consumer Manages | > |---|---|---|---| > | Top | **SaaS** — Software as a Service | Everything (app + platform + infra) | Only user data & access | > | Middle | **PaaS** — Platform as a Service | Platform + infrastructure | Application + data | > | Bottom | **IaaS** — Infrastructure as a Service | Only infrastructure (raw VMs, storage, network) | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | --- ### 1.5 Software as a Service (SaaS) — Formal Definition **Software as a Service (SaaS)** is a cloud delivery model in which a provider hosts an application centrally and makes it available to end-users **over the Internet**, typically through a web browser or thin client. The provider owns, manages, and maintains the entire software stack — application code, data, runtime, middleware, operating system, virtualization, servers, storage, and networking. The user interacts only with the *finished software product* — usually via a subscription or usage-based fee — without installing, patching, or operating anything locally. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Use verbatim in 3-mark questions):** > *"SaaS is a software distribution model in which applications are hosted by a third-party provider and delivered to customers over the Internet on a subscription basis, eliminating the need for local installation, maintenance, and infrastructure ownership."* --- ### 1.6 Intuitive Analogy — SaaS as Rented vs Owned Apartment Think of living arrangements: | Model | Apartment Analogy | |---|---| | **Traditional Software** (on-premise) | You *own a house* — you build it, paint it, fix the plumbing, pay property tax. | | **IaaS** | You *rent an empty flat* — landlord gives walls/floor, you bring furniture & appliances. | | **PaaS** | You *rent a furnished flat* — landlord provides furniture, you bring your personal items. | | **SaaS** | You *book a fully-stayed hotel room* — toiletries, bed, breakfast, Wi-Fi all included. You just show up and *use* it. | **Examples of SaaS you already use daily:** - **Gmail, Outlook.com** (email SaaS) - **Google Docs, Microsoft Office 365** (productivity SaaS) - **Salesforce** (CRM SaaS) - **Zoom, Microsoft Teams** (communication SaaS) - **Dropbox, Google Drive** (storage SaaS) - **Netflix, Spotify** (media SaaS) --- ### 1.7 Why SaaS Matters — The Shift in IT Economics > [!NOTE] > **Traditional vs SaaS Comparison (Board-exam favorite):** > > | Dimension | Traditional On-Premise | SaaS | > |---|---|---| > | Installation | Local installation required | Browser-based, no install | > | Upfront cost | High license + hardware cost | Subscription (monthly/yearly) | > | Updates | Manual, by IT team | Automatic, transparent | > | Accessibility | Tied to specific device | Anywhere, any device | > | Maintenance | Customer's responsibility | Provider's responsibility | > | Scalability | Buy more licenses/ hardware | Automatic / pay-as-you-go | > | Customization | High | Limited (configurable, not customizable) | --- ### 1.8 Course Outcome Mapping > **Mapped KTU 2024 CO:** *CO1 — Understand the fundamental concepts, architecture, and service models of Cloud Computing.* > **Bloom's Level (Revised Taxonomy):** L2 (Understand) — Define and explain. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Evolution: Mainframe → Utility → Cloud → SaaS To appreciate Utility Computing and SaaS, we trace the architectural evolution: 1. **Mainframe Era (1960s–1980s):** Dumb terminals connected to a central mainframe. (Early hint of utility model.) 2. **PC Era (1980s–1990s):** Distributed computing, local installations. 3. **ASP Era (late 1990s):** *Application Service Providers* hosted apps for rent — precursor to SaaS. 4. **Utility Computing (early 2000s):** Conceptual model — IT as a metered commodity (e.g., Sun Microsystems' SunGrid, IBM's on-demand). 5. **Cloud Computing (2006 onward):** Amazon Web Services, Google App Engine, Salesforce — the operational realization. 6. **SaaS Dominance (2010s–present):** Mass-market cloud-delivered software (Office 365, Slack, Notion). --- ### 2.2 Utility Computing — The Economic Foundation Utility Computing rests on **three economic principles** that the KTU examiner expects you to enumerate: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Utility Computing:** > 1. **Pay-per-use** — Consumer pays only for consumed resources. No wasted capacity. > 2. **Elasticity** — Provision/release resources dynamically to match demand curve. > 3. **Economies of scale** — Provider serves many tenants from massive infrastructure → lower per-unit cost. --- ### 2.3 Utility Computing vs Cloud Computing — Distinction Students frequently confuse these two terms. Here is the precise KTU-level distinction: > [!NOTE] > **Utility Computing** is the *theoretical/economic model*. > **Cloud Computing** is the *practical/implementation* of that model, leveraging virtualization, the Internet, and large-scale data centers. > > One sentence: *"All Cloud Computing follows the Utility Computing model, but not all Utility Computing implementations are Cloud (e.g., an internal private grid can be utility-style without being 'the cloud')."* --- ### 2.4 SaaS — Architectural Layers A SaaS application is typically delivered in **multi-tier architecture**: $$\text{SaaS Stack} = \underbrace{\text{Presentation Layer}}_{\text{Browser/UI}} + \underbrace{\text{Application Layer}}_{\text{Business Logic}} + \underbrace{\text{Data Layer}}_{\text{Database/Storage}}$$ Each tenant's data is logically separated even though it may reside in the **same physical database** — this is called **multi-tenancy**. --- ### 2.5 Multi-Tenancy Models in SaaS > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU frequently asked — Multi-Tenancy Approaches:** > > | Model | Description | Isolation Level | Cost | > |---|---|---|---| > | **Shared Everything** (Single Instance, Single DB) | All tenants share one app instance and one database; data separated by tenant_id column | Lowest | Cheapest | > | **Shared App, Separate DB** | One app instance, but each tenant has its own database | Medium | Moderate | > | **Separate App, Separate DB** | Each tenant has its own application instance and database | Highest | Most expensive | --- ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Formulas & Metrics While SaaS is conceptual, Utility Computing and Cloud economics use **standard quantitative metrics**. KTU may ask you to compute TCO, ROI, or cost savings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Formula Sheet — Cloud Utility Economics:** > > | Formula | Expression | Meaning | > |---|---|---| > | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx}_{\text{lifetime}}$ | True cost of owning/running IT over its life | > | **Pay-per-use Cost** | $C_{\text{cloud}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \times U_i \times P_i)$ | Sum of (Resource × Usage hours × Per-unit Price) | > | **Cost Savings (SaaS vs On-Prem)** | $\text{Savings} = \dfrac{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - \text{TCO}_{\text{SaaS}}}{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}}} \times 100\%$ | % reduction in total cost | > | **Utilization Rate** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Used Capacity}}{\text{Total Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of provisioned resources | > | **Breakeven Point** | $\text{CapEx}_{\text{cloud}} + (n \times \text{OpEx}_{\text{cloud}}) = \text{CapEx}_{\text{on-prem}} + (n \times \text{OpEx}_{\text{on-prem}})$ | Months/years until cloud becomes cheaper | > | **Scalability Speed** | $S_t = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Resources}}{\Delta t}$ | Rate of resource provisioning | > | **Availability (SLA)** | $\text{Avail} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | % time the service is up (e.g., **99.9%** ≈ 8.76 hrs/yr downtime) | --- ### 2.7 SLA Availability Reference (Frequently Cited) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Reference Table — SLA Tiers:** > > | SLA Tier | Availability | Max Downtime/Year | > |---|---|---| > | One nine | 90.0% | 876 hours | > | Two nines | 99.0% | 87.6 hours | > | Three nines | 99.9% | 8.76 hours | > | Four nines | 99.99% | 52.6 minutes | > | Five nines | 99.999% | 5.26 minutes | --- ### 2.8 Engineering Utility of SaaS In real engineering practice, SaaS eliminates: - **Software license auditing** headaches. - **Version mismatch** across teams. - **Disaster recovery** complexity (provider handles it). - **Procurement cycles** for new servers. This is why startups (e.g., Kerala-based UST, Toonz Animation) adopt SaaS tools (Slack, GitHub, Salesforce) immediately rather than building IT infrastructure. --- ### 2.9 Course Outcome Mapping > **Mapped KTU 2024 CO:** *CO1 — Understand the fundamental concepts, architecture, and service models of Cloud Computing.* > **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply, for cost formulas). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Pay-per-use Billing for SaaS A SaaS provider meters **three resource dimensions** for billing: users, storage, and API calls. Let us derive the total monthly bill. **Step 1 — Identify resource variables.** Let: - $U$ = number of active users in the month - $S$ = average storage consumed per user (GB) - $A$ = average API calls per user per month - $p_u, p_s, p_a$ = unit prices for user, storage, API call respectively **Step 2 — Express each cost component.** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{users}} = U \times p_u$$ $$\text{Cost}_{\text{storage}} = U \times S \times p_s$$ $$\text{Cost}_{\text{API}} = U \times A \times p_a$$ **Step 3 — Sum all components (additive billing model).** $$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = U \cdot p_u \; + \; U \cdot S \cdot p_s \; + \; U \cdot A \cdot p_a}$$ **Step 4 — Factor out common term $U$.** $$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = U \, (p_u + S \, p_s + A \, p_a)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The total SaaS bill scales *linearly* with the number of users. This is the **linear scaling property** of utility-based SaaS pricing. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical — SaaS Monthly Bill **Problem (KTU-style):** A startup uses a SaaS CRM. Pricing is: - **₹500** per active user per month - **₹10** per GB of storage per month - **₹0.05** per API call The startup has **50 active users**, each using **5 GB storage** and making **1000 API calls/month**. Compute the monthly bill. **Step 1 — Identify parameters.** $$U = 50, \quad S = 5 \text{ GB}, \quad A = 1000, \quad p_u = 500, \quad p_s = 10, \quad p_a = 0.05$$ **Step 2 — Compute each component.** $$\text{Cost}_{\text{users}} = 50 \times 500 = 25{,}000$$ $$\text{Cost}_{\text{storage}} = 50 \times 5 \times 10 = 2{,}500$$ $$\text{Cost}_{\text{API}} = 50 \times 1000 \times 0.05 = 2{,}500$$ **Step 3 — Sum.** $$C_{\text{total}} = 25{,}000 + 2{,}500 + 2{,}500 = 30{,}000$$ $$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = \text{₹}30{,}000 \text{ per month}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Cost Savings: SaaS vs On-Premise **Problem:** A company is considering migrating from on-premise ERP to SaaS ERP. - On-premise TCO over 5 years = **₹50,00,000** - SaaS subscription over 5 years = **₹30,00,000** - Migration cost (one-time) = **₹2,00,000** Compute net savings and percentage savings. **Step 1 — Compute effective SaaS cost (including migration).** $$C_{\text{SaaS, total}} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 = 32{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Compute absolute savings.** $$\text{Savings} = 50{,}00{,}000 - 32{,}00{,}000 = 18{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Compute percentage savings.** $$\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{18{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = 36\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Net Savings} = \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{36\% reduction})}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation — SLA Downtime Calculation **Problem:** A SaaS provider offers an SLA of **99.95%** availability. Compute the maximum allowed downtime per year and per month. **Step 1 — Convert percentage to decimal.** $$\text{Availability} = 0.9995$$ **Step 2 — Compute total hours in a year.** $$T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 3 — Compute downtime per year.** $$D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 8760 = 0.0005 \times 8760 = 4.38 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4 — Compute downtime per month (average).** $$D_{\text{month}} = \dfrac{4.38}{12} = 0.365 \text{ hours} \approx 21.9 \text{ minutes}$$ $$\boxed{D_{\text{year}} = 4.38 \text{ hours}, \quad D_{\text{month}} \approx 21.9 \text{ minutes}}$$ --- ### 3.5 Derivation — Utilization Rate and Wasted Capacity **Problem:** A company provisions a SaaS plan with capacity for **1000 users** but only has **600 active users** in a given month. Compute: 1. Utilization rate 2. Wasted capacity in % and users **Step 1 — Apply utilization formula.** $$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Used}}{\text{Total}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{600}{1000} \times 100\% = 60\%$$ **Step 2 — Compute wasted capacity.** $$\text{Waste \%} = 100\% - 60\% = 40\%$$ $$\text{Wasted Users} = 1000 - 600 = 400$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world insight:** Pay-per-use SaaS resolves this waste — companies like AWS charge only for *active* resources. Static licensing wastes 40% here. --- ### 3.6 Derivation — Elastic Scaling Trigger **Problem:** A SaaS application observes the following traffic pattern: | Hour | Active Users | |---|---| | 0–6 | 100 | | 6–12 | 500 | | 12–18 | 1200 | | 18–24 | 300 | If the SaaS provisions **1 server per 250 users**, compute the number of servers needed in each window. **Step 1 — Compute servers per window.** $$\text{Window 0–6:} \quad N_1 = \lceil 100/250 \rceil = 1 \text{ server}$$ $$\text{Window 6–12:} \quad N_2 = \lceil 500/250 \rceil = 2 \text{ servers}$$ $$\text{Window 12–18:} \quad N_3 = \lceil 1200/250 \rceil = 5 \text{ servers}$$ $$\text{Window 18–24:} \quad N_4 = \lceil 300/250 \rceil = 2 \text{ servers}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Elasticity in action:** Server count swings from 1 → 5 → 2 over the day. In a static on-premise model, the company would have provisioned 5 servers for the entire day — wasting **62.5%** of capacity during off-peak. --- ### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — SaaS Maturity Stages A common KTU 14-mark question asks you to classify SaaS into maturity levels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **SaaS Maturity Model (Adapted from industry standard):** > > | Level | Maturity | Characteristics | Example | > |---|---|---|---| > | **Level 1** | Ad-Hoc / Custom | Single tenant, on-premise-hosted version delivered via Internet | Early 2000s ASPs | > | **Level 2** | Configurable | Single instance, multi-tenant, limited configuration | Mid-stage Salesforce | > | **Level 3** | Configurable + Multi-Tenant Efficient | Single instance, scalable, configurable per tenant | Modern Google Workspace | > | **Level 4** | Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant | Full multi-tenancy, auto-scaling, rich integration | Salesforce, ServiceNow | --- ### 3.8 Course Outcome Mapping > **Mapped KTU 2024 CO:** *CO1, CO2* — Apply cost models and understand service delivery. > **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply) — compute bills and savings. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Utility Computing Conceptual Flow This diagram shows how Utility Computing abstracts IT resources as on-demand, metered services. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph USER["End User / Organization"] U1[Application Needs] U2[Compute Needs] U3[Storage Needs] end subgraph CLOUD["Cloud Provider Utility Platform"] M1[Metering Engine] M2[Billing System] M3[SLA Monitor] M4[Resource Pool] end subgraph SOURCES["Underlying Resource Pool"] R1[CPU / Compute] R2[Memory RAM] R3[Storage] R4[Network Bandwidth] end U1 --> M4 U2 --> M4 U3 --> M4 M4 --> R1 M4 --> R2 M4 --> R3 M4 --> R4 M1 --> M2 M4 --> M1 M4 --> M3 M2 --> USER M3 --> USER ``` --- ### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Cloud Service Models Layered Stack (KTU Favorite) The **classic pyramid** showing what each layer manages. ```mermaid flowchart TB L0["End User - Browser / Mobile App"] L1["SaaS - Software as a Service
Provider manages EVERYTHING
User only uses software"] L2["PaaS - Platform as a Service

Provider manages Infra only

Open SaaS URL W->>A: 2. Send login credentials A->>A: 3. Verify tenant_id A->>W: 4. Issue session token W->>S: 5. Request app page with token S->>D: 6. Query data filtered by tenant_id D->>S: 7. Return tenant-specific data S->>W: 8. Render HTML response W->>U: 9. Display app interface ``` --- ### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Utility Computing Evolution Timeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mainframe Era 1960s] --> B[PC Client-Server 1980s] B --> C[ASP Era 1990s] C --> D[Utility Computing Concept 2000s] D --> E[Cloud Computing Realized 2006] E --> F[SaaS Dominance 2010s Onwards] F --> G[Serverless SaaS 2020s] ``` --- ### 4.6 Diagram 6 — SaaS Deployment Models vs Service Models (Decoupling) Many students confuse these. Here is the **decoupled view**. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DEPLOY["Deployment Models"] D1[Public Cloud] D2[Private Cloud] D3[Hybrid Cloud] D4[Community Cloud] end subgraph SERVICE["Service Models"] S1["SaaS - Top Layer"] S2["PaaS - Middle Layer"] S3["IaaS - Bottom Layer"] end D1 --- S1 D1 --- S2 D1 --- S3 D2 --- S1 D2 --- S2 D2 --- S3 D3 --- S1 D3 --- S2 D3 --- S3 D4 --- S1 D4 --- S2 D4 --- S3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** A SaaS application can be deployed over Public, Private, Hybrid, or Community cloud — the *service model* (what you consume) is orthogonal to the *deployment model* (where it runs). ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define Utility Computing. List any four characteristics. **Model Answer:** Utility Computing is a service-provisioning model in which computing resources (compute, storage, software, platform) are delivered to users **on-demand over a network** and billed based on **metered consumption**, similar to traditional public utilities like electricity or water. **Four characteristics:** 1. **Pay-per-use / Metered billing** — Cost proportional to consumption. 2. **Elasticity** — Resources scale up/down dynamically. 3. **Abstraction** — User unaware of underlying hardware. 4. **Multi-tenancy** — Multiple users share the same infrastructure securely. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for 4 characteristics)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define SaaS. Give two real-world examples and explain why SaaS is preferred over traditional on-premise software. **Model Answer:** **Software as a Service (SaaS)** is a cloud delivery model in which applications are hosted centrally by a provider and delivered to users over the Internet, typically via a browser, on a subscription basis. **Examples:** 1. **Google Workspace** (Gmail, Docs, Sheets). 2. **Salesforce** (Customer Relationship Management). **Why SaaS is preferred:** 1. **No installation/maintenance** — Provider handles updates and patches. 2. **Lower upfront cost** — Subscription instead of bulk license purchase. 3. **Accessibility** — Accessible from any device with an Internet connection. 4. **Automatic scaling** — Provider manages capacity. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for examples + 1 for advantages)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]* **(a)** Explain the concept of Utility Computing in detail. Discuss how it forms the foundation for Cloud Computing. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the three cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — with a comparison table and real-world examples. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Q(a) **1. Definition of Utility Computing:** Utility Computing is a model in which computing resources — processing, storage, applications, and platform services — are packaged and delivered to consumers as **metered services** on demand, analogous to electricity or water supply. **2. Origin and Evolution:** - Rooted in **John McCarthy's 1961 vision** that computing could be organized as a public utility. - Formalized in the early 2000s by IBM (on-demand computing), Sun Microsystems (SunGrid), and HP (Utility Computing Services). - Became the *conceptual parent* of modern Cloud Computing. **3. Key Principles:** - **Pay-per-use:** Billing based on consumption (CPU-hr, GB, API call). - **Elastic provisioning:** Resources expand/contract with demand. - **Service-oriented delivery:** Everything exposed as a service (XaaS). - **High availability:** Provider guarantees uptime via SLAs. - **Multi-tenancy:** Shared infrastructure with logical isolation. **4. How Utility Computing leads to Cloud Computing:** Cloud Computing is the **operational implementation** of the Utility Computing model over the Internet, powered by: - **Virtualization** (resource pooling and abstraction). - **Broad network access** (the Internet). - **Massive data centers** (economies of scale). - **Self-service portals** (on-demand provisioning). Thus, Utility Computing provides the *economic and conceptual blueprint*; Cloud Computing provides the *technological realization*. **5. Real-world analogy:** Just as Kerala's KSEB delivers electricity on a metered, always-on basis, a cloud provider delivers compute, storage, and software on the same principles. *(7 marks: 2 for definition + 2 for principles + 3 for Cloud relationship)* --- #### Model Solution — Q(b) **Comparison Table — IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS:** | Dimension | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |---|---|---|---| | **What user gets** | Raw VMs, storage, network | Platform to deploy apps | Ready-to-use software | | **User manages** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | App + data | Only user data | | **Provider manages** | Infrastructure | Infrastructure + OS + middleware + runtime | Everything | | **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest | | **Ease of use** | Lowest (requires admin skills) | Medium | Highest | | **Examples** | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce | | **Typical user** | Sysadmins, DevOps | Developers | End users / business teams | | **Analogy** | Empty plot of land | Prefab workshop | Finished apartment | **Detailed explanations:** - **IaaS:** The provider offers virtualized hardware resources. The user installs OS, middleware, and apps. Best when the user needs full control — e.g., a startup migrating an existing application to the cloud using AWS EC2. - **PaaS:** The provider offers a runtime environment, databases, and development tools. The user focuses only on writing application code and uploading it. Best for developers building new cloud-native apps — e.g., deploying a Node.js app on Heroku. - **SaaS:** The provider delivers complete applications over the Internet. Users simply log in and use the software. No coding or infrastructure work — e.g., a sales team using Salesforce to track leads. *(7 marks: 3 for comparison table + 2 each for detailed explanation of PaaS and SaaS or IaaS and SaaS)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]* **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a typical SaaS application. Discuss the concept of multi-tenancy and its models. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A company is evaluating migration from on-premise ERP (5-year TCO = ₹80 lakh) to a SaaS ERP (subscription = ₹18 lakh/year). Migration cost = ₹5 lakh. Compute: (i) Total SaaS 5-year cost. (ii) Absolute savings. (iii) Percentage savings. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Solution — Q(a) **SaaS Architecture Diagram (to be drawn):** ``` [Tenant A Browser] [Tenant B Browser] [Tenant C Browser] \ | / \ | / -----> [Load Balancer] <-------- | [Authentication Gateway] | [Application Server Cluster] | [Multi-Tenant Database] | [Tenant A Data] [Tenant B Data] [Tenant C Data] ``` **Architecture layers explained:** 1. **Presentation Layer:** Browser-based UI accessed by tenants. 2. **Routing/Load Balancer Layer:** Distributes traffic across servers; routes requests to the correct tenant. 3. **Authentication & Tenant Resolution Layer:** Validates user credentials and identifies tenant_id. 4. **Application Layer:** Business logic — shared across tenants but logically partitioned. 5. **Data Layer:** Multi-tenant database with logical or physical separation per tenant. **Multi-Tenancy Definition:** Multi-tenancy is an architectural pattern in which a *single instance* of a software application serves *multiple customer organizations (tenants)*, with each tenant's data isolated and invisible to other tenants. **Multi-Tenancy Models:** | Model | App Instances | Databases | Isolation | Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | (i) Shared Everything | 1 | 1 (shared schema, tenant_id column) | Logical only | Lowest | | (ii) Shared App, Separate DB | 1 | N (one per tenant) | Database-level | Moderate | | (iii) Separate App, Separate DB | N | N | Full physical isolation | Highest | **Trade-offs:** - Model (i) is cheapest but offers weakest isolation. - Model (iii) is most expensive but strongest compliance posture (used by banks, hospitals). - Most consumer SaaS (e.g., Google Workspace) uses model (i) or (ii). *(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 2 for layers + 3 for multi-tenancy models)* --- #### Model Solution — Q(b) **Given data:** $$\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem, 5yr}} = \text{₹}80{,}00{,}000$$ $$\text{SaaS subscription} = \text{₹}18{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ $$\text{Migration cost (one-time)} = \text{₹}5{,}00{,}000$$ **(i) Total 5-year SaaS cost (including migration):** $$C_{\text{SaaS, total}} = (18{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + 5{,}00{,}000$$ $$C_{\text{SaaS, total}} = 90{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 95{,}00{,}000$$ $$\boxed{C_{\text{SaaS, total}} = \text{₹}95{,}00{,}000}$$ **(ii) Absolute savings:** $$\text{Savings} = \text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - C_{\text{SaaS, total}}$$ $$\text{Savings} = 80{,}00{,}000 - 95{,}00{,}000 = -15{,}00{,}000$$ $$\boxed{\text{Savings} = -\text{₹}15{,}00{,}000 \quad (\text{negative, i.e., a loss of ₹15 lakh})}$$ **(iii) Percentage savings:** $$\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{-15{,}00{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% = -18.75\%$$ $$\boxed{\text{Savings \%} = -18.75\% \quad (\text{SaaS is 18.75\% more expensive over 5 years})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for the valuation key:** In this scenario, **SaaS is NOT economically justified** over the 5-year horizon — the company should continue on-premise or renegotiate the SaaS contract. This tests the student's ability to interpret the **negative result** rather than blindly conclude "cloud is always cheaper." *(7 marks: 2 for (i) + 2 for (ii) + 3 for (iii) with interpretation)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing Utility Computing with Cloud Computing** — Utility Computing is the *concept*; Cloud is the *implementation*. Marks deducted for using them interchangeably. > 2. **Forgetting the migration cost** — In cost comparison problems, students often compute only subscription vs on-premise cost. Always include **migration, training, and integration costs** in the SaaS side. > 3. **Ignoring multi-tenancy in SaaS answers** — For 14-mark answers on SaaS architecture, mentioning multi-tenancy *and its models* is mandatory. Skipping this loses 2–3 marks. > 4. **Listing service models without examples** — Always cite real-world examples (AWS EC2 for IaaS, Heroku for PaaS, Gmail for SaaS). Bare definitions without examples score partial marks only. > 5. **Negative savings confusion** — If the math yields a *negative* savings (SaaS costs more), state the interpretation clearly. Do not fudge the answer. > 6. **Confusing service models with deployment models** — SaaS/IaaS/PaaS (what you consume) ≠ Public/Private/Hybrid (where it runs). Examiners actively test this distinction. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Utility Computing & SaaS:** > > - [ ] **Utility Computing** = IT resources as on-demand, metered services (like electricity). > - [ ] Three pillars: **Pay-per-use, Elasticity, Economies of scale**. > - [ ] Utility Computing is the *conceptual foundation*; Cloud Computing is the *realization*. > - [ ] Three cloud service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS** (bottom to top of the stack). > - [ ] **SaaS definition:** Provider hosts and delivers complete software over the Internet; user pays subscription. > - [ ] **SaaS examples:** Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Zoom, Google Drive. > - [ ] **SaaS user controls:** Only user data and access configuration. Everything else is the provider's job. > - [ ] **Multi-tenancy:** One app instance serves many tenants with logical data isolation. > - [ ] **Three multi-tenancy models:** Shared everything / Shared app + separate DB / Fully separate. > - [ ] **SaaS advantages:** No installation, low upfront cost, automatic updates, anywhere access, scalability. > - [ ] **SaaS disadvantages:** Limited customization, dependency on Internet, data residency concerns, vendor lock-in. > - [ ] **Cost formula:** $C_{\text{total}} = U(p_u + S \cdot p_s + A \cdot p_a)$. > - [ ] **Savings formula:** $\text{Savings \%} = \dfrac{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}} - \text{TCO}_{\text{SaaS}}}{\text{TCO}_{\text{on-prem}}} \times 100\%$. > - [ ] **SLA tiers:** Three nines = 99.9% = 8.76 hrs/yr downtime. > - [ ] **Service Models ≠ Deployment Models:** SaaS can run on Public/Private/Hybrid cloud. > - [ ] Always give **real-world examples** in 14-mark answers. > - [ ] Always include **migration + training costs** in cost comparison problems.

topics.title: Platform as a Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Platform as a Service

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Platform as a Service (PaaS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which the cloud provider delivers a **ready-to-use platform** — comprising hardware, operating system, middleware, runtime environment, development tools, and database management systems — over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis. The consumer does **not** manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure (network, servers, OS, or storage) but retains control over the **deployed applications** and possibly **configuration settings** of the host environment.

In the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Model**, PaaS sits as the **middle layer** of the three primary service models:

$$
\text{Cloud Service Stack} = \underbrace{\text{SaaS}}_{\text{Software}} \;>\; \underbrace{\text{PaaS}}_{\text{Platform}} \;>\; \underbrace{\text{IaaS}}_{\text{Infrastructure}}
$$

The provider abstracts everything *below* the application layer, freeing the developer to focus purely on **code, data, and application logic**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Fully Equipped Kitchen Rental"**
>
> Imagine three ways you could prepare a meal:
>
> | Service Model | Real-World Analogy | You Manage | Provider Manages |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **IaaS** | Renting an *empty kitchen* (only walls, gas line, plumbing) | You bring utensils, ingredients, recipes, even the chef | Kitchen shell, gas, water |
> | **PaaS** | Renting a *fully equipped kitchen* with oven, mixers, pre-stocked pantry | You bring only the **recipe and final dish** | Kitchen, appliances, pantry, utilities |
> | **SaaS** | Ordering a *ready-to-eat meal from a restaurant* | You just *eat* (use) the final product | Everything |
>
> **PaaS = "Bring your code, the rest is provided."**

A developer using PaaS uploads their source code (the "recipe") and the platform handles **provisioning servers, installing the language runtime, configuring the database, scaling, load balancing, and patching** — exactly like a fully-stocked kitchen automatically supplies the chef with ingredients and tools.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Glossary Terms You Must Memorize:**
>
> - **Middleware** — Software layer between the OS and the application (e.g., WebSphere, Tomcat, .NET Framework). PaaS providers pre-install and manage this.
> - **Runtime Environment** — The execution engine for a specific language (e.g., JVM for Java, Node.js runtime for JavaScript, CLR for .NET).
> - **SDK (Software Development Kit)** — A collection of libraries, APIs, and tools provided by the PaaS vendor to build applications targeting that platform.
> - **Multi-tenancy** — A single physical platform instance serves multiple customers (tenants) with logical isolation.
> - **Elasticity** — Automatic scaling of resources based on demand (scale-out/scale-in).
> - **Sticky Session** — A session-routing strategy where a user's requests are consistently directed to the same backend instance.
> - **Service-Level Agreement (SLA)** — A formal contract defining uptime guarantees (typically **99.9%**, **99.95%**, or **99.99%** — the famous "**nines**").

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Industry Constants

The following standard uptime percentages (the **"nines"**) are **bolded for memorization** as they appear in nearly every KTU question:

| Availability % | Downtime per Year | Common Name |
|---|---|---|
| **99%** | 3.65 days | Two nines |
| **99.9%** | 8.77 hours | Three nines (typical PaaS SLA) |
| **99.95%** | 4.38 hours | Three-and-a-half nines |
| **99.99%** | 52.6 minutes | Four nines (Premium PaaS) |
| **99.999%** | 5.26 minutes | Five nines (Carrier-grade) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of PaaS — Layered Breakdown

PaaS operates as a **multi-layered abstraction stack**. From the consumer's perspective, visibility and control decrease as we go deeper.

### Layer 1 — Application Layer (Visible to Consumer)
- Source code of the deployed application
- Application configuration (environment variables, feature flags)
- Data and user-uploaded content

### Layer 2 — Development & Deployment Tools (Mostly Visible)
- **IDE integration** (e.g., Eclipse plug-ins, VS Code extensions, IntelliJ plugins)
- **Version control hooks** (Git integration, CI/CD pipelines)
- **CLI / Web Console** for managing the application

### Layer 3 — Platform Services (Provider-Managed, Configurable)
- **Runtime engines** (Python 3.11, Node.js 20, Java 17, Go 1.21, .NET 8)
- **Middleware** (Web servers like Nginx, Apache, application servers like Tomcat)
- **Database as a Service** (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis)
- **Caching, Message Queues, Search indexes**
- **Identity & Access Management (IAM)**

### Layer 4 — Operating System & Virtualization (Hidden)
- Guest OS (Linux distributions like Ubuntu, Alpine, or Windows Server)
- Hypervisor (KVM, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V)
- Container engines (Docker, containerd, runC)

### Layer 5 — Physical Infrastructure (Hidden)
- Servers, storage arrays, network switches, data center facilities, power, cooling

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind this abstraction:** PaaS exists to *collapse layers 4 and 5 into a black box*, eliminating an estimated **30–40% of developer time** historically spent on environment provisioning, dependency management, and infrastructure operations.

---

## 2.2 How PaaS Works — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Developer Onboarding** — The developer signs up for a PaaS account and selects a target runtime stack (e.g., Python 3.11 + PostgreSQL).
2. **Code Upload / Push** — Code is pushed via `git push`, CLI command, or web upload.
3. **Build Phase** — The platform's **buildpack system** detects the language, installs dependencies (e.g., via `pip install`, `npm install`), and compiles if necessary.
4. **Containerization** — The application + dependencies are packaged into a **container image** (typically OCI-compliant).
5. **Deployment** — The container is scheduled onto the underlying compute fleet via an orchestrator (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm, or proprietary equivalent).
6. **Auto-Scaling** — Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) monitors CPU, memory, or request metrics and adjusts the replica count.
7. **Health Monitoring & Healing** — Liveness/readiness probes restart or replace unhealthy instances.
8. **Routing & Load Balancing** — An ingress controller distributes incoming HTTP requests across healthy instances.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

### Comparison 1 — Service Models (IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS)

| Dimension | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What you get** | Raw VMs, storage, network | Platform with runtime + tools | Finished application |
| **What you manage** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | App and data only (sometimes config) | Nothing (or just user settings) |
| **What provider manages** | Virtualization, hardware, network | OS, middleware, runtime, virtualization, hardware | Everything |
| **Target user** | Sysadmins, network engineers | Application developers | End users |
| **Examples** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine | Heroku, Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, OpenShift | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Dropbox |
| **Control level** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Vendor lock-in** | Low | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Time to deploy** | Hours–Days | Minutes–Hours | Immediate |

### Comparison 2 — Public vs Private vs Hybrid PaaS

| Deployment Model | Description | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Public PaaS** | Hosted on public cloud (multi-tenant) | Startups, web apps, MVPs |
| **Private PaaS** | On-premises single-tenant platform | Banks, defense, regulated industries |
| **Hybrid PaaS** | Burst-out to public when on-prem capacity exhausts | Enterprise with compliance + elasticity needs |
| **Community PaaS** | Shared by organizations with common concerns | Government consortia, research labs |

### Comparison 3 — PaaS vs On-Premises Development

| Feature | On-Premises | PaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **CapEx vs OpEx** | Capital expense (buy hardware) | Operational expense (pay per use) |
| **Time to provision** | Weeks–Months | Minutes |
| **Scaling** | Manual, capacity-planned | Automatic, demand-driven |
| **Maintenance** | Customer owns everything | Provider owns everything below the app |
| **Upfront cost** | Very High | Near Zero |
| **Skill required** | Sysadmin, DevOps, Networking | Application development only |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently write "PaaS eliminates *all* management overhead." This is **wrong**. The consumer still manages the **application code, configuration, and data**. The provider manages everything *below* the application layer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of PaaS in Engineering

- **Rapid MVP Development:** Startups deploy production-grade web apps in **under 1 hour**.
- **Microservices Architecture:** Each microservice runs as an independent PaaS "service" with isolated scaling.
- **IoT Backends:** Millions of device telemetry messages handled by PaaS-managed message queues and time-series databases.
- **AI/ML Model Deployment:** ML models served via PaaS-managed inference endpoints (e.g., Google AI Platform, Azure ML).
- **API Gateways & BaaS:** Backend-as-a-Service (Firebase, Supabase) extends PaaS with authentication, real-time databases, and storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Matrices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although PaaS is largely conceptual, KTU 2024 Scheme questions often demand **quantitative analysis** of availability, cost, scaling, and capacity planning. Below are the full derivations of the **four most-tested formulas** in this topic.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Annual Downtime from Availability Percentage

### Statement
Given an availability target of $A\%$ (e.g., $99.9\%$), compute the **maximum permissible annual downtime**.

### Setup
A year contains:

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525600 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
T_{\text{year}} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

### Derivation
The availability fraction is:

$$
A_{\text{frac}} = \frac{A\%}{100} = \frac{A}{100}
$$

The fraction of the year that is **downtime** is the complement:

$$
D_{\text{frac}} = 1 - A_{\text{frac}}
$$

The actual downtime per year is:

$$
D_{\text{year}} = D_{\text{frac}} \times T_{\text{year}}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{year}} = \left(1 - \frac{A}{100}\right) \times 8760 \text{ hours}}
$$

### Worked Example
Compute the annual downtime for a PaaS provider advertising **99.95%** availability.

$$
A_{\text{frac}} = \frac{99.95}{100} = 0.9995
$$

$$
D_{\text{frac}} = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 0.0005 \times 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 4.38 \text{ hours per year}
$$

**Interpretation:** The platform is allowed to be unavailable for a cumulative **4 hours 22 minutes 48 seconds** per year before breaching the SLA.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Monthly Downtime from Availability

### Derivation
A month (using 30 days) contains:

$$
T_{\text{month}} = 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours}
$$

Applying the same logic:

$$
\boxed{D_{\text{month}} = \left(1 - \frac{A}{100}\right) \times 720 \text{ hours}}
$$

### Worked Example
For $A = 99.9\%$:

$$
D_{\text{month}} = (1 - 0.999) \times 720 = 0.001 \times 720 = 0.72 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
0.72 \text{ hours} = 0.72 \times 60 = 43.2 \text{ minutes per month}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost Comparison: On-Premises vs PaaS

### Statement
Compare the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** over $n$ years between deploying on-premises and using PaaS.

### Cost Components for On-Premises

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{onprem}} = (C_{\text{hardware}} + C_{\text{software}}) + n \cdot (C_{\text{power}} + C_{\text{cooling}} + C_{\text{admin}} + C_{\text{space}})
$$

### Cost Components for PaaS

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{PaaS}} = C_{\text{migration}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( C_{\text{instance}} \cdot t_{\text{active}} + C_{\text{storage}} \cdot s_{\text{used}} + C_{\text{egress}} \cdot b_{\text{out}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{instance}}$ = per-hour compute charge
- $t_{\text{active}}$ = total active instance-hours in year $i$
- $C_{\text{storage}}$ = per-GB-month storage charge
- $s_{\text{used}}$ = average storage in GB-months
- $C_{\text{egress}}$ = per-GB data-egress charge
- $b_{\text{out}}$ = total outbound bandwidth in GB

### Break-Even Condition

The PaaS option is cheaper when:

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{\text{PaaS}}(n) \;<\; \text{Cost}_{\text{onprem}}(n)}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Scenario:** A startup needs 4 vCPUs, 16 GB RAM, 500 GB storage, and 2 TB egress/month.

| Cost Head | On-Premises (INR) | PaaS (INR/year) |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware (one-time) | 8,00,000 | 0 |
| Software licenses (one-time) | 2,00,000 | 0 |
| Migration cost | 0 | 50,000 |
| Annual power + cooling | 60,000 | 0 |
| Annual admin salary (allocated) | 4,80,000 | 0 |
| Compute (yearly) | 0 | 1,20,000 |
| Storage (yearly) | 0 | 30,000 |
| Egress (yearly) | 0 | 40,000 |
| **Year 1 Total** | **15,40,000** | **2,40,000** |

**Year 1 Savings with PaaS:**

$$
\text{Savings}_1 = 15,40,000 - 2,40,000 = 13,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Year 2 Total:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{onprem}}^2 = 15,40,000 + 5,40,000 = 20,80,000
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{PaaS}}^2 = 2,40,000 + 1,90,000 = 4,30,000
$$

**Cumulative 5-Year Analysis:**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{onprem}}^{5\text{yr}} = 10,00,000 + 5 \times 5,40,000 = 37,00,000
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{PaaS}}^{5\text{yr}} = 50,000 + 5 \times 1,90,000 = 10,00,000
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{5-Year Savings} = 37,00,000 - 10,00,000 = 27,00,000 \text{ INR}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scaling Decision (When to Scale Out)

### Statement
A PaaS application currently runs on $k$ instances. Each instance handles a maximum of $R_{\max}$ requests per second. Determine the required number of instances to handle a peak load of $L$ requests per second.

### Derivation

Total request capacity of $k$ instances:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = k \times R_{\max}
$$

To satisfy peak load without queuing saturation:

$$
C_{\text{total}} \geq L
$$

$$
k \times R_{\max} \geq L
