$$

$$
\boxed{k_{\text{required}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{R_{\max}} \right\rceil}
$$

The $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling) function ensures we always round up to the next whole instance — you cannot deploy **0.4 of a server**.

### Worked Example
An app receives peak traffic of $L = 2400$ requests/second. Each instance handles $R_{\max} = 500$ requests/second.

$$
k_{\text{required}} = \left\lceil \frac{2400}{500} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.8 \rceil = 5 \text{ instances}
$$

**Headroom check:**

$$
\text{Headroom} = \frac{5 \times 500 - 2400}{2400} \times 100\% = \frac{100}{2400} \times 100\% \approx 4.17\%
$$

This is the **safety buffer** to absorb traffic bursts without triggering immediate auto-scaling oscillation (known as **flapping**).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Should You Use PaaS?

| Factor | Use PaaS ✅ | Avoid PaaS ❌ |
|---|---|---|
| Development speed is critical | High priority | Low priority |
| Team size | Small (1–10 devs) | Large with dedicated ops |
| Regulatory constraints | Minimal | Strict data-residency laws |
| Vendor lock-in tolerance | Comfortable | Strongly prefer portability |
| Predictable workload | Variable/unpredictable | Very stable |
| Need for low-level OS tuning | Not required | Required (custom kernel modules, drivers) |
| Budget model | OpEx preferred | CapEx preferred |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — PaaS Architecture Stack (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1["LAYER 1 - APPLICATION - CONSUMER MANAGES"]
        appCode["Application Source Code"]
        appConfig["Application Configuration"]
        appData["Application Data"]
    end

    subgraph layer2["LAYER 2 - DEV TOOLS - PARTIALLY VISIBLE"]
        cliTool["CLI and Web Console"]
        ciCd["CI CD Pipeline"]
        gitHook["Git Integration"]
    end

    subgraph layer3["LAYER 3 - PLATFORM SERVICES - PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        runtime["Language Runtimes - Python, Node, Java, .NET"]
        middleware["Middleware - Tomcat, Nginx, WebSphere"]
        dbService["Database as a Service - PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis"]
        cache["Caching and Message Queues"]
        iam["Identity and Access Management IAM"]
    end

    subgraph layer4["LAYER 4 - OS AND VIRTUALIZATION - HIDDEN"]
        guestOS["Guest OS - Ubuntu, Alpine, Windows"]
        hypervisor["Hypervisor - KVM, VMware, Hyper-V"]
        container["Container Engine - Docker, containerd"]
    end

    subgraph layer5["LAYER 5 - PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE - HIDDEN"]
        servers["Physical Servers"]
        storageArr["Storage Arrays"]
        networkSw["Network Switches"]
        dataCenter["Data Center Facility - Power Cooling"]
    end

    appCode --> cliTool
    cliTool --> ciCd
    ciCd --> runtime
    runtime --> middleware
    middleware --> guestOS
    guestOS --> hypervisor
    hypervisor --> servers
    dbService --> storageArr
    cache --> networkSw
    networkSw --> dataCenter
    iam --> runtime
    gitHook --> cliTool
    container --> hypervisor
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — PaaS Deployment Workflow (Request Lifecycle)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Git as Git Repository
    participant PaaS as PaaS Platform
    participant Builder as Buildpack Engine
    participant Container as Container Registry
    participant Orch as Orchestrator Kubernetes
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant User as End User

    Dev->>Git: git push main branch
    Git->>PaaS: Webhook triggers deployment
    PaaS->>Builder: Detect language and install dependencies
    Builder->>Container: Package application into OCI image
    Container->>Orch: Schedule pod on healthy node
    Orch->>LB: Register new instance with ingress
    User->>LB: HTTPS request arrives
    LB->>Orch: Route to least-loaded healthy pod
    Orch-->>User: Response served
    Note over Orch,PaaS: Continuous health checks restart failed pods
    Note over PaaS,Orch: HPA monitors CPU and request rate to auto-scale replicas
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS Responsibility Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph saas["SaaS - Provider Manages ALL"]
        saasApp["Application"]
        saasData["Data"]
        saasRuntime["Runtime"]
        saasOS["Operating System"]
        saasVirt["Virtualization"]
        saasPhys["Physical Infrastructure"]
    end

    subgraph paas["PaaS - Consumer Manages App + Data ONLY"]
        paasApp["Application"]
        paasData["Data"]
        paasRuntime["Runtime - Provider"]
        paasOS["Operating System - Provider"]
        paasVirt["Virtualization - Provider"]
        paasPhys["Physical Infrastructure - Provider"]
    end

    subgraph iaas["IaaS - Consumer Manages OS Upwards"]
        iaasApp["Application - Consumer"]
        iaasData["Data - Consumer"]
        iaasRuntime["Runtime - Consumer"]
        iaasOS["Operating System - Consumer"]
        iaasVirt["Virtualization - Provider"]
        iaasPhys["Physical Infrastructure - Provider"]
    end

    Note1["Legend: Consumer-managed vs Provider-managed"]:::legend
    classDef legend fill:#fff5d4,stroke:#aa8800
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Public PaaS Deployment Reference Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userClient["User Devices - Browser Mobile"] --> cdn["CDN - CloudFront Cloudflare"]
    cdn --> waf["Web Application Firewall"]
    waf --> dns["DNS and Route 53"]
    dns --> loadbalancer["Application Load Balancer"]
    loadbalancer --> appTier1["App Instance 1 - PaaS Managed"]
    loadbalancer --> appTier2["App Instance 2 - PaaS Managed"]
    loadbalancer --> appTier3["App Instance N - Auto Scaled"]
    appTier1 --> dbaas["Managed PostgreSQL - Multi-AZ"]
    appTier2 --> dbaas
    appTier3 --> dbaas
    appTier1 --> cache["Redis Cache Cluster"]
    appTier2 --> cache
    appTier3 --> cache
    appTier1 --> objstore["Object Storage - S3 Blob"]
    appTier2 --> objstore
    appTier3 --> objstore
    dbaas --> monitor["Monitoring - Logs Metrics Traces"]
    cache --> monitor
    objstore --> monitor
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Platform as a Service (PaaS). List any four characteristics of PaaS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
> Platform as a Service (PaaS) is a cloud computing service model that provides a **ready-to-use development and deployment platform** — including operating system, middleware, runtime environment, development tools, and database — over the internet. The consumer manages only the application and data while the provider manages the underlying infrastructure.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
1. **Runtime and Middleware Management** — Pre-installed language runtimes (Java, Python, Node.js) and middleware (Tomcat, Nginx).
2. **Integrated Development Tools** — SDKs, CLI tools, IDE plug-ins, and CI/CD integration.
3. **Built-in Scalability** — Automatic horizontal scaling based on demand.
4. **Multi-tenant Architecture** — A single physical platform serves multiple customers with logical isolation.
5. *(Bonus, replace any)* **Pay-per-use Pricing** — Pay only for consumed compute, storage, and bandwidth.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS based on what the consumer manages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format expected):**

| Service Model | Consumer Manages | Provider Manages |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | OS, runtime, middleware, application, data | Virtualization, servers, storage, networking |
| **PaaS** | Application and data only | OS, runtime, middleware, virtualization, hardware |
| **SaaS** | User data and access settings only | Everything including the application itself |

(1 mark per row, 3 marks total. Marks awarded even for partial differentiation.)

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of Platform as a Service (PaaS) with a neat diagram. Describe any **five responsibilities of the cloud provider** in a PaaS environment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**
*(Draw a layered stack diagram with the following five layers, top to bottom — application, development tools, platform services, OS & virtualization, physical infrastructure. The diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes may be reproduced.)*

**Architecture Explanation (3 marks):**
The PaaS architecture is a **layered stack** with five distinct layers:

- **Layer 1 (Application):** The consumer uploads source code, configuration files, and data. This is the only layer fully controlled by the consumer.
- **Layer 2 (Development Tools):** IDE plug-ins, version control hooks, CI/CD pipelines, and CLI consoles enable the developer to push code without manually managing infrastructure.
- **Layer 3 (Platform Services):** The provider offers managed runtime engines (Python, Java, Node.js), middleware (Tomcat, Nginx), managed databases (PostgreSQL, MongoDB), caching layers, and identity services.
- **Layer 4 (OS & Virtualization):** The provider manages the guest OS (Linux/Windows), hypervisor layer (KVM, VMware), and container runtime (Docker).
- **Layer 5 (Physical Infrastructure):** The underlying servers, storage arrays, network switches, and data center facilities are fully managed by the provider.

**Five Provider Responsibilities (2 marks — any five of these):**
1. Provisioning and patching the operating system.
2. Installing and updating language runtimes and middleware.
3. Managing database servers, including backups and replication.
4. Implementing automatic scaling and load balancing.
5. Ensuring network security, firewall rules, and DDoS protection.
6. Maintaining physical security of data centers.
7. Providing monitoring, logging, and SLA reporting.
8. Managing API gateways, DNS, and SSL certificate renewal.

---

**(b)** A PaaS provider advertises **99.95% availability**. Compute the **(i)** maximum annual downtime and **(ii)** maximum monthly downtime. If a company currently runs on-premises infrastructure costing **₹15,00,000/year** (hardware amortization + admin + power), and the equivalent PaaS deployment costs **₹4,50,000/year**, calculate the **5-year cumulative savings** if the migration cost is **₹1,00,000** one-time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Annual Downtime (2 marks):**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = \left(1 - \frac{A}{100}\right) \times 8760
$$

$$
D_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 8760 = 0.0005 \times 8760 = 4.38 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer:** Maximum annual downtime ≈ **4 hours 22 minutes 48 seconds**.

**(ii) Monthly Downtime (1 mark):**

$$
D_{\text{month}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 720 = 0.0005 \times 720 = 0.36 \text{ hours}
$$

**Answer:** Maximum monthly downtime ≈ **21.6 minutes**.

**5-Year Cost Calculation (4 marks):**

On-Premises 5-year cost:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{onprem}}^{5} = 5 \times 15,00,000 = 75,00,000 \text{ INR}
$$

PaaS 5-year cost (including migration):

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{PaaS}}^{5} = 1,00,000 + 5 \times 4,50,000 = 1,00,000 + 22,50,000 = 23,50,000 \text{ INR}
$$

Savings:

$$
\text{Savings}_{5\text{yr}} = 75,00,000 - 23,50,000 = 51,50,000 \text{ INR}
$$

**Answer:** Cumulative 5-year savings = **₹51,50,000**.

---

### ❓ Question Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Compare **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** across the following dimensions: **(i)** Target user, **(ii)** Control level, **(iii)** Examples, **(iv)** Vendor lock-in. Discuss the **advantages and disadvantages** of PaaS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Dimension | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Target user | Sysadmins, infra engineers | Application developers | End users / business |
| (ii) Control level | High (manages OS up) | Medium (manages app only) | Low (just uses) |
| (iii) Examples | AWS EC2, GCP Compute Engine | Heroku, Google App Engine, OpenShift | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce |
| (iv) Vendor lock-in | Low | Medium-High | Very High |

**Advantages of PaaS (2 marks — any five):**
1. **Faster Development** — No infrastructure setup; developers focus on code.
2. **Cost-Effective** — Pay-as-you-go eliminates upfront hardware investment.
3. **Auto-Scaling** — Built-in elasticity handles traffic spikes.
4. **Reduced Operational Overhead** — Provider manages OS, runtime, middleware patching.
5. **Built-in High Availability** — Multi-zone redundancy and disaster recovery included.
6. **Pre-integrated Services** — Managed databases, caching, queues, AI/ML APIs.

**Disadvantages of PaaS (1 mark — any three):**
1. **Vendor Lock-in** — Proprietary APIs make migration difficult.
2. **Limited Customization** — Cannot install custom OS-level software.
3. **Data Security Concerns** — Sensitive data on third-party infrastructure.
4. **Recurring Costs** — Long-term TCO may exceed on-premises for steady, predictable workloads.
5. **Network Dependency** — Requires stable, high-bandwidth internet connectivity.

---

**(b)** A web application receives peak traffic of **3,500 requests/second**. Each PaaS instance can handle a maximum of **600 requests/second**. Calculate the **minimum number of instances required** and the **safety headroom percentage**. If the provider charges **₹2.5 per instance-hour** and the application runs **24×7×365**, what is the **annual compute cost**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Minimum Instances Required (3 marks):**

$$
k_{\text{required}} = \left\lceil \frac{L}{R_{\max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{3500}{600} \right\rceil = \lceil 5.833... \rceil = 6 \text{ instances}
$$

**Safety Headroom (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Headroom} = \frac{(6 \times 600) - 3500}{3500} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Headroom} = \frac{3600 - 3500}{3500} \times 100\% = \frac{100}{3500} \times 100\% \approx 2.857\%
$$

**Annual Compute Cost (2 marks):**

Total hours in a year:

$$
T = 24 \times 365 = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

Annual cost:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{annual}} = 6 \times 8760 \times 2.5 = 6 \times 21,900 = 1,31,400 \text{ INR}
$$

**Final Answer:** Minimum 6 instances, **2.857% headroom**, annual compute cost = **₹1,31,400**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on PaaS Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PaaS with SaaS** — Students often write "PaaS is when you use Gmail." This is **SaaS**. PaaS is for **building** applications (e.g., Heroku), not consuming finished ones.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Ceiling Function** — When computing required instances, students write $3500 / 600 = 5.83$ and conclude "5.83 instances." You **cannot deploy fractional servers**; always apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and report **6 instances**.
>
> 3. **SLA Math Mistakes** — Using $525600$ minutes (year) and accidentally mixing with hours. Always write the unit in your final answer.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Diagram** — For "Explain PaaS architecture" questions, a **diagram is mandatory** and carries **2 marks** separately. Skipping it is an instant **−2 to −3 mark** loss.
>
> 5. **Stating "No management required"** — Always clarify: *consumer manages app and data; provider manages everything below the application layer.*
>
> 6. **Ignoring Migration Cost in TCO** — Always include the one-time migration cost when computing cumulative PaaS cost; the KTU key specifically checks for this.
>
> 7. **Saying "PaaS is cheaper always"** — It is **not always cheaper**. For very stable, high-utilization workloads (e.g., 24×7 full-capacity), on-premises may break-even after **4–5 years**. Mention the break-even condition.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Platform as a Service (PaaS)**

- ✅ **Definition:** PaaS provides a **platform** (runtime + middleware + dev tools + DB) on which developers build applications without managing underlying infrastructure.
- ✅ **Three Service Models:** IaaS (infra) < PaaS (platform) < SaaS (software) — each successive layer abstracts more and gives less control.
- ✅ **NIST Layer Position:** PaaS is the **middle layer** of the three primary cloud service models.
- ✅ **Provider Manages:** Hardware, networking, virtualization, OS, runtime, middleware, database servers.
- ✅ **Consumer Manages:** Application code, configuration, data.
- ✅ **Core Characteristics:** Multi-tenancy, elasticity, pay-per-use, integrated DevOps, built-in HA.
- ✅ **Popular Examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Microsoft Azure App Service, Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift, Salesforce Lightning Platform.
- ✅ **Standard SLA "Nines":** 99% (3.65 d/yr) · 99.9% (8.77 h/yr) · 99.99% (52.6 min/yr) · 99.999% (5.26 min/yr).
- ✅ **Annual Downtime Formula:** $D_{\text{year}} = (1 - A/100) \times 8760$ hours.
- ✅ **Monthly Downtime Formula:** $D_{\text{month}} = (1 - A/100) \times 720$ hours.
- ✅ **Scaling Formula:** $k_{\text{required}} = \lceil L / R_{\max} \rceil$ instances.
- ✅ **TCO Comparison:** Always include **migration cost** (one-time) + **annual recurring** costs.
- ✅ **Deployment Models:** Public PaaS, Private PaaS, Hybrid PaaS, Community PaaS.
- ✅ **Advantages:** Faster dev, lower CapEx, auto-scaling, less ops overhead, integrated services.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** Vendor lock-in, limited OS-level customization, security trust, recurring OpEx.
- ✅ **Deployment Tools:** Buildpacks, containers (Docker/OCI), orchestrators (Kubernetes), CI/CD pipelines.
- ✅ **PaaS vs Serverless:** PaaS instances run **continuously** (you pay for idle); serverless runs **on-demand** (zero cost when idle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrastructure as a Service. Cloud Deployment Models - Private Cloud
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Infrastructure as a Service. Cloud Deployment Models - Private Cloud

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)

### Formal Definition (NIST-Aligned, KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which the provider delivers **on-demand, networked, virtualized computing resources** — including raw processing power, storage, and networking — over the public internet or a private network. The consumer provisions these resources dynamically, typically through a self-service portal or API, and is billed using a **pay-per-use** (consumption-based) economic model.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IaaS is one of the three foundational SPI service models (Software, Platform, Infrastructure) originally defined by NIST SP 800-145. In IaaS, the **Cloud Provider manages the physical hardware, hypervisor, and network fabric**, while the **Cloud Consumer manages the Operating System, Middleware, Runtime, Data, and Application** layers (the upper half of the cloud stack).

### Intuitive Analogy: "The Empty Plot of Land"

Imagine you want to build a house. You have two options:

| Approach | Real Estate Analogy | Cloud Equivalent |
|----------|--------------------|------------------|
| **Traditional IT (On-Premise)** | **Buy a plot of land.** You pay the full cost upfront, you must build a fence, drill a borewell, install electricity, and maintain the soil. If you don't build, the land sits empty — wasted. | Buying physical servers, racks, cooling, and power backup. **CAPEX-heavy.** Underutilized ~**80%** of the time. |
| **IaaS (Cloud)** | **Rent a ready-to-use plot with utilities already connected.** You only pay for the square footage you actually use this month. Want a bigger plot next week? Just call the landlord. | Spinning up a **Virtual Machine** on AWS EC2 / Azure VM / OpenStack. You pay for vCPU-hours, GB-months of storage, and GB of egress. **OPEX-heavy, elastic.** |

The fundamental value proposition of IaaS is the **transformation of capital expenditure (CAPEX) into operational expenditure (OPEX)** and the **abstraction of physical infrastructure into programmable, API-driven resources**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** IaaS delivers *raw building blocks* (VMs, raw block storage, virtual networks, load balancers). It is **NOT** the same as PaaS (where the OS and runtime are also managed) or SaaS (where the entire application is consumed). Students frequently lose marks by confusing these boundaries.

---

## 1.2 Cloud Deployment Models — Focus on Private Cloud

### Deployment Models Overview

The **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture** defines four deployment models. IaaS can be delivered over **any** of them, but this module's focus is **Private Cloud**.

| Deployment Model | Who Owns the Infrastructure? | Who Uses It? | Open to Public? |
|------------------|------------------------------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | General public | Yes |
| **Private Cloud** | **A single organization** exclusively | That organization only (or its authorized partners) | **No** |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of private + public, bound by orchestration | The organization | Partial |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by several organizations with common concerns | Members of a defined community | No |

### Formal Definition of Private Cloud

A **Private Cloud** is a cloud computing deployment model in which the cloud infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** comprising multiple business units or consumers. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization itself, a third party, or some combination of both, and it may exist **on-premise (on the organization's own data center) or off-premise (hosted in a colocation facility).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The defining feature of a Private Cloud is **NOT** location — it is **exclusivity of tenancy**. A Private Cloud can physically reside inside the company's data center (on-premise) OR in a vendor-managed colocation (off-premise). What makes it "private" is that no other organization shares the compute, storage, or network fabric.

### Intuitive Analogy: "The Gated Community Mansion"

Think of cloud deployment models as real-estate communities:

- **Public Cloud** → A **shopping mall** — open to the public, shared elevators, shared parking, anyone can walk in.
- **Private Cloud** → A **gated villa community** — built exclusively for the residents of one housing society. The gate, security, and roads are managed by the society's own management committee (or hired staff). No outsider is allowed in. The community may be inside the city (on-premise) or on the city's outskirts in a managed township (off-premise/hosted).
- **Hybrid Cloud** → A villa with a **club membership** — your private rooms for sleeping (private cloud) and the public gym/party hall for burst capacity (public cloud).
- **Community Cloud** → A **co-operative housing society** — multiple organizations share one secured complex because they all need banking-level security.

### Essential Characteristics of a Private Cloud

1. **Single-Tenancy** — A single organization has exclusive control over the infrastructure.
2. **Self-Service Provisioning** — Users can request and deploy resources through a portal without manual IT intervention (e.g., OpenStack Horizon, VMware vRealize).
3. **Elasticity & Scalability** — Resources can be scaled up/down based on demand, even if the total ceiling is limited by physical hardware.
4. **Resource Pooling** — Internal users share a common pool of virtualized resources.
5. **Measured Service** — Usage is metered, reported, and billed back to internal departments (chargeback/showback).
6. **Higher Control & Security** — Customizable security policies, compliance regimes (HIPAA, PCI-DSS, FedRAMP), and network isolation.
7. **Dedicated SLA** — Internal SLAs typically more predictable than public cloud, since contention is bounded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A Private Cloud is *not* synonymous with "On-Premise Virtualization." A VMware vSphere cluster running on 10 servers **without a self-service API, automated provisioning, and metered usage is just virtualization, NOT a cloud.** To qualify as a cloud, NIST requires the **five essential characteristics**: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IaaS — Architectural Components

IaaS is built on a layered virtualization stack. Understanding this stack is critical for KTU module-end questions.

### Layer 1: Physical Infrastructure (Provider-Managed)

- **Servers** — Commodity x86_64 or ARM-based machines, typically mounted in standardized 19-inch rack units (1U = **1.75 inches / 44.45 mm**).
- **Storage Arrays** — SAN (Storage Area Network) using Fibre Channel or iSCSI; NAS using NFS/SMB.
- **Network Fabric** — Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches, spine-leaf topology, redundant 10/25/40/100 Gbps uplinks.
- **Power & Cooling** — Redundant UPS, diesel generators, CRAC units maintaining **ASHRAE A1** class environment (**18°C–27°C**, 40–55% RH).

### Layer 2: Hypervisor (Provider-Managed)

The **hypervisor (Virtual Machine Monitor / VMM)** is the software that creates and runs virtual machines. Two types:

| Type | Examples | Where Used |
|------|----------|------------|
| **Type-1 (Bare-Metal)** | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen | Production data centers — runs directly on hardware |
| **Type-2 (Hosted)** | Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation | Developer laptops, testing only |

### Layer 3: Virtualization Resources (Consumer-Visible IaaS Resources)

The consumer interacts **only** with these abstracted resources via APIs:

- **Compute** → Virtual CPU (vCPU) cores, virtual RAM (GiB).
- **Storage** → Block volumes (e.g., AWS EBS), object storage (e.g., AWS S3), file storage (e.g., AWS EFS).
- **Network** → Virtual Private Clouds (VPC), subnets, security groups, load balancers, public IPs, VPN gateways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IaaS is the most flexible SPI model:** Because the consumer controls the OS layer, they can install *any* operating system, *any* middleware, *any* runtime, and *any* application. The provider's responsibility ends at the hypervisor API.

---

## 2.2 IaaS — Core Characteristics

1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Provision resources without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Accessible over standard networks (HTTP, SSH, RDP) from thin/thick clients.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using multi-tenant models.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Capacity can be scaled out or in, often automatically (auto-scaling groups).
5. **Measured Service** — Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts).

### Economic Principle of IaaS

IaaS follows the **Utility Computing** model — analogous to electricity or water:

$$ \text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times P_i) + \text{Fixed Overheads} $$

Where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of resource $i$ consumed (e.g., vCPU-hours, GB-months)
- $P_i$ = Per-unit price of resource $i$
- $n$ = Number of distinct resource types billed

---

## 2.3 Private Cloud — Deep Analysis

### Why Organizations Choose Private Cloud

| Driver | Explanation |
|--------|-------------|
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Industries like Banking, Healthcare, and Defense must keep data within jurisdictional boundaries (e.g., RBI, HIPAA, ITAR). |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Data must physically reside within a specific country/region. |
| **Legacy Workload Integration** | Mainframes, high-performance computing (HPC), or specialized hardware (GPU clusters for ML) that cannot easily migrate. |
| **Predictable Performance** | No "noisy neighbor" effect since the pool is exclusive. |
| **Existing CAPEX Investment** | Companies that have already invested millions in data centers want to virtualize them cloud-style rather than abandon them. |
| **Custom Security Policies** | Granular control over network segmentation, encryption keys (BYOK — Bring Your Own Key), and audit logs. |

### Private Cloud — Delivery Models

The NIST definition recognizes **three** delivery flavors of private cloud:

| Delivery Model | Owner | Operator | Location |
|----------------|-------|----------|----------|
| **On-Premise Private Cloud** | Organization | Organization | Inside the organization's data center |
| **Outsourced Private Cloud (Off-Premise)** | Organization | Third-party provider | Provider's data center or colocation facility |
| **Managed Private Cloud** | Provider (leased exclusively) | Provider | Provider's data center |

### Private Cloud — Enabling Technologies

A true Private Cloud is built using the same technologies as Public Cloud, but deployed on dedicated hardware:

| Technology | Role | Examples |
|------------|------|----------|
| **Open-source IaaS platforms** | Build a private cloud in-house | **OpenStack** (most popular), Apache CloudStack, Eucalyptus |
| **Hyper-converged Infrastructure (HCI)** | Combines compute, storage, networking in one appliance | Nutanix, VMware vSAN, Cisco HyperFlex |
| **Virtualization layer** | Provides compute virtualization | VMware vSphere, KVM, Microsoft Hyper-V |
| **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** | Programmatically manages network flows | Open vSwitch, Cisco ACI, VMware NSX |
| **Software-Defined Storage (SDS)** | Decouples storage software from hardware | Ceph, GlusterFS, MinIO |
| **Orchestration & Automation** | Self-service portals, workflows | OpenStack Heat, Terraform, Ansible |

### Private Cloud vs. Public Cloud — KTU Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Private Cloud | Public Cloud |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Tenancy** | Single organization | Multi-tenant |
| **Capex/Opex** | Mostly CAPEX, with OPEX for power/cooling | Pure OPEX / Pay-as-you-go |
| **Elasticity** | Limited by physical capacity | Virtually unlimited |
| **Control** | Full | Limited (you trust the provider) |
| **Compliance** | Easy (within your walls) | Complex (shared responsibility) |
| **Cost Predictability** | High | Variable (must be monitored) |
| **Time to Deploy** | Weeks to months (hardware procurement) | Minutes to seconds |
| **Skill Required** | High (need in-house cloud engineers) | Low (managed services) |
| **Workload Examples** | Core banking, EHR systems, classified workloads | Web apps, big data, dev/test, AI training |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Favourite:** Be prepared to write an in-depth comparison between Private, Public, Hybrid, and Community clouds along multiple dimensions: deployment, ownership, control, scalability, security, cost, and use cases.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Metric | Unit | Use Case |
|---|---------|------------------|------|----------|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = \text{CAPEX} + \text{OPEX}_{3yr} + \text{Migration Cost} - \text{Salvage Value}$ | ₹ / USD | Compare on-premise vs IaaS |
| 2 | **Pay-per-Use Cost** | $\text{Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(Q_i \times P_i)$ | ₹/hour etc. | IaaS monthly bill calculation |
| 3 | **Server Utilization** | $\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Actual CPU Used}}{\text{Total CPU Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | % | Measure waste in on-premise |
| 4 | **Provisioning Time Reduction** | $\text{Reduction}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cloud}}}{T_{\text{manual}}}\right) \times 100\%$ | % | IaaS business case |
| 5 | **Storage Capacity (RAID 6 usable)** | $\text{Usable} = (N - 2) \times D$, $N$ disks of size $D$ | TB | Private cloud storage design |
| 6 | **Storage Capacity (RAID 10 usable)** | $\text{Usable} = \dfrac{N \times D}{2}$ | TB | High-performance IaaS volumes |
| 7 | **CPU Oversubscription Ratio** | $\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Total vCPUs allocated}}{\text{Physical cores}}$ | dimensionless | IaaS density planning (typical 2:1 to 6:1) |
| 8 | **Cloud ROI** | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Benefit}}{\text{Total Cost}} \times 100\%$ | % | Investment justification |
| 9 | **Break-Even Point** | $\text{BEP} = \dfrac{\text{Fixed Cost}}{\text{Unit Price} - \text{Variable Cost per unit}}$ | months | Private vs public cost crossover |
| 10 | **SLA Availability (Composite)** | $\text{Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | fraction | Downtime budgeting |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In LaTeX-markdown tables, the vertical bar $\vert$ and $\mid$ symbols have been used in formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value) to avoid breaking markdown table cell boundaries.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IaaS** powers modern DevOps pipelines (CI/CD runners, container hosts, ephemeral test environments).
- **Private Cloud** is the backbone of India's national digital initiatives — the **National Informatics Centre (NIC)** operates a private cloud hosting e-Governance applications like DigiLocker, UMANG, and GeM.
- **Hybrid setups** are the de-facto norm in Indian banking: customer-facing apps on public cloud, core banking and ledger data on private cloud.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through the **quantitative reasoning** behind IaaS and Private Cloud decisions — calculations that frequently appear in KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: IaaS Monthly Bill Calculation

### Problem Statement
A startup provisions the following resources on AWS for one month (30 days):

- **Compute:** 4 EC2 instances (t3.medium = 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) running 24×7.
- **Storage:** 500 GB of EBS gp3 volumes.
- **Data Transfer:** 2 TB outbound to internet.
- **Price card:** Compute = ₹5/hour per instance, Storage = ₹2.5/GB-month, Egress = ₹6/GB.

**Compute the total monthly bill.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Convert month to hours.**

$$
H_{month} = 30 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} = 720 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the compute cost.**

$$
C_{compute} = N_{instances} \times P_{hour} \times H_{month}
$$

$$
C_{compute} = 4 \times 5 \times 720 = \text{₹ } 14{,}400
$$

**Step 3: Compute the storage cost.**

$$
C_{storage} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB\text{-}month}
$$

$$
C_{storage} = 500 \times 2.5 = \text{₹ } 1{,}250
$$

**Step 4: Compute the data transfer cost.**

$$
C_{egress} = D_{GB} \times P_{GB}
$$

$$
C_{egress} = 2048 \text{ GB} \times 6 = \text{₹ } 12{,}288
$$

**Step 5: Sum all components.**

$$
C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{egress}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 14{,}400 + 1{,}250 + 12{,}288 = \text{₹ } 27{,}938
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The key is converting all time units to a common base (hours), all storage to GB, and all data to GB before multiplication. The total cost model is **linear and additive** in IaaS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Utilization Improvement Metric

### Problem Statement
An on-premise data center has 50 physical servers, each with 16 cores = **800 physical cores** total. Average CPU utilization is **12%**. After migrating to a private cloud with virtualization, the same workload now uses **240 vCPUs** distributed on the same hardware.

**Compute:**
- (a) The original wasted compute capacity.
- (b) The new utilization.
- (c) The percentage improvement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute original effective compute.**

$$
U_{orig} = \text{Total Cores} \times \text{Utilization}\%
$$

$$
U_{orig} = 800 \times 0.12 = 96 \text{ effective cores}
$$

**Step 2: Compute original waste.**

$$
W_{orig} = 800 - 96 = 704 \text{ wasted cores}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the new effective compute (assuming the vCPUs run at full target load).**

$$
U_{new} = 240 \times 1.00 = 240 \text{ effective cores}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the new percentage utilization on the same 800 physical cores.**

$$
\eta_{new} = \dfrac{240}{800} \times 100\% = 30\%
$$

**Step 5: Compute the improvement factor.**

$$
\Delta\eta = \eta_{new} - \eta_{orig} = 30\% - 12\% = 18 \text{ percentage points}
$$

$$
\text{Relative Improvement} = \dfrac{18}{12} \times 100\% = 150\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This kind of calculation demonstrates the **business value of cloud computing** — the same hardware delivers **2.5× more effective compute** through consolidation and right-sizing. This is a strong justification answer for "Why IaaS / Why private cloud?"

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: TCO Comparison — Private Cloud vs. Public IaaS

### Problem Statement
A mid-size company needs **100 vCPUs, 50 TB storage, and 10 Gbps network** for 3 years. Two options:

- **Option A — On-Premise Private Cloud:** CAPEX ₹80 lakh (servers + storage + networking). OPEX ₹12 lakh/year (power, cooling, admin staff).
- **Option B — Public IaaS:** ₹25/vCPU-month, ₹2/GB-month storage, no upfront CAPEX.

**Compute the 3-year TCO for each and recommend.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute Option A TCO.**

$$
\text{TCO}_A = \text{CAPEX} + \text{OPEX}_{3yr}
$$

$$
\text{TCO}_A = 80{,}00{,}000 + (3 \times 12{,}00{,}000) = \text{₹ } 1{,}16{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 1.16 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Option B Compute Cost (3 years).**

$$
C_{compute,B} = 100 \text{ vCPUs} \times 25 \text{ ₹/vCPU-month} \times 36 \text{ months}
$$

$$
C_{compute,B} = 100 \times 25 \times 36 = \text{₹ } 90{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 3: Compute Option B Storage Cost (3 years).**

$$
C_{storage,B} = 50{,}000 \text{ GB} \times 2 \text{ ₹/GB-month} \times 36 \text{ months}
$$

$$
C_{storage,B} = 50{,}000 \times 2 \times 36 = \text{₹ } 3{,}60{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4: Sum Option B TCO.**

$$
\text{TCO}_B = 90{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4.50 \text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 5: Compare.**

$$
\Delta\text{TCO} = \text{TCO}_B - \text{TCO}_A = 4.50 - 1.16 = \text{₹ } 3.34 \text{ Crore}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic / Real-world interpretation:** In this scenario, the on-premise private cloud is **cheaper by ₹3.34 crore** over 3 years, **but** this calculation ignores migration cost, business continuity cost, and the opportunity cost of staff managing hardware. The "correct" answer in a KTU essay is: **"The choice depends on workload predictability, compliance needs, and existing infrastructure investment. A sensitivity analysis varying the discount rate or utilization typically reverses the result if utilization falls below ~60%."**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Composite SLA Availability

### Problem Statement
A private cloud is built from 4 components, each with an independent availability:
- Servers: 99.95%
- Storage: 99.99%
- Network: 99.9%
- Power (UPS + Generator): 99.5%

**Compute the composite end-to-end availability and the expected annual downtime in minutes.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the unavailability (downtime probability) of each component.**

$$
U_i = 1 - A_i
$$

$$
U_{server} = 0.0005, \quad U_{storage} = 0.0001, \quad U_{network} = 0.001, \quad U_{power} = 0.005
$$

**Step 2: Compute composite availability using the product rule (series system).**

$$
A_{composite} = 1 - (U_{server} + U_{storage} + U_{network} + U_{power})
$$

*This approximation holds only when unavailabilities are small. For greater accuracy, we use:*

$$
A_{composite} = 1 - \left[1 - \prod_{i=1}^{4}(1 - U_i^{indep})\right]
$$

A more practical approach for an exam is to compute the **composite unavailability as a sum of small independent failure probabilities** (since they are independent and small):

$$
U_{total} \approx U_{server} + U_{storage} + U_{network} + U_{power}
$$

$$
U_{total} = 0.0005 + 0.0001 + 0.001 + 0.005 = 0.0066
$$

**Step 3: Compute composite availability.**

$$
A_{composite} = 1 - 0.0066 = 0.9934 = 99.34\%
$$

**Step 4: Convert to annual downtime in minutes.**

$$
\text{Minutes per year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 = 5{,}25{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{Downtime} = 5{,}25{,}600 \times 0.0066 \approx 3{,}469 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 57.8 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The **weakest link** is power (0.5% downtime = ~44 hours/year). KTU students often miss this — adding redundant servers cannot fix power outages. A 99.99% SLA is meaningless if the UPS fails.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What

| Decision Criterion | Public IaaS | Private Cloud | Hybrid |
|--------------------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| Workload is variable / unpredictable | ✅ Best | ❌ Risk of over-provisioning | ✅ |
| Strict data residency laws | ❌ Risk | ✅ Best | ✅ |
| Need for 24×7 in-house ops team | Low | High | Medium |
| Need 100+ TB egress per month | ❌ Expensive egress | ✅ Free internal traffic | ✅ |
| Project duration < 6 months | ✅ | ❌ High sunk cost | — |
| Project duration > 3 years | ⚠️ Cost may exceed CAPEX | ✅ Best | ✅ |
| Use of legacy mainframes / GPUs | ❌ Hard to migrate | ✅ Best | ⚠️ |
| Real-time low-latency trading | ✅ Proximity co-located zones | ✅ Dedicated | ✅ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IaaS High-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONSUMER["CONSUMER LAYER - Managed by Customer"]
        APP1[Application Software]
        DATA1[Application Data]
        RUNTIME1[Runtime and Middleware]
        OS1[Operating System]
    end

    subgraph IAAS["IaaS SERVICE LAYER - Exposed via APIs"]
        VMC[Virtual Machines and Containers]
        VST[Virtual Block and Object Storage]
        VNW[Virtual Networks and Load Balancers]
        API1[Self-Service API and Portal]
        MTR[Metering and Billing Engine]
    end

    subgraph PROVIDER["PROVIDER LAYER - Managed by Cloud Provider"]
        HYP[Hypervisor - ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V]
        DCC[Data Center Facility - Power, Cooling, Physical Security]
        PHYSRV[Physical Servers]
        PHYNET[Physical Network Fabric ToR Spine Leaf]
        PHYSTO[Physical Storage Arrays SAN and NAS]
    end

    CONSUMER --> IAAS
    IAAS --> PROVIDER
    API1 --> VMC
    API1 --> VST
    API1 --> VNW
    MTR --> VMC
    MTR --> VST
    MTR --> VNW
    HYP --> PHYSRV
    HYP --> PHYSTO
    HYP --> PHYNET
    PHYSRV --> DCC
    PHYSTO --> DCC
    PHYNET --> DCC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The upper block (Consumer Layer) is the responsibility of the customer. The middle block (IaaS Service Layer) is what the customer *sees and consumes* — they get VMs, storage, and networks through an API. The lower block (Provider Layer) is fully managed by the IaaS vendor and is invisible to the customer.

---

## 4.2 Private Cloud Deployment Architecture (OpenStack Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USERS["End Users and Admins"]
        U1[Developer]
        U2[IT Admin]
        U3[Billing Auditor]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL PLANE - OpenStack Services"]
        KEYS[Keystone - Identity]
        NOVA[Nova - Compute]
        NEUT[Neutron - Networking]
        CIND[Cinder - Block Storage]
        SWIF[Swift - Object Storage]
        GLAN[Glance - Image Service]
        HORZ[Horizon - Web Dashboard]
        HRLY[Heat - Orchestration]
    end

    subgraph HVLAYER["HYPERVISOR AND VIRTUALIZATION"]
        KVM1[KVM Hypervisor 1]
        KVM2[KVM Hypervisor 2]
        KVM3[KVM Hypervisor N]
    end

    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL DATA CENTER - EXCLUSIVE TO ONE ORG"]
        SRVA[Compute Server A]
        SRVB[Compute Server B]
        SRVC[Compute Server C]
        SANX[Dedicated SAN Storage]
        TORX[Top of Rack Switch]
    end

    U1 --> HORZ
    U2 --> HORZ
    U3 --> KEYS
    HORZ --> NOVA
    HORZ --> NEUT
    HORZ --> CIND
    HORZ --> SWIF
    NOVA --> KVM1
    NOVA --> KVM2
    NOVA --> KVM3
    KVM1 --> SRVA
    KVM2 --> SRVB
    KVM3 --> SRVC
    CIND --> SANX
    SWIF --> SANX
    NEUT --> TORX
    GLAN --> SRVA
    HRLY --> NOVA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Note for KTU:** This is the canonical OpenStack-based private cloud. Notice the **exclusivity boundary** — every physical box sits inside the single organization's data center, satisfying the "single-tenant" requirement. The control-plane services (Nova, Neutron, Cinder, etc.) collectively provide the self-service API and resource pooling that *qualify* it as a cloud (not just virtualization).

---

## 4.3 Cloud Deployment Models — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CLOUD[Cloud Computing Deployment Models]

    CLOUD --> PUB[Public Cloud]
    CLOUD --> PRIV[Private Cloud]
    CLOUD --> HYB[Hybrid Cloud]
    CLOUD --> COM[Community Cloud]

    PUB --> PUB1[Owned by 3rd party provider]
    PUB --> PUB2[Open to general public]
    PUB --> PUB3[Multi tenant]

    PRIV --> PRV1[Single organization exclusive use]
    PRIV --> PRV2[On premise or off premise]
    PRIV --> PRV3[Single tenant]
    PRIV --> PRV4[Built on OpenStack or VMware]

    HYB --> HYB1[Private plus Public bound together]
    HYB --> HYB2[Bursting to public during peak]
    HYB --> HYB3[Unified orchestration layer]

    COM --> COM1[Shared by specific community]
    COM --> COM2[Common policy and compliance]
    COM --> COM3[Eg government banks hospitals]
```

---

## 4.4 IaaS Service Stack vs. PaaS vs. SaaS (Responsibility Boundary)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Application]
    L1[Data]
    L2[Runtime]
    L3[Middleware]
    L4[Operating System]
    L5[Virtualization]
    L6[Servers Storage Networking]

    P1[Customer manages L0 to L4]
    P2[Provider manages L5 and L6]
    P1A[IaaS]

    L0 --- L1 --- L2 --- L3 --- L4 --- L5 --- L6
    L0 -.-> P1
    L5 -.-> P2
    L4 -.-> P1A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question:** "In IaaS, who manages the OS?" — **Answer: the customer.** Many students incorrectly say the provider. The provider only manages the hypervisor and physical hardware. This is a very common 3-mark or 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Define Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS). List any four essential characteristics of IaaS.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) is a cloud computing service model in which the cloud provider delivers **on-demand, virtualized computing resources** — including processing power, storage, and networking — over the internet. The consumer can provision these resources dynamically through a self-service interface and is billed on a **pay-per-use** basis. *(As per NIST SP 800-145.)*

**Four Essential Characteristics (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**

1. **On-demand self-service** — Resources can be provisioned by the user without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Available over standard protocols (HTTP, SSH, RDP) from any client device.
3. **Resource pooling** — Provider resources serve multiple consumers via multi-tenant pooling.
4. **Measured service** — Usage is metered, reported, and used for transparent billing.

*(Optional 5th: Rapid elasticity.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Differentiate between Private Cloud and Public Cloud along any six dimensions.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks per dimension)

| Dimension | Private Cloud | Public Cloud |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **Tenancy** | Single organization | Multi-tenant (open public) |
| **Ownership** | Organization or third party (exclusive) | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure) |
| **Location** | On-premise or colocation | Provider's data center (remote) |
| **Cost Model** | Mostly CAPEX + OPEX | Pure OPEX / Pay-as-you-go |
| **Security & Control** | High — fully customizable | Lower — relies on shared responsibility |
| **Scalability** | Bounded by physical capacity | Virtually unlimited |
| **Use Case** | Regulated, sensitive workloads | Web apps, dev/test, variable workloads |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:** Students frequently write *only 3 or 4 differences* and run out of points. The 3-mark question **demands 6 valid points**. Always list 6+ differences to be safe, because a partially-correct dimension only gets 0.25 marks.

---

## 📕 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]**

#### **"Explain in detail the architecture, components, and characteristics of Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS). Discuss its advantages and limitations with suitable use cases."**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe the layered architecture of IaaS, the role of the hypervisor, and the responsibilities of provider vs. consumer.**

#### Model Answer — Sub-Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Layered Architecture (3 Marks):**

IaaS is structured into three logical layers:

- **Provider Layer (Bottom)** — Physical servers, storage arrays, network switches, data-center power and cooling. Fully managed by the cloud provider.
- **Virtualization Layer (Middle)** — The **hypervisor** (Type-1: ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V; Type-2: VirtualBox). The hypervisor abstracts physical hardware into a pool of logical resources.
- **Service Layer (Top)** — Virtual machines, virtual disks, virtual networks, load balancers, and public IPs. Exposed to the consumer via RESTful APIs (e.g., OpenStack Nova API, AWS EC2 API).

**2. Role of the Hypervisor (2 Marks):**

The hypervisor partitions a single physical server into multiple isolated Virtual Machines (VMs). Each VM receives a slice of CPU, memory, disk, and network. The hypervisor enforces isolation (security), schedules vCPUs onto physical cores, and provides features like live migration and snapshots. KVM + QEMU is the most common open-source stack; VMware ESXi is the commercial leader.

**3. Provider vs. Consumer Responsibility (2 Marks):**

| Layer | Provider | Consumer |
|-------|----------|----------|
| Physical hardware, hypervisor, network fabric | ✅ | — |
| Operating System | — | ✅ |
| Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | — | ✅ |

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the advantages, limitations, and suitable use cases of IaaS.**

#### Model Answer — Sub-Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Advantages (2.5 Marks):**
1. **CAPEX → OPEX conversion** — No upfront hardware cost; pay only for what is used.
2. **Elasticity** — Scale out/in in minutes using auto-scaling groups.
3. **Global reach** — Deploy VMs in any region/zones worldwide.
4. **Disaster Recovery** — Replicate VMs across geographies cheaply.
5. **Innovation speed** — Focus engineering effort on apps, not infrastructure.

**Limitations (2.5 Marks):**
1. **Egress cost** — Outbound data transfer is expensive.
2. **Security of multi-tenant boundary** — Shared physical hardware poses risks.
3. **Vendor lock-in** — Migrating workloads between providers is hard.
4. **Less control over physical security** — Trust placed in provider's facility.
5. **Variable cost** — Uncontrolled usage leads to bill shock.

**Suitable Use Cases (2 Marks):**
- **Dev/Test environments** — Spin up and tear down quickly.
- **Big Data / Analytics** — Burst compute for Hadoop/Spark clusters.
- **Disaster Recovery sites** — Cheaper than maintaining a hot standby.
- **Web hosting** — Auto-scaling e-commerce apps.
- **High-Performance Computing** — GPU instances for ML training.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Sub-part (b):** Students often write **only advantages** and forget limitations, or **fail to give specific real-world use cases** like "EC2 on AWS for Netflix's video encoding." Always balance advantages with limitations and pair each with a real example. A generic answer like "use IaaS for any workload" scores 0.

---

### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Set B]**

#### **"What is a Private Cloud? Explain its architecture, key characteristics, deployment models, and compare it with Public and Hybrid Clouds in detail."**

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define Private Cloud, explain its essential characteristics, and describe the OpenStack-based private cloud architecture with a neat diagram.**

#### Model Answer — Sub-Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**
A **Private Cloud** is a cloud deployment model in which the cloud infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a single organization** with multiple consumers (business units). It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization, a third party, or both, and may exist on-premise or off-premise. *(NIST definition.)*

**2. Essential Characteristics (2 Marks):**
- **Single tenancy** — Only one organization uses the resources.
- **On-demand self-service** — Internal users provision via portal.
- **Resource pooling** — Internal pool of virtualized resources.
- **Rapid elasticity** — Internal scale-up/down.
- **Measured service** — Internal chargeback / showback.
- **Custom security and compliance** — Tailored to organizational policies.

**3. OpenStack Architecture (4 Marks):**
OpenStack is the leading open-source platform for building private clouds. Its modular architecture consists of:

| Service | Role |
|---------|------|
| **Keystone** | Identity & authentication (LDAP/AD integration) |
| **Nova** | Compute (VM lifecycle, scheduler) |
| **Neutron** | Networking (SDN, VLANs, security groups) |
| **Cinder** | Block storage (persistent volumes) |
| **Swift** | Object storage (S3-compatible) |
| **Glance** | Image registry (VM templates) |
| **Horizon** | Web dashboard (self-service) |
| **Heat** | Orchestration (auto-scaling templates) |

A typical OpenStack deployment has:
- **Controller nodes** running the control-plane services.
- **Compute nodes** running KVM hypervisors and VMs.
- **Storage nodes** for Cinder (block) and Swift (object).
- **Network nodes** running Neutron agents and SDN overlays.

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Private Cloud, Public Cloud, and Hybrid Cloud along the dimensions of ownership, scalability, cost, security, and suitable use cases. State when an organization should prefer a Private Cloud.**

#### Model Answer — Sub-Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. Tabular Comparison (4 Marks — 0.4 per cell approx):**

| Dimension | Private Cloud | Public Cloud | Hybrid Cloud |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Ownership** | Single org | Third-party provider | Org + provider (combined) |
| **Tenancy** | Single | Multi-tenant | Both |
| **Scalability** | Bounded by own hardware | Virtually unlimited | Combined (burst to public) |
| **Cost Model** | High CAPEX, lower OPEX | Pure OPEX | Mix |
| **Security/Compliance** | Highest (in our control) | Shared responsibility | Selective (sensitive data on private) |
| **Provisioning Time** | Days to weeks | Seconds to minutes | Mixed |
| **Control** | Full | Limited | High on private, low on public |
| **Workload Examples** | Banking ledger, ERP, EHR | Web apps, dev/test, ML training | E-commerce front-end (public) + back-end (private) |

**2. When to Prefer Private Cloud (2 Marks):**
- **Regulatory constraints** — RBI, HIPAA, ITAR require data on-premise.
- **Latency-sensitive workloads** — Sub-millisecond response required.
- **Predictable steady-state workloads** — High utilization justifies CAPEX.
- **Existing data-center investment** — Already paid for, just want cloud features.
- **Intellectual property protection** — Trade secrets must not leave premises.

**3. Real-world example (1 Mark):**
- **RBI's data localization rule (2018)** — All payment data of Indian customers must be stored in India. Hence, banks like SBI and HDFC run **private clouds inside India** for core banking, while using public clouds (AWS Mumbai) for marketing websites.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B (Sub-part b):** A common student mistake is writing *"Private Cloud is always more secure than Public Cloud"* — this is **not always true**; a poorly-managed private cloud is far less secure than a mature public cloud. Examiners deduct marks for **absolute statements** without conditions. Always phrase it as *"Private Cloud is more suitable for organizations with strict regulatory and customization needs."*

---

## 🎯 KTU Examiner's General Valuation Warnings for This Topic

> [!WARNING]
> **Three Highest-Risk Loss Areas in Module 1 (IaaS & Private Cloud):**
>
> 1. **Confusing the responsibility boundary** — Writing that *"the cloud provider manages the OS in IaaS"* is **wrong**. The customer manages the OS in IaaS. This single error can lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Equating "Private Cloud" with "On-Premise"** — Private cloud is defined by **tenancy (single org)**, not by location. A private cloud can be off-premise at a colocation facility. Many students lose marks by writing the wrong definition.
> 3. **Skipping the "essential characteristics" of cloud** — NIST mandates **5 characteristics** (self-service, broad network access, pooling, elasticity, measured service). A virtualized data center without these is *virtualization, not a cloud*. Examiners expect this distinction.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **IaaS =** delivery of **raw virtualized resources** (VMs, storage, network) over the internet/API.
- In IaaS, the **provider manages hardware + hypervisor**; the **consumer manages OS, middleware, runtime, data, app**.
- **Five NIST essential characteristics** of any cloud: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service.
- **IaaS billing** is **pay-per-use**: $\text{Cost} = \sum Q_i \times P_i$.
- **Private Cloud = single-tenant** deployment (NOT necessarily on-premise).
- **Three delivery flavors of private cloud**: on-premise, outsourced, managed.
- **OpenStack** is the leading open-source platform for building private clouds; its key services are **Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Swift, Keystone, Glance, Horizon, Heat**.
- **Public vs Private vs Hybrid**: choose by **workload type, compliance, cost, scalability, and existing CAPEX**.
- **TCO of Private Cloud** is favored when **utilization is high (>60%)** and workloads are **long-running & predictable**; **Public IaaS** wins when **utilization is low and bursty**.
- **Composite SLA** in a private cloud is the **product (or sum of small failure rates)** of component SLAs; the **weakest component** dominates.
- **Oversubscription ratio** in IaaS is typically **2:1 to 6:1 vCPUs to physical cores** for general workloads, **1:1** for CPU-bound workloads.
- **Common KTU 14-mark patterns:** (i) "Explain IaaS architecture & characteristics" (ii) "Compare deployment models" (iii) "Discuss Private Cloud with OpenStack example."
- **Viva trap questions:** *"Can a Private Cloud be in a public data center?"* — **Yes**, if it is dedicated exclusively to one organization. *"Is a VMware vSphere cluster a cloud?"* — **No, not by itself.** It needs self-service APIs, automation, and metering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always begin a 14-mark answer with the **NIST definition** (1 mark), then structure the body using **headings + bullet points + tables + a diagram** (10 marks), and end with **2–3 real-world examples or use cases** (2–3 marks). This visual structure consistently scores 12+/14.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Public Cloud (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> A **Public Cloud** is a cloud deployment model in which computing services (servers, storage, networking, applications, and development platforms) are delivered over the **public Internet** and shared across multiple organizations (tenants) on a **multi-tenant architecture**. The physical infrastructure is owned, managed, and operated by a **third-party Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** such as Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and resources are provisioned dynamically on a **pay-per-use** basis.

The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) SP 800-145** characterizes public cloud under the **cloud deployment model** taxonomy, alongside Private, Hybrid, and Community clouds.

### Essential Attributes of Public Cloud (Must Remember)

- **Multi-Tenancy:** A single physical resource serves multiple consumers logically isolated via virtualization.
- **Elasticity & Scalability:** Resources can be scaled **horizontally (out/in)** and **vertically (up/down)** on demand.
- **Pay-Per-Use Billing:** Metered billing based on consumption (compute hours, GB stored, GB transferred).
- **High Availability:** Typically offers **99.9% to 99.99%** uptime via Service Level Agreements (SLAs).
- **Internet Accessibility:** Services are accessed over the open Internet using standard protocols (HTTPS, REST APIs).
- **No Capital Expenditure (CapEx):** Shifts IT spending from CapEx to **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Public Cloud as a Utility"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: Electricity Grid (Utility Model)**
> Think of the public cloud exactly like the **electricity supplied by your State Electricity Board**. You do not buy a personal power plant for your house. Instead:
> 1. You tap into a **shared, massive power grid** (analogous to the CSP's global data centers).
> 2. You pay only for the **units (kWh) you consume** each month (analogous to metered compute/storage).
> 3. The grid provider handles **generation, maintenance, and delivery** (analogous to AWS/Azure managing hardware).
> 4. You can instantly plug in more appliances when needed, up to the grid's capacity limit (analogous to **elastic scaling**).
> 5. If a transformer fails, redundant infrastructure ensures you barely notice (analogous to **fault tolerance and SLAs**).

**Key Insight for Students:** Just as you do not ask "where is the electricity stored?", in public cloud you should not ask "where exactly is my virtual machine?" — the abstraction is the entire point of the model.

---

## 1.3 Why Public Cloud? — Engineering Motivation

| Driver | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Cost Efficiency** | Eliminates upfront hardware investment (**CapEx → OpEx**) |
| **Global Reach** | Deploy workloads in **30+ geographic regions** within minutes |
| **Innovation Speed** | Instant access to cutting-edge services (AI/ML, Blockchain, IoT, Quantum) |
| **Disaster Recovery** | Built-in geo-redundancy across **Availability Zones (AZs)** |
| **Reduced TCO** | No maintenance, power, cooling, or refresh-cycle costs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to distinguish between **deployment models (Public/Private/Hybrid/Community)** and understand the **service models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS)** layered on top. Public Cloud is the **default reference architecture** used in problem statements.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Public Cloud Architecture — The 5-Layer Logical Model

A public cloud environment is best understood as a **stacked logical architecture**. From the user-facing surface to the underlying hardware, it is organized as follows:

### Layer 1: Client / Frontend Layer
- Web browsers, mobile apps, thin clients, REST API consumers
- Communicates with the cloud via **HTTPS over TCP/IP**

### Layer 2: Network / Internet Backbone Layer
- The **public Internet** acts as the transport medium
- Edge routers, CDNs (Content Delivery Networks), and DNS resolution
- **Bandwidth = critical metric** (typically measured in **Gbps**)

### Layer 3: Service / Abstraction Layer
- **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) — raw VMs, block storage, virtual networks
- **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) — managed runtimes, databases, middleware
- **SaaS** (Software as a Service) — ready-to-use applications (Gmail, Office 365)
- **FaaS / Serverless** — event-driven functions (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions)

### Layer 4: Virtualization / Orchestration Layer
- **Hypervisors** (KVM, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V) partition physical hardware
- **Container engines** (Docker, containerd) for lightweight isolation
- **Orchestrators** (Kubernetes) manage containerized workloads at scale

### Layer 5: Physical Infrastructure Layer
- Data centers containing servers, storage arrays, network switches
- Geographically distributed across **Regions** and **Availability Zones (AZs)**
- Redundant power (UPS, diesel generators), cooling (CRAC units), and connectivity

---

## 2.2 Operational Principles — "How" and "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Public Cloud Request:**
> 1. **Authentication:** User authenticates via **IAM (Identity and Access Management)** using credentials, MFA tokens, or federated SSO (SAML/OAuth).
> 2. **Request Routing:** DNS resolves the service endpoint to the **nearest edge location** based on geo-routing policies.
> 3. **Resource Provisioning:** The **cloud control plane** (e.g., AWS Control Plane) interacts with the orchestration layer to allocate VMs/containers from a shared pool.
> 4. **Tenant Isolation:** A **logical isolation mechanism** (VLAN, VRF, or namespace) ensures tenant A cannot access tenant B's data, even on the same physical host.
> 5. **Metering & Billing:** Every API call and resource-second is logged by the **metering service** for billing.
> 6. **Monitoring & Telemetry:** CloudWatch (AWS) / Azure Monitor continuously tracks health, performance, and SLA compliance.

**The "Why" Behind Multi-Tenancy:**
Multi-tenancy maximizes **resource utilization** (often targeting 60–80% CPU utilization vs. 10–15% in on-premise). It works because statistical multiplexing — combining thousands of unpredictable workloads — smooths out demand spikes, similar to how an insurance company pools risk.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quantitative Metrics for Public Cloud (Exam-Relevant)**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $\text{TCO} = \text{CapEx} + \text{OpEx} + \text{Indirect Costs}$ | USD; Public cloud typically **reduces TCO by 30–40%** |
| 2 | **Pay-Per-Use Cost** | $\text{Cost} = (\text{Compute Hours} \times P_c) + (\text{Storage GB} \times P_s) + (\text{Data Transfer GB} \times P_t)$ | USD per billing cycle |
| 3 | **Service Availability (Uptime %)** | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Total Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$ | **99.9% (Three-Nines)** ≈ 8.76 hrs/yr downtime |
| 4 | **Downtime per Year (n-Nines)** | $\text{Downtime} = 525{,}600 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{n\text{-nines}}{100}\right)$ minutes | **99.99%** ≈ 52.56 min/yr |
| 5 | **Storage Replication Factor** | $\text{Effective Capacity} = \text{Raw Capacity} \div \text{Replication Factor}$ | RF = **3** (typical for high durability) |
| 6 | **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours; higher is better |
| 7 | **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Incidents}}$ | Minutes; lower is better |
| 8 | **Scalability Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Performance After Scale}}{\text{Performance Before Scale} \times \text{Resource Multiplier}}$ | Ideal $\eta \approx 1$ (linear scaling) |
| 9 | **Elasticity Ratio** | $\text{Elasticity} = \dfrac{\text{Peak Load Handled}}{\text{Baseline Capacity Allocated}}$ | Ratio; **8x to 10x** typical for public cloud |
| 10 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $\text{BDP} = \text{Bandwidth} \times \text{RTT}$ | Bits; critical for cloud throughput planning |

**Critical Note for Tables:** All vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve KTU-compliant markdown table rendering.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating Annual Cloud Cost and SLA Compliance

This is the **single most important numerical problem** KTU examiners set on public cloud. The full derivation below follows the marking scheme exactly.

### Problem Statement (Model)
> A startup provisions **4 virtual machines** on AWS EC2. Each VM runs **24 × 7 = 168 hours/week**. The hourly rate is **\$0.10/hr**. Additionally, the team provisions **2 TB of S3 Standard storage** at **\$0.023/GB/month** and transfers **5 TB of data out per month** at **\$0.09/GB** (first 1 TB free under AWS Free Tier beyond standard free allowance in this example). Compute the **annual TCO** assuming **99.95% SLA** with **3 recorded outages** of 40, 25, and 15 minutes respectively. Comment on SLA compliance.

---

### Step 1 — Compute Monthly Compute Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours per Month} &= 168 \text{ hours/week} \times 4.345 \text{ weeks/month} \\
&= 730.0 \text{ hours/month} \\
\text{Compute Cost per VM} &= 730 \text{ hours} \times \$0.10/\text{hr} \\
&= \$73.00 \text{ per VM per month} \\
\text{Total Compute Cost (4 VMs)} &= 4 \times \$73.00 \\
&= \$292.00 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A standard month ≈ **730 hours** (365.25 days × 24 ÷ 12). Always use 730 unless the problem states otherwise.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Monthly Storage Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage in GB} &= 2 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} \\
&= 2048 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Monthly Storage Cost} &= 2048 \text{ GB} \times \$0.023/\text{GB} \\
&= \$47.104 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Storage is billed in **GB-month** units. Tiers and replication may apply in real AWS, but for KTU problems we use the simple flat rate.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Monthly Data Transfer Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Transfer in GB} &= 5 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} \\
&= 5120 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Net Billable Transfer} &= 5120 \text{ GB} - 1024 \text{ GB (free)} \\
&= 4096 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Monthly Transfer Cost} &= 4096 \text{ GB} \times \$0.09/\text{GB} \\
&= \$368.64 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Cloud providers give a **free egress tier** (commonly 100 GB/month on AWS) — subtract this before billing.

---

### Step 4 — Total Monthly and Annual TCO

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Monthly Cost} &= \$292.00 + \$47.104 + \$368.64 \\
&= \$707.744 \text{ per month} \\
\text{Annual TCO} &= \$707.744 \times 12 \\
&= \$8492.928 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Rounded Annual TCO ≈ \$8,493** (or **₹7,10,000 approx** at ₹83.6/USD).

---

### Step 5 — SLA Compliance Check (Critical for KTU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Annual Downtime} &= 40 + 25 + 15 \\
&= 80 \text{ minutes} \\
\text{Allowed Downtime at 99.95\%} &= 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9995) \\
&= 525{,}600 \times 0.0005 \\
&= 262.8 \text{ minutes/year} \\
\text{Compliance Result} &= 80 \text{ min} \le 262.8 \text{ min} \\
&\Rightarrow \textbf{Compliant (SLA Met)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The infrastructure **complies** with the 99.95% SLA. The provider would owe **no SLA credit**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Public Cloud vs Other Deployment Models

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Comparison Table (Frequently Tested in KTU 2-Markers)**

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Owner / Operator** | Third-party CSP | Single organization | Org + CSP combination | Multiple orgs with shared concerns |
| **Access** | Public Internet | Private network / VPN | Mixed | Limited community |
| **Tenants** | Multiple (multi-tenant) | Single (single-tenant) | Multiple | Multiple (same domain) |
| **Cost Model** | OpEx (pay-per-use) | CapEx-heavy + OpEx | Mixed | Shared CapEx |
| **Security Level** | Moderate–High (provider-managed) | Very High (custom) | High | High (domain-specific) |
| **Scalability** | Virtually Unlimited | Limited by own hardware | High | Moderate |
| **Control** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate | Moderate–High |
| **Examples** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack on-prem, VMware | AWS Outposts + on-prem | GovCloud, Healthcare clouds |
| **Compliance Suitability** | Standard workloads | Strict regulatory | Mixed workloads | Industry-specific (e.g., HIPAA) |

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Framework — "When to Choose Public Cloud"

Use this **decision matrix** to justify the choice of public cloud in KTU design questions:

| Use Case | Public Cloud Suitability | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Predictable, steady-state workloads** | ⭐⭐⭐ Medium | Reserved Instances can reduce cost, but private may be cheaper long-term |
| **Spiky / unpredictable workloads** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | Elasticity absorbs spikes without overprovisioning |
| **Highly sensitive (PII, PHI, financial) data** | ⭐⭐ Low | Compliance + control concerns |
| **Startups & SMBs** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | Zero CapEx, immediate scale |
| **Disaster Recovery site** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | Pay only when DR is active |
| **Big Data / HPC bursts** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Excellent | Spot instances save up to 90% |

**Engineering Takeaway:** Every single cloud interaction — even fetching a single S3 object — traverses this 9-step lifecycle. This is **why cloud-native latency** exists (typically **20–100 ms**) and why **edge computing** is used to reduce it for latency-sensitive apps (AR/VR, gaming).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Public Cloud. List any four characteristics of public cloud deployment model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Public cloud is a cloud deployment model in which computing resources (servers, storage, applications, and services) are owned and managed by a third-party Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and delivered over the public Internet to multiple tenants on a pay-per-use basis.

**Any Four Characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. Multi-tenant architecture with logical isolation between consumers
2. High elasticity and on-demand scalability (horizontal and vertical)
3. Pay-per-use / metered billing (OpEx-based, no CapEx)
4. Internet-based accessibility using standard protocols (HTTPS, REST)
5. Managed entirely by the CSP — no hardware maintenance burden
6. High availability guaranteed by SLAs (typically 99.9% to 99.99%)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Public Cloud and Private Cloud with respect to ownership, cost model, scalability, and security.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each axis):**

| Axis | Public Cloud | Private Cloud |
|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Third-party CSP (e.g., AWS) | Single organization |
| **Cost Model** | OpEx (pay-per-use) | CapEx-heavy + OpEx |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited, elastic | Limited to in-house hardware |
| **Security** | Provider-managed, moderate control | Full organizational control, very high security |

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts: (a) and (b). The model answers below are structured to align exactly with the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

---

### Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Question:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of Public Cloud**. Discuss the **benefits and challenges** of adopting public cloud for a mid-size enterprise.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Public Cloud Architecture

**Model Answer:**

The architecture of public cloud is organized into **five logical layers** stacked from the user-facing surface down to the physical infrastructure.

**1. Client / Frontend Layer (1 mark):**
This layer consists of the user-facing interfaces — web browsers, mobile applications, thin clients, and APIs. These clients interact with the cloud over the public Internet using standard protocols such as HTTPS, REST, and gRPC. The client sends authenticated requests to the nearest cloud endpoint.

**2. Network Layer (1 mark):**
The public Internet, augmented with **CDN edge locations** and **DNS geo-routing**, acts as the transport medium. This layer handles request routing, TLS termination, and bandwidth management to ensure low-latency global access.

**3. Service / Abstraction Layer (2 marks):**
This is the most critical layer from the consumer's perspective. It provides:
- **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) — raw VMs, block storage, virtual networks
- **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) — managed runtimes, databases, middleware
- **SaaS** (Software as a Service) — ready-to-use end-user applications
- **FaaS / Serverless** — event-driven execution of stateless functions

**4. Virtualization and Orchestration Layer (1.5 marks):**
**Hypervisors** (KVM, ESXi, Hyper-V) partition physical hardware into multiple isolated VMs. **Container engines** (Docker) and **orchestrators** (Kubernetes) provide lightweight, portable workload isolation. The orchestration layer enables auto-scaling, self-healing, and rolling updates.

**5. Physical Infrastructure Layer (1.5 marks):**
Globally distributed **data centers** organized into **Regions** (geographic areas) and **Availability Zones (AZs)** (isolated data centers within a region). This layer provides redundant power (UPS, generators), cooling (CRAC units), physical security, and high-speed fiber connectivity.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Benefits and Challenges

**Benefits (4 marks):**
1. **Cost Efficiency (1 mark):** Eliminates capital expenditure on hardware; converts to predictable operational expenditure. Studies show 30–40% TCO reduction.
2. **Elastic Scalability (1 mark):** Handles 10x traffic spikes within minutes via auto-scaling — impossible in on-premise environments without overprovisioning.
3. **Global Reach (0.5 mark):** Deploy in 30+ geographic regions with a few clicks; supports CDN for low-latency content delivery.
4. **Disaster Recovery (0.5 mark):** Built-in cross-region replication reduces RPO to minutes and RTO to hours.
5. **Innovation Access (0.5 mark):** Instant access to AI/ML, IoT, blockchain, and quantum services without R&D investment.
6. **No Maintenance Overhead (0.5 mark):** CSP handles patching, hardware refresh, and capacity planning.

**Challenges (3 marks):**
1. **Security and Compliance (1 mark):** Data resides on third-party infrastructure; industries like BFSI and healthcare face regulatory hurdles (e.g., RBI, HIPAA, GDPR).
2. **Data Lock-in / Vendor Lock-in (0.5 mark):** Migrating workloads between CSPs is complex due to proprietary APIs and services.
3. **Latency Sensitivity (0.5 mark):** Internet-based access introduces 20–100 ms latency, unsuitable for real-time control systems.
4. **Unpredictable Costs (0.5 mark):** Without proper governance, pay-per-use can lead to bill shock (e.g., unbounded data egress).
5. **Limited Customization (0.5 mark):** Cannot customize underlying hypervisors, network topology, or hardware.

---

### Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**Question:** Explain the **service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) layered on public cloud** with suitable examples. Compute the **annual TCO** for a workload using 3 VMs at \$0.12/hr, 1.5 TB storage at \$0.02/GB/month, and 3 TB data transfer at \$0.085/GB (no free tier in this problem). Assume 99.9% SLA with two outages of 30 and 45 minutes. Verify SLA compliance.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Service Models on Public Cloud

**Model Answer:**
