> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram abstracts any technical use case — a healthcare portal, an e-commerce site, a KTU internal ERP — into the same canonical **edge → LB → compute → data → observability** topology.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q1.** Define a *technical use case* in the context of cloud computing. List the four essential elements that characterize it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *technical use case* is a structured description of how a specific real-world engineering problem is solved using cloud computing resources (compute, storage, network, platform, or software services). The four essential elements are:
1. **Actor** — the user, system, or event that initiates the workload.
2. **Preconditions** — the state of the system before the use case starts (e.g., credentials, network, data).
3. **Flow of Events** — the sequence of interactions between actor and cloud services.
4. **Postconditions / NFRs** — the resulting state plus non-functional requirements such as latency, availability, scalability, and compliance.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q2.** Differentiate between *IaaS*, *PaaS*, *SaaS*, and *FaaS* with one example technical use case for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Raw virtual machines and storage rented on demand. Example use case — hosting a custom **e-commerce checkout service** on AWS EC2.
- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Managed runtime and middleware; the developer only writes application code. Example use case — deploying a **REST API** on Google App Engine.
- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Ready-to-use end-user application delivered over the internet. Example use case — using **Microsoft 365** for word processing and email.
- **FaaS (Function-as-a-Service):** Event-driven, sub-second-billed, stateless code execution. Example use case — **image-resizing Lambda** triggered whenever a user uploads a photo to S3.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A.** *(a)* Discuss in detail **five major technical use cases** of cloud computing across different industry verticals, highlighting the cloud characteristics (elasticity, pay-per-use, self-service) that make each use case suitable. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A healthcare startup stores 10 TB of patient MRI data that grows by 1 TB every month. Compute the **monthly storage cost** using (i) hot S3 Standard storage, and (ii) S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval. Assume S3 Standard = **$0.023/GB/month** and S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval = **$0.004/GB/month**. Recommend which tier is suitable. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

| # | Use Case | Industry | Cloud Characteristic Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Genomic Sequencing Pipeline** | Bioinformatics / Healthcare | **Massive elasticity** — burst to 10,000 cores for 4 hrs using Spot VMs |
| 2 | **Video Lecture Streaming (EdTech)** | Education | **CDN + auto-scaling** — handle 50,000 concurrent users during exams |
| 3 | **Real-time Fraud Detection (FinTech)** | Banking | **Low-latency compute + streaming** — sub-100 ms ML inference at the edge |
| 4 | **IoT Telemetry Ingestion (Smart City)** | Government | **Managed message queues + time-series DB** — absorb 1 M events/sec |
| 5 | **AI Chatbot Inference (Retail)** | E-Commerce | **Serverless GPU** — pay only per query, no idle ML servers |

Each use case leverages the **five essential cloud characteristics** (on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, measured service).

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Current data} &= 10 \text{ TB} = 10{,}240 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Growth in month} &= 1 \text{ TB} = 1{,}024 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Total at month-end} &= 11{,}264 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Average stored} &\approx \frac{10{,}240 + 11{,}264}{2} = 10{,}752 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost on S3 Standard:**
$$
C_1 = 10{,}752 \times 0.023 = \$247.30 \text{ per month}
$$

**Cost on S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval:**
$$
C_2 = 10{,}752 \times 0.004 = \$43.01 \text{ per month}
$$

**Annualized savings:** $(247.30 - 43.01) \times 12 = \$2{,}451.48$.

**Recommendation:** **S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval** is suitable if MRI retrieval latency of **< 1 second** is acceptable (typical for diagnostic review). If real-time intra-operative access is needed, S3 Standard is mandatory despite the higher cost.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**B.** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **reference architecture of a cloud-native e-commerce use case**. Identify the role of each layer: Edge/CDN, Load Balancer, Auto-Scaling Group, Managed Database, Object Storage, and Observability. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A trading platform requires **99.999% availability**. If the current system has **MTBF = 5000 hours** and **MTTR = 1 hour**, calculate:
  (i) Current availability
  (ii) How much must MTTR be reduced to meet the SLA if MTBF remains the same?
  (iii) Suggest two architectural techniques to achieve this SLA. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (a) (7 marks)

**Reference Architecture Layers (matches the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):**

1. **Edge / CDN Layer** — Terminates user requests nearest to geography, caches static content (logos, CSS, JS), reducing origin load.
2. **Global Load Balancer** — Distributes traffic across multiple regions / availability zones; performs health checks.
3. **Auto-Scaling Group (App Tier)** — Stateless application servers that scale out under load and scale in during quiet hours.
4. **Managed Database Tier** — Multi-AZ relational (e.g., Amazon RDS) or NoSQL (DynamoDB) with automated failover.
5. **Object Storage Layer** — Stores product images, videos, invoices (S3), with lifecycle rules moving old data to cold tiers.
6. **Observability Layer** — Centralized logging (CloudWatch, ELK), metrics, distributed tracing (X-Ray, OpenTelemetry), and alerting.

#### Model Solution — (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Current Availability:**
$$
A = \frac{5000}{5000 + 1} \times 100 = 99.98\%
$$

**(ii) Required MTTR for 99.999% with MTBF = 5000 hrs:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.99999 &= \frac{5000}{5000 + MTTR} \\
5000 + MTTR &= \frac{5000}{0.99999} = 5000.05 \\
MTTR &= 0.05 \text{ hours} = 3 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

So MTTR must drop from **60 minutes → 3 minutes** — a **20× improvement**.

**(iii) Architectural Techniques:**
1. **Active-Active Multi-Region Deployment** — Replicate the application across two geographically separate regions with automated DNS failover (Route 53 health checks). Failure of one region is invisible to end users.
2. **Database Multi-AZ with Read Replicas + Automated Failover** — Pair the primary DB with a synchronous standby in another AZ; use connection-pool retry logic to absorb the ~30–60 second failover window.
3. *(Bonus)* **Chaos Engineering** with tools like AWS Fault Injection Service to continuously validate the SLA.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** confuse *availability* with *durability*. **Availability = uptime of the service.** **Durability = long-term survival of the data** (e.g., S3 Standard = **99.999999999% — 11 nines**).
> - **Do not** state the 99.999% SLA implies "no downtime ever" — it permits **5.26 minutes/year**. Many students incorrectly write "zero downtime".
> - In TCO derivations, **always include egress cost**. Forgetting egress is the #1 reason students lose 2 marks in cost calculations.
> - When describing use cases, **name the cloud characteristic** (elasticity / pay-per-use / self-service) explicitly. Vague answers like *"cloud is good"* get zero credit on the 7-mark sub-parts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Technical Use Cases (Module 1)**
> - A **technical use case** = actor + precondition + flow + NFRs + postcondition.
> - Cloud characteristics (NIST): **5 essential** + **3 service models** + **4 deployment models**.
> - **Use cases** split into: Compute-dominant, Data-dominant, Latency-dominant, Compliance-dominant.
> - **Service model selection** depends on control vs convenience trade-off: IaaS (max control) → FaaS (max convenience).
> - **Availability formula** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ is the single most-tested equation in this module.
> - **Five-nines SLA** = 5.26 minutes/year downtime; **three-nines** = 8.77 hours/year.
> - **TCO includes CapEx + discounted OpEx** over the project life; **never omit egress costs**.
> - **RTO vs RPO**: RTO = how fast you recover; RPO = how much data you can afford to lose.
> - **Data gravity** dictates where compute should live — heavy data pulls compute to it, not the reverse.
> - **Hybrid cloud** is the answer when compliance + elasticity must coexist.
> - **Cloud Suitability Index (CSI)** rises with elasticity, variable load, OpEx preference, and global reach.
> - Reference architecture for *any* use case: **CDN → LB → Auto-Scaled Compute → Managed Data → Object Storage → Observability**.
> - KTU 2024 emphasis: connect each use case to a specific **cloud characteristic** and a specific **service model**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtualization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Virtualization** is the foundational technology of cloud computing that enables the creation of a *virtual* (rather than actual/physical) version of computing resources — including servers, storage devices, networks, operating systems, and applications — by abstracting the underlying physical hardware through a software layer known as a **hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> *"Virtualization is a technology that decouples the physical hardware from the operating system and applications by introducing a software abstraction layer, allowing multiple operating systems and applications to run concurrently on a single physical machine while sharing the same underlying hardware resources."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hypervisor (VMM)** | The software layer that creates, runs, and manages Virtual Machines (VMs) |
| **Virtual Machine (VM)** | A software-emulated computer system that executes programs like a physical machine |
| **Host OS / Host Machine** | The underlying physical hardware plus its primary OS (in Type 2) |
| **Guest OS** | The operating system running *inside* a VM |
| **Virtualization Layer** | The abstraction layer between hardware and VMs |
| **Resource Pooling** | Aggregation of physical resources that are dynamically assigned to VMs |

> [!NOTE]
> The CPU instruction set extensions **Intel VT-x** and **AMD-V** are *hardware-assisted virtualization* technologies that allow the CPU to trap and forward privileged instructions directly to the hypervisor, drastically improving VM performance.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Apartment Building"

Imagine a single-story house (your **physical server**) with one kitchen, one living room, and one bedroom. Only one family can live in it. If a second family moves in, there's a fight for space.

Now imagine a **20-story apartment building** built on the same plot of land. The building's architect (the **hypervisor**) divides the floors into identical, self-contained apartments. Each apartment has its own kitchen, bedroom, door, and even address — but they all share the same foundation, water supply, electricity grid, and elevator system (the **shared physical hardware**).

- Each apartment = a **Virtual Machine**
- The building's architect/floor manager = the **Hypervisor**
- The shared water and electricity = **CPU, RAM, Disk, Network** of the host
- Each apartment's lock and key = **VM isolation** (one VM cannot peek into another)

**The crucial point:** Just as 20 families can now live on the same plot that earlier fit only one, virtualization lets you run **dozens of independent "computers" (VMs)** on a single physical server.

---

## 1.4 Why Virtualization? The Core Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Highlight:** Examiners frequently ask *"Why is virtualization critical for cloud computing?"* The answer must contain at least three of the following four pillars:

1. **Server Consolidation** — Run many VMs on one physical host (increases utilization from the industry average of **~10–15%** for bare-metal servers to **60–80%**).
2. **Resource Abstraction** — Decouples workloads from physical hardware, enabling **portability** and **live migration**.
3. **Isolation & Security** — A crash, malware, or misconfiguration in one VM does not affect other VMs on the same host.
4. **Rapid Provisioning** — A new VM can be spun up in **seconds** versus hours for a physical server.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Hypervisor Overhead:** Typically **2%–10%** of host CPU/RAM capacity.
- **VM Density:** Industry standard of **10–20 VMs per physical core**, depending on workload.
- **Live Migration Downtime:** Modern hypervisors like VMware vMotion achieve **< 2 seconds** of downtime.
- **Memory Page Size (x86-64):** Standard **4 KB**, with **2 MB HugePages** and **1 GB Giant Pages** common in virtualized environments.

---

## 1.6 The Three Pillars of Virtualization (Exam Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 explicitly divides virtualization into **three pillars**. Memorize these — they appear almost every semester.

| Pillar | What it Virtualizes | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute Virtualization** | CPU + RAM | VMware vSphere, KVM |
| **Storage Virtualization** | Disks / Storage Arrays | SAN, NAS, Amazon EBS |
| **Network Virtualization** | Switches, Routers, Firewalls | VMware NSX, Cisco ACI |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hypervisor: Heart of Virtualization

A **hypervisor (VMM)** is the most critical component of any virtualized environment. It is responsible for:

- **VM Lifecycle Management** — Create, start, pause, resume, migrate, destroy.
- **Resource Scheduling** — Decide which VM gets a CPU core at a given instant.
- **Memory Management** — Map guest physical memory to host physical memory.
- **I/O Virtualization** — Emulate or pass-through disks, NICs, USB devices.
- **Isolation Enforcement** — Ensure no VM can read/write another VM's memory.

---

## 2.2 Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisor (Board Favorite)

### Type 1 — Bare-Metal Hypervisor
- Installed **directly on the physical hardware**; no host OS exists.
- Used in **enterprise data centers** and **public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP)**.
- Examples: **VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V (Server Core), Citrix XenServer, KVM**.

### Type 2 — Hosted Hypervisor
- Runs **as an application on top of a host OS** (Windows, Linux, macOS).
- Used for **development, testing, and learning**.
- Examples: **VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop, QEMU**.

---

## 2.3 Types of Virtualization (Detailed Taxonomy)

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Virtualization** | Guest OS is *unmodified*; hypervisor traps privileged instructions. | Running legacy OSes |
| **Para-Virtualization** | Guest OS is *modified* to make hypercalls for performance. | Xen with Linux |
| **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization** | CPU provides **VT-x/AMD-V** extensions to trap instructions in hardware. | Modern ESXi, KVM |
| **OS-Level Virtualization** | Kernel allows multiple *isolated user-space instances* (containers). | Docker, LXC |
| **Application Virtualization** | Apps run in a sandboxed virtual environment, decoupled from OS. | Citrix XenApp |
| **Desktop Virtualization (VDI)** | Entire desktop OS runs on a remote server. | VMware Horizon |

---

## 2.4 CPU Virtualization — The Core Mechanism

### 2.4.1 Privilege Levels (x86 Rings)

The x86 architecture defines **four privilege rings (0–3)**:

- **Ring 0** — Kernel mode (most privileged; can execute any instruction)
- **Ring 1, 2** — Device drivers (rarely used)
- **Ring 3** — User mode (least privileged)

A normal OS runs in **Ring 0**. In a VM, the guest OS *thinks* it is in Ring 0, but the hypervisor intercepts the actual privileged operations.

### 2.4.2 Three Classical Approaches

1. **Full Virtualization (Software Emulation)**
   - Guest OS is unmodified.
   - Privileged instructions are *trapped* and *emulated* in software.
   - **Slower** — emulation overhead.
   - Example: early VMware Workstation.

2. **Para-Virtualization**
   - Guest OS is *modified* to make explicit **hypercalls** to the hypervisor.
   - **Faster** than full emulation, but requires guest OS source code.
   - Example: early Xen.

3. **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization**
   - CPU provides new execution modes (**VMX non-root mode** on Intel, **SVM guest mode** on AMD).
   - Privileged instructions are trapped **directly in hardware** with zero overhead.
   - **Fastest** and the modern standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**FPH** = Full, Para, Hardware-assisted" — list these three in *that* order when asked to "explain CPU virtualization techniques."

---

## 2.5 Memory Virtualization

Memory virtualization introduces **two layers of address translation**:

1. **Guest Virtual Address (GVA)** → **Guest Physical Address (GPA)**
2. **Guest Physical Address (GPA)** → **Host Physical Address (HPA)**

The **Shadow Page Table** approach (used in early full virtualization) maintains hidden copies of page tables in hardware. Modern hypervisors use **Extended Page Tables (EPT)** on Intel or **Rapid Virtualization Indexing (RVI / NPT)** on AMD to do the translation in hardware, dramatically reducing overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> The data structure that maps GVA → GPA is the **Guest Page Table**, and GPA → HPA is the **Extended Page Table (EPT)**. KTU examiners love this terminology.

---

## 2.6 I/O Virtualization

Three approaches exist for I/O devices:

| Approach | Mechanism | Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **Emulation** | Hypervisor emulates the device in software | Slowest |
| **Para-virtualization (virtio)** | Shared ring buffer between guest and host | Fast |
| **PCI Passthrough (VT-d)** | Guest gets *direct* access to the physical device | Fastest (near-native) |
| **SR-IOV** | Single physical NIC appears as many *virtual functions* | Fastest + scalable |

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table for KTU ESE Problems.** Memorize these formulas — they appear in both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Server Consolidation Ratio** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{physical, before}}}{N_{\text{physical, after}}}$ | Dimensionless | Higher is better |
| **VM CPU Allocation** | $vCPU_{i} = \lfloor \dfrac{\text{Host Cores} \times \text{Cap}_{i}}{\sum_{j} \text{Cap}_{j}} \rfloor$ | vCPUs | `Cap` is shares/capacity |
| **Memory Over-provisioning Ratio** | $\text{OPR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{VM Memory}}{\text{Host Physical Memory}}$ | Dimensionless | `OPR > 1` = overcommit |
| **VM Density** | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{N_{\text{VMs}}}{N_{\text{physical cores}}}$ | VMs/core | Industry norm: 10–20 |
| **Hypervisor Overhead** | $O_{\text{hypervisor}} \approx 0.02 \text{ to } 0.10 \times C_{\text{host}}$ | % of capacity | 2–10% |
| **Live Migration Bandwidth** | $BW_{\text{mig}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{VM}}}{\Delta t_{\text{mig}}}$ | MB/s | Mem copy time |
| **Effective VM Throughput** | $T_{\text{eff}} = T_{\text{native}} \times (1 - O_{\text{hypervisor}})$ | Ops/sec | Models virtualization loss |
| **HugePage Saving** | $\text{Saving} = 1 - \dfrac{4 \text{ KB}}{2 \text{ MB}}$ (per page) | Ratio | 2 MB vs 4 KB |
| **PCIe Passthrough Limit** | $\vert \text{VFs} \vert \le 256$ per device | VFs | SR-IOV spec limit |
| **CPU Steal Time** | $t_{\text{steal}} = t_{\text{ready}} - t_{\text{run}}$ | % | A vCPU waiting for pCPU |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS, Azure, GCP** run on Type 1 hypervisors to host millions of tenants.
- **VMware vSphere** dominates private enterprise data centers.
- **KVM** is the de-facto standard in Linux-based clouds (OpenStack).
- **Containers (Docker)** are a *lightweight alternative* to full VMs but **do not replace** them — VMs provide stronger isolation because each VM has its own kernel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Virtualization Type

The following **analytical matrix** is the standard KTU approach for "compare and contrast" 14-mark questions. Use this as your answer skeleton.

| Dimension | Full Virtualization | Para-Virtualization | Hardware-Assisted | OS-Level (Containers) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Guest OS Modification | None | Required | None | Shares host kernel |
| Performance | Moderate | High | Near-Native | Near-Native |
| Isolation | Strong | Strong | Strong | Weak (shared kernel) |
| Examples | VMware ESXi (legacy) | Xen (early) | KVM, ESXi, Hyper-V | Docker, LXC |
| Boot Time | 30–60 s | 20–40 s | 20–40 s | < 1 s |
| Memory Overhead | High | Medium | Medium | Very Low |

---

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Derivation — Server Consolidation & Overcommitment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently gives a *numerical scenario* (4–7 marks inside a 14-mark question). Practice the steps below.

### **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question):**
A company has **20 physical servers**, each running at **15% average CPU utilization**. Each server has **8 cores** and **32 GB RAM**. The company consolidates them onto **3 new servers**, each with **32 cores** and **128 GB RAM**, using a hypervisor. Compute:
1. The **server consolidation ratio**.
2. The **aggregate core capacity** before and after.
3. Whether **memory overcommitment is required**, and by what factor.
4. The **expected CPU utilization** post-consolidation (assuming the same workload).

---

### **Step 1 — Server Consolidation Ratio (CR)**

We have:

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{N_{\text{physical, before}}}{N_{\text{physical, after}}}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\text{CR} = \dfrac{20}{3} \approx 6.67
$$

**Interpretation:** The new infrastructure runs the workload of 6.67 old servers on each new server.

---

### **Step 2 — Aggregate Core Capacity**

**Before consolidation:**

$$
C_{\text{before}} = 20 \text{ servers} \times 8 \text{ cores/server} = 160 \text{ cores}
$$

**After consolidation:**

$$
C_{\text{after}} = 3 \text{ servers} \times 32 \text{ cores/server} = 96 \text{ cores}
$$

**Total core demand vs supply:**

$$
\text{Demand} = 160 \text{ pCPU equivalents at } 15\% \text{ util}
$$

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 160 \times 0.15 = 24 \text{ pCPU}
$$

After consolidation, total core *capacity* is 96, and the *effective* demand is only 24 cores worth. So the new farm is **vastly over-provisioned for CPU** (good — headroom for spikes).

---

### **Step 3 — Memory Overcommitment Analysis**

**Total memory demand (assuming each server's RAM was 70% utilized on average):**

$$
M_{\text{demand}} = 20 \times 32 \text{ GB} \times 0.70 = 448 \text{ GB}
$$

**Total physical memory available on the new 3-server farm:**

$$
M_{\text{supply}} = 3 \times 128 \text{ GB} = 384 \text{ GB}
$$

**Overcommit Ratio (OPR):**

$$
\text{OPR} = \dfrac{M_{\text{demand}}}{M_{\text{supply}}} = \dfrac{448}{384} = 1.167
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since OPR = 1.167 > 1, **overcommitment IS required**. This is safe only if memory is reclaimed via **ballooning**, **transparent page sharing (TPS)**, or **memory compression** by the hypervisor.

---

### **Step 4 — Expected CPU Utilization Post-Consolidation**

$$
U_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{\text{Total effective demand}}{\text{Total new capacity}} = \dfrac{24 \text{ pCPU}}{96 \text{ pCPU}} = 0.25 = 25\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Going from 15% to 25% may *look* small, but absolute capacity freed is enormous. The remaining 75% of cores can be used for **bursty workloads**, **failover headroom**, or **DRS (Distributed Resource Scheduler) migrations**.

---

### **Step 5 — Full Clean Summary Table (Drop-in for Answer Sheet)**

| Metric | Before | After | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical servers | 20 | 3 | **−85% hardware** |
| Total cores | 160 | 96 | **−40% cores** |
| Total RAM (GB) | 640 | 384 | **−40% RAM** |
| Effective CPU used (pCPU) | 24 | 24 | Same |
| Effective RAM used (GB) | 448 | 448 | Same |
| Utilization % | 15% | 25% | **+10 pp** |
| OPR (RAM) | 1.0 | 1.167 | Overcommit needed |

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Live Migration Bandwidth Requirement

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 sometimes asks: *"If a VM with 16 GB RAM must be live-migrated over 10 seconds, what is the minimum link bandwidth?"*

**Step 1 — Convert RAM to MB:**

$$
M_{\text{VM}} = 16 \text{ GB} = 16 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 16384 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the migration bandwidth formula:**

$$
BW_{\text{mig}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{VM}}}{\Delta t_{\text{mig}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
BW_{\text{mig}} = \dfrac{16384 \text{ MB}}{10 \text{ s}} = 1638.4 \text{ MB/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Gbps (1 MB/s = 8 Mb/s):**

$$
BW_{\text{mig}} = 1638.4 \times 8 = 13107.2 \text{ Mb/s} \approx 13.1 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **10 GigE NIC** is sufficient (with a small margin).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — VM Density & Headroom Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic 7-mark sub-question. Walk through it step by step.

**Given:**
- Host has **32 physical cores**.
- Hypervisor reserves **2 cores** for itself.
- Industry guideline: **12 VMs per physical core**.

**Step 1 — Usable cores for VMs:**

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ cores}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum VM count:**

$$
N_{\text{VM, max}} = 30 \times 12 = 360 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 3 — With 30% headroom for failover:**

$$
N_{\text{VM, target}} = 360 \times (1 - 0.30) = 252 \text{ VMs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A board examiner's *model answer* will explicitly state the units and interpretation at each step. Always write the unit and a one-line "interpretation sentence" after every numerical result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Type 1 (Bare-Metal) Hypervisor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[VM1 Guest OS Linux] --> H[Hypervisor ESXi KVM Hyper-V]
    B[VM2 Guest OS Windows] --> H
    C[VM3 Guest OS Linux] --> H
    D[VM4 Guest OS Windows] --> H
    E[VM5 Guest OS Linux] --> H
    H --> P[Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk NIC]
    P --> CPU[Multi Core x86 CPU with VT-x AMD-V]
    P --> MEM[Server Grade ECC RAM]
    P --> DISK[SAS SATA NVMe Storage]
    P --> NET[10G 25G 100G NICs with SR-IOV]
    H -. manages .- VM1
    H -. manages .- VM2
    H -. manages .- VM3
    H -. manages .- VM4
    H -. manages .- VM5
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Type 2 (Hosted) Hypervisor Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[VM1 Guest OS Linux] --> HV[Hosted Hypervisor VirtualBox VMware Workstation]
    B[VM2 Guest OS Windows] --> HV
    C[VM3 Guest OS Linux] --> HV
    HV --> OS[Host OS Windows 11 Ubuntu macOS]
    OS --> HW[Physical Hardware]
    HV -. translates privileged instructions .-> OS
    A -. user workload .-> HV
    B -. user workload .-> HV
    C -. user workload .-> HV
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Flow — Full vs Para vs Hardware-Assisted

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Guest OS sends privileged instruction] --> R{Which mode}
    R -->|Full Virt| S[Hypervisor traps and emulates in software]
    R -->|Para Virt| T[Modified guest makes hypercall to hypervisor]
    R -->|HW Assisted| U[CPU traps in hardware VT-x AMD-V and forwards]
    S --> X[Moderate performance]
    T --> Y[High performance]
    U --> Z[Near native performance]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Memory Virtualization (EPT / Shadow Page Tables)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application running in VM] --> B[Guest Virtual Address GVA]
    B --> C[Guest Page Table CR3 in guest OS]
    C --> D[Guest Physical Address GPA]
    D --> E[Extended Page Table EPT or NPT managed by hypervisor]
    E --> F[Host Physical Address HPA]
    F --> G[DRAM chips on motherboard]
    E -. maintained by .-> HV[Hypervisor]
    C -. maintained by .-> GUEST[Guest OS]
```

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Server Consolidation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[20 physical servers 15 percent CPU] --> B[Capacity planning tool]
    B --> C[3 new physical servers 32 cores each]
    C --> D[Install Type 1 hypervisor ESXi KVM]
    D --> E[Create 20 VMs one per workload]
    E --> F[Enable DRS HA vMotion]
    F --> G[Target utilization 60 to 80 percent]
    G --> H[Power off 17 old servers save power and cooling]
```

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Architecture — The Three Pillars of Virtualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VC[Cloud Workload] --> P[Virtualization Platform]
    P --> P1[Compute Virtualization]
    P --> P2[Storage Virtualization]
    P --> P3[Network Virtualization]
    P1 --> P1A[CPU and RAM are abstracted into vCPUs and vRAM]
    P2 --> P2A[Disks and SAN are abstracted into virtual disks and datastores]
    P3 --> P3A[Switches and routers are abstracted into virtual switches and overlays]
    P1A --> OUT[Unified Cloud Resource Pool]
    P2A --> OUT
    P3A --> OUT
```
