<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define bisection bandwidth of a data center network. Why is it considered a critical design metric?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Bisection bandwidth is the sum of the bandwidths of the links that cross an imaginary cut that partitions the network into two equal halves (each containing $N/2$ servers).
>
> **Why critical (2 marks):**
> (i) It represents the **worst-case** bandwidth available between the two halves of the data center. If a workload is balanced such that half the servers send to the other half, the bisection bandwidth is the bottleneck.
> (ii) It determines the **all-to-all communication throughput**, which is fundamental for MapReduce, Spark, and shuffle-heavy applications.
> (iii) A low bisection bandwidth causes congestion at the upper-tier switches, leading to increased tail latency — a violation of cloud SLAs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Differentiate between switch-centric and server-centric data center architectures with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Switch-Centric (1.5 marks):** The network intelligence and packet forwarding is done entirely by the switches. Servers are passive endpoints with a single NIC. Examples: **Fat-Tree (Al-Fares et al.)**, VL2, Jellyfish.
>
> **Server-Centric (1.5 marks):** The servers themselves have multiple NICs and participate in packet forwarding using a server-local switching module. This provides multiple disjoint paths and high fault tolerance. Examples: **BCube (Guo et al.)**, DCell, FiConn.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Fat-Tree Analysis**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a $k$-ary Fat-Tree with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages over a traditional 3-tier tree. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hyperscale cloud provider wants to deploy a $k$-ary Fat-Tree to support **$N = 13{,}824$ servers** with **$B_{\text{port}} = 40$ Gbps**. Compute:
   (i) The minimum value of $k$.
   (ii) The total number of switches and links required.
   (iii) The bisection bandwidth in Tbps.
   (iv) The number of equal-cost paths between any two servers in different pods. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Fat-Tree Architecture (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A $k$-ary Fat-Tree is a special instance of a *3-stage Clos network* built entirely from $k$-port commodity switches. It consists of $k$ pods, with $k/2$ Edge and $k/2$ Aggregation switches per pod, and $(k/2)^2$ Core switches in the core layer. Each switch has $k$ ports, of which $k/2$ face downward and $k/2$ face upward.

**Layer-wise description (3 marks):**
- **Edge Layer:** Connects servers within a pod. Each edge switch has $k/2$ ports to servers and $k/2$ ports to aggregation switches in the same pod.
- **Aggregation Layer:** Each aggregation switch has $k/2$ ports to edge switches (in the same pod) and $k/2$ ports to core switches.
- **Core Layer:** $(k/2)^2$ core switches form the backbone. Each core switch connects to one aggregation switch in each pod.

**Advantages over 3-tier tree (2 marks):**
- **No oversubscription:** Bisection bandwidth equals the aggregate server bandwidth.
- **Path diversity:** $(k/2)^2$ equal-cost paths between any two pods → excellent fault tolerance.
- **Commodity hardware:** All switches are identical $k$-port devices → lower CapEx and easier maintenance.

**(Refer to the Fat-Tree diagram in SECTION 4.2 for the visual explanation.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Minimum $k$ (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{k^3}{4} \implies k^3 = 4N = 4 \times 13824 = 55296
$$

$$
k = (55296)^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{55296} \approx 38.07
$$

Since $k$ must be even, we choose the next even integer:

$$
\boxed{k = 40}
$$

(For $k=40$, $N = 40^3/4 = 16{,}000$ servers — slight over-provisioning is acceptable.)

**(ii) Number of switches and links (2 marks):**

$$
S = \frac{3k^2}{2} = \frac{3 \times 1600}{2} = 2400 \text{ switches}
$$

$$
L = \frac{3k^3}{4} = \frac{3 \times 64000}{4} = 48{,}000 \text{ links}
$$

**(iii) Bisection Bandwidth (2 marks):**

$$
BB = \frac{k^3}{8} \cdot B_{\text{port}} = \frac{64000}{8} \times 40 = 8000 \times 40 = 320{,}000 \text{ Gbps} = 320 \text{ Tbps}
$$

**(iv) Number of equal-cost paths between pods (1 mark):**

$$
P = \left(\frac{k}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{40}{2}\right)^2 = 20^2 = 400 \text{ paths}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — BCube and Server-Centric Design**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style, Adapted]**

**(a)** Describe the BCube server-centric architecture with a neat diagram. How does it differ from a switch-centric design in terms of **fault tolerance, cost, and routing complexity**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A data center operator plans to build a **BCube$_2$** using $n = 8$ port switches.
   (i) Compute the total number of servers supported.
   (ii) Compute the total number of switches (across all levels).
   (iii) Each server has 3 NIC ports (for $L=2$). If the diameter is $2(L+1)$, find the maximum hop count.
   (iv) If one level-2 switch fails, what is the impact on the network? Is the data center still operational? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) BCube Description (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** BCube is a *recursive*, *server-centric* architecture where each server has multiple NIC ports and participates in packet forwarding. The structure $BCube_L$ of dimension $L$ is defined recursively over $n$-port commodity switches.

**Recursive Construction (3 marks):**
- $BCube_0$: $n$ servers connected to a single $n$-port switch.
- $BCube_1$: $n$ switches of $BCube_0$ arranged in $n$ level-1 switches.
- $BCube_L$: $n$ copies of $BCube_{L-1}$ are interconnected by $n^L$ level-$L$ switches.

**Comparison (2 marks):**
| Aspect | BCube (Server-Centric) | Fat-Tree (Switch-Centric) |
|---|---|---|
| Fault tolerance | Multiple disjoint paths via different servers | Multiple paths via core switches |
| Cost | Higher (multi-NIC servers) | Lower (single-NIC servers) |
| Routing complexity | Server-based (Bcube Source Routing) | Switch-based (ECMP, OSPF) |

**(Refer to the BCube diagram in SECTION 4.3 for the visual explanation.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total servers (2 marks):**

$$
N_2 = n^{L+1} = 8^{2+1} = 8^3 = 512 \text{ servers}
$$

**(ii) Total switches (2 marks):** A $BCube_L$ uses $n^L$ switches at each level $l = 0, 1, \dots, L$. For $L=2, n=8$:

- Level 0: $8^1 = 8$ switches
- Level 1: $8^2 = 64$ switches
- Level 2: $8^3 = 512$ switches

Wait — actually in standard BCube notation, level $l$ uses $n^l$ switches for $l=1$ to $L$, plus $n$ level-0 switches. For $L=2$:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = n + n^2 = 8 + 64 = 72 \text{ switches}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{total}} = 72 \text{ switches}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum hop count (1 mark):**

$$
D = 2(L+1) = 2(2+1) = 6 \text{ hops}
$$

**(iv) Impact of one level-2 switch failure (2 marks):**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "the data center is fully operational" without explaining the failure domain!

A single $BCube_2$ level-2 switch failure disconnects **1/n = 1/8 = 12.5%** of the servers from the rest of the fabric via that level. However, BCube's *multi-path* design means that **each pair of servers still has multiple disjoint paths** through lower-level switches. The data center remains **operational with degraded performance** — specifically, traffic is rerouted via level-0 and level-1 switches, increasing the average hop count and reducing aggregate bandwidth by 12.5% for inter-pod traffic. BCube does *not* suffer from a single point of failure (SPOF), unlike the root switch in a 3-tier tree.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons students LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k$ with $k/2$:** Fat-Tree uses $k/2$ ports *in each direction*. Students often write the number of servers as $k^3$ instead of $k^3/4$. **Always re-derive on paper** before writing.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the bisection factor of 2:** Some textbooks write $BB = k^3/4 \cdot B_{\text{port}}$ (using "aggregate server bandwidth" as the definition), while KTU expects the "cut" definition $BB = k^3/8 \cdot B_{\text{port}}$. **State your definition explicitly** in the answer.
>
> 3. **Missing the diagram:** A 7-mark architectural question WITHOUT a diagram is automatically penalized 1–2 marks. Always draw **even a rough sketch**.
>
> 4. **Conflating VL2 with Fat-Tree:** VL2 (Microsoft) uses a different addressing scheme and Valiant Load Balancing, but is *not* the same as Fat-Tree. Don't mix them up.
>
> 5. **Oversubscription = Bottleneck = Latency:** Students often write "oversubscription is bad" but don't link it to **tail latency** in cloud SLAs.
>
> 6. **BCube recursive formula:** Memorize $N_L = n^{L+1}$ — examiners love to ask $n=16, L=3$ type problems.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before the exam):**
>
> - **Fat-Tree = 3-stage Clos** built from identical $k$-port commodity switches.
> - $N_{\text{servers}} = k^3/4$, $S_{\text{switches}} = 3k^2/2$, $L_{\text{links}} = 3k^3/4$.
> - **Diameter** of Fat-Tree = **5 hops** (server ↔ server).
> - **Bisection Bandwidth** of Fat-Tree = $k^3/8 \cdot B_{\text{port}}$ (cut definition) = $N/2 \cdot B_{\text{port}}$.
> - **Number of equal-cost paths** between two pods in Fat-Tree = $(k/2)^2$.
> - **BCube** is **server-centric**, multi-NIC, recursive: $N_L = n^{L+1}$, diameter $= 2(L+1)$.
> - **VL2** is a switch-centric Clos variant using Valiant Load Balancing for uniform random traffic.
> - **DCell** is a server-centric recursive structure using *mini-switches* and dual-NIC servers.
> - **Oversubscription** = Downlink BW / Uplink BW. Ideal = $1{:}1$, cloud typical = $1{:}4$ to $1{:}8$.
> - **PUE** = Total Facility Power / IT Power. Lower is better. World-class $\approx 1.1$.
> - **3-tier Tree** = Legacy design. Has oversubscription and root SPOF. Not used in hyperscale clouds anymore.
> - **Fat-Tree pros:** No oversubscription, path diversity, commodity hardware.
> - **BCube pros:** Higher fault tolerance via server redundancy, supports any-to-any traffic.
> - **Fat-Tree cons:** Cabling complexity, fixed by $k$ at design time.
> - **BCube cons:** Higher server cost (multi-NIC), complex server-based routing.
> - **MDC (Modular Data Center):** Pre-fabricated containers with built-in Fat-Tree, deployed in 90 days.
> - **Torus/Dragonfly** = Direct topology for HPC, low-diameter, predictable latency.
> - **Design pillars (4):** Bandwidth uniformity, fault tolerance, cost efficiency, energy proportionality.
> - **KTU buzzwords:** "warehouse-scale computer," "commodity switch," "bisection bandwidth," "ECMP," "multi-rooted tree."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architectural Design of Computing Clouds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architectural Design of Computing Clouds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Design of a Computing Cloud** is the systematic structuring of cloud components—hardware, virtualization, middleware, and application layers—into a coherent blueprint that delivers **on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources** (NIST SP 800-145 definition baseline) with the essential characteristics of *on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, cloud architecture is treated as a **multi-layered, loosely-coupled, service-oriented design** that decouples the *workload logic* from the *infrastructure dependencies*, enabling horizontal scaling, fault isolation, and provider-independence.

## 1.2 Key Architectural Layers (KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus Focus)

The architecture is conceptually divided into **four conceptual strata**:

| Layer | Responsibility | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Application / User Layer** | End-user interfaces, business logic | Web portals, APIs, SaaS front-ends |
| **Middleware / PaaS Layer** | Platform services, runtime, orchestration | App servers, message queues, container engines |
| **Infrastructure / IaaS Layer** | Virtual compute, storage, networks | Hypervisors, VMs, SDN controllers, Object stores |
| **Physical / Hardware Layer** | Raw physical resources | Servers, switches, storage arrays, data centers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** When answering theory questions, students must explicitly mention that cloud architecture is *layered* and that **virtualization** is the *abstraction technology* sitting between the IaaS and Hardware layers.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hotel" Model

Think of a cloud architecture as a **modern automated hotel chain**:

- The **physical building (servers, racks, cooling)** = the *Hardware Layer*. You never see it; you just know rooms exist.
- The **room allocation system (front-desk software, key-cards)** = the *Virtualization/Hypervisor Layer*. It dynamically assigns any guest to any room.
- The **amenities (pool, gym, conference hall, restaurant)** = the *Middleware/PaaS Layer*. These are pre-built services any guest can use.
- The **customer's outfit, business, and luggage (apps, data, user logic)** = the *Application Layer*.

When more guests arrive (load increases), the hotel **provisions more rooms automatically (elasticity)** without asking the guest to do paperwork. When a guest leaves, resources are **reclaimed (de-provisioning)**. The hotel charges based on nights stayed and amenities used (**measured service / pay-per-use**).

## 1.4 The "Essential Characteristics" — KTU Must-Know

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Architecture (NIST):**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — automatic provisioning without human intervention.
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — accessible over standard networks via thin/thick clients.
> 3. **Resource Pooling** — multi-tenant model with dynamic reassignment of physical/virtual resources.
> 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — capabilities scaled out/in commensurate with demand.
> 5. **Measured Service** — metering at appropriate abstraction level (storage, CPU, bandwidth).

## 1.5 Service and Deployment Models — Quick Reference

**Service Models (the "SPI" triad + extensions):**
- **SaaS (Software as a Service)** — Gmail, Microsoft 365
- **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** — Google App Engine, Heroku
- **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** — AWS EC2, Azure VMs
- Plus extensions: **FaaS (Function as a Service)**, **CaaS (Container as a Service)**

**Deployment Models:**
- **Public Cloud** — open for general public use
- **Private Cloud** — exclusive to a single organization
- **Hybrid Cloud** — composition of two or more clouds bound by standardized/ proprietary technology
- **Community Cloud** — shared by organizations with common concerns

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Principles of Cloud Architecture (The "Cloud-Native" Pillars)

A robust cloud architecture must satisfy the following engineering principles. These appear frequently in **KTU 14-mark essay questions**.

### 2.1.1 Loose Coupling
- **Definition:** Components interact through well-defined interfaces (APIs, message queues) with **minimal inter-dependency**.
- **Why:** Allows independent scaling, deployment, and failure isolation.
- **Implementation Patterns:** Asynchronous messaging (RabbitMQ, Kafka), RESTful APIs, Event-driven architectures.

### 2.1.2 Scalability (Horizontal vs Vertical)
- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up):** Add more resources (CPU, RAM) to a *single* node.
  - *Pros:* Simpler, no code changes.
  - *Cons:* Hardware ceiling, single point of failure, costly.
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out):** Add *more* nodes behind a load balancer.
  - *Pros:* Theoretically infinite, fault tolerant.
  - *Cons:* Requires stateless application design, distributed coordination.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** "Cloud computing favors **horizontal scaling** over vertical scaling because it aligns with commodity hardware and elasticity."

### 2.1.3 Elasticity
- **Definition:** The system's ability to *automatically* scale resources in proportion to workload changes, ideally with no service degradation.
- **Two flavors:**
  - *Reactive Elasticity:* Triggered by monitoring thresholds (e.g., CPU > 70%).
  - *Predictive (Proactive) Elasticity:* Triggered by forecast models (time-series analysis, ML-based load prediction).

### 2.1.4 Availability & Fault Tolerance
- **Availability** = Uptime / Total Time (typically expressed in nines: 99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%).
- **Fault Tolerance** = system's ability to continue operating despite component failures.
- Achieved through **redundancy** (N+1, 2N, 2N+1) and **replication**.

### 2.1.5 Security & Multi-Tenancy
- **Multi-tenancy:** A single software instance serves multiple client organizations (tenants) with logical isolation.
- **Isolation levels:**
  1. **Shared everything** (cheapest, least isolated)
  2. **Shared application, separate database**
  3. **Separate application, shared database**
  4. **Shared nothing** (most isolated, expensive)

## 2.2 The Cloud Reference Architecture (NIST SP 500-292)

The NIST reference architecture identifies **five major actors**:

1. **Cloud Consumer** — uses the services.
2. **Cloud Provider** — makes the services available.
3. **Cloud Auditor** — independent assessment of security, performance, SLAs.
4. **Cloud Broker** — manages use, performance, delivery of cloud services; negotiates relationships between providers and consumers.
5. **Cloud Carrier** — provides connectivity and transport of cloud services (e.g., ISPs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-layer Activities in NIST Reference Architecture:** *Portability, Interoperability, Security, Privacy* — these activities are *transverse* and apply across every layer.

## 2.3 Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) vs. Microservices

| Feature | SOA | Microservices |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Coarse-grained services | Fine-grained services |
| Coupling | Often tightly coupled (ESB) | Loosely coupled |
| Communication | SOAP/ESB heavy | REST/gRPC, lightweight messaging |
| Data | Shared database common | Database per service |
| Deployment | Monolithic deployments | Independent deployments |
| Suitability for Cloud | Moderate | Highly suitable |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Cloud-native architectures are predominantly **microservices-based**, not classical SOA, because of the need for *independent deployability* and *horizontal scaling*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | Ratio (often %) | SLA computation |
| 2 | **Annual Downtime (T_d)** | $T_d = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes/year | SLA penalties |
| 3 | **Number of Nines** | $n = -\log_{10}(1 - A)$ | dimensionless | "Five-nines" = 99.999% |
| 4 | **Reliability (R(t))** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | probability | Mean Time Between Failures |
| 5 | **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | hours | Hardware reliability |
| 6 | **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{Total\ Down\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | hours | Service restoration |
| 7 | **Capacity Required (C)** | $C = \dfrac{Peak\ Demand \times Safety\ Factor}{Utilization}$ | units | Resource planning |
| 8 | **Cost per User (CPU)** | $CPU = \dfrac{Total\ Monthly\ Cost}{Monthly\ Active\ Users}$ | currency/user | Pricing models |
| 9 | **Throughput (T)** | $T = \dfrac{Requests\ Completed}{Time\ Interval}$ | req/s | Load testing |
| 10 | **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = Service\ Time + Wait\ Time + Network\ Latency$ | seconds (ms) | Performance SLA |
| 11 | **Scaling Factor (SF)** | $SF = \dfrac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}$ | dimensionless | Elasticity ratio |
| 12 | **Storage Tiering Cost** | $C_{total} = C_{hot} \cdot S_{hot} + C_{cold} \cdot S_{cold}$ | currency | Storage optimization |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Integrity Note:** Per the KTU-Premier-Engine protocol, the vertical pipe symbol is intentionally replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in formulas. In the *availability* equation above, the division is shown with `\dfrac` and fraction rendering to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.5 The CAP Theorem — Cloud's Inherent Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any distributed cloud system, you can only simultaneously guarantee **two of the three**:
> - **C** — Consistency (every read sees the latest write)
> - **A** — Availability (every request gets a response)
> - **P** — Partition tolerance (system operates despite network partitions)

Cloud systems typically choose **AP** (e.g., DynamoDB, Cassandra) or **CP** (e.g., HBase, MongoDB with majority writes), with **P** being non-negotiable in real distributed systems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce platforms** (Amazon, Flipkart) — use horizontal scaling during festive sales (elasticity).
- **Streaming services** (Netflix) — use microservices to isolate failures (e.g., recommendation engine down ≠ playback down).
- **Banking systems** — use private/hybrid cloud for data sovereignty with public cloud bursting for analytics.
- **IoT and Edge** — use distributed cloud architecture (fog/edge computing) for low-latency processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Converting "Number of Nines" to Annual Downtime

This is a **frequently asked 3-mark derivation** in KTU ESE.

**Given:** Availability $A = 99.99\%$ ("four nines")

**Step 1:** Identify the formula for annual downtime.

$$T_d = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

Where the constant 525600 is the total minutes in a non-leap year.

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical value of unavailability.

$$1 - A = 1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

**Step 3:** Compute the product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_d &= 0.0001 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
&= 0.0001 \times 525600 \\
&= 52.56 \text{ minutes per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. A service with "four-nines" availability can be down for at most **52.56 minutes per year**.

**General Table for KTU Recall:**

| Availability | Annual Downtime (approx.) |
|---|---|
| 99% (two nines) | 3.65 days |
| 99.9% (three nines) | 8.76 hours |
| 99.99% (four nines) | 52.56 minutes |
| 99.999% (five nines) | 5.26 minutes |
| 99.9999% (six nines) | 31.5 seconds |

## 3.2 Derivation: Reliability Function from Failure Rate

**Given:** A server has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 0.0001$ failures per hour.

**Step 1:** Express the exponential reliability function.

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 2:** Compute reliability for $t = 1000$ hours.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(1000) &= e^{-0.0001 \times 1000} \\
&= e^{-0.1} \\
&\approx 0.9048
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).

$$MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0001} = 10{,}000 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. The probability that the server will function *without failure* for 1000 hours is approximately **90.48%**, with an expected time between failures of **10,000 hours**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cost-Per-User Pricing Model

A SaaS provider wants to compute the cost per active user for a tiered subscription model.

**Given:**
- Total infrastructure cost: ₹5,00,000/month
- Support cost: ₹1,00,000/month
- Total monthly active users (MAU): 10,000
- Free-tier users are not counted; only paying users.

**Step 1:** Sum total monthly cost.

$$C_{total} = C_{infra} + C_{support} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Divide by active paying users.

$$CPU = \frac{C_{total}}{MAU} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 60 \text{ per user}$$

**Step 3:** Add desired profit margin (say 25%).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{final} &= CPU \times (1 + \text{Margin}) \\
&= 60 \times 1.25 = 75 \text{ per user}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation. The minimum subscription price to maintain a 25% profit margin is **₹75/user/month**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Capacity Planning for a Web Application

**Given:**
- Peak demand = 5000 requests/minute
- Average request service time = 200 ms = 0.2 s
- Target CPU utilization = 70% (safety factor accounted for)

**Step 1:** Convert to requests per second.

$$R_{peak} = \frac{5000}{60} \approx 83.33 \text{ req/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute per-server capacity (Little's Law adaptation).

$$C_{server} = \frac{1}{t_{service}} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ req/s per server}$$

**Step 3:** Apply utilization factor.

$$C_{effective} = C_{server} \times U = 5 \times 0.7 = 3.5 \text{ req/s per server}$$

**Step 4:** Compute number of servers needed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{R_{peak}}{C_{effective}} \\
&= \frac{83.33}{3.5} \\
&\approx 23.81 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 24 \text{ servers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Add N+1 redundancy for fault tolerance.

$$N_{total} = N + 1 = 25 \text{ servers}$$

**Step 6:** Interpretation. The architecture requires **25 servers** (24 active + 1 standby) to serve peak load with 70% utilization and fault tolerance.

## 3.5 Derivation: Elasticity Scaling Factor

**Given:** An application scales from 4 instances to 16 instances to handle a traffic spike.

**Step 1:** Apply the scaling factor formula.

$$SF = \frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}} = \frac{16}{4} = 4$$

**Step 2:** Compute percentage increase.

$$\Delta\% = (SF - 1) \times 100\% = 300\%$$

**Step 3:** Interpretation. The architecture has **scaled out 4× (300% increase)** in capacity, demonstrating *horizontal elasticity*.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Architecture Pattern Selection

| Workload Type | Best Architectural Pattern | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Static brochure website | **Single-tier + CDN** | Low variability, edge caching sufficient |
| E-commerce site | **Multi-tier (3-tier) + LB** | Clear separation: presentation, logic, data |
| Streaming service | **Microservices + Message Queue** | Independent scaling of ingest, transcode, recommend |
| Real-time analytics | **Lambda / Kappa architecture** | Both batch + stream processing required |
| IoT telemetry | **Fog / Edge computing** | Latency-sensitive, distributed nodes |
| Enterprise ERP | **Private/Hybrid cloud + SaaS** | Sensitive data, compliance needs |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the architectural design of a computing cloud and list its essential characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — points-wise):**

The **architectural design of a computing cloud** is the structured blueprint of components and layers that deliver computing services over the internet, comprising hardware, virtualization, middleware, and application tiers integrated to provide on-demand, scalable, and measured resources.

**Five Essential Characteristics (1 mark for the list, 0.5 marks for brief explanation of any 4):**
1. **On-demand self-service** — automatic provisioning.
2. **Broad network access** — accessible via standard protocols.
3. **Resource pooling** — multi-tenant dynamic allocation.
4. **Rapid elasticity** — scale in/out with demand.
5. **Measured service** — usage-based metering.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — comparison table format expected):**

| Parameter | Vertical Scaling | Horizontal Scaling |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Add resources to one node | Add more nodes |
| **Example** | Increase RAM from 16GB to 64GB on a single VM | Add 5 more EC2 instances behind a load balancer |
| **Hardware limit** | Yes (ceiling) | No (theoretically infinite) |
| **Downtime** | Usually required | None (rolling addition) |
| **Cost** | Linear, expensive | Commodity, cheaper |
| **Fault tolerance** | Single point of failure | High |
| **Cloud suitability** | Limited | Excellent |

(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for differentiating points)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically contains *one full 14-mark question* with internal choice. Below, Question A and Question B are **independent alternatives** the examiner may offer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]

**Q. (a)** Explain the layered architecture of cloud computing in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the virtualization layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Layered Architecture Overview (3 marks):**

The cloud architecture is divided into **four conceptual layers**:

1. **Application Layer (SaaS):** The topmost layer where end-users interact with software applications. Examples: Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365. It hides infrastructure complexity from the user.

2. **Platform Layer (PaaS):** Provides a runtime environment, middleware, databases, and development tools for developers to build, test, and deploy applications. Examples: Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service.

3. **Infrastructure Layer (IaaS):** Provides virtualized computing resources — virtual machines, virtual storage, virtual networks — on demand. Examples: AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine.

4. **Hardware Layer:** The physical foundation — servers, storage arrays, networking equipment, data centers, power, and cooling systems.

**(ii) Role of Virtualization Layer (3 marks):**

The **virtualization layer** sits between the IaaS and Hardware layers. It performs the following critical roles:

- **Abstraction:** Decouples the operating system and applications from the underlying hardware.
- **Resource Pooling:** Combines multiple physical resources into a single logical pool.
- **Partitioning:** Divides a single physical server into multiple isolated VMs.
- **Encapsulation:** Packages the entire VM state into portable files (OVA/VMDK) for migration.
- **Hypervisor Types:** Type 1 (bare-metal, e.g., VMware ESXi, Xen) and Type 2 (hosted, e.g., VirtualBox).

**(iii) Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Diagram A in Section 4.2 of these notes.

---

**(b)** Discuss the design principles of cloud architecture. Explain *loose coupling* and *elasticity* in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Design Principles (3 marks):**

- **Loose Coupling** — components interact via standard interfaces with minimal dependency.
- **Elasticity** — automatic, dynamic scaling aligned with demand.
- **Scalability** — ability to handle increased load by adding resources.
- **Availability & Fault Tolerance** — system continues operating despite failures.
- **Security** — multi-layered defense with isolation, encryption, identity management.
- **Measurability** — metering and monitoring of resource consumption.

**Loose Coupling in Detail (2 marks):**
- Achieved through *asynchronous messaging* (e.g., Kafka, RabbitMQ) and *RESTful APIs*.
- Allows each component to be *scaled, updated, and failed independently*.
- Real-world example: An e-commerce site where the *payment service* and *recommendation service* are decoupled — failure of one does not break the other.

**Elasticity in Detail (2 marks):**
- **Reactive elasticity:** Triggered by real-time metrics (CPU > 70% → add instance).
- **Predictive elasticity:** Triggered by forecast models using historical patterns.
- **Auto-Scaling Groups** in AWS, **Virtual Machine Scale Sets** in Azure implement this.
- Example: A news website scales out 10× during breaking news and scales back to baseline within minutes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NIST Cloud Reference Architecture**. Identify and briefly describe its five major actors. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**NIST Reference Architecture (1 mark for introduction, 3 marks for diagram, 3 marks for actors)**

The **NIST SP 500-292** reference architecture provides a generic, high-level framework for understanding cloud computing. It identifies **five major actors** and **four cross-layer activities** (*portability, interoperability, security, privacy*).

**Five Major Actors:**

1. **Cloud Consumer** — The individual or organization that uses the cloud services. *Role:* Provisions and uses services from a provider.

2. **Cloud Provider** — The entity responsible for making cloud services available. *Role:* Manages the infrastructure, platform, and applications; provides SLAs.

3. **Cloud Auditor** — A third party that conducts independent assessment of cloud services. *Role:* Evaluates security controls, privacy impacts, and performance against SLAs.

4. **Cloud Broker** — An intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services. *Role:* Aggregates services from multiple providers, optimizes for the consumer.

5. **Cloud Carrier** — The intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services. *Role:* Ensures network access between consumer and provider (e.g., ISPs, telecom operators).

**Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Diagram C in Section 4.4 of these notes.

---

**(b)** Explain the **CAP theorem** with a real-world example. Discuss how cloud databases typically make the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**CAP Theorem (3 marks):**
- Stated by **Eric Brewer (2000)**, proven by Gilbert and Lynch (2002).
- In a **distributed data store**, you can simultaneously guarantee only **two of the three** properties:
  - **Consistency (C):** Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
  - **Availability (A):** Every request receives a non-error response (no guarantee of latest data).
  - **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite an arbitrary number of messages being dropped (or delayed) between nodes.
- Since network partitions are **inevitable in real distributed systems**, the choice is effectively between **CP** and **AP**.

**Trade-offs in Cloud Databases (2 marks):**

| System Type | Choice | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **CP** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance) | MongoDB (default config), HBase, Redis | Banking, inventory systems |
| **AP** (Availability + Partition Tolerance) | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB | Social media, shopping carts |
| **CA** (Consistency + Availability) | Not realistic in distributed clouds | Single-node RDBMS |

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**
- An **e-commerce shopping cart** uses an **AP** system (e.g., DynamoDB): users can always add items to the cart even if the network is slow; the data will eventually become consistent across regions.
- A **banking transaction** uses a **CP** system (e.g., MongoDB with majority writes): the system may reject the request if it cannot guarantee consistency, but it will never show wrong balances.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Architecture" with "Service Model":** Students often write only about IaaS/PaaS/SaaS without explaining the *layered structure* and *components* — this loses **at least 4 marks** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Omitting diagrams:** A neat, labeled cloud architecture diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Hand-draw and label even if you don't have tools.
> 3. **Mixing up CAP choices:** Saying "MongoDB chooses AP" is wrong — default MongoDB is CP. Always verify examples.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention "loose coupling" and "elasticity"** explicitly when asked for design principles — these are the two **highest-weight** principles.
> 5. **Calculation errors in nines-to-downtime conversion:** Always multiply by **525600** (minutes in a year), not 365 or 8760.
> 6. **Not defining the actor's role in NIST:** Writing only the actor name without the *role* costs 0.5 to 1 mark per actor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Architectural Design of Computing Clouds: High-Yield Revision List**

- ✅ **Architecture = Layered** (Application / PaaS / IaaS / Hardware) — *must-know structure*.
- ✅ **Five Essential Characteristics** of cloud (NIST): On-demand, Broad access, Pooling, Elasticity, Measured.
- ✅ **Three Service Models (SPI):** SaaS, PaaS, IaaS — plus FaaS as modern extension.
- ✅ **Four Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ✅ **Five NIST Actors:** Consumer, Provider, Auditor, Broker, Carrier.
- ✅ **Loose Coupling** → independent deployability & fault isolation (microservices).
- ✅ **Horizontal scaling preferred** over vertical in cloud (commodity hardware, no ceiling).
- ✅ **Elasticity** has two flavors: **Reactive** (threshold-based) and **Predictive** (forecast-based).
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** is achieved via shared resource pools with logical isolation.
- ✅ **CAP Theorem:** Pick any **two** of Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance — P is non-negotiable in distributed clouds.
- ✅ **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ — Annual downtime = $(1 - A) \times 525600$ minutes.
- ✅ **MTBF** $= \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ ; **MTTR** measures repair/restoration time.
- ✅ **Cloud Bursting** = private cloud overflows to public cloud on demand.
- ✅ **NIST Cross-Layer Activities:** Portability, Interoperability, Security, Privacy.
- ✅ **SOA vs Microservices:** Microservices are finer-grained, loosely coupled, ideal for cloud-native.
- ✅ **Reference Models to cite:** NIST SP 800-145 (definition), NIST SP 500-292 (reference architecture).
- ✅ **Reliability equation:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ — exponential decay of system health over time.
- ✅ **Cloud architecture must be** *virtualization-driven* — hypervisor is the abstraction engine.

> **End of Module 3 — Architectural Design of Computing Clouds | PEITT523 — CLOUD COMPUTING | KTU 2024 SCHEME**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Service Models and Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Service Models** are the layered, on-demand service abstractions through which a Cloud Service Provider (CSP) provisions pooled, elastic, and metered computing resources to consumers over a network (typically the Internet). As per the **NIST SP 800-145** reference model, these abstractions are categorized along a vertical stack of responsibility, where the consumer progressively offloads more operational concerns to the provider as they move up the stack.

The three canonical service models defined by NIST are:

1. **Software as a Service (SaaS)** — The consumer uses the provider's applications running on a cloud infrastructure.
2. **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** — The consumer deploys their own (or acquired) applications onto the cloud infrastructure, using supported programming languages, libraries, services, and tools.
3. **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** — The consumer provisions processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources, and is able to deploy and run arbitrary software (OS + applications).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 3 focus is the *taxonomy* of these models, the *responsibility boundary* (Shared Responsibility Model), and the *commercial platforms* (AWS, Azure, GCP, OpenStack) that instantiate them.

## 1.2 The "Pizza as a Service" Conceptual Analogy

To instantly grasp the layered nature of cloud service models, consider ordering a pizza:

| Analogy Layer | Cloud Equivalent | What YOU do | What PROVIDER does |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Make pizza from scratch at home** | On-Premise Datacenter | Buy flour, oven, electricity, cook, serve | Nothing |
| **Buy a frozen pizza & bake it** | **IaaS** (e.g., AWS EC2) | Bring dough (OS), bake (configure VMs) | Provides oven, gas, electricity (raw VMs, storage, network) |
| **Order pizza delivery, hot & ready** | **PaaS** (e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk) | Just specify toppings (write app code) | Bakes and delivers the platform (runtime, middleware, OS) |
| **Dine out at a pizzeria** | **SaaS** (e.g., Gmail, Office 365) | Just eat (use the software) | Everything: kitchen, chef, serving, billing |

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Bold Constants and Metrics)

- **Hypervisor:** The software layer that creates, runs, and manages Virtual Machines (VMs). Standard metrics: **vCPU**, **GiB RAM**, **IOPS** (Input/Output Operations Per Second).
- **Multitenancy:** A single software instance serves multiple tenants (customers), with logical isolation.
- **Elasticity:** The ability to scale resources horizontally or vertically in near real-time, often measured in **seconds to minutes**.
- **Metering:** Pay-per-use billing on resources like **compute-hour**, **GB-month**, or **per-API-call**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Shared Responsibility Model:** In every cloud service model, security is *split* between the CSP and the consumer. As you move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the **consumer's responsibility decreases** while the **provider's responsibility increases**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture — The Cloud Service Stack

The service models can be visualized as a **stack of abstraction layers**. Each layer encapsulates the complexity of the layer below it.

### 2.1.1 Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
- **What is provided:** Raw virtualized resources — VMs, virtual networks, block/object storage, load balancers, firewalls.
- **What the user manages:** Guest OS, middleware, runtime, application code, data.
- **Why use it:** Maximum control and flexibility; ideal for lift-and-shift migrations, disaster recovery, and high-performance computing.
- **Engineering Utility:** Powers the back-end of Netflix, Airbnb, and Dropbox (storage tier).

### 2.1.2 Platform as a Service (PaaS)
- **What is provided:** A managed runtime environment (OS + middleware + dev tools + DBs + queues) on which the user deploys code.
- **What the user manages:** Application code and configuration only.
- **Why use it:** Removes infrastructure management overhead; accelerates DevOps, CI/CD, and microservices deployment.
- **Engineering Utility:** Used for app development, API hosting, ML model deployment, IoT back-ends.

### 2.1.3 Software as a Service (SaaS)
- **What is provided:** Fully finished, ready-to-use applications hosted by the provider.
- **What the user manages:** Only user-specific data and access settings.
- **Why use it:** Zero install; accessible via thin client (browser); automatic updates.
- **Engineering Utility:** CRM (Salesforce), collaboration (Slack, Teams), email (Gmail), ERP.

## 2.2 Extended Service Models (Modern KTU-relevant)

| Model | Full Form | Description | Example Platform |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Serverless, event-driven, pay-per-execution. Code runs in stateless containers. | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, GCP Cloud Functions |
| **CaaS** | Container as a Service | Managed container orchestration (Kubernetes-based). | AWS EKS, Azure AKS, GCP GKE |
| **DBaaS** | Database as a Service | Managed relational/NoSQL databases with auto-backup, patching. | AWS RDS, Azure Cosmos DB, GCP Cloud SQL |
| **DaaS** | Desktop as a Service / Data as a Service | Virtual desktop streaming OR curated dataset delivery. | Azure Virtual Desktop, AWS WorkSpaces |
| **MBaaS** | Mobile Backend as a Service | Pre-built backend features (auth, push, storage) for mobile apps. | Firebase, AWS Amplify |
| **STaaS** | Storage as a Service | Object/blob/file storage rented on demand. | AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage |

## 2.3 Commercial Cloud Platforms

### 2.3.1 Amazon Web Services (AWS)
- **Market position:** Largest market share (~32% globally).
- **Flagship services:** EC2 (compute), S3 (storage), RDS (DB), Lambda (FaaS), VPC (network).

### 2.3.2 Microsoft Azure
- **Strength:** Deep integration with Windows Server, Active Directory, .NET ecosystem.
- **Flagship services:** Azure VMs, Azure SQL, Azure Functions, Azure DevOps.

### 2.3.3 Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
- **Strength:** Data analytics, AI/ML, Kubernetes (originator of K8s).
- **Flagship services:** Compute Engine, BigQuery, Cloud Run, GKE.

### 2.3.4 OpenStack (Open Source IaaS)
- **Nature:** Open-source IaaS framework, written in Python, used to build private/public clouds.
- **Core components:** Nova (compute), Neutron (networking), Swift (object storage), Keystone (identity), Horizon (dashboard), Glance (image service).

## 2.4 Deployment Models (Necessary context for Service Models)

| Deployment Model | Owner | Users | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Third-party CSP | General public | AWS, Azure, GCP |
| **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Internal users | On-prem OpenStack, VMware |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Mixed (public + private) | Organization + public | AWS Outposts, Azure Arc |
| **Community Cloud** | Multiple orgs with shared concerns | Specific community | GovCloud, medical consortiums |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Although cloud computing is non-formulaic in the calculus sense, the following **engineering metrics and cost formulas** are *highly* tested in KTU numerical questions.

| Symbol / Quantity | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{total}$ | Total cost of cloud resource | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)$ |
| $P_i$ | Unit price of resource $i$ (e.g., per hour, per GB) | USD |
| $U_i$ | Usage quantity of resource $i$ | Hours, GB, requests |
| $n$ | Number of distinct metered resources | Integer $\geq 1$ |
| $T_{provision}$ | Time to provision resources | Typically **seconds to minutes** in IaaS |
| $SLA_{uptime}$ | Service Level Agreement uptime % | e.g., **99.99%** = "four nines" |
| $D_{downtime}$ | Max allowed downtime per year | $D_{downtime} = (1 - SLA) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ (minutes) |
| $R_{scale}$ | Scaling ratio | $R_{scale} = \frac{L_{peak}}{L_{avg}}$ |
| $Cost_{on\text{-}demand}$ | Pay-as-you-go cost | $Cost = C_{hourly} \times H_{used}$ |
| $Cost_{reserved}$ | Reserved/committed cost | $Cost = C_{reserved} + C_{overage}$ |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by FinOps teams to forecast cloud spend, optimize reserved instance purchases, and negotiate enterprise discount programs (EDPs) with CSPs.

## 2.6 Shared Responsibility Model — Quick Reference

| Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Customer | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| **Application** | Customer | Customer | Customer | Provider |
| **Runtime / Middleware** | Customer | Customer | Provider | Provider |
| **OS** | Customer | Customer | Provider | Provider |
| **Virtualization** | Customer | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Server / Storage / Network** | Customer | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| **Physical Datacenter** | Customer | Provider | Provider | Provider |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Cloud Cost Calculation (KTU Numerical Standard)

> **Problem:** A startup uses an AWS EC2 `t3.medium` instance at **\$0.0416/hour** running **24 × 7** for **30 days**. They also store **500 GB** in S3 Standard at **\$0.023/GB-month**, and make **2 million** Lambda invocations at **\$0.20 per million**. Compute the total monthly bill.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the metering primitives and unit prices.**

The problem gives us three cost components:

- Compute: $P_{EC2} = 0.0416$ USD per hour
- Storage: $P_{S3} = 0.023$ USD per GB-month
- Serverless: $P_{\lambda} = 0.20$ USD per million invocations

The general total cost formula is:

$$
C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (P_i \times U_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total compute usage $U_{EC2}$.**

The instance runs 24 hours/day, 7 days/week, 30 days/month:

$$
U_{EC2} = 24 \times 7 \times 30
$$

Expanding:

$$
U_{EC2} = 24 \times 210 = 5040 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the EC2 component.**

$$
C_{EC2} = P_{EC2} \times U_{EC2} = 0.0416 \times 5040
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
C_{EC2} = 0.0416 \times 5000 + 0.0416 \times 40
$$

$$
C_{EC2} = 208.00 + 1.664 = 209.664 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the S3 storage component.**

$$
C_{S3} = P_{S3} \times U_{S3} = 0.023 \times 500
$$

$$
C_{S3} = 0.023 \times 500 = 11.50 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Lambda component.**

The usage is 2 million invocations. Price is \$0.20 per million:

$$
C_{\lambda} = P_{\lambda} \times U_{\lambda} = 0.20 \times 2 = 0.40 \text{ USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum the components.**

$$
C_{total} = C_{EC2} + C_{S3} + C_{\lambda}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 209.664 + 11.50 + 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{total} = 221.564 \text{ USD/month}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — SLA-to-Downtime Conversion

> **Problem:** An enterprise signs up for a cloud SLA guaranteeing **99.95%** monthly uptime. Calculate the maximum allowed monthly downtime in minutes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Use the downtime conversion formula:**

$$
D_{downtime} = (1 - SLA_{uptime}) \times T_{period}
$$

where $T_{period}$ is the total time in the same unit as the desired $D_{downtime}$.

**Step 2 — Convert SLA fraction to its complement.**

$$
1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005
$$

This is the *fraction* of time the system is allowed to be down.

**Step 3 — Compute total minutes in a month (using 30 days).**

$$
T_{period} = 30 \times 24 \times 60
$$

$$
T_{period} = 30 \times 1440 = 43200 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
D_{downtime} = 0.0005 \times 43200
$$

$$
D_{downtime} = 21.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to seconds for clarity.**

$$
D_{downtime} = 21.6 \times 60 = 1296 \text{ seconds} \approx 21 \text{ min } 36 \text{ sec}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{downtime} = 21.6 \text{ minutes/month}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Variant:** If the question asks for *yearly* downtime, replace 30 days with **365 days** → $T_{year} = 525600$ minutes. For **99.99% (four nines)**, yearly downtime is just **52.56 minutes/year**.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling (Elasticity Derivation)

> **Problem:** A web app has an **average load** of **2000 requests/sec** and a **peak load** of **8000 requests/sec**. Each VM instance handles **1000 req/s**. Compute:
> (a) the average number of VMs required,
> (b) the peak number required,
> (c) the scaling ratio $R_{scale}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Define the per-VM capacity constant.**

$$
C_{vm} = 1000 \text{ requests/sec per VM}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute average VM count.**

$$
N_{avg} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{avg}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2000}{1000} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute peak VM count.**

$$
N_{peak} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{peak}}{C_{vm}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8000}{1000} \right\rceil = 8 \text{ VMs}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute scaling ratio.**

$$
R_{scale} = \frac{L_{peak}}{L_{avg}} = \frac{8000}{2000} = 4
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.** The cloud auto-scaler must be capable of *4× horizontal scale-out* — this is precisely the kind of elasticity metric that distinguishes IaaS/PaaS from on-premise deployments.

$$
\boxed{N_{avg} = 2,\ N_{peak} = 8,\ R_{scale} = 4\times}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Service Models

| Criterion | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consumer manages** | OS, middleware, runtime, app, data | App, data | Data only |
| **Provider manages** | Virtualization → Datacenter | OS → Datacenter | Everything |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Medium | Lowest |
| **Time to deploy** | Minutes to hours | Seconds to minutes | Immediate (signup) |
| **User type** | Sysadmins, DevOps engineers | Application developers | End users |
| **Cost model** | Pay per VM/hour, GB | Pay per app instance or request | Per-user subscription |
| **Examples** | EC2, Azure VMs, GCE | Elastic Beanstalk, App Engine, Heroku | Office 365, Salesforce, Gmail |
| **Analogy** | Frozen pizza + oven | Delivered pizza | Restaurant meal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What you get** | Raw VMs, storage, network | Managed runtime + dev tools | Ready-to-use software |
| **You manage** | OS, middleware, app | App, data | Only data |
| **Example** | AWS EC2 | Google App Engine | Microsoft Office 365 |
| **Target user** | Sysadmins | Developers | End users |

(Any valid example + correct management scope = 3 marks)

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud computing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Shared Responsibility Model is a security framework that divides security duties between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **cloud consumer**.

- The **CSP** is always responsible for *security **of** the cloud* — physical infrastructure, network, hosts, and the virtualization layer.
- The **consumer** is always responsible for *security **in** the cloud* — data, access credentials, and (depending on the model) the OS, middleware, and application.

In IaaS the consumer's burden is highest; in SaaS it is lowest. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for division, 1 mark for IaaS→SaaS gradient.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, you must answer **one full 14-mark question** from a module. Below are two alternative options (a) and (b) — the student answers **one option** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three primary cloud service models — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — with diagrams of the **responsibility layers** managed by the provider and the consumer.

**Model Solution:**

**IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service):**
- Provides virtualized hardware resources: compute (VMs), storage (block/object), and network.
- Provider manages: physical datacenter, servers, storage devices, network equipment, and the hypervisor.
- Consumer manages: guest OS, middleware, runtime, application code, and data.
- Examples: **AWS EC2**, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine.
- Best for: lift-and-shift migrations, custom architectures, high-performance workloads. *(2 marks)*

**PaaS (Platform as a Service):**
- Provides a managed platform including OS, middleware, runtime, and dev tools.
- Provider manages everything from OS down to physical infrastructure.
- Consumer manages only the application code and configuration (and data).
- Examples: **Google App Engine**, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Azure App Service.
- Best for: rapid application development, microservices, DevOps pipelines. *(2 marks)*

**SaaS (Software as a Service):**
- Delivers fully functional, ready-to-use applications over the Internet.
- Provider manages everything — application, runtime, OS, virtualization, hardware.
- Consumer only provides data and configures user access.
- Examples: **Gmail**, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Slack, Dropbox.
- Best for: end-user productivity, CRM, collaboration. *(2 marks)*

**Responsibility Diagram (drawn in answer book):** A horizontal stack with 6 layers (Application, Data, Runtime, OS, Virtualization, Infrastructure), with shaded cells indicating provider-owned layers increasing from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS. *(1 mark)*

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare the AWS, Azure, and GCP platforms in terms of (i) market position, (ii) flagship compute service, (iii) flagship storage service, and (iv) unique strength.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Market position** | Largest (~32% share), launched 2006 | Second largest, strong enterprise | Third, strong in data & AI |
| (ii) **Flagship compute** | **EC2** (Elastic Compute Cloud) | **Azure Virtual Machines** | **Google Compute Engine (GCE)** |
| (iii) **Flagship storage** | **S3** (Simple Storage Service) | **Azure Blob Storage** | **Cloud Storage** |
| (iv) **Unique strength** | Largest service catalog (200+ services) | Tight AD/.NET/Windows integration | BigQuery, Kubernetes (GKE), AI/ML leadership |

*Any 4 valid comparison points = 7 marks. AWS services mentioned = +1 bonus in valuation.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **FaaS (Function as a Service)** model. List its characteristics, advantages, limitations, and two real-world provider examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** FaaS is a serverless, event-driven cloud model where the consumer uploads discrete functions (snippets of code) and the provider dynamically allocates resources to execute them in response to triggers. *(1 mark)*

**Characteristics:** *(3 marks)*
1. **Stateless:** Each function invocation is independent; no in-memory state persists.
2. **Event-driven:** Triggered by HTTP requests, queue messages, file uploads, schedules, or DB events.
3. **Auto-scaled to zero:** When no requests arrive, no compute resources are billed.
4. **Sub-second billing:** Metered in **GB-seconds** of compute and **number of invocations**.
5. **Polyglot:** Supports multiple runtimes (Python, Node.js, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby).

**Advantages:** *(2 marks)*
- Zero server management.
- Cost-efficient for spiky/unpredictable workloads.
- Built-in high availability and fault tolerance.
- Faster time-to-market.

**Limitations:** *(1 mark)*
- Cold start latency.
- Execution time limits (e.g., AWS Lambda: 15 minutes max).
- Vendor lock-in; debugging distributed functions is harder.

**Examples:** AWS Lambda, Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions, IBM Cloud Functions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain **OpenStack** as an open-source IaaS platform. List and briefly describe **any six** of its core components.

**Model Solution:**

**OpenStack** is a free, open-source cloud operating system that controls large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources, deployed as a **private** or **public** IaaS cloud. It is written largely in Python and orchestrated via REST APIs and a web dashboard (Horizon). *(1 mark)*

**Six core components:** *(6 × 1 mark)*

1. **Nova (Compute):** Provisions and manages VMs; supports KVM, VMware, Hyper-V, Xen.
2. **Neutron (Networking):** Provides "Networking-as-a-Service" — virtual networks, routers, subnets, load balancers, and firewalls.
3. **Swift (Object Storage):** Highly available, distributed object/blob storage (similar to AWS S3).
4. **Cinder (Block Storage):** Persistent block storage volumes attached to VMs.
5. **Keystone (Identity):** Centralized authentication, authorization, and service catalog (LDAP-compatible).
6. **Glance (Image):** Manages VM disk images (templates) — upload, register, discover, and clone.
7. *(Bonus)* **Horizon (Dashboard):** Web-based GUI for managing all OpenStack services.
8. *(Bonus)* **Heat (Orchestration):** Template-driven (TOSCA) resource provisioning, similar to AWS CloudFormation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse OpenStack with OpenNebula or Eucalyptus** — these are different open-source cloud stacks.
> - **Do NOT write "OpenStack is a hypervisor"** — it is a *cloud orchestrator*; it *uses* hypervisors (KVM, Xen).
> - In the 14-mark answer, students often lose 2–3 marks for **not drawing the responsibility block diagram** for IaaS/PaaS/SaaS. Always include a clear labeled figure.
> - For FaaS, forgetting to mention **"cold start"** and **"stateless"** loses at least 1 mark.
> - Use **full names** (e.g., *Elastic Compute Cloud*, not just *EC2*) in long answers — examiners reward precision.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ The three NIST service models are **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS**, forming a vertical stack of increasing provider-managed layers.
- ✅ **IaaS** = raw VMs (e.g., **EC2**); **PaaS** = managed platform (e.g., **Elastic Beanstalk**, **App Engine**); **SaaS** = ready software (e.g., **Gmail**).
- ✅ **FaaS** (e.g., **AWS Lambda**) is *serverless*, *event-driven*, *stateless*, and *metered in milliseconds/GB-seconds*.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility:** CSP owns "security *of* the cloud"; customer owns "security *in* the cloud". Liability shifts upward as you go from IaaS → SaaS.
- ✅ **AWS** leads in market share; **Azure** leads in enterprise/Microsoft integration; **GCP** leads in data/AI/Kubernetes.
- ✅ **OpenStack** is open-source IaaS; key modules: **Nova** (compute), **Neutron** (network), **Swift** (object storage), **Cinder** (block storage), **Keystone** (identity), **Glance** (images), **Horizon** (dashboard).
- ✅ Cloud deployment models: **Public, Private, Hybrid, Community**.
- ✅ SLA conversion: $D_{downtime} = (1 - SLA) \times T_{period}$ (e.g., 99.99% uptime ↔ 52.56 min/year downtime).
- ✅ Elasticity metric: $R_{scale} = L_{peak} / L_{avg}$; a key IaaS/PaaS advantage over on-premise.
- ✅ Cloud cost formula: $C_{total} = \sum (P_i \times U_i)$ — *unit price × usage*.
- ✅ The **"Pizza as a Service"** analogy is a high-scoring, easy-to-recall KTU exam mnemonic for distinguishing IaaS/PaaS/SaaS.
- ✅ Modern extensions: **CaaS** (containers), **DBaaS** (databases), **DaaS** (desktop/data), **MBaaS** (mobile backend), **STaaS** (storage).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resource Management and Design Challenges. Fundamental Cloud Security - Threat Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resource Management and Design Challenges. Fundamental Cloud Security - Threat Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Resource Management in Cloud Computing

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Resource Management** in cloud computing is the coordinated, policy-driven process of provisioning, allocating, scheduling, monitoring, and releasing computational, storage, network, and service resources across virtualized, multi-tenant data centers to satisfy Quality of Service (QoS), Service Level Agreement (SLA), and cost-efficiency objectives defined by the cloud provider and consumer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Resource Management is *not* just VM allocation. It is a **holistic discipline** spanning the complete lifecycle: **Provisioning → Allocation → Scheduling → Monitoring → Release**, with a feedback loop driven by SLA violations.

**Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The Hotel Chain):**
Imagine a luxury hotel chain (your Cloud) with thousands of rooms (Virtual Machines), restaurants (Storage), conference halls (Network bandwidth), and spa services (Platform services). A **Resource Manager** is the hotel's Central Reservation System.
- A guest (cloud user) books a room for 3 nights (VM instance for 3 hours).
- If a hurricane warning comes in, 200 extra guests arrive unexpectedly (load spike) — the manager must **dynamically reassign** rooms, convert the conference hall into temporary lodging, and bill guests accurately (**elasticity + metering**).
- A VIP guest (gold SLA tenant) must always get a sea-view room — the manager has **priority queues**.
- The hotel must never reveal that Guest A and Guest B share the same floor kitchen (**multi-tenancy isolation**).

The hotel chain also faces **Design Challenges**: How many rooms to keep vacant? How to handle a fire alarm (fault tolerance)? How to clean rooms between guests fast enough to maintain hygiene SLA (VM boot storms)?

---

## 1.2 Design Challenges in Cloud Resource Management

The eight canonical design challenges identified in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big 8 Cloud Design Challenges (KTU Module 3)**
> 1. **Scalability** — handling exponential user/data growth
> 2. **Fault Tolerance & Reliability** — surviving hardware/software failure
> 3. **Virtualization Overhead** — the hidden cost of the hypervisor
> 4. **Energy Efficiency** — green computing and Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)
> 5. **Multi-Tenancy & Isolation** — many users on shared infrastructure
> 6. **Heterogeneity** — mixing CPU, GPU, FPGA, ARM, x86 nodes
> 7. **SLA Management** — translating legal contracts to technical metrics
> 8. **Interoperability & Vendor Lock-in** — moving workloads across providers

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Cloud Security – Threat Agents

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Threat Agent** (also called *threat actor* or *adversary*) is any **entity, circumstance, or event** that has the *capability*, *intent*, and *opportunity* to exploit a vulnerability in a cloud system, causing harm to the **Confidentiality, Integrity, or Availability (CIA Triad)** of assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** A *threat* is the *potential* violation. A **threat agent** is the *who/what* that can execute that violation. A *vulnerability* is the *weakness* exploited. These three are always **linked but distinct** — examiners love testing this triad.

**Intuitive Real-World Analogy (The Apartment Building):**
- **The building** = Cloud data center
- **Apartments** = Tenant VMs
- **The security guard** = Firewall / IAM
- **Threat agents** are the people who *could* break in:
  - A **stranger** (Anonymous Attacker) trying every door
  - **A delivery boy with a master key** (Malicious Service Agent)
  - **The building's own security guard** (Malicious Insider)
  - **A tenant trying the neighbor's door** (Trusted Attacker)
  - **A tenant's angry ex-boyfriend** (Motivated External Attacker)
  - **Natural calamity** (Natural Disaster Agent)
- **Vulnerabilities** = Unlocked windows, weak locks
- **Threats** = "Someone could steal the jewellery"

Each type of threat agent requires a **different defense strategy** — you don't protect against an earthquake the same way you protect against a burglar.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

- **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)** = Total Facility Power / IT Equipment Power — industry target **≈ 1.2** (Google averages 1.10)
- **SLA Availability** typically measured in **"number of 9s"**: 99.9% = 8.77 hours downtime/year
- **VM Sprawl** ratio: average VMs per administrator (healthy < 100)
- **MTTR** (Mean Time To Recover) and **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures)
- **CVSS Base Score** for vulnerability severity: 0.0 to **10.0**

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of Cloud Resource Management

Resource management is a **closed-loop control system** with five core phases:

### Phase 1 — Resource Provisioning
Two complementary paradigms:
- **Static Provisioning:** Reserved in advance; cheaper but wasteful. Like booking a 50-seat bus for a 30-person wedding.
- **Dynamic Provisioning:** Just-In-Time (JIT) allocation; uses **auto-scaling groups**, **horizontal pod autoscaler (HPA)**, and **predictive load forecasting** (ARIMA, LSTM models).

### Phase 2 — Resource Allocation
Three classical policies:
| Policy | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Best-Fit** | Place workload on *smallest sufficient* resource | Cost optimization |
| **First-Fit** | Place on *first available* resource | Speed |
| **Worst-Fit** | Place on *largest available* resource | Load balancing |
| **Round-Robin** | Cyclic distribution | Homogeneous clusters |

### Phase 3 — Task Scheduling
Classical heuristics:
- **FCFS** (First-Come, First-Served) — simple, high average waiting time
- **Min-Min** — schedule smallest task first to the machine that completes it fastest
- **Max-Min** — schedule largest task first to avoid starvation of long jobs
- **HEFT (Heterogeneous Earliest Finish Time)** — for DAG workflows, used in AWS Batch / Azure Batch

### Phase 4 — Monitoring & Metering
Uses **telemetry pipelines** (Prometheus → Grafana, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) feeding the **control loop** for re-allocation.

### Phase 5 — Release (De-provisioning)
Includes **VM reclamation**, **garbage collection of orphaned storage**, and **billing reconciliation**.

---

## 2.2 The Eight Design Challenges — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Scalability
Two types:
- **Horizontal Scaling (Scale-Out):** Add more VMs. Used in **stateless** web tiers.
- **Vertical Scaling (Scale-Up):** Add CPU/RAM to existing VM. Used in **stateful** databases.
- The **AKF Scale Cube** model prescribes X, Y, Z axes of scaling.

### 2.2.2 Fault Tolerance
Achieved via:
- **Redundancy** (N+1, 2N, 2N+1)
- **Replication factor** (e.g., HDFS default = 3)
- **Failure Detector** (Phi Accrual, gossip protocol in Cassandra)

### 2.2.3 Virtualization Overhead
- **Type-1 Hypervisor (Bare-metal):** Xen, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V — overhead **2–5%**
- **Type-2 Hypervisor (Hosted):** VirtualBox, VMware Workstation — overhead **10–30%**
- **Containerization (Docker):** overhead **< 1%** (shares kernel)
- **Unikernels:** near-zero overhead, single-purpose OS+app images

### 2.2.4 Energy Efficiency
Two complementary strategies:
- **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling):** reduce CPU clock when idle
- **VM Consolidation:** pack VMs onto fewer physical hosts, switch off the rest
- Metric: **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)**

### 2.2.5 Multi-Tenancy & Isolation
Three isolation levels:
- **Physical isolation** (different hardware)
- **Logical isolation** (different VLANs, subnets)
- **Virtual isolation** (different VMs / containers / namespaces)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** "Multi-tenancy" is *not* the same as "multi-user". A single tenant can have thousands of users. Tenants are **billing/security boundaries**.

### 2.2.6 Heterogeneity
- Mix of **CPU, GPU, TPU, FPGA, ARM, x86, RISC-V**
- Requires **abstraction layer** (e.g., Kubernetes ResourceQuota, OCI spec)

### 2.2.7 SLA Management
- An **SLA** is a *legal* document; an **SLO** (Service Level Objective) is a *technical* metric
- **SLI** (Service Level Indicator) is the *measurement* (e.g., latency p99)
- Formula: **SLO compliance = (successful responses / total responses) × 100%**

### 2.2.8 Interoperability & Vendor Lock-in
- **Vendor Lock-in** = cost of switching providers
- Mitigations: **OpenStack**, **Kubernetes**, **Terraform**, **Multi-cloud abstraction**

---

## 2.3 Threat Agents — Deep Classification

The **OWASP Cloud Top 10** and **NIST SP 800-144** define threat agents along three axes: **Capability**, **Intent**, and **Opportunity**.

### The Six Canonical Threat Agents (KTU Board Favourite)

| # | Threat Agent | Capability | Intent | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anonymous Attacker** | Low–Medium | Variable | Random hacker scanning ports |
| 2 | **Malicious Service Agent** | High (has credentials) | Malicious | Compromised API gateway |
| 3 | **Trusted Attacker** | High (legitimate access) | Variable | Disgruntled employee with valid IAM |
| 4 | **Malicious Insider** | Very High (privileged) | Malicious | Rogue cloud admin / DBA |
| 5 | **Motivated External Attacker** | Very High (well-funded) | Targeted | Nation-state APT group |
| 6 | **Natural / Accidental Agent** | N/A | None | Earthquake, flood, fire, operator error |

### Additional Specialized Agents
- **Botnet Operator** — controls thousands of zombie VMs for DDoS
- **Spy / Industrial Espionage Agent** — long-term covert data exfiltration
- **Script Kiddie** — uses pre-built tools, low skill
- **Hacktivist** — ideologically motivated (Anonymous)
- **Cloud Tenant Cross-VM Attacker** — exploits hypervisor bugs to escape VM

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under KTU 2024 Module 3.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)** | $\text{PUE} = \dfrac{P_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{IT}}}$ | Dimensionless; ideal ≈ 1.0 |
| 2 | **DCiE (Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency)** | $\text{DCiE} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |
| 3 | **SLA Availability** | $\text{Avail} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 4 | **Annual Downtime** | $D_{\text{yr}} = (1 - \text{Avail}) \times 8760$ | Hours/year |
| 5 | **Resource Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Used Capacity}}{\text{Total Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 6 | **VM Consolidation Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{\text{Active VMs}}{\text{Active Hosts}}$ | VMs/Host |
| 7 | **SLO Compliance** | $\text{SLO} = \dfrac{N_{\text{success}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 8 | **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}}$ | Currency/year |
| 9 | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}}$ | Currency |
| 10 | **Annualized Rate of Occurrence** | $\text{ARO} \in [0, \infty)$ | Events/year |
| 11 | **CVSS Base Score Bucket** | $0.0 \le S \le 10.0$ | Low < 4.0, Med 4.0–7.0, High 7.0–9.0, Critical ≥ 9.0 |
| 12 | **Hypervisor Overhead** | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{VM}} - T_{\text{native}}}{T_{\text{native}}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 13 | **Schedule Length (Makespan)** | $C_{\max} = \max(C_i)$ | Time units |
| 14 | **Speedup** | $S_p = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | **Energy per Task** | $E = \int_{0}^{T} P(t)\, dt$ | Joules |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The **ALE = SLE × ARO** formula appears in nearly every security valuation question. Memorize it cold, including its *units* and *interpretation*.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google's DeepMind Cooling:** Reduced data center cooling bill by **40%** using AI-driven PUE optimization.
- **AWS S3 SLA:** 99.9% availability → max 8.77 hours downtime/year; customers get **service credits** for breach.
- **Microsoft Azure:** Uses **Confidential Computing** with **SGX enclaves** to defend against malicious insider threat agent.
- **Capital One Breach (2019):** A *malicious service agent* (former AWS employee + misconfigured WAF) — ALE exceeded **$150M**.
