# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — PUE & Energy Cost Calculation

**Problem:** A cloud data center consumes a total of **12 MW** of facility power, of which the IT equipment (servers, storage, network) draws **8 MW**. Compute:
(a) PUE
(b) DCiE
(c) If electricity costs ₹8 per kWh and the operator reduces IT waste by switching off idle servers, PUE drops to **1.15**. Find the annual savings.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute initial PUE.**

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{IT}}} = \frac{12\text{ MW}}{8\text{ MW}} = 1.50
$$

This means **6 MW (50%)** is overhead (cooling, lighting, UPS losses).

**Step 2 — Compute DCiE.**

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.50} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute new total power with PUE = 1.15, IT load unchanged.**

$$
P'_{\text{total}} = \text{PUE}_{\text{new}} \times P_{\text{IT}} = 1.15 \times 8\text{ MW} = 9.20\text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute power saved.**

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{total}} - P'_{\text{total}} = 12 - 9.20 = 2.80\text{ MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to annual energy (8760 hours/year).**

$$
\Delta E = 2.80\text{ MW} \times 8760\text{ h} = 24{,}528\text{ MWh} = 24{,}528{,}000\text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute annual monetary savings at ₹8/kWh.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 24{,}528{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹}19{,}62{,}24{,}000 \approx \text{₹}19.62\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** A **0.35** reduction in PUE (from 1.50 → 1.15) saved nearly **₹20 Crore/year** — proving that energy-efficient design is *not* CSR, it is **hard economics**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) for a Cloud Tenant

**Problem:** A SaaS company stores customer PII (Personally Identifiable Information) worth ₹50 Crore on a public cloud. A breach would expose **60%** of the data. Industry data suggests **2 breaches per year** are typical for similar firms. Compute:
(a) SLE
(b) ALE
(c) If a security control reduces breaches to **0.5/year** at a cost of ₹8 Lakh/year, is it justified? Compute **ROSIs** (Return on Security Investment).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} = 50\text{ Cr} \times 0.60 = 30\text{ Crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**

$$
\text{ARO} = 2.0\ \text{breaches/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} = 30\text{ Crore} \times 2.0 = 60\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ALE *after* deploying the control.**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{new}} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}_{\text{new}} = 30\text{ Cr} \times 0.5 = 15\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the annual benefit of the control.**

$$
\text{Benefit} = \text{ALE}_{\text{old}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{new}} = 60 - 15 = 45\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute ROSI.**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{45\text{ Cr} - 0.08\text{ Cr}}{0.08\text{ Cr}} \times 100\% = 56{,}150\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The control is **overwhelmingly justified** — every ₹1 spent saves ₹561.50 in expected loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show the **unit** (Crore/year) and end with a *qualitative* one-sentence conclusion. Examiners reward that.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — SLA Availability & Downtime Budget

**Problem:** A cloud provider advertises **99.95%** availability. Compute:
(a) Annual downtime in minutes.
(b) If a tenant's workload requires **four-nines (99.99%)**, can they rely solely on this provider?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Annual minutes available.**

$$
T_{\text{up}} = 0.9995 \times (365 \times 24 \times 60) = 0.9995 \times 525{,}600 = 525{,}336.6\text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual downtime.**

$$
D = 525{,}600 - 525{,}336.6 = 263.4\text{ minutes/year} \approx 4.39\text{ hours/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Tenant requirement.**

$$
D_{\text{required}} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525{,}600 = 52.56\text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare.**

Since **263.4 min > 52.56 min**, the provider alone is *insufficient*. The tenant must deploy **multi-region active-active architecture** to bridge the gap.

**Conclusion:** Single-region cloud ≠ four-nines SLA. **Redundancy across zones** is mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Threat Agent vs Countermeasure

| Threat Agent | Primary Risk | Countermeasure (CIA mapped) | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Anonymous Attacker** | Confidentiality breach | Firewalls, rate limiting, CAPTCHA | NIST SC-7 |
| **Malicious Service Agent** | Integrity compromise | mTLS, service-mesh, code signing | CSA CCM |
| **Trusted Attacker** | Lateral movement | Least-privilege IAM, JIT access | Zero-Trust |
| **Malicious Insider** | Data exfiltration | DLP, UEBA, separation of duties | SOC 2 CC6.1 |
| **Motivated External (APT)** | Long-term espionage | EDR, NDR, threat intel, deception tech | MITRE ATT&CK |
| **Natural / Accidental** | Availability loss | Multi-AZ replication, backups, DR drills | ISO 22301 |

---

## 3.5 Resource Management Scheduling — Min-Min Worked Example

**Problem:** Two VMs (M1, M2) have capacities **5 MIPS** and **10 MIPS**. Three tasks (T1, T2, T3) with lengths **60, 30, 90** MI must be scheduled. Compute makespan using **Min-Min**.

**Step 1 — Compute completion time matrix.**

$$
C_{ij} = \frac{L_j}{P_i}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= 60/5 = 12,\ C_{12} = 30/5 = 6,\ C_{13} = 90/5 = 18 \\
C_{21} &= 60/10 = 6,\ C_{22} = 30/10 = 3,\ C_{23} = 90/10 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Min-Min picks minimum completion time per iteration.**

- T2 to M2 → 3 (smallest). M2 available at 3.
- T1 to M2 → 3 + 6 = 9. T1 to M1 → 12. Pick T1 → M2. M2 available at 9.
- T3 to M1 → 18. T3 to M2 → 9 + 9 = 18. Both equal. Pick T3 → M1.
- **Final makespan** = max(M1_finish = 18, M2_finish = 9) = **18 units**.

**Conclusion:** Min-Min minimizes *average* completion but may not minimize *makespan*. Max-Min would have given makespan = max(60/10, 30/5, 90/5) = **18** here — equivalent by coincidence.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** 
*Define a Threat Agent. List and briefly explain any FOUR categories of threat agents relevant to cloud computing environments.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Threat Agent** is any entity (human, system, or natural) that has the *capability*, *intent*, and *opportunity* to exploit a cloud vulnerability, thereby violating the **CIA triad**.

**Four Categories:**

1. **Anonymous Attacker (1 mark)** — An unidentified external adversary with no privileged access. Uses scanning, brute force, exploit kits. Example: random bot scanning cloud-exposed SSH on port 22.

2. **Malicious Service Agent (½ mark)** — A compromised legitimate cloud service (e.g., a hijacked API gateway, trojaned container image) that performs malicious actions under the guise of trust.

3. **Trusted Attacker (½ mark)** — A legitimate user (employee, contractor) with valid credentials but intent to exceed their authorization. Example: a developer reading production customer PII for curiosity.

4. **Malicious Insider (1 mark)** — A privileged administrator (DBA, cloud root user) who abuses their access for personal gain or sabotage. *Highest risk* category because of elevated privileges.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** 
*Explain the term "Virtualization Overhead" in cloud resource management. Compare Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with respect to overhead.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Virtualization Overhead** is the **performance penalty** (in CPU, memory, I/O latency) introduced by the hypervisor layer mediating between guest VMs and physical hardware. (1 mark)

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | On top of host OS |
| Overhead | **2–5%** | **10–30%** |
| Performance | Near-native | Noticeable degradation |
| Use case | Production data centers (Xen, ESXi) | Development, testing (VirtualBox) |
| Security | Higher (smaller attack surface) | Lower (host OS is attack surface) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3(a) [7 marks].** *Explain the FIVE phases of cloud resource management with a neat diagram. Discuss the design challenges of multi-tenancy and scalability in detail.*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Provisioning (1.5 marks):** Static vs Dynamic; role of predictive auto-scaling.
2. **Allocation (1.5 marks):** Best-Fit, First-Fit, Worst-Fit, Round-Robin policies with example.
3. **Scheduling (1 mark):** Min-Min, Max-Min, HEFT — formulas, pros/cons.
4. **Monitoring (1 mark):** Telemetry, SLA violation detection.
5. **Release (1 mark):** VM reclamation, billing closure.
6. **Multi-tenancy challenge (0.5 marks):** Isolation at VM, network, storage level.
7. **Scalability challenge (1 mark):** Horizontal vs vertical; AKF cube; stateless design.

**Suggested diagram (1 mark):** Re-draw the closed-loop control diagram from Section 4.1.

---

**Q3(b) [7 marks].** *A cloud data center has total facility power = 15 MW and IT power = 10 MW. After deploying an AI-based cooling system, the PUE reduces to 1.10. Electricity cost is ₹9/kWh. Compute: (i) Original PUE (ii) New total power (iii) Annual monetary savings in ₹ Crore. Show all steps.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Original PUE (1 mark):**

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.50
$$

**(ii) New total power (2 marks):**

$$
P'_{\text{total}} = 1.10 \times 10 = 11\text{ MW}
$$

**Power saved (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P = 15 - 11 = 4\text{ MW}
$$

**Annual energy saved (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta E = 4\text{ MW} \times 8760\text{ h} = 35{,}040\text{ MWh} = 3.504 \times 10^7\text{ kWh}
$$

**Annual savings (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Savings} = 3.504 \times 10^7 \times 9 = 3.154 \times 10^8\ \text{₹} = \text{₹}31.54\text{ Crore/year}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q4(a) [7 marks].** *Define Threat Agent. Classify threat agents with a taxonomy diagram. Discuss the difference between a threat, a vulnerability, and a threat agent using a real-world cloud example.*

**Model Solution Outline:**

1. **Definition of Threat Agent (1 mark):** Entity with capability, intent, opportunity to violate CIA.
2. **Taxonomy (3 marks):** Draw the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 (re-sketch on paper).
3. **Distinction (3 marks):**
   - **Threat:** Potential violation (e.g., "data exfiltration possible")
   - **Vulnerability:** Weakness that enables the threat (e.g., open S3 bucket)
   - **Threat Agent:** Who/what can exploit (e.g., Anonymous Attacker)
   - **Example:** Capital One 2019 — Vulnerability = misconfigured WAF, Threat = S3 data theft, Threat Agent = former AWS employee (Trusted Attacker/Malicious Service Agent hybrid).

---

**Q4(b) [7 marks].** *A SaaS company stores customer credit-card data worth ₹100 Crore. A single breach exposes 40% of the data. Industry data indicates 3 breaches per year. Compute (i) SLE (ii) ALE. If a WAF + encryption solution reduces breaches to 0.5/year and costs ₹12 Lakh/year, compute the ROSI and comment on whether the investment is justified.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) SLE (2 marks):**

$$
\text{SLE} = 100 \times 0.40 = 40\text{ Crore}
$$

**(ii) ALE (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ALE} = 40 \times 3 = 120\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**(iii) New ALE (2 marks):**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{new}} = 40 \times 0.5 = 20\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**(iv) Benefit (1 mark):**

$$
B = 120 - 20 = 100\text{ Crore/year}
$$

**(v) ROSI (1 mark):**

$$
\text{ROSI} = \frac{100 - 0.12}{0.12} \times 100\% = 83{,}233\%
$$

**Conclusion (0 mark in rubric, but adds 1–2 grace marks):** The investment is overwhelmingly justified — every ₹1 spent saves ₹832 in expected loss.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing Threat vs Threat Agent vs Vulnerability** — students often use the three terms interchangeably. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in PUE/ALE derivations — always write **MW, MWh, ₹ Crore/year**. A correct number without units is **half-credit at best**.
> 3. **Skipping the qualitative conclusion** in numerical problems — KTU awards **1 grace mark** for "comment on result". Never end with a bare number.
> 4. **Treating SLA = SLO** — SLA is *legal*, SLO is *technical*. A common 2-mark question in Part A.
> 5. **Missing the SLA violation feedback loop** in resource management diagrams — the *closed-loop* nature is what distinguishes *cloud* RM from *traditional* RM. Worth **1 mark**.
> 6. **Calling "multi-tenancy" same as "multi-user"** — they are different. Tenants = billing boundary; users = identity within a tenant.
> 7. **Forgetting energy unit conversion** — 1 MW × 8760 h = 8760 MWh = 8.76 × 10⁶ kWh, **not** 8760 kWh. Many students make this 1000× error.
> 8. **Not mentioning defense-in-depth** when asked about threat agent countermeasures — this is the *expected framework* answer for 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Resource Management Quick-Fire Checklist

- [x] **Five phases:** Provisioning → Allocation → Scheduling → Monitoring → Release
- [x] **Static vs Dynamic Provisioning** — static is cheaper, dynamic is elastic
- [x] **Four allocation policies:** Best-Fit, First-Fit, Worst-Fit, Round-Robin
- [x] **Three scheduling algorithms:** FCFS, Min-Min, Max-Min (+ HEFT for DAGs)
- [x] **PUE** = P_total / P_IT; ideal ≈ 1.0; Google ≈ 1.10
- [x] **SLA Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)
- [x] **Annual Downtime** = (1 − Availability) × 8760 hours
- [x] **Hypervisor overhead:** Type-1 (2–5%) vs Type-2 (10–30%) vs Container (<1%)
- [x] **AKF Scale Cube** — X (horizontal clones), Y (functional split), Z (data partition)
- [x] **Multi-tenancy isolation:** physical < logical < virtual (in security order)
- [x] **SLA vs SLO vs SLI:** legal vs technical vs measurement

### Cloud Security Threat Agent Quick-Fire Checklist

- [x] **Threat Agent definition** — entity with capability, intent, opportunity
- [x] **Six canonical agents:** Anonymous, Malicious Service, Trusted, Malicious Insider, Motivated External APT, Natural/Accidental
- [x] **Vulnerability vs Threat vs Threat Agent** — weakness vs potential vs perpetrator
- [x] **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
- [x] **ALE = SLE × ARO** — the *single most important security formula*
- [x] **SLE = Asset Value × Exposure Factor**
- [x] **ROSI** = (Benefit − Cost) / Cost
- [x] **Defense-in-Depth:** Perimeter → Network → Identity → Data → App → Recovery
- [x] **OWASP Cloud Top 10** is the syllabus-standard reference
- [x] **Capital One 2019** is the *go-to* real-world case study for malicious service agent

### High-Yield Mnemonics

- **"SMART-N"** for Threat Agents: **S**ervice agent, **M**alicious insider, **A**non-attacker, **R**eliable (Trusted), **T**argeted APT, **N**atural
- **"PAMSR"** for Resource Management phases: **P**rovisioning, **A**llocation, **M**onitoring, **S**cheduling, **R**elease
- **"PUSE"** for the PUE family: **P**UE = 1 / **D**CiE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** In 14-mark answers, **always** combine a labelled diagram (2 marks) + bullet-pointed theory (3 marks) + a numerical worked example (2 marks). This structure **guarantees ≥ 11/14** even on an unfamiliar variant question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Security Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Security Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Security Threats** are defined as any circumstance, event, agent, or action that has the potential to compromise the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of data, applications, services, or infrastructure hosted within a cloud computing environment. According to the **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** and the **NIST SP 500-292** reference model, these threats exploit architectural weaknesses inherent in the multi-tenant, network-dependent, and API-driven nature of cloud platforms.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, cloud security threats are studied as a part of the **Risk Management Lifecycle** in distributed systems, encompassing technical vulnerabilities, policy gaps, and human-factor risks that emerge when on-premises data centers are replaced or augmented by public, private, hybrid, or community cloud deployments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Apartment Building

Imagine that you previously lived in your **own independent house** (traditional on-premises data center). You built your own walls, chose your own locks, hired your own watchman, and decided who could enter your compound. Now, imagine you move into a **high-rise apartment building in a shared city** (the cloud). The advantages are massive — shared plumbing, shared electricity, shared security gate, and a landlord (the Cloud Service Provider) managing the building.

However, you now face new problems you never had before:
- The **neighbor's plumber** could accidentally open your door.
- A **fire in another apartment** could spread to yours.
- The **shared elevator** could be broken, trapping everyone inside.
- The **landlord's master key** could be misused.
- A **stranger at the gate** could impersonate a delivery person.

This is precisely the situation of cloud computing. You surrender direct physical control over hardware, network, and storage in exchange for scalability and cost efficiency, but you inherit a new class of **shared-responsibility risks** that did not exist in the standalone world.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars at Risk — The CIA Triad

Every cloud security threat ultimately attacks one or more of these three foundational properties:

| Pillar | Property Violated | Real-World Meaning | Example Cloud Threat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **C**onfidentiality | Unauthorized disclosure | Secrets leak to the wrong person | Data Breach via misconfigured S3 bucket |
| **I**ntegrity | Unauthorized modification | Data is silently altered | SQL Injection modifying billing records |
| **A**vailability | Service denial | Legitimate users cannot access service | Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly maps cloud threats to the **CIA Triad** as the foundational evaluation model. Any answer that does not reference CIA loses marks under Course Outcome CO3.

### 1.4 Why Cloud Security is Fundamentally Different

Unlike traditional IT security, cloud security introduces **new threat surfaces** due to the architectural shift:

1. **Loss of Physical Control** — The customer no longer owns the hardware holding the data.
2. **Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple customers share the same physical servers, creating side-channel risks.
3. **API Dependency** — Every cloud action (provisioning, deletion, query) is performed via RESTful APIs that can be abused.
4. **Data Sovereignty** — Data may traverse international borders, triggering legal and compliance risks.
5. **Dynamic Resource Provisioning** — Elastic resources can be spun up by attackers (e.g., cryptomining via stolen credentials).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cloud Security Alliance (CSA) Threat Taxonomy — "The Notorious Nine & Treacherous Twelve"

The CSA, a globally recognized authority, periodically publishes a ranked list of cloud-specific threats. The KTU 2024 syllabus draws heavily from the **CSA Treacherous Twelve (Top Threats to Cloud Computing)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CSA Treacherous Twelve — Top Cloud Computing Threats:**
>
> 1. **Data Breaches** — Unauthorized access to sensitive data
> 2. **Misconfiguration and Inadequate Change Control** — Default or erroneous settings exposing assets
> 3. **Lack of Cloud Security Architecture and Strategy** — Ad-hoc migration without security planning
> 4. **Insufficient Identity, Credential, Access and Key Management** — Weak authentication and key rotation
> 5. **Account Hijacking** — Theft of login credentials via phishing or credential stuffing
> 6. **Insider Threat** — Malicious or careless employees
> 7. **Insecure Interfaces and APIs** — Poorly designed cloud provider APIs
> 8. **Weak Control Plane** — Subordinate account compromised giving attacker cloud-wide control
> 9. **Metastructure and Applistructure Failures** — Cloud infrastructure APIs and service interfaces exposed
> 10. **Limited Cloud Usage Visibility** — Unsanctioned apps (Shadow IT) operating outside IT's view
> 11. **Hijacking of Services or Accounts** — Use of stolen tokens and sessions
> 12. **Denial of Service (DoS/DDoS)** — Resource exhaustion attacks

### 2.2 Detailed Threat Anatomy

#### 2.2.1 Data Breach
A **data breach** is an incident in which confidential, protected, or sensitive information is accessed or disclosed without authorization. In the cloud, breaches often occur through:
- Misconfigured storage buckets (e.g., AWS S3 left public)
- Compromised application credentials
- Inadequate encryption of data-at-rest
- Side-channel attacks exploiting shared hardware

**Impact:** Regulatory fines (GDPR up to **4% of global annual turnover** or **20 million EUR**, whichever is higher), reputational damage, intellectual property theft.

#### 2.2.2 Denial of Service (DoS) and Distributed DoS (DDoS)
A DoS attack aims to make a cloud-hosted service unavailable by overwhelming it with traffic, exploiting software bugs, or exhausting compute/memory resources. The **distributed** variant (DDoS) uses botnets of thousands of compromised machines (zombies) to generate attack traffic.

**Attack Vectors:**
- **Volumetric attacks** — Saturate network bandwidth (measured in Gbps/Tbps)
- **Protocol attacks** — Exhaust server state tables (SYN flood)
- **Application-layer attacks** — Mimic legitimate user behavior (HTTP flood at Layer 7)

#### 2.2.3 Insecure APIs
Cloud providers expose **RESTful APIs** for every management function. If these APIs are poorly designed (weak authentication, missing rate limiting, excessive data exposure), they become a primary attack vector. Examples include the **2017 AWS S3 outage** caused by a typo during API debugging and the **2019 Capital One breach** exploiting a misconfigured Web Application Firewall (WAF) API.

#### 2.2.4 Account Hijacking
Attackers use **phishing, credential stuffing, or session hijacking** to steal valid cloud account credentials. Once in possession of valid credentials, they can spin up resources, exfiltrate data, or deploy crypto-mining malware.

#### 2.2.5 Insider Threat
A **malicious insider** is a current or former employee, contractor, or business partner with authorized access who misuses that access to compromise systems. The **2013 Edward Snowden incident** at the NSA is the canonical case study referenced in KTU examinations.

#### 2.2.6 Shared Technology Vulnerabilities
Cloud providers use **shared infrastructure** (hypervisors, CPUs, memory, disk). A vulnerability in one component (e.g., **Spectre/Meltdown CPU flaws of 2018**) can allow attackers to break isolation between tenants.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics and formulas are the **standard vocabulary** expected in KTU university exam answers for cloud security. Memorize the symbol, the equation, and the unit.

| Metric / Formula | Mathematical Expression | Description & Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Qualitative or quantitative risk index |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | Expected monetary loss per year in **INR / USD** |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = AV \times EF$ | Loss from a single incident in **INR / USD** |
| **Asset Value (AV)** | $AV$ (in currency) | Total value of the asset |
| **Exposure Factor (EF)** | $EF \in [0, 1]$ | Fraction of asset lost in a single incident |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | $ARO \in [0, \infty)$ | Estimated number of incidents per year |
| **CVSS Base Score** | $Base = 0.6 \times Impact + 0.4 \times Exploitability - 1.5$ | Severity score in range **0.0 to 10.0** |
| **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)** | $ROSI = \dfrac{ALE \times Mitigation - Cost\ of\ Control}{Cost\ of\ Control}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{Total\ Downtime}{Number\ of\ Incidents}$ | Measured in **minutes or hours** |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operational\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Measured in **hours** |
| **Availability Percentage** | $Availability\ \% = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | SLA target typically **99.9%** ("three nines") or higher |
| **DDoS Bandwidth Saturation** | $T_{sat} = \dfrac{C_{pipe}}{P_{avg}}$ | Time to saturate the pipe in **seconds** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** When writing $SLE = AV \times EF$, do **NOT** write $SLE = AV \times \vert EF \vert$ inside a table. The vertical bar will break the markdown table parser. Use the symbol $EF$ as a dimensionless fraction between 0 and 1, and state "no absolute value needed" in your answer script.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not academic curiosities. They are used by:
- **Cloud Security Architects** to justify budgets to Chief Information Security Officers (CISOs).
- **Insurance underwriters** to price cyber-insurance premiums.
- **Auditors** to verify compliance with ISO 27001, SOC 2, and PCI-DSS.
- **DevSecOps engineers** to prioritize patch deployments using CVSS scores.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Risk Quantification from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A healthcare startup hosts a patient database on a public cloud. The database contains **5,00,000 patient records**. Each record has a regulatory replacement cost of **INR 2,000** if breached (HIPAA/GDPR penalty equivalent). The security team estimates that the probability of a data breach in any given year is **0.3** (i.e., 30% chance). If a breach occurs, **60%** of the records are typically exposed.

**Step 1 — Identify the Asset Value (AV).**

$$AV = \text{Number of Records} \times \text{Value per Record}$$

$$AV = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 2000$$

$$AV = 10{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 10 \text{ Crore INR}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Exposure Factor (EF).**

The problem states that 60% of records are exposed per incident. Since EF is a fraction, we write:

$$EF = 0.60$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE).**

$$SLE = AV \times EF$$

$$SLE = 10{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60$$

$$SLE = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 6 \text{ Crore INR}$$

This is the expected monetary loss from a single breach incident.

**Step 4 — Identify the Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO).**

$$ARO = 0.3$$

This means we expect a breach roughly once every $1 / 0.3 \approx 3.33$ years.

**Step 5 — Calculate the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

$$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$

$$ALE = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3$$

$$ALE = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 1.8 \text{ Crore INR per year}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result.**

The organization stands to lose approximately **INR 1.8 Crore per year** if it does not invest in additional cloud security controls. This is the **risk exposure** that must be weighed against the cost of mitigation controls (encryption, WAF, MFA, intrusion detection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show units in every step. KTU examiners award 1 mark for the final numerical answer and 1 mark for the correct units and interpretation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Return on Security Investment (ROSI) Calculation

**Problem Setup:** The same healthcare startup considers deploying an enterprise-grade encryption solution that costs **INR 40,00,000 per year**. The solution is expected to reduce the probability of breach by **70%** (i.e., reduce ARO from 0.3 to $0.3 \times (1 - 0.7) = 0.09$). Calculate the ROSI.

**Step 1 — Calculate the new ALE after mitigation.**

$$ARO_{new} = 0.3 \times (1 - 0.7)$$

$$ARO_{new} = 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.09$$

$$ALE_{new} = SLE \times ARO_{new}$$

$$ALE_{new} = 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.09$$

$$ALE_{new} = 54{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 54 \text{ Lakh INR per year}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the risk reduction benefit.**

$$\text{Mitigation Benefit} = ALE_{old} - ALE_{new}$$

$$\text{Mitigation Benefit} = 1{,}80{,}00{,}000 - 54{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Mitigation Benefit} = 1{,}26{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 1.26 \text{ Crore INR}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ROSI formula.**

$$ROSI = \dfrac{ALE \times \text{Mitigation} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}}$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{1{,}26{,}00{,}000 - 40{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{86{,}00{,}000}{40{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = 2.15$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A ROSI of **2.15** means that for every **INR 1** spent on encryption, the organization saves **INR 2.15** in expected losses. This is a strongly positive investment and should be approved by management.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Countermeasure Mapping

This matrix is a high-yield KTU table. Memorize the pairing.

| Threat Category | Primary Cloud Vector | Countermeasure | Relevant Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Breach** | Misconfigured storage, SQL Injection | AES-256 encryption, WAF, DLP tools | ISO 27001, GDPR Art. 32 |
| **DDoS** | Volumetric and L7 traffic floods | AWS Shield, Cloudflare, Rate limiting | NIST SP 800-61 |
| **Account Hijacking** | Phishing, credential stuffing | MFA, hardware tokens (FIDO2), SSO | NIST SP 800-63B |
| **Insecure APIs** | Broken authentication, excessive data exposure | OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, API Gateway | OWASP API Security Top 10 |
| **Insider Threat** | Privileged user misuse | Least privilege, JIT access, audit logs | ISO 27001 A.7 (HR Security) |
| **Shared Tech. Vulnerability** | Spectre, Meltdown, hypervisor escape | CPU microcode patches, confidential computing | CVE database, MITRE ATT&CK |
| **Data Loss** | Accidental deletion, ransomware | Versioned backups, 3-2-1 rule, immutable storage | ISO 27001 A.12.3 |
| **Misconfiguration** | Default credentials, public buckets | IaC scanning (Terraform), CSPM tools | CIS Benchmarks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid

```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*List and briefly explain the **CSA Top Threats to Cloud Computing** (the Treacherous Twelve). Why is each considered a critical risk in a public cloud deployment?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** identifies the following major threats to cloud computing:

1. **Data Breaches** — Sensitive data such as PII, PHI, or IP is exposed due to misconfiguration or compromised credentials. In public clouds, multi-tenancy increases the attack surface.

2. **Misconfiguration and Inadequate Change Control** — Cloud services come with many configurable parameters (storage permissions, security groups). A single misconfigured S3 bucket or open security group can expose millions of records.

3. **Insufficient Identity, Credential, Access, and Key Management** — Weak passwords, absence of MFA, and improper rotation of cryptographic keys give attackers an easy entry point.

4. **Account Hijacking** — Through phishing, credential stuffing, or session token theft, attackers gain full control of a legitimate cloud account.

5. **Insider Threat** — Employees with privileged access may misuse it intentionally or accidentally.

6. **Insecure APIs and Interfaces** — Every cloud service is exposed via APIs; flaws in these interfaces become exploitable attack vectors.

7. **Weak Control Plane** — A compromised subordinate account can be used to manipulate the entire cloud environment.

8. **Metastructure and Applistructure Failures** — Vulnerabilities in cloud-provisioning layers can lead to widespread compromise.

9. **Limited Cloud Usage Visibility** — Shadow IT (unsanctioned cloud apps) operates outside the security team's monitoring scope.

10. **Hijacking of Services or Accounts** — Stolen session tokens allow attackers to act as legitimate users.

11. **Denial of Service (DoS) and DDoS** — Attackers overwhelm cloud-hosted services, disrupting availability.

12. **Shared Technology Vulnerabilities** — Bugs in shared hypervisors, CPUs, or memory controllers can break tenant isolation.

These are critical because they directly attack the **CIA Triad** in a multi-tenant, internet-facing, and dynamically provisioned environment where the customer has limited direct control.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Explain the **Shared Responsibility Model** in cloud computing. How does it divide security accountability between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **customer** across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** is a security framework that defines which security tasks are handled by the **CSP** (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP) and which are the customer's obligation, varying by service type.

- **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** — The CSP manages the physical data center, servers, storage, and networking. The customer is responsible for the **Operating System, middleware, runtime, applications, and data**. The customer therefore has the largest security burden.

- **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** — The CSP also manages the OS, middleware, and runtime. The customer manages only the **applications and data** they deploy. The security burden shifts partially back to the provider.

- **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** — The CSP manages nearly everything including the application code. The customer is responsible only for **user access management and data governance**. This is the lightest customer burden but not zero.

In **all three models**, the customer always retains responsibility for **data classification, identity and access management (IAM), and endpoint security**. Misunderstanding this division is a leading cause of cloud breaches.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the major **cloud security threats** as identified by the Cloud Security Alliance (CSA). For each threat, give a **real-world example** and recommend a specific countermeasure. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fintech company hosts a payment processing application on a public cloud. The application handles **10,00,000 transactions per day**, with an average transaction value of **INR 5,000**. If a DDoS attack causes 4 hours of downtime, the company estimates it will lose **1% of daily transaction value** as direct revenue loss, plus a **regulatory penalty of INR 50,00,000** per incident. The probability of a DDoS attack in a year is **0.25**. The company is considering investing **INR 1 Crore per year** in a DDoS protection service that will reduce the probability of attack to **0.05** and limit downtime to **30 minutes**. Calculate the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** before and after mitigation, and determine the **Return on Security Investment (ROSI)**. Recommend whether the investment should be approved. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Detailed Discussion of CSA Cloud Security Threats (7 Marks):**

| # | Threat | Real-World Example | Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Breach** | 2017 Equifax breach exposed 147 million customer records due to an unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability. | Encryption at rest and in transit, patch management, DLP. |
| 2 | **Misconfiguration** | 2017 AWS S3 bucket misconfiguration leaked 198 million US voter records. | CSPM tools, infrastructure-as-code (Terraform) scanning, CIS benchmarks. |
| 3 | **Insufficient IAM** | 2019 Capital One breach via compromised IAM role credentials. | MFA, least privilege, role-based access control (RBAC), AWS IAM Access Analyzer. |
| 4 | **Account Hijacking** | 2014 iCloud celebrity photo leak via phishing. | Hardware MFA tokens (FIDO2), session timeout, conditional access. |
| 5 | **Insider Threat** | Snowden 2013 NSA data exfiltration. | Privileged Access Management (PAM), audit logs, separation of duties. |
| 6 | **Insecure APIs** | 2018 Facebook API flaw exposing 50 million user tokens. | OAuth 2.0, input validation, rate limiting, API Gateway throttling. |
| 7 | **DDoS** | 2016 Dyn DNS DDoS attack using Mirai botnet disrupted Twitter, Netflix, Reddit. | AWS Shield Advanced, Cloudflare, traffic scrubbing, anycast networks. |
| 8 | **Shared Tech Vulnerabilities** | Spectre and Meltdown CPU vulnerabilities (2018) broke memory isolation. | Microcode patches, confidential computing (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), virtual patching. |
| 9 | **Data Loss** | 2021 OVHcloud fire destroyed a data center with no off-site backup. | 3-2-1 backup rule, multi-region replication, immutable backups. |
| 10 | **Weak Control Plane** | Compromised DevOps account used to delete production AWS resources (CodeSpaces 2014). | Break-glass procedures, MFA on root account, AWS Organizations SCPs. |

**Closing statement:** Each of these threats targets one or more pillars of the **CIA Triad**, and the countermeasures must be deployed in a **defense-in-depth** manner, not as isolated fixes.

**(b) ALE and ROSI Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute daily revenue exposure.**

$$\text{Daily Revenue} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 5000 = 50{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

This equals 500 Crore INR per day. (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE) for a 4-hour downtime event.**

A 4-hour downtime represents $4 / 24 = 1/6$ of the day.

$$\text{Revenue Loss per incident} = 50{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 \times \frac{1}{6}$$

$$\text{Revenue Loss per incident} = 8{,}33{,}33{,}333 \text{ INR} \approx 83.33 \text{ Lakh INR}$$

Adding the regulatory penalty:

$$SLE = 8{,}33{,}33{,}333 + 50{,}00{,}000 = 8{,}83{,}33{,}333 \text{ INR}$$

Approximately **8.83 Crore INR per incident**. (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Compute ALE before mitigation.**

$$ARO_{old} = 0.25$$

$$ALE_{old} = SLE \times ARO_{old} = 8{,}83{,}33{,}333 \times 0.25$$

$$ALE_{old} = 2{,}20{,}83{,}333 \text{ INR} \approx 2.21 \text{ Crore INR per year}$$ (1 mark)

**Step 4 — Compute SLE for the post-mitigation 30-minute downtime.**

A 30-minute downtime represents $30 / (60 \times 24) = 1/48$ of the day.

$$\text{Revenue Loss (mitigated)} = 50{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 \times \frac{1}{48}$$

$$\text{Revenue Loss (mitigated)} = 10{,}41{,}667 \text{ INR} \approx 10.42 \text{ Lakh INR}$$

$$SLE_{new} = 10{,}41{,}667 + 50{,}00{,}000 = 60{,}41{,}667 \text{ INR}$$

Approximately **60.42 Lakh INR per incident**. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Compute ALE after mitigation.**

$$ARO_{new} = 0.05$$

$$ALE_{new} = 60{,}41{,}667 \times 0.05 = 30{,}20{,}833 \text{ INR} \approx 30.21 \text{ Lakh INR per year}$$ (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Compute the ROSI.**

$$\text{Mitigation Benefit} = ALE_{old} - ALE_{new} = 2{,}20{,}83{,}333 - 30{,}20{,}833 = 1{,}90{,}62{,}500 \text{ INR}$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{1{,}90{,}62{,}500 - 1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}$$

$$ROSI = \dfrac{90{,}62{,}500}{1{,}00{,}00{,}000} = 0.906$$ (1 mark)

**Step 7 — Recommendation.**

A ROSI of **0.906** indicates a positive but not overwhelming return. However, when factoring in **intangible benefits** — brand reputation, customer trust, regulatory goodwill, and SLA compliance penalties avoided — the investment of INR 1 Crore should be **approved**. The company additionally reduces the breach frequency by 80% (from 0.25 to 0.05), which is a substantial qualitative improvement. (1 mark)

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the **CIA Triad** in the context of cloud security. For each pillar, describe a specific cloud attack that violates it, the attack mechanism, and a defense mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Cyber Kill Chain** as applied to a cloud environment. Using a labeled diagram, walk through each of the seven phases, and explain how a Security Operations Center (SOC) can break the chain at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CIA Triad with Cloud Attack Mapping (7 Marks):**

The **CIA Triad** is the cornerstone model of information security, comprising three properties that every cloud security measure must protect.

**1. Confidentiality (C) — Preventing unauthorized disclosure.**

- *Attack*: **Cloud Storage Misconfiguration Exposure** — An S3 bucket is left with public read permissions, exposing customer PII.
- *Mechanism*: The attacker scans the public internet using tools like `S3Scanner` or `BucketStream` to find open buckets.
- *Defense*: Default-deny bucket policies, AWS Macie for sensitive data discovery, periodic CSPM scans, and encryption with customer-managed keys (CMK).

**2. Integrity (I) — Preventing unauthorized modification.**

- *Attack*: **SQL Injection in a Cloud-Hosted Web App** — The attacker inserts malicious SQL into a login form, altering database records (e.g., inflating account balances).
- *Mechanism*: Unsanitized user inputs are concatenated into SQL queries, allowing the attacker to manipulate the database.
- *Defense*: Parameterized queries (prepared statements), input validation, Web Application Firewalls (WAF), and database integrity constraints.

**3. Availability (A) — Ensuring timely access by authorized users.**

- *Attack*: **SYN Flood DDoS** — The attacker sends a flood of TCP SYN packets to a cloud-hosted web server, exhausting its connection table.
- *Mechanism*: The attacker spoofs source IP addresses so the server's SYN-ACK responses never complete the three-way handshake, leaving half-open connections that fill the backlog queue.
- *Defense*: AWS Shield, Cloudflare Magic Transit, SYN cookies, rate limiting at the application layer, and anycast routing to distribute attack traffic.

**Closing statement:** A robust cloud security posture addresses all three pillars simultaneously, and the choice of countermeasure must be guided by risk-based prioritization. (1 mark reserved for conclusion and clarity)

**(b) Cyber Kill Chain in Cloud Environment (7 Marks):**

The **Cyber Kill Chain**, originally developed by Lockheed Martin, decomposes a cyberattack into seven sequential phases. When applied to a cloud environment, the kill chain helps SOC teams identify breakpoints where an attack can be detected and stopped.

**Phase 1 — Reconnaissance:**
The attacker gathers information about the target cloud environment by scanning public IP ranges, identifying exposed services, and harvesting employee emails from LinkedIn.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: Deploy honeypots, monitor DNS queries, and use threat intelligence feeds to identify scanning activity.

**Phase 2 — Weaponization:**
The attacker crafts a malicious payload — for example, a phishing email with a link to a fake AWS SSO login page.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: Email gateway filtering, sandboxing attachments, and URL reputation checks.

**Phase 3 — Delivery:**
The phishing email is sent to a target cloud administrator.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: User awareness training, DMARC/SPF/DKIM email authentication.

**Phase 4 — Exploitation:**
The victim enters credentials into the fake SSO page, which forwards them to the attacker in real time (adversary-in-the-middle).
- *SOC Breakpoint*: FIDO2 hardware tokens that resist phishing, conditional access policies that block sign-ins from impossible-travel locations.

**Phase 5 — Installation:**
The attacker uses the stolen credentials to create a new IAM user with admin privileges or deploy a web shell in an S3-hosted static site.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: CloudTrail monitoring, GuardDuty anomaly detection, IAM Access Analyzer.

**Phase 6 — Command and Control (C2):**
The attacker establishes a C2 channel via HTTPS webhooks or DNS tunneling to control the compromised cloud resources.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: Egress traffic monitoring, DNS sinkholing, threat hunting in VPC flow logs.

**Phase 7 — Actions on Objectives:**
The attacker exfiltrates data, deploys cryptominers, or encrypts S3 buckets for ransomware.
- *SOC Breakpoint*: DLP tools, immutable S3 buckets with object lock, MFA-delete on critical buckets.

A **well-designed cloud SOC** deploys controls at every phase so that even if one defense fails, the attack is detected and stopped at a later stage — the principle of **defense in depth**. (1 mark reserved for integrated summary and reference to defense-in-depth principle)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cloud Security Threat Questions:**
>
> 1. **Listing threats without examples** — KTU examiners deduct 1 to 2 marks if you simply enumerate threats without citing a real-world case (Equifax 2017, Capital One 2019, OVHcloud 2021, etc.).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the CIA Triad mapping** — Always explicitly link each threat to the CIA pillar it violates. A question worth 7 marks that asks for "major threats" expects at least 4–5 threats with CIA mapping.
>
> 3. **Numerical errors in ALE/ROSI** — Confusing SLE with ALE, using percentage form for ARO instead of decimal (e.g., writing `ARO = 25` instead of `0.25`), and missing units. Always carry units in every step.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Shared Responsibility Model** — A common mistake is to blame the cloud provider for application-layer breaches. KTU expects students to identify **who is responsible** for what.
>
> 5. **Using absolute value bars in markdown tables** — When writing $EF \in [0,1]$, do NOT write $\vert EF \vert$ inside a markdown table cell. The vertical pipe breaks the table parser. Use plain $EF$ with a textual note instead.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Mermaid syntax rules** — In diagrams, do not use reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` as node names. Always prefix with letters (e.g., `nodeA`, `phase1`).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud Security Threats**
>
> ✅ **CIA Triad** is the foundational evaluation model — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Map every threat to at least one pillar.
>
> ✅ **CSA Treacherous Twelve** is the canonical threat list — memorize at least the top 6 (Data Breach, Misconfiguration, Weak IAM, Account Hijacking, Insecure APIs, DDoS).
>
> ✅ **Shared Responsibility Model** — In IaaS, the customer manages OS upward; in PaaS, only apps and data; in SaaS, only data and access. The CSP always owns "security **of** the cloud"; the customer owns "security **in** the cloud".
>
> ✅ **Risk Formula** — $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. Use this to justify security investments.
>
> ✅ **ALE Formula** — $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ where $SLE = AV \times EF$. Always convert percentage ARO to decimal.
>
> ✅ **ROSI Formula** — Positive ROSI means investment is justified. Intangible benefits (reputation, compliance) often tip the balance.
>
> ✅ **CVSS Base Score Range** — 0.0 to 10.0; scores above **7.0** are High severity, above **9.0** are Critical.
>
> ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** — Six layers: Perimeter, IAM, Application, Data, Infrastructure, Monitoring. No single layer is sufficient.
>
> ✅ **Cyber Kill Chain** — Seven phases: Recon → Weaponize → Deliver → Exploit → Install → C2 → Actions. Identify SOC breakpoints at each phase.
>
> ✅ **Real-World Case Studies** — Know at least 3: Equifax 2017 (Data Breach), Capital One 2019 (IAM), OVHcloud 2021 (Data Loss), Dyn DNS 2016 (DDoS), Snowden 2013 (Insider Threat).
>
> ✅ **Common Attack Vectors** — Misconfigured S3 buckets, weak S3 credentials, exposed API keys in GitHub repos, vulnerable Docker images, plaintext secrets in Terraform files.
>
> ✅ **Standards & Frameworks** — ISO 27001, NIST SP 800-53, NIST SP 800-63B, OWASP API Security Top 10, CIS Benchmarks, SOC 2, PCI-DSS, GDPR Art. 32, HIPAA Security Rule.
>
> ✅ **Key Metrics to Memorize** — Availability "three nines" = **99.9%**, "four nines" = **99.99%**, "five nines" = **99.999%**. GDPR maximum fine = **4% of global turnover or 20M EUR**.
>
> ✅ **Exam Strategy** — Always end numerical answers with a **recommendation sentence**. Always end descriptive answers with a **conclusion** that ties back to CIA Triad or Defense-in-Depth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating System Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operating System Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Operating System (OS) Security** refers to the set of policies, mechanisms, controls, and procedures implemented at the OS layer to protect computing resources, data, and execution environments from unauthorized access, malicious software, and exploitation of kernel-level vulnerabilities. In the context of **Cloud Computing**, OS Security is foundational because cloud workloads (Virtual Machines, Containers, Serverless functions) all depend on an underlying OS whose compromise directly threatens **multi-tenant isolation, hypervisor integrity, and customer data confidentiality**.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, OS Security encompasses:

- **Authentication** — verifying the identity of a user/process
- **Authorization / Access Control** — enforcing *who* can do *what* on *which* resource
- **Audit & Accountability** — logging security-relevant events
- **Protection against malware** — viruses, worms, trojans, rootkits
- **Memory protection** — preventing buffer overflows, use-after-free, etc.
- **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** — the small, verifiable subset of the OS that enforces security
- **Security Kernel** — the core of TCB enforcing the reference monitor concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition**
> *"Operating System Security is the process of ensuring the integrity, confidentiality, and availability of the OS resources by implementing authentication, access control, auditing, and protection mechanisms against internal and external threats."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine an **Operating System as a high-rise apartment building**:

| OS Security Component | Apartment Building Analogy | Real-world Mapping |
|----------------------|----------------------------|--------------------|
| **Authentication** | Showing ID at the lobby security desk | Username + Password, Biometrics, MFA |
| **Access Control (DAC/MAC/RBAC)** | The key card that opens *only your floor and your apartment* | File permissions, SELinux policies |
| **Reference Monitor** | The security guard who checks every door | Kernel enforcing system calls |
| **Buffer Overflow** | A thief writing past the mailbox slot into the neighbor's mailbox | Stack/heap memory corruption |
| **Trojan/Virus/Worm** | A delivery person who looks legit but smuggles contraband | Malicious code injected into trusted apps |
| **Rootkit** | A maintenance worker with a *fake uniform* who hides in the basement | Kernel-level stealth malware |
| **Security Kernel / TCB** | The reinforced panic room that holds master keys | Hardened, verified portion of OS |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** In cloud environments, a single compromised OS instance can pivot laterally to attack other tenants on the same physical host — this is why cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) invest heavily in hardened OS images and micro-VMs (e.g., AWS Nitro, Firecracker).

---

## 1.3 Core Security Goals — The **CIA Triad**

The three pillars evaluated in every KTU exam question on OS Security:

1. **Confidentiality** — Data is accessible *only* to authorized entities.
2. **Integrity** — Data is *unaltered* by unauthorized parties.
3. **Availability** — Systems remain operational when needed (defense against DoS).

Extended CIA+: **Authentication, Authorization, Auditing (AAA)** and **Non-repudiation**.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **Trusted Computing Base (TCB):** the totality of protection mechanisms within a computer system responsible for enforcing security policy.
- **Reference Monitor:** an abstract machine that mediates all access of subjects to objects. It must be *complete, isolated, and verifiable* (the three properties).
- **Security Kernel:** the implementation of the reference monitor — must be small enough to be verified.
- **Least Privilege:** every user/program operates with the *minimum set of privileges* necessary.
- **Defense in Depth:** multiple overlapping security layers (network + OS + application + data).
- **Salt:** a random value added to a password before hashing to defeat rainbow-table attacks.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA):** requires ≥ 2 of: *something you know, have, are*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillars of OS Security — Structured Logic

### A. Authentication
The process of proving the identity claimed by a user or process.

**Authentication Factors:**

| Factor Type | Examples | Strength |
|-------------|----------|----------|
| Knowledge (Type I) | Password, PIN, security question | Weakest alone |
| Possession (Type II) | Smart card, OTP token, phone | Moderate |
| Biometric (Type III) | Fingerprint, iris, face | Strong |
| Location/Behavior (Type IV) | IP address, keystroke dynamics | Contextual |

**Password Entropy Formula** (high-yield for KTU numerics):

$$
H = L \times \log_2(N)
$$

Where:
- $H$ = password entropy in **bits**
- $L$ = length of the password
- $N$ = size of the character set (e.g., 26 for lowercase, 94 for printable ASCII)

> [!NOTE]
> An entropy of $\ge 80$ bits is considered cryptographically strong by NIST SP 800-63B (2024).

---

### B. Access Control Models (Most-Tested Topic)

#### 1. **Discretionary Access Control (DAC)**
- Owner of a resource decides who can access it (e.g., Unix `chmod`, Windows NTFS permissions).
- Vulnerable to **Trojan Horse attacks** — a malicious program inherits the user's privileges.

#### 2. **Mandatory Access Control (MAC)**
- System-enforced; users **cannot** override policies.
- Uses security labels (e.g., *Confidential, Secret, Top Secret*).
- Implemented by **SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux)**, AppArmor.
- **Bell-LaPadula Model** (Confidentiality-focused):
  - **No Read Up (NRU)** — *Simple Security Property*
  - **No Write Down (NWD)** — *Star Property ($\star$-property)*
- **Biba Model** (Integrity-focused):
  - **No Read Down (NRD)**
  - **No Write Up (NWU)**

#### 3. **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)**
- Permissions are assigned to *roles*, not users. Users are assigned roles.
- Used in cloud IAM (e.g., AWS IAM roles, Azure RBAC).
- Reduces administrative overhead for large organizations.

#### 4. **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)**
- Decisions based on attributes of subject, object, and environment.
- More flexible than RBAC; used in modern cloud-native security.

---

### C. Buffer Overflow Attack — Stepwise Logic

A **buffer overflow** occurs when a program writes data beyond the allocated boundary of a fixed-size buffer in memory, overwriting adjacent stack frames, return addresses, or function pointers.

**Why it works:**
1. C/C++ do **not** perform automatic bounds checking.
2. Stack grows *downward*; function local buffers are placed *above* the saved frame pointer and return address.
3. Attacker crafts input that overwrites the **return address** with the address of injected shellcode.

**Stack frame layout (high to low address):**

$$
\underbrace{[\text{Args}]}_{\text{High addr}} \rightarrow [\text{Return Addr}] \rightarrow [\text{Saved FP}] \rightarrow [\text{Local Buffer}]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buffer Overflow Defenses (must-know for KTU):**
> - **Stack canaries** (GCC `-fstack-protector`): place a random value before the return address; verify before function returns.
> - **DEP / NX bit** (No-Execute): mark stack as non-executable.
> - **ASLR** (Address Space Layout Randomization): randomize base addresses of stack, heap, libs.
> - **Safe languages:** Java, Python, Rust — bounds-checked by default.

---

### D. Malware Taxonomy

| Malware Type | Definition | Propagation | Example |
|--------------|------------|-------------|---------|
| **Virus** | Code attached to a host program; needs user action | File sharing, email | ILOVEYOU |
| **Worm** | Self-replicating; spreads via network *autonomously* | Network vulnerabilities | Blaster, Conficker |
| **Trojan Horse** | Disguised as legitimate software | Social engineering | Emotet |
| **Logic Bomb** | Triggers on a condition (date, event) | Insider | Insider attacks |
| **Rootkit** | Hides deep in OS; subverts kernel | Post-exploitation | Stuxnet |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypts data; demands payment | Phishing, worms | WannaCry, LockBit |
| **Spyware/Adware** | Monitors user behavior | Bundled software | CoolWebSearch |
| **Bot / Botnet** | Network of compromised hosts | Worm + C2 server | Mirai |

---

### E. Trusted OS & Reference Monitor Concept

The **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** = total of all hardware, software, and firmware components that enforce security.

The **Reference Monitor** must satisfy three properties:
1. **Complete mediation** — *every* access is checked.
2. **Tamper-proof** — cannot be modified by attackers.
3. **Small enough to be verifiable** — minimal attack surface.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Security Kernel** is the OS portion that implements the reference monitor. In SELinux, this corresponds to the LSM (Linux Security Module) hooks inside the kernel.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---------|---------------|----------------|------|
| **Password Entropy** | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = charset size | bits |
| **Crack Time (avg)** | $T = \dfrac{2^{H}}{2 \cdot R}$ | $R$ = guesses/sec | seconds |
| **MFA Strength** | $\prod_{i=1}^{k} P_i$ | $P_i$ = probability of breaching factor $i$ | dimensionless |
| **Bell-LaPadula Read** | $\text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)$ | Subject level ≥ Object level | security levels |
| **Biba Read** | $\text{integrity}(S) \le \text{integrity}(O)$ | Subject ≤ Object integrity | integrity levels |
| **Access Time (RBAC)** | $O(R + P)$ vs $O(U \cdot P)$ | RBAC linear, ACL exponential | operations |
| **Buffer size (safe)** | $n \ge \text{strlen(input)} + 1$ | plus null terminator | bytes |
| **Risk** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Threat × Vulnerability × Impact | qualitative |
| **Brute-force keyspace** | $K = 2^k$ | $k$ = key length in bits | combinations |
| **AES-256 entropy** | $H = 256$ | 256-bit key | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - If $H \ge 128$ bits → password is **cryptographically strong**.
> - If $L = 0$ or $N = 1$ → $H = 0$ (degenerate).
> - Bell-LaPadula applies only to **read** (NRU) and **write** (NWD) operations on labeled objects.
> - Biba is the **dual** of Bell-LaPadula for integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS Nitro System** uses a hardened security micro-controller (Vortex) to offload I/O — minimizes the hypervisor's TCB.
- **Google's gVisor** implements a *user-space kernel* for containers — additional isolation between app and host OS.
- **Confidential VMs (AMD SEV-SNP, Intel TDX)** encrypt VM memory to protect against malicious hypervisor.
- **SELinux** is the de-facto MAC implementation in RHEL, CentOS, Fedora, and Android.
- **Windows Defender Credential Guard** uses virtualization-based security to isolate LSA secrets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Password Entropy and Crack Time

**Problem (typical KTU 3-mark):**
A password is 12 characters long and is drawn from a charset of 95 printable ASCII characters. Compute:
(a) Entropy in bits
(b) Average time to crack at $10^{10}$ guesses/sec using brute force.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

$$
L = 12, \quad N = 95
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the entropy formula.**

$$
H = L \times \log_2(N)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_2(95)$.**

$$
\log_2(95) = \frac{\ln(95)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5539}{0.6931} = 6.5699 \text{ bits per character}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by length $L$.**

$$
H = 12 \times 6.5699 = 78.839 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the average crack-time formula.**

$$
T = \frac{2^{H}}{2 \times R}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $2^{H}$.**

$$
2^{78.839} = 2^{78} \times 2^{0.839} = 3.0223 \times 10^{23} \times 1.7856 = 5.396 \times 10^{23}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute $T$ in seconds.**

$$
T = \frac{5.396 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 10^{10}} = 2.698 \times 10^{13} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to years.**

$$
T_{\text{years}} = \frac{2.698 \times 10^{13}}{60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365} = 8.55 \times 10^{5} \text{ years}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{H \approx 78.84 \text{ bits}, \quad T_{\text{crack}} \approx 855{,}000 \text{ years}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even an 8-character password of the same charset gives only $\approx 52.5$ bits → crack time $\approx 2.5$ days at the same rate. **This is why length matters more than complexity.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Risk Computation (Quantitative Security Metric)

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A cloud server hosts a financial application. Estimated values:
- Threat probability $T = 0.4$
- Vulnerability score $V = 0.6$
- Impact value $I = \$1{,}000{,}000$

Compute the Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the ALE formula.**

$$
\text{ALE} = T \times V \times I
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\text{ALE} = 0.4 \times 0.6 \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step.**

$$
\text{ALE} = 0.24 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 240{,}000
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{ALE} = \$240{,}000 \text{ per year}}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A security investment of less than \$240,000/year is *justified* if it eliminates the vulnerability.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bell-LaPadula Access Decision

**Problem:**
Subjects $S_1, S_2, S_3$ have clearance levels $\{ \text{Top Secret}, \text{Secret}, \text{Unclassified} \}$.
Objects $O_1, O_2$ have classification levels $\{ \text{Secret}, \text{Confidential} \}$.

Determine which read operations are **allowed** under Bell-LaPadula.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the rule.**

$$
\text{Read allowed} \iff \text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)
$$

**Step 2 — Build the access decision matrix.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 \rightarrow O_1: \quad & \text{Top Secret} \ge \text{Secret} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{ALLOW (Read)} \\
S_1 \rightarrow O_2: \quad & \text{Top Secret} \ge \text{Confidential} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{ALLOW (Read)} \\
S_2 \rightarrow O_1: \quad & \text{Secret} \ge \text{Secret} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{ALLOW (Read)} \\
S_2 \rightarrow O_2: \quad & \text{Secret} \ge \text{Confidential} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{ALLOW (Read)} \\
S_3 \rightarrow O_1: \quad & \text{Unclassified} \ge \text{Secret} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{DENY (No Read Up)} \\
S_3 \rightarrow O_2: \quad & \text{Unclassified} \ge \text{Confidential} \; \Rightarrow \;\text{DENY (No Read Up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Summary table.**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Subject} & \textbf{Object} & \textbf{Bell-LaPadula Decision} \\
\hline
S_1 \text{ (TS)} & O_1 \text{ (S)} & \text{ALLOW} \\
S_1 \text{ (TS)} & O_2 \text{ (C)} & \text{ALLOW} \\
S_2 \text{ (S)}  & O_1 \text{ (S)} & \text{ALLOW} \\
S_2 \text{ (S)}  & O_2 \text{ (C)} & \text{ALLOW} \\
S_3 \text{ (U)}  & O_1 \text{ (S)} & \text{DENY} \\
S_3 \text{ (U)}  & O_2 \text{ (C)} & \text{DENY} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The corresponding **write rule (Star property)** is: a subject can write *up* but *never down* — prevents leaking sensitive data to lower-classified objects.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Buffer Overflow Stack Damage (Memory Offset Analysis)

**Problem:**
A 32-bit process has a local buffer of 32 bytes. The return address is stored at offset $+36$ from the start of the buffer (4 bytes saved FP + 32 bytes buffer + 4 bytes ret-addr alignment). The attacker inputs 50 bytes starting with shellcode of 20 bytes followed by 4 bytes of new return address `0xBFFFF100`. Draw the post-overflow stack layout.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Pre-overflow stack layout (high addr at top).**

$$
\underbrace{[ \text{Arguments} ]}_{\text{High}} \quad [ \text{Return Addr}_{old} ] \quad [ \text{Saved FP} ] \quad [ \text{Buffer (32 B)} ] \quad \underbrace{[ \text{Free Stack} ]}_{\text{Low}}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify byte offsets from buffer start.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes } 0 \rightarrow 31 &: \text{Original buffer} \\
\text{Bytes } 32 \rightarrow 35 &: \text{Saved FP overwritten} \\
\text{Bytes } 36 \rightarrow 39 &: \text{Return address overwritten} \\
\text{Bytes } 40 \rightarrow 49 &: \text{Spillover (shellcode tail)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compose the post-overflow layout.**

$$
\underbrace{[ \text{Args} ]}_{\text{High}} \quad [ \texttt{0xBFFFF100} ] \quad [ \text{GARBAGE} ] \quad \underbrace{[ \texttt{20 B Shellcode} \; \mid \; \texttt{4 B FP} \; \mid \; \texttt{4 B RET} \; \mid \; \texttt{10 B NOPs} ]}_{\text{Buffer area}} \quad \underbrace{[ \text{Remainder} ]}_{\text{Low}}
$$

**Step 4 — Outcome.**
When the function returns, the CPU pops `0xBFFFF100` into EIP and jumps into the attacker's shellcode → **arbitrary code execution**.

**Defense recap:**

$$
\text{Risk}_{\text{overflow}} \propto \frac{1}{\text{ASLR strength} \times \text{NX bit} \times \text{Canary}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 OS Security Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[User Process A] --> sysCall{SYSCALL Interface}
    userB[User Process B] --> sysCall
    userC[User Process C] --> sysCall
    sysCall --> refMon[Reference Monitor]
    refMon --> authMod[Authentication Module]
    refMon --> accessCtrl[Access Control Module]
    refCall[Audit Logger] -.logs.-> refMon
    refMon --> secKernel[Security Kernel - TCB]
    secKernel --> macPol[MAC Policy - SELinux]
    secKernel --> fileSys[File System Protection]
    secKernel --> memProt[Memory Protection - ASLR NX]
    secKernel --> procIso[Process Isolation]
    secKernel --> cryptoMod[Crypto Module]
    attacker[Malicious Actor] -.targets.-> userB
    attacker -.targets.-> memProt
    refMon -.denies.-> attacker
```

## 4.2 Access Control Flow — RBAC vs DAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    actorUser[User] -->|assigned to| roleObj[Role]
    roleObj -->|grants| permObj[Permission]
    permObj -->|on| resourceObj[Resource]
    actorOwner[Resource Owner DAC] -.manual grant.-> resourceObj
    macPolSys[Mandatory Policy System] -.enforces.-> resourceObj
    rbacDec{RBAC Engine} -->|checks| roleObj
    rbacDec -->|checks| permObj
    rbacDec -->|ALLOW or DENY| actorUser
    macPolSys -->|ALLOW or DENY| actorUser
```

## 4.3 Buffer Overflow Attack & Defense Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputVec[Malicious Input] --> strcpyV[Unsafe strcpy - no bounds check]
    strcpyV --> bufArea[Stack Buffer 32 bytes]
    bufArea --> retAddr[Return Address overwritten]
    retAddr --> shellExec[Arbitrary Code Execution]
    shellExec --> rootShell[Attacker gains shell]
    canaryDef[Stack Canary] -.verifies.-> retAddr
    nxDef[NX / DEP bit] -.blocks.-> shellExec
    aslrDef[ASLR randomization] -.randomizes.-> retAddr
    safeLang[Safe Language Rust Go] -.prevents.-> strcpyV
    rootShell -.impact.-> dataLoss[(Data Breach)]
```

## 4.4 Cloud OS Security Stack (Layered Defense)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tenantApp[Tenant Application] --> vmLayer[VM or Container]
    vmLayer --> guestOS[Guest OS - Hardened Image]
    guestOS --> hyperv[Hypervisor - VMX SVM]
    hyperv --> hostOS[Host OS - Minimal TCB]
    hostOS --> firmware[UEFI Secure Boot Measured Boot]
    firmware --> hardware[TPM - Trusted Platform Module]
    hardwareRoot[Hardware Root of Trust] --> firmware
    sgx[Intel SGX AMD SEV Enclaves] -.isolates.-> tenantApp
    ssp[AWS Nitro Security Chip] -.isolates.-> hostOS
    cloudProv[Cloud Provider SOC] -.monitors.-> hostOS
```
