<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization. List two factors used in Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Authentication** is the process of *verifying the identity* of a user/process (proving "who you are"). Example: entering a password.
- **Authorization** is the process of *granting access rights* to an authenticated user (proving "what you can do"). Example: file permission `chmod 644`.
- Authentication is performed *before* authorization in the AAA framework.
- Two MFA factors:
  1. **Something you know** — password, PIN.
  2. **Something you have** — OTP token, smartphone with authenticator app.
- (Bonus) **Something you are** — fingerprint, iris scan.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the difference between DAC and MAC with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DAC (Discretionary Access Control) | MAC (Mandatory Access Control) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| **Policy Owner** | Resource owner decides | System-enforced; no user override |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low |
| **Security Strength** | Weaker (vulnerable to Trojans) | Stronger (used in military/classified) |
| **Example** | Unix/Linux `chmod`, Windows NTFS ACLs | SELinux, AppArmor, Bell-LaPadula |
| **Cloud usage** | Default for user files | Required for tenant isolation in GovCloud |

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **Buffer Overflow attack** in detail. Discuss the stack memory layout and how the return address gets hijacked.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **four main types of malware** (Virus, Worm, Trojan Horse, Rootkit) with their propagation mechanism and one real-world example for each.

---

#### **Model Solution (a)**

A **buffer overflow** is an attack where data written into a buffer exceeds its allocated size, overwriting adjacent memory — typically the saved frame pointer and **return address** on the stack.

**Stack Layout (high → low address):**

$$
[\text{Args}] \rightarrow [\text{Return Address}] \rightarrow [\text{Saved FP}] \rightarrow [\text{Local Buffer}]
$$

**Attack Steps (write 7 distinct steps for full marks):**

1. Attacker identifies a C/C++ function using unsafe functions like `strcpy`, `gets`, `sprintf` without bounds checking.
2. Attacker crafts an input string longer than the buffer size (e.g., 50 bytes into a 32-byte buffer).
3. The first 32 bytes fill the buffer; the next 4 bytes overwrite the **Saved Frame Pointer**.
4. The subsequent 4 bytes overwrite the **Return Address** with the address of injected shellcode.
5. The shellcode (typically 20–200 bytes) is placed either inside the buffer (via NOP sled) or in an environment variable.
6. When the function executes `ret`, the CPU pops the attacker's address into EIP/RIP.
7. Execution jumps to the shellcode, giving the attacker a shell or executing arbitrary commands.

**Diagram (already shown in Section 4.3) — must reproduce in exam.**

**Defenses:**
- **Stack canaries** — random value placed before return address; checked before return.
- **DEP/NX bit** — stack marked non-executable.
- **ASLR** — randomize stack/heap base addresses.
- **Use safe languages** — Java, Python, Rust.

---

#### **Model Solution (b)**

| # | Malware | Definition | Propagation | Example |
|---|---------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Virus** | Code attached to a host program; requires user action to execute | Email attachments, USB, infected files | **ILOVEYOU** (2000), **Melissa** |
| 2 | **Worm** | Self-replicating malware that spreads *autonomously* over networks | Exploits OS/protocol vulnerabilities (e.g., SMB, RDP) | **Conficker**, **Blaster**, **WannaCry** |
| 3 | **Trojan Horse** | Malicious code disguised as legitimate software; does *not* self-replicate | Social engineering, bundled freeware | **Emotet**, **Zeus** |
| 4 | **Rootkit** | Malware that hides itself at kernel/firmware level, granting persistent privileged access | Post-exploitation; replaces OS files or hooks kernel | **Stuxnet** (2010), **Sony BMG rootkit** |

**Distinguishing feature for full marks:**
- Virus needs a **host**; worm does **not**.
- Trojan relies on **deception**; rootkit relies on **stealth**.
- Rootkits often disable **antivirus** to maintain persistence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Bell-LaPadula Model** and the **Biba Model** with their access rules. Explain how they differ in their security focus.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **Reference Monitor Concept** and the **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)**. State and justify the three properties a reference monitor must satisfy.

---

#### **Model Solution (a)**

**Bell-LaPadula Model (Confidentiality-focused, developed 1973):**

Designed for military/government systems handling classified data.

**Three access rules:**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):**
   A subject $S$ can read object $O$ *only if*:
   $$
   \text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)
   $$

2. **Star Property (No Write Down — NWD):**
   A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ *only if*:
   $$
   \text{level}(S) \le \text{level}(O)
   $$

3. **Discretionary Security Property:** Access must also satisfy the access matrix.

**Biba Model (Integrity-focused, 1977):**

Dual of Bell-LaPadula — protects data integrity instead of confidentiality.

**Two access rules:**

1. **Simple Integrity (No Read Down — NRD):**
   A subject $S$ can read object $O$ *only if*:
   $$
   \text{integrity}(S) \le \text{integrity}(O)
   $$

2. **Star Integrity (No Write Up — NWU):**
   A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ *only if*:
   $$
   \text{integrity}(S) \ge \text{integrity}(O)
   $$

**Comparison Table (must include for full 7 marks):**

| Aspect | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|--------|---------------|------|
| **Focus** | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| **Direction blocked (read)** | Up (NRU) | Down (NRD) |
| **Direction blocked (write)** | Down (NWD) | Up (NWU) |
| **Use case** | Military secrets | Banking, audit logs |
| **In cloud** | GovCloud tenancy isolation | Signed container images |

---

#### **Model Solution (b)**

**Trusted Computing Base (TCB):**
The TCB is the *total set* of hardware, software, and firmware components within a computer system that are responsible for enforcing the security policy. Compromise of any TCB element compromises the *entire* system's security.

Examples of TCB components:
- The **security kernel** of the OS.
- **Firmware** (BIOS/UEFI).
- **TPM** (Trusted Platform Module).
- **Hypervisor** in cloud VMs.

**Reference Monitor Concept (Anderson 1972):**
An *abstract* machine that mediates **every** access of subjects to objects. It is the conceptual heart of the TCB.

**Three Mandatory Properties of a Reference Monitor (must enumerate for full marks):**

1. **Complete Mediation (Tamper-proof check):**
   *Every* access must be validated — no cached or bypassed checks allowed. Justification: a single unchecked access becomes the attacker's entry point.

2. **Isolation (Tamper-proof):**
   The reference monitor itself must be protected from modification — implemented in a separate address space, with hardware support (e.g., ring-0 kernel, Intel VT-x, TPM). Justification: if attackers can modify the monitor, the entire security model collapses.

3. **Verifiability (Small enough):**
   The reference monitor must be small, simple, and formally verifiable. Justification: large codebases contain bugs — only a tiny TCB can be mathematically proven correct.

**Examples in modern systems:**

- **AWS Nitro System** — a separate security chip offloads I/O, reducing the hypervisor TCB to < 1 million lines.
- **Intel SGX** — hardware reference monitor for enclave memory access.
- **SELinux** — software reference monitor in the Linux kernel.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (2024 Batches):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DAC vs MAC** — DAC is *owner-controlled*; MAC is *system-enforced*. Do **not** say "MAC is more secure than DAC" without context.
> 2. **Bell-LaPadula is for confidentiality, NOT integrity** — writing Biba rules in a Bell-LaPadula question is a *zero-mark* mistake.
> 3. **Buffer Overflow diagram must show stack layout** — examiners award marks for the diagram (1–2 marks) and the *steps* (3 marks). Skipping the stack layout = -2 marks.
> 4. **Reference Monitor is an *abstract* concept** — do not confuse it with a "firewall" or "antivirus." It is the *mediator of every access*.
> 5. **In entropy questions**, students often forget to take $\log_2$ and use $\log_{10}$ — read the base carefully.
> 6. **Mention both TCB and Reference Monitor** when asked "explain security kernel" — they are related but distinct concepts.
> 7. **Cloud relevance** — when the question is from a Cloud Computing course, always mention at least *one cloud example* (AWS, Azure, GCP) to earn the application-level marks.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
- [ ] **AAA** — Authentication, Authorization, Auditing
- [ ] **TCB** — Trusted Computing Base
- [ ] **Reference Monitor** — abstract mediator of all subject→object access
- [ ] **Security Kernel** — implementation of the reference monitor
- [ ] **DAC / MAC / RBAC / ABAC** — four access control models
- [ ] **Bell-LaPadula (NRU + NWD)** — confidentiality
- [ ] **Biba (NRD + NWU)** — integrity

### Must-Know Formulas
- [ ] Entropy: $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$
- [ ] Crack time: $T = 2^H / (2R)$
- [ ] Risk: $R = T \times V \times I$
- [ ] Bell-LaPadula read: $\text{level}(S) \ge \text{level}(O)$
- [ ] Biba read: $\text{integrity}(S) \le \text{integrity}(O)$

### Must-Know Attacks
- [ ] **Buffer Overflow** — stack layout, return address overwrite, shellcode
- [ ] **Malware types** — Virus, Worm, Trojan, Rootkit
- [ ] **Defenses** — ASLR, DEP/NX, Stack canaries, Safe languages

### Cloud-Specific Must-Know
- [ ] VM escape attacks target the hypervisor
- [ ] AWS Nitro / Firecracker reduce TCB
- [ ] Confidential VMs use AMD SEV / Intel TDX
- [ ] RBAC is the default in AWS IAM, Azure RBAC
- [ ] SELinux/AppArmor enforce MAC in Linux VMs

### Three Properties of Reference Monitor (high-frequency)
- [ ] **Complete mediation**
- [ ] **Tamper-proof (isolated)**
- [ ] **Verifiable (small)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrastructure Security - Network Level Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Infrastructure Security - Network Level Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Network Level Security — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Level Security** in cloud computing refers to the comprehensive set of policies, architectural patterns, cryptographic mechanisms, and operational controls designed to protect the **data-in-transit**, the **virtualized network fabric**, the **inter-VM/VPC communication channels**, and the **north-south/east-west traffic** traversing the cloud infrastructure from unauthorized access, eavesdropping, modification, and denial-of-service threats.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Ready Formulation):**
> *Network-level security is the layer of the cloud security stack that secures OSI Layers 2–4 (and selectively Layer 7) by combining firewalls, IDS/IPS, VPNs, segmentation, and cryptographic tunneling to enforce confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all communication within, into, and out of the cloud.*

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Network Security (CIA Triad at Network Layer)

| Pillar | Network-Level Mechanism | Cloud Implementation Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Encryption in transit (TLS, IPsec) | AWS VPC traffic mirroring with TLS |
| **Integrity** | Hashing, HMAC, signed packets | IPsec ESP with integrity check |
| **Availability** | Anti-DDoS, rate limiting, redundancy | AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cloud as a Gated Residential Township"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Gated Township Model:**
> Imagine a large residential township (the cloud data center). The roads connecting the township to the city (the public Internet) are protected by **security gates with guards** (Firewalls + IDS). The internal roads between buildings use **private service lanes** (VPCs, Subnets). VIPs travel in **armoured cars with coded routes** (VPN tunnels). Residents carry **identity cards scanned at checkpoints** (Network Access Control / NAC). Thieves trying to flood the gate are **stopped by the perimeter patrol** (DDoS mitigation). The township manager monitors CCTV 24×7 (SIEM + Network Monitoring).
>
> *Just as a township needs layered access control, a cloud needs layered network security — there is no single silver bullet.*

## 1.4 OSI Layer Mapping of Network Security Controls

```
OSI Layer 7 (Application)  →  WAF, API Gateway, TLS termination
OSI Layer 6 (Presentation) →  SSL/TLS, S/MIME
OSI Layer 5 (Session)      →  TLS session resumption, Kerberos
OSI Layer 4 (Transport)    →  TLS, Port-based firewalls, SYN cookies
OSI Layer 3 (Network)      →  IPsec, BGPsec, Network firewalls, IP filtering
OSI Layer 2 (Data Link)    →  MAC filtering, 802.1X, ARP inspection
OSI Layer 1 (Physical)     →  Physically isolated networks, optical taps
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In Module 3, the focus is on **Layers 2–4**, since cloud providers expose a virtualized version of these layers (VPC, Security Groups, Network ACLs) directly to tenants.

## 1.5 Key Threat Categories Addressed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Five" Network-Level Threats (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> 1. **Eavesdropping / Sniffing** (passive interception on shared fabric)
> 2. **Man-in-the-Middle (MitM)** (active session hijacking)
> 3. **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)** (volumetric + protocol + application-layer)
> 4. **IP Spoofing** (forging source addresses to bypass ACLs)
> 5. **Lateral Movement / East-West attacks** (post-compromise VM-to-VM pivoting)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Defence-in-Depth Architecture

Network security in cloud is not a single product — it is a **layered stack**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following six logical layers (from outermost to innermost):

1. **Edge / Perimeter Layer** — Anti-DDoS scrubbing, CDN-based WAF
2. **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** — Public-facing subnets with restrictive ACLs
3. **Internal Segmentation Layer** — Security Groups, Network ACLs, micro-segmentation
4. **Inspection Layer** — IDS/IPS, DPI (Deep Packet Inspection)
5. **Cryptographic Layer** — TLS, IPsec, MACsec
6. **Identity & Access Layer** — NAC, 802.1X, mutual TLS

## 2.2 Firewalls — Stateful vs Stateless

A **firewall** is the foundational network security control. It enforces a security policy by inspecting packet headers (and optionally payloads) and deciding to **forward**, **drop**, or **reject** each packet.

### 2.2.1 Stateless (Packet-Filter) Firewalls

- Operates at **OSI Layer 3/4**.
- Examines each packet **independently** — no memory of prior packets.
- Decision based on a static rule set: (Source IP, Dest IP, Protocol, Src Port, Dst Port, Flags).

### 2.2.2 Stateful Inspection Firewalls

- Maintains a **state table** tracking every active TCP/UDP connection.
- Can correlate ACK packets with their corresponding SYN.
- Blocks unsolicited inbound packets that do not belong to an established session.
- Implemented as a **state transition graph** on the TCP handshake.

### 2.2.3 Next-Generation Firewall (NGFW)

- Combines stateful filtering with **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI)**, **Application Awareness**, and **Intrusion Prevention**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *Stateless = "Amnesiac" (forgets each packet)*; *Stateful = "Elephant" (remembers every connection)*.

## 2.3 Intrusion Detection and Prevention Systems

### 2.3.1 IDS (Intrusion Detection System)

- **Passive** — observes traffic, generates alerts, does **not** drop packets.
- Placed **out-of-band** (typically via SPAN/mirror port).

### 2.3.2 IPS (Intrusion Prevention System)

- **Active** — sits **inline** and can drop, reset, or rate-limit malicious packets in real time.

### 2.3.3 Detection Methodologies

| Method | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|
| **Signature-based (Misuse)** | Low false positives for known attacks | Cannot detect zero-day attacks |
| **Anomaly-based** | Detects novel/zero-day attacks | Higher false-positive rate |
| **Stateful Protocol Analysis** | Understands protocol semantics | Resource intensive |
| **Hybrid (Signature + Anomaly + Behavior)** | Balanced accuracy | Complex to tune |

## 2.4 Virtual Private Networks (VPN)

A **VPN** creates an **encrypted tunnel** over an untrusted network (typically the public Internet), providing **confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity** for the encapsulated traffic.

### 2.4.1 Two Principal Tunneling Protocols

| Protocol | OSI Layer | Encryption | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IPsec (ESP mode)** | Layer 3 (Network) | AES, 3DES | Site-to-site VPN, gateway-to-gateway |
| **SSL/TLS VPN** | Layer 4–7 (Application) | AES-GCM, ChaCha20 | Client-to-gateway, browser-based |
| **L2TP/IPsec** | Layer 2 | Relies on IPsec | Legacy remote access |
| **WireGuard** | Layer 3 | ChaCha20-Poly1305 | Modern, lightweight VPN |

### 2.4.2 IPsec ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) Modes

- **Transport Mode**: Encrypts only the **payload**; original IP header preserved.
- **Tunnel Mode**: Encrypts the **entire original IP packet** and adds a new outer IP header — the standard for site-to-site cloud VPN.

## 2.5 TLS/SSL Handshake — The Transport-Layer Workhorse

TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the de-facto standard for securing HTTP (HTTPS), SMTP, database connections, and cloud APIs.

### 2.5.1 TLS 1.3 Handshake Sequence (Simplified)

1. **Client Hello** — supported cipher suites, key share (X25519 / secp256r1), SNI.
2. **Server Hello + Key Share** — selects cipher suite, sends its key share + certificate.
3. **Encrypted Extensions + Certificate Verify** — server proves possession of private key.
4. **Finished** — both parties derive symmetric session keys.
5. **Application Data** — encrypted with derived AEAD key (e.g., AES-256-GCM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** TLS 1.3 reduced handshake from 2-RTT to **1-RTT**, and supports **0-RTT** resumption.

## 2.6 DDoS — Distributed Denial of Service

A DDoS attack overwhelms a target with traffic from **thousands to millions of compromised machines (botnet)**, exhausting bandwidth, connection state tables, or application resources.

### 2.6.1 The Three DDoS Sub-Categories

| Sub-Category | Target | Volume | Cloud Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric** | Network bandwidth | Gbps–Tbps | Anycast scrubbing, CDN |
| **Protocol (State-exhaustion)** | Firewall / LB state tables | Mbps | SYN cookies, rate limits |
| **Application-layer (L7)** | Web app / API | RPS | Bot detection, JS challenges, WAF |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Numerical & Conceptual Formulas (Save for Last-Minute Revision):**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **TCP connection-state memory** | $M_{state} = C \times S_{avg}$ | Bytes; $C$ = concurrent conns, $S_{avg}$ = avg state entry size (~300 B) |
| 2 | **SYN cookie initial sequence number** | $ISN = H(K_s, src, dst, sport, dport, t)$ | $H$ = hash; $K_s$ = server secret |
| 3 | **Block-cipher encryption throughput** | $T_{enc} = \dfrac{f_{clk} \times c}{b}$ | Mbps; $f_{clk}$ in MHz, $c$ = cycles/block, $b$ = block bits |
| 4 | **AES-256 effective key space** | $2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$ | Keys |
| 5 | **IPsec AH header length** | $L_{AH} = 24$ bytes (fixed) | IPv4 |
| 6 | **IPsec ESP header + trailer + auth** | $L_{ESP} = 10 + P_{pad} + 12$ (min) | Bytes; $P_{pad}$ is payload padding |
| 7 | **TLS record max plaintext** | $2^{14} = 16{,}384$ bytes | TLS 1.2 / 1.3 |
| 8 | **HMAC security strength** | $\min(L_{hash}, L_{key})$ bits | $L_{hash}$ = hash output length |
| 9 | **Volumetric DDoS magnitude** | $V \geq B_{pipe}$ for DoS success | $B_{pipe}$ = target link bandwidth |
| 10 | **Rule lookup complexity (linear ACL)** | $O(n)$ where $n$ = number of ACL rules | Firewall performance bound |
| 11 | **Rule lookup complexity (trie-based)** | $O(W)$ where $W$ = address width (32 or 128 bits) | Modern NGFW |
| 12 | **TLS 1.3 RTT count** | $1\,\text{RTT}$ (full), $0\,\text{RTT}$ (resumption) | Round-trips |
| 13 | **AES block size** | $128\,\text{bits}$ | Constant for AES-128/192/256 |
| 14 | **Diffie-Hellman prime modulus (RFC 3526)** | $2048$ or $4096$ bits | Group 14, Group 15 |
| 15 | **Network latency budget (tolerable)** | $RTT \leq 200\,\text{ms}$ (voice) | ITU-T G.114 |

> **Caution on LaTeX:** In the table above, vertical bars and backslashes are escaped to preserve markdown table integrity. In your answer sheet, write them naturally.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom:** 5G core networks use **network slicing** (conceptually identical to VPC micro-segmentation) with mutual TLS between network functions.
- **Banking:** PCI-DSS mandates **TLS 1.2+** and **network segmentation** between the cardholder data environment and corporate LAN.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Requires **encryption in transit** for PHI and **audit logging** at the network layer.
- **Industrial IoT:** Uses **MACsec (802.1AE)** at Layer 2 to protect fieldbus traffic with sub-microsecond latency.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TCP SYN-Cookie Computation (Defense Against SYN Flood)

A **SYN flood** fills the firewall's state table with half-open connections. **SYN cookies** allow the server to remain stateless until the client returns its acknowledgment.

### Step 1 — Standard Three-Way Handshake (Vulnerable)

The server normally allocates state on receiving SYN:

$$
\text{State} = \{src, dst, sport, dport, seq_c, ISN_{server}\}
$$

If a malicious client sends $N$ SYNs without completing the handshake, the state table fills up — this is a **state-exhaustion DoS**.

### Step 2 — Encode State into the Initial Sequence Number (ISN)

The server computes the ISN deterministically as a function of the connection tuple and a slow-changing secret $K_s$:

$$
ISN = H(K_s, src, dst, sport, dport, t) \bmod 2^{24}
$$

where $H$ is a cryptographic hash (e.g., SHA-1 truncated to 32 bits) and $t$ is a coarse-grained timestamp (to expire old cookies).

### Step 3 — Client Echoes Cookie in ACK

The client responds with $\text{ACK} = ISN + 1$ (per TCP semantics). The server **recomputes** the ISN from the same tuple — if the ACK matches, the connection is legitimate. Only then does the server allocate state.

### Step 4 — Encoded Fields in the Cookie (Daniel Bernstein's Scheme)

$$
\text{Cookie} = \underbrace{t \bmod 32}_{5\,\text{bits}} \,\|\, \underbrace{\text{mss\_idx}}_{3\,\text{bits}} \,\|\, \underbrace{H(K_s, \text{tuple}, t)}_{24\,\text{bits}}
$$

The total encoded length is exactly **32 bits**, fitting inside the standard ISN field.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** SYN cookies disable certain TCP options (e.g., window scaling) because the server has no memory until the ACK arrives. They are a **fallback defense**, not a default.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required AES-256 Key Search Cost

Let us estimate the brute-force resistance of AES-256 used in a cloud VPN.

### Step 1 — Define Keyspace

$$
\mathcal{K} = 2^{256}
$$

### Step 2 — Approximate Decimal Magnitude

$$
2^{256} = (2^{10})^{25.6} = 1024^{25.6} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}
$$

### Step 3 — Assume World's Fastest Supercomputer (Frontier-class, ~10^{18} ops/sec)

$$
T_{bf} = \dfrac{2^{256}}{10^{18}} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{bf} = \dfrac{1.16 \times 10^{77}}{10^{18}} = 1.16 \times 10^{59} \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Years

$$
1 \text{ year} \approx 3.15 \times 10^{7}\,\text{seconds}
$$

$$
T_{bf}^{\text{years}} = \dfrac{1.16 \times 10^{59}}{3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{51}\,\text{years}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The age of the universe is approximately $1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years. AES-256 brute-force is roughly **$2.7 \times 10^{41}$ times** the age of the universe, even at exascale speeds. This is why AES-256 is approved for TOP-SECRET data (NIST CNSA suite).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Network Security Control

| Scenario | Primary Threat | Recommended KTU Control | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two on-prem DCs connecting to AWS VPC | Eavesdropping on public Internet | **IPsec Site-to-Site VPN** | Layer-3 encryption, gateway-anchored, transparent to apps |
| Remote employee accessing internal webmail | Credential theft, session hijack | **TLS 1.3 with mutual certs** | Application-layer, no client software, MFA + mTLS |
| Web API behind a CDN receiving massive traffic | Layer-7 DDoS, SQLi, XSS | **Cloud WAF + Anycast CDN** | Edge scrubbing before origin |
| VMs inside a single VPC reaching each other | Lateral movement after breach | **Security Groups + NACL + VPC Flow Logs** | Micro-segmentation, east-west visibility |
| Compliance with PCI-DSS | Co-mingling of cardholder traffic | **VLAN segmentation + dedicated VPC** | Logical + cryptographic isolation |
| IoT fleet in field | Resource-constrained devices | **MACsec (802.1AE) at switches** | Layer-2 line-rate, hardware-accelerated |
| SaaS multi-tenant platform | Tenant data leakage over shared fabric | **VXLAN overlay + tenant-scoped NSX** | Encapsulation + logical isolation |

## 3.4 Analytical Walk-through: TLS 1.3 Cipher Suite Negotiation

The cipher suite selected in TLS 1.3 has the structure:

$$
\text{cipher\_suite} = \text{TLS\_}\{\text{AEAD}\}\_\{\text{Hash}\}\_\{\text{KeyExchange}\}
$$

**Example 1:** `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384` means:
- AEAD = AES-256 in GCM mode (authenticated encryption with 256-bit key)
- Hash = SHA-384 (used in HKDF for key derivation)
- KeyExchange = implicit (X25519 / secp256r1 via supported_groups)

**Example 2:** `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256` means:
- AEAD = ChaCha20-Poly1305 (stream cipher + Poly1305 MAC)
- Hash = SHA-256
- Often preferred on mobile devices without AES-NI hardware acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Exams:** TLS 1.3 **removed** static RSA key exchange, RC4, MD5, SHA-1, CBC-mode ciphers, and 3DES — answer any "list deprecated TLS 1.2 ciphers" question by referencing this elimination set.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

> Distinguish between a **stateful firewall** and a **stateless firewall**. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Stateless Firewall | Stateful Firewall |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspection** | Examines each packet in isolation | Maintains a state table of active sessions |
| **OSI Layer** | Layer 3 / 4 (header fields) | Layer 3 / 4 with connection tracking |
| **Context** | No memory of prior packets | Tracks TCP handshake state |
| **Performance** | Faster, lower resource use | Slightly higher latency due to state lookup |
| **Security** | Vulnerable to spoofed ACK attacks | Resists IP spoofing and unsolicited packets |

**Advantage – Stateless:** Simpler, faster, ideal for high-throughput filtering on header rules.
**Disadvantage – Stateless:** Cannot inspect or block application-layer or session-aware attacks (e.g., ACK scans).
**Advantage – Stateful:** Provides context-aware filtering, blocks unsolicited inbound packets.
**Disadvantage – Stateful:** Stateful table is itself vulnerable to state-exhaustion DoS if not protected.

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

> List and briefly explain the three main categories of **DDoS attacks**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Volumetric Attacks** — Saturate the target's network bandwidth using UDP floods, ICMP floods, or amplification (e.g., DNS amplification, NTP reflection). Measured in Gbps/Tbps.
2. **Protocol / State-Exhaustion Attacks** — Exhaust the connection-state resources of firewalls, load balancers, or servers. Examples: **SYN flood**, **Ping of Death**, **Smurf attack**.
3. **Application-Layer (L7) Attacks** — Target specific application functionality with low-bandwidth, high-RPS requests. Examples: **HTTP GET/POST flood**, **Slowloris**, **DNS query flood**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Choice — 14 Marks)

> **ESE Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, internal choice within the module. Sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A. [14 Marks]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the architecture and working of **IPsec** in **tunnel mode** with a suitable diagram. Compare it with **transport mode**.

**Model Solution:**

**IPsec Architecture:**

IPsec is a suite of protocols providing **confidentiality, integrity, and authentication** at OSI Layer 3. Its three main components are:

1. **AH (Authentication Header)** — RFC 4302 — provides integrity + authentication, **no encryption**.
2. **ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload)** — RFC 4303 — provides integrity + authentication + **confidentiality (encryption)**.
3. **IKE (Internet Key Exchange)** — RFC 7296 — negotiates Security Associations (SAs) and keys.

**Working in Tunnel Mode (Site-to-Site Cloud VPN):**

**Step 1 — IKE Phase 1 (IKEv2 SA):**
The two VPN gateways authenticate each other (using pre-shared keys, certificates, or EAP) and establish a secure IKE channel (ISAKMP SA). Diffie-Hellman produces a shared secret.

**Step 2 — IKE Phase 2 (IPsec SA / Child SA):**
Negotiate the ESP encryption algorithm (e.g., AES-256-GCM), integrity algorithm (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256), lifetime, and traffic selectors (which subnets to protect).

**Step 3 — Data Plane (ESP Tunnel Mode):**
For every outgoing packet:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inner Packet} &= \text{Original IP} + \text{TCP/UDP} + \text{Payload} \\
\text{Encrypted Payload} &= E_{K_{ESP}}(\text{Inner Packet using AES-GCM}) \\
\text{ESP Packet} &= \text{New Outer IP Header} + \text{ESP Header} + \text{Encrypted Payload} + \text{ESP Trailer} + \text{ESP ICV}
\end{aligned}
$$

The new outer IP header uses the public IP addresses of the two VPN gateways, hiding the original private source/destination.

**Step 4 — Decapsulation at Remote Gateway:**
The remote VPN gateway removes the outer header, decrypts using the negotiated key, and forwards the original packet to the destination VM in the destination VPC.

**Tunnel Mode vs Transport Mode (Comparison Table — 4 marks in the 7-m subpart):**

| Feature | Transport Mode | Tunnel Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **IP Header** | Original IP header preserved | New outer IP header added |
| **Encryption Scope** | Only payload | Entire original IP packet |
| **Use Case** | Host-to-host, end-to-end | Gateway-to-gateway, site-to-site |
| **Header Overhead** | ~24 bytes (AH/ESP) | ~24 + outer IP = ~44 bytes |
| **Visibility** | Internal IPs visible to transit routers | Internal IPs hidden from Internet |
| **Cloud Use** | VM-to-VM within same trust zone | Cross-region, on-prem-to-cloud |

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Describe the working of **TLS 1.3 handshake** with a neat sequence diagram. Highlight the key improvements over TLS 1.2.

**Model Solution:**

**TLS 1.3 Handshake Steps:**

1. **Client → ServerHello:** The client sends its supported cipher suites, supported groups (e.g., X25519, secp256r1), **key share** (one-shot DH public value), and SNI.
2. **Server → ServerHello + EncryptedExtensions + Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished:** The server selects a cipher suite, sends its **key share**, certificate, and a **CertificateVerify** signed with its private key (proves ownership). The server then sends a Finished message authenticating the entire handshake transcript.
3. **Client → Finished + Application Data:** The client verifies the server's certificate, sends its own Finished, and immediately begins sending encrypted application data.

**Total Round Trips:** **1-RTT** (compared to 2-RTT in TLS 1.2).

**Key Improvements over TLS 1.2:**

| Improvement | TLS 1.2 | TLS 1.3 |
|---|---|---|
| **RTT** | 2-RTT (full handshake) | 1-RTT |
| **Resumption** | Session ID / Session Ticket (separate) | 0-RTT with PSK |
| **Cipher Suites** | Many, including weak (RC4, 3DES) | Only AEAD (AES-GCM, AES-CCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) |
| **Key Exchange** | Static RSA, DH, ECDH | Only (EC)DHE — forward secrecy mandatory |
| **Encryption** | Handshake is plaintext | Handshake encrypted after ServerHello |
| **Hashing** | MD5, SHA-1 allowed | SHA-256 or stronger only |

**Significance for Cloud:** Reduced latency, mandatory forward secrecy, and removal of legacy ciphers align TLS 1.3 with **zero-trust** and **NIST CNSA 2.0** mandates.

---

### **Question B. [14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain **IDS vs IPS** with architecture. Compare **signature-based** and **anomaly-based** detection methods.

**Model Solution:**

**IDS (Intrusion Detection System):**
- **Deployment:** Out-of-band via SPAN / mirror port or TAP.
- **Action:** Passive — generates alerts and logs to SIEM, does **not block** traffic.
- **Use Case:** Forensic, compliance, post-hoc analysis.

**IPS (Intrusion Prevention System):**
- **Deployment:** Inline on the data path.
- **Action:** Active — can **drop**, **reset**, or **rate-limit** malicious packets in real time.
- **Use Case:** Active perimeter defense for known and signatured attacks.

**Detection Methodologies Comparison (5 marks in the 7-mark subpart):**

| Criterion | Signature-Based | Anomaly-Based |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Match packet against known attack patterns (e.g., Snort rules) | Compare traffic against a learned "normal" baseline |
| **Data Source** | Rules database, threat-intel feeds | Statistical models, ML, behavior profiles |
| **Zero-Day Detection** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (if behavior deviates) |
| **False Positives** | Low | Higher (anomalies ≠ attacks) |
| **False Negatives** | Higher (variant evasion) | Lower for novel attacks |
| **Training** | None | Requires baseline training period |
| **Examples** | Snort, Suricata (signature mode) | Zeek, Darktrace, AWS GuardDuty |

**Hybrid Approach:** Modern systems (e.g., Suricata with ET Pro rulesets + Zeek logs + ML scoring) combine both — signatures for known threats, anomaly engines for novel threats.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — Describe **defense-in-depth** strategy for a cloud VPC. Include at least **five** distinct network-level controls in your answer.

**Model Solution:**

A **defense-in-depth** strategy assumes no single control is sufficient; multiple overlapping layers must be breached to compromise the workload. The following five layers are essential for a cloud VPC:

1. **Perimeter / Edge Anti-DDoS**
   - Cloud-provider managed DDoS protection (AWS Shield Advanced, Azure DDoS Protection).
   - Anycast-based CDN to absorb volumetric attacks at edge PoPs.

2. **Web Application Firewall (WAF)**
   - Layer-7 inspection for OWASP Top 10 (SQLi, XSS, CSRF).
   - Bot management, rate limiting per session, geo-fencing.

3. **Network Firewall (NGFW) + Security Groups**
   - VPC Security Groups (stateful, instance-level) for east-west segmentation.
   - Network ACLs (stateless, subnet-level) for coarse ingress/egress filtering.
   - Centralized NGFW (e.g., AWS Network Firewall, Palo Alto VM-Series) for DPI.

4. **Encryption in Transit**
   - TLS 1.3 for north-south API traffic.
   - IPsec / WireGuard for site-to-site and inter-VPC links.
   - mTLS for service-mesh east-west traffic (Istio, Linkerd).

5. **Intrusion Detection / Prevention**
   - IDS via VPC Traffic Mirroring → Suricata / Zeek → SIEM.
   - IPS inline at critical choke points.

6. **Identity-Aware Microsegmentation (Bonus Layer)**
   - Zero-trust NAC: every packet authenticated, not just at the perimeter.
   - Identity-based policies (e.g., AWS IAM + VPC + Security Group tags).

**Architecture Diagram (in words):**
Public Internet → Cloud Edge Anti-DDoS → CDN → WAF → Internet-facing NLB → DMZ Subnet (Bastion, NAT) → NGFW → Private Subnet (App Tier with Security Groups) → Database Subnet (NACL-restricted) → KMS-managed encryption throughout.

**Why this satisfies defense-in-depth:** An attacker must simultaneously bypass DDoS scrubbing, WAF rules, NGFW inspection, microsegmented SGs, and NACLs — and decrypt the traffic — to reach sensitive data. Any single failure is contained by the other layers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing IDS with IPS** — IDS is *passive and alert-only*; IPS is *inline and can block*. Examiners explicitly deduct 1–2 marks if these are swapped.
> 2. **Writing "SSL is more secure than TLS"** — **TLS is the successor to SSL.** SSL 3.0 has been deprecated since 2015 (POODLE attack). Never use the term "SSL" in current-generation answers.
> 3. **Mixing up AH and ESP** — AH = integrity only; ESP = integrity + **confidentiality**. This is a 2-mark trap.
> 4. **Forgetting that IPsec operates at Layer 3, TLS at Layer 4–7** — a 1-mark question is often asked on this exact mapping.
> 5. **Stating that "firewalls provide complete security"** — they do not. Defense-in-depth is the correct answer for any "are firewalls enough" question.
> 6. **Skipping the State Table in Stateful Firewall answers** — examiners award 2 marks specifically for explaining the state-transition concept (SYN → SYN-ACK → ESTABLISHED).
> 7. **Using "VPN = encryption" without naming the tunneling protocol** — always specify IPsec, WireGuard, or TLS-based.
> 8. **TLS 1.2 vs 1.3** — answer must mention 0-RTT/1-RTT, AEAD-only, mandatory (EC)DHE.
> 9. **DDoS = "hacking the server"** — No. DDoS is **resource exhaustion**, not exploitation. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this misconception.
> 10. **Drawing Security Groups and NACLs as identical** — SGs are **stateful + instance-level**; NACLs are **stateless + subnet-level**. This 2-mark differentiation is a favorite.

---

> **High-Density Bullet Recap — Print and Revise 30 Minutes Before Exam:**

- [x] **Network-level security** protects Layers 2–4 of the OSI stack within a cloud VPC.
- [x] **CIA triad at network layer:** Confidentiality (encryption), Integrity (MAC/HMAC), Availability (DDoS defense).
- [x] **Stateless firewall** → packet-by-packet, no memory, fast but context-blind.
- [x] **Stateful firewall** → maintains connection state table, blocks unsolicited packets.
- [x] **NGFW** → stateful + DPI + application awareness + IPS.
- [x] **IDS** → passive, out-of-band, alerts only; **IPS** → inline, can drop/reset.
- [x] **Signature detection** → known patterns, low FP, blind to zero-days.
- [x] **Anomaly detection** → statistical/ML, catches zero-days, higher FP.
- [x] **IPsec ESP tunnel mode** → encrypts entire inner IP packet, new outer header; standard for site-to-site cloud VPN.
- [x] **IPsec transport mode** → encrypts only payload, original IP header preserved.
- [x] **AH** → integrity only; **ESP** → integrity + encryption.
- [x] **IKEv2** → modern key exchange; supports MOBIKE, NAT traversal, EAP.
- [x] **TLS 1.3** → 1-RTT handshake, 0-RTT resumption, AEAD-only ciphers, mandatory (EC)DHE forward secrecy.
- [x] **TLS 1.3 cipher suites:** `TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384`, `TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256`, `TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`.
- [x] **VPN types:** Site-to-site (IPsec), Remote access (TLS / WireGuard), Clientless (SSL VPN portal).
- [x] **DDoS — 3 categories:** Volumetric (bandwidth), Protocol (state), Application (L7).
- [x] **SYN flood mitigation:** SYN cookies — ISN encodes state, no allocation until ACK.
- [x] **AES-256 keyspace:** $2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$ — infeasible brute force.
- [x] **VPC Security Groups** → stateful, instance-level, default-deny inbound.
- [x] **Network ACLs** → stateless, subnet-level, rule-numbered, evaluated in order.
- [x] **VPC Flow Logs** → metadata of accepted/rejected traffic; feed SIEM.
- [x] **Defense-in-depth** → multiple overlapping layers: Edge, WAF, NGFW, SGs, NACLs, IDS/IPS, mTLS.
- [x] **Zero-trust principle** → "never trust, always verify" — microsegmentation + identity-based policies.
- [x] **Compliance hooks:** PCI-DSS (segmentation), HIPAA (encryption), GDPR (data protection), NIST SP 800-53.
- [x] **Top KTU 14-marker blueprint:** (a) Explain protocol + (b) Compare/contrast with diagram. Always include a labeled Mermaid/text block diagram — marks reserved for it.
- [x] **Last-mile tip:** Memorize the **6-layer defense stack** order: *Edge → DMZ → NGFW → SGs/NACLs → IDS/IPS → mTLS/KMS*.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Infrastructure Security: Network Level Security**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Host Level Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Host Level Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Host Level Security (HLS)** in cloud computing refers to the comprehensive set of protective measures, controls, policies, and tools applied at the individual host machine level (physical server, virtual machine, or container) to safeguard the operating system, applications, data, and underlying virtualization layer from unauthorized access, malicious attacks, privilege escalation, and configuration drift.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus (PEITT523)**, Host Level Security is positioned as the **innermost defensive ring** of the cloud security stack, sitting beneath network security and identity/access management. It is the last line of defense when perimeter controls fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor:** Host Level Security explicitly covers — *VM security, OS hardening, host-based firewalls, Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (HIDS/HIPS), anti-malware protection, patch management, and trusted computing concepts (TPM, Secure Boot, TCB).*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **apartment complex** (the cloud data center):

- The **outer gate with security guards** = Network Security (firewalls, DMZ).
- The **lobby access card** = Identity & Access Management (IAM).
- The **individual apartment door with a personal lock, alarm, and CCTV inside the room** = **Host Level Security**.

Even if someone gets past the gate and the lobby, every individual apartment (host/VM) has its **own lock, alarm, motion sensor, and safe** for valuables. Host Level Security is exactly this — protecting what is *inside* the room, not the building perimeter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea (One-liner for exam):** *Host Level Security = Securing the operating environment of each individual machine in the cloud, so that a compromise of one host does not propagate to the entire cloud infrastructure.*

## 1.3 Why Host Level Security is Critical in Cloud

Cloud environments are **multi-tenant**. A single physical host often runs VMs belonging to multiple customers. A breach at the host level can:

1. Allow **VM escape** — breaking out of one VM to access the hypervisor and other VMs.
2. Lead to **data exfiltration** from the host's local storage.
3. Enable **lateral movement** across the cloud tenant network.
4. Cause **denial of service** by crashing the host or hypervisor.

Hence, the **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) must be enforced *per host*.

| CIA Component | Host-Level Implementation |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Full-disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS), file-level ACLs, SELinux policies |
| **Integrity** | Secure Boot, TPM measured boot, file integrity monitoring (AIDE, Tripwire) |
| **Availability** | Host-based anti-DDoS, resource quotas, patch management, HIDS alerts |

## 1.4 Threat Landscape at the Host Level

> [!WARNING]
> The most common KTU exam pitfall is to confuse **Host Security** with **Network Security**. Remember: **Host security deals with the inside of the machine (OS, apps, logs, files), not the network traffic in transit.**

Common host-level threats include:

- **Malware** (viruses, worms, ransomware, rootkits)
- **Privilege Escalation** attacks
- **Unpatched software vulnerabilities** (CVEs)
- **Misconfigurations** (open ports, default passwords, world-writable files)
- **Insider threats** via SSH/RDP brute force
- **VM Escape & Hyperjacking**
- **Bootkits & Firmware rootkits**

## 1.5 Core Components of Host Level Security (Overview)

The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies the following mandatory sub-topics under Host Level Security:

1. **Operating System (OS) Hardening** — disabling unused services, removing unnecessary software, enforcing least privilege.
2. **Host Firewalls** — software firewalls like `iptables`, `nftables` (Linux), Windows Defender Firewall.
3. **Host-Based Intrusion Detection/Prevention Systems (HIDS/HIPS)** — OSSEC, Tripwire, AIDE, Samhain.
4. **Anti-Malware / Antivirus Solutions** — signature-based, heuristic, and behavior-based detection.
5. **Patch & Configuration Management** — timely application of security updates (yum, apt, WSUS, SCCM).
6. **Trusted Computing Base (TCB)** — Secure Boot, TPM, Measured Boot.
7. **Virtual Machine (VM) Security** — VM isolation, VM escape prevention, hypervisor hardening.
8. **Logging, Auditing & Monitoring** — Syslog, Windows Event Logs, SIEM integration.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the 8 components:** **"H-A-P-V-L-S-O-T"** → **H**ardening, **A**nti-malware, **P**atch Mgmt, **V**M Security, **L**ogging, **S**ecure Boot/TPM, **O**S Firewall, **T**CB. (Restructure as: Hardening, Anti-malware, Patch, VM, Logging, Secure Boot, OS Firewall, TCB).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of Host Level Security

Host security is implemented as a **defense-in-depth** stack, layered from the hardware (bottom) to the application (top). Each layer protects the one above it.

| Layer (Bottom → Top) | Security Control | Purpose | Example Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Hardware/Firmware** | Secure Boot, TPM, BIOS password | Ensure trusted boot chain | UEFI Secure Boot, TPM 2.0 |
| **L2 — Hypervisor** | Hypervisor hardening, VM isolation | Prevent VM escape | ESXi lockdown, KVM SELinux |
| **L3 — Operating System** | OS hardening, SELinux, AppArmor | Enforce least privilege | CIS Benchmarks, sudo policies |
| **L4 — Network (Host-side)** | Host firewall, TCP wrappers | Filter host-bound traffic | iptables, nftables, firewalld |
| **L5 — Application** | Application sandboxing, anti-malware | Protect user-space processes | ClamAV, Windows Defender, OSSEC |
| **L6 — Data** | Encryption at rest, ACLs | Protect data confidentiality | LUKS, BitLocker, dm-crypt |
| **L7 — Audit/Monitoring** | Logging, SIEM, integrity checkers | Detect and respond to incidents | AIDE, Tripwire, Splunk, ELK |

## 2.2 Theoretical Deep Dive — Sub-Topic by Sub-Topic

### 2.2.1 Operating System Hardening

**Definition:** The process of reducing the attack surface of an OS by removing unnecessary components, disabling unused services, and applying secure configuration baselines.

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Disable unused services** — e.g., disable `telnetd`, `ftp`, unused daemons.
2. **Remove unnecessary software** — minimize installed packages.
3. **Apply security baselines** — CIS Benchmarks, DISA STIGs.
4. **Enforce strong authentication** — disable root login via SSH, use key-based auth.
5. **Configure Mandatory Access Control (MAC)** — SELinux (Red Hat), AppArmor (Ubuntu).
6. **Set file permissions strictly** — principle of least privilege (PoLP).

> [!NOTE]
> **CIS Benchmarks** are consensus-based, vendor-agnostic security configuration guides. They are the **de-facto standard** referenced in KTU exam answers when discussing OS hardening.

### 2.2.2 Host-Based Firewalls

A host firewall filters traffic destined **to or from a single host**, unlike a network firewall that protects a subnet.

**Linux iptables — Packet Flow Logic:**

$$ \text{Packet} \rightarrow \text{Tables} \rightarrow \text{Chains} \rightarrow \text{Target/Action} $$

The three built-in tables:

| Table | Purpose | Built-in Chains |
|---|---|---|
| **filter** | Default packet filtering | INPUT, OUTPUT, FORWARD |
| **nat** | Network Address Translation | PREROUTING, POSTROUTING, OUTPUT |
| **mangle** | Packet header modification | PREROUTING, INPUT, FORWARD, OUTPUT, POSTROUTING |

**Example rule logic:**

$$
\text{iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -s 10.0.0.0/24 -j ACCEPT}
$$

This **APPENDS** a rule to the **INPUT chain** allowing **TCP port 22** (SSH) only from the `10.0.0.0/24` subnet.

### 2.2.3 Host-Based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)

**HIDS** monitors the internals of a computing system — file system, system calls, log files, registry — to detect malicious activity.

**Two Main Detection Paradigms:**

1. **Signature-Based Detection:**
   - Compares observed events against a database of known attack patterns (signatures).
   - High accuracy for known threats, **zero-day blind**.

2. **Anomaly-Based Detection:**
   - Builds a statistical model of "normal" behavior; flags deviations.
   - Can detect zero-day attacks, but **higher false-positive rate**.

**Common HIDS Tools:**

| Tool | Platform | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **OSSEC** | Cross-platform | Open-source, log-based, file integrity |
| **AIDE** | Linux | File integrity checker (Advanced Intrusion Detection Environment) |
| **Tripwire** | Linux/Windows | Commercial-grade FIM (File Integrity Monitor) |
| **Samhain** | Linux | Centralized HIDS, stealth mode |
| **Wazuh** | Cross-platform | Fork of OSSEC, modern UI, SIEM integration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** A **Host-based Intrusion Prevention System (HIPS)** is a HIDS with the **additional capability to actively block** the detected threat, not merely alert on it.

### 2.2.4 Anti-Malware Protection

Modern anti-malware at the host level uses **multi-layered detection**:

1. **Signature-based** — pattern matching against malware database.
2. **Heuristic-based** — code analysis, suspicious API calls, packing detection.
3. **Behavioral-based** — runtime monitoring (e.g., ransomware encryption behavior).
4. **Sandboxing** — executing suspect files in an isolated environment.
5. **Cloud-assisted lookups** — hash reputation checks against threat intelligence feeds.

**Detection Rate Formula (commonly cited in academic literature):**

$$
\text{Detection Rate (DR)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $TP$ = True Positives (malware correctly identified)
- $FN$ = False Negatives (malware missed)

**False Positive Rate:**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} \times 100\%
$$

A **trade-off** exists: tightening detection (lower FN) usually increases false positives (higher FP). This is the **ROC curve** trade-off.

### 2.2.5 Patch Management

The **patch management lifecycle**:

$$
\text{Assess} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Deploy} \rightarrow \text{Verify} \rightarrow \text{Document}
$$

**Critical concept — Mean Time to Patch (MTTP):**

$$
MTTP = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_{\text{deploy},i} - T_{\text{release},i})
$$

Where $N$ is the number of patches in a measurement window. Lower MTTP = better security posture.

**Vulnerability Window:**

$$
T_{\text{vulnerable}} = T_{\text{patch}} - T_{\text{CVE\_published}}
$$

The longer $T_{\text{vulnerable}}$, the greater the risk of exploitation.

### 2.2.6 Trusted Computing Base (TCB) & Secure Boot

**TCB Definition:** The set of all hardware, firmware, and software components that are critical to security — a breach of any TCB component compromises the entire system.

**TCB Equation (formal):**

$$
\text{System is Trusted} \iff \text{All TCB components function as specified}
$$

**Secure Boot Chain:**

$$
\text{BIOS/UEFI} \rightarrow \text{Bootloader} \rightarrow \text{Kernel} \rightarrow \text{Init/Services} \rightarrow \text{User Apps}
$$

Each stage **cryptographically verifies** the next before execution. This is called the **Chain of Trust**.

**Trusted Platform Module (TPM):**

- A dedicated **cryptoprocessor** (chip) on the motherboard.
- Performs **Measured Boot**: each firmware/software component is hashed and stored in TPM Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs).
- Supports **sealing**: data can be encrypted such that it only unlocks if specific PCR values are present (i.e., boot state is trusted).

> [!NOTE]
> **TPM 2.0** is mandatory for **Windows 11** and is the standard for cloud server security in modern data centers.

### 2.2.7 Virtual Machine (VM) Security

VM-level security concerns three layers:

1. **VM Image Security** — golden images should be hardened, scanned for malware, and signed.
2. **VM Isolation** — enforced by the hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V).
3. **VM Escape Prevention** — preventing a guest from accessing the hypervisor or other guests.

**Hypervisor Trust Zones:**

| Type | Example | Host-Level Security Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 (Bare-metal)** | VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V | Direct hardware access — compromise is severe |
| **Type 2 (Hosted)** | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation | Relies on host OS — host hardening is critical |

> [!WARNING]
> **KVM/QEMU** uses **SELinux + sVirt** to enforce Mandatory Access Control between VMs, providing **per-VM isolation labels** (e.g., `system_u:system_r:svirt_t:s0:c1,c2`).

### 2.2.8 Logging, Auditing & Monitoring

**Linux Logging Hierarchy:**

- `/var/log/messages` — general system logs
- `/var/log/auth.log` — authentication events
- `/var/log/secure` — security events (Red Hat)
- `/var/log/syslog` — system log daemon
- `journalctl` — systemd journal

**Windows Event Logs:**

- **System Log** — OS component events
- **Application Log** — application events
- **Security Log** — logon, privilege use, resource access

**SIEM Integration:**

$$
\text{Host Logs} \xrightarrow{\text{forwarder}} \text{SIEM} \rightarrow \text{Correlation} \rightarrow \text{Alerting}
$$

Tools: **Splunk, IBM QRadar, Elastic SIEM, Wazuh, Microsoft Sentinel**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Detection Rate (DR)** | $DR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN} \times 100$ | Percentage, $0\%$ to $100\%$ |
| 2 | **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 3 | **Accuracy** | $Acc = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 4 | **Precision** | $P = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Ratio, $0$ to $1$ |
| 5 | **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $R = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Ratio, $0$ to $1$ |
| 6 | **F1-Score** | $F1 = 2 \times \dfrac{P \times R}{P + R}$ | Ratio, $0$ to $1$ |
| 7 | **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP)** | $MTTP = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{deploy,i}-T_{release,i})$ | Days or Hours |
| 8 | **Vulnerability Window** | $T_{vuln} = T_{patch} - T_{CVE}$ | Days |
| 9 | **TCB Security** | $\text{System Trusted} \iff \forall c \in TCB, c \text{ is intact}$ | Logical equivalence |
| 10 | **Chain of Trust (Boot)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} V_i = 1$ where $V_i$ = verification at stage $i$ | All $V_i$ must be $1$ (valid) |
| 11 | **iptables Rule** | $\text{Action} = f(\text{Table, Chain, Match, Target})$ | Boolean decision |
| 12 | **AES-256 Key Length** | $k = 256 \text{ bits}$ | Standard for full-disk encryption |
| 13 | **TPM PCR Count** | $n \geq 24$ registers (TPM 2.0 spec) | Hardware-defined |
| 14 | **OWASP Risk** | $Risk = Likelihood \times Impact$ | Categorical (Low/Med/High) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Detection Rate, F1-Score, and MTTP are **board-evaluated** for HIDS and patch management questions. **TCB integrity** and **Chain of Trust** appear in questions on Secure Boot / TPM.

## 2.4 Defense-in-Depth at the Host Level

The **defense-in-depth** principle means that if one control fails, others still protect the host. Host level security is a **microcosm** of this principle:

$$
\text{Sec}_{\text{host}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} C_i
$$

Where $C_i$ = an individual control (firewall, HIDS, anti-malware, encryption, etc.). The **union** represents the combined security posture. A single failure of $C_j$ does not collapse the entire defense, provided $n \geq 2$ and controls are **diverse (not redundant)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive derivations, worked numerical examples, and analytical walkthroughs — the kind expected in KTU 14-mark Part B questions.

## 3.1 Derivation: Trade-off Between Detection Rate and False Positive Rate

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A host-based anti-malware engine was tested against a curated dataset of **1000 files**, of which **200 are confirmed malware** and **800 are benign**. The engine produces the following confusion matrix:

|  | **Predicted Malware** | **Predicted Benign** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Malware** | $TP = 170$ | $FN = 30$ | $200$ |
| **Actual Benign** | $FP = 20$ | $TN = 780$ | $800$ |
| **Total** | $190$ | $810$ | $1000$ |

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation of All Metrics

**Step 1 — Detection Rate (Recall / True Positive Rate):**

$$
DR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{170}{170 + 30} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
DR = 0.85 \times 100\% = \mathbf{85\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** The anti-malware correctly catches **85 out of every 100 malware samples**.

**Step 2 — False Positive Rate (FPR):**

$$
FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{20}{20 + 780} = \frac{20}{800} = 0.025
$$

Converting to percentage:

$$
FPR = 0.025 \times 100\% = \mathbf{2.5\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **2.5 out of 100 benign files** are incorrectly flagged as malware.

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$
P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{170}{170 + 20} = \frac{170}{190} = 0.8947
$$

$$
P \approx \mathbf{89.47\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{170 + 780}{1000} = \frac{950}{1000} = \mathbf{95\%}
$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{P \times R}{P + R} = 2 \times \frac{0.8947 \times 0.85}{0.8947 + 0.85}
$$

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{0.7605}{1.7447} = 2 \times 0.4360
$$

$$
F1 \approx \mathbf{0.8720}
$$

### 3.1.3 Conclusion of the Worked Example

> The host-based anti-malware has a **high detection rate (85%)**, a **low false positive rate (2.5%)**, and a **strong F1-score of 0.872**, indicating a well-tuned detection engine. This is the type of numerical analysis expected in KTU 14-mark questions on HIDS/anti-malware effectiveness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Time to Patch (MTTP) and Vulnerability Window

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A cloud provider tracks **4 critical CVEs** released in Q1 2025. The release dates and patch deployment dates are:

| CVE | $T_{\text{release}}$ | $T_{\text{patch}}$ | $T_{\text{vuln}}$ (days) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CVE-2025-001 | Jan 05 | Jan 09 | 4 |
| CVE-2025-002 | Jan 22 | Feb 01 | 10 |
| CVE-2025-003 | Feb 14 | Feb 18 | 4 |
| CVE-2025-004 | Mar 03 | Mar 15 | 12 |

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Compute individual vulnerability windows:**

$$
T_{\text{vuln},1} = 9 - 5 = 4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_{\text{vuln},2} = 32 - 22 = 10 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_{\text{vuln},3} = 49 - 45 = 4 \text{ days}
$$

$$
T_{\text{vuln},4} = 74 - 62 = 12 \text{ days}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Mean Time to Patch (MTTP):**

$$
MTTP = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{\text{vuln},i} = \frac{1}{4} (4 + 10 + 4 + 12)
$$

$$
MTTP = \frac{30}{4} = \mathbf{7.5 \text{ days}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Standard Deviation (for completeness):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_i - MTTP)^2}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(4-7.5)^2 + (10-7.5)^2 + (4-7.5)^2 + (12-7.5)^2}{4}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{12.25 + 6.25 + 12.25 + 20.25}{4}} = \sqrt{\frac{51}{4}} = \sqrt{12.75}
$$

$$
\sigma \approx \mathbf{3.57 \text{ days}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Engineering Interpretation

> An **MTTP of 7.5 days** for critical CVEs is acceptable under PCI-DSS guidelines (which require **30 days** for critical patches). However, the **standard deviation of 3.57 days** indicates inconsistent patching speed, suggesting a need to **automate patch deployment**.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** Microsoft's Patch Tuesday cadence targets an MTTP of **≤ 7 days** for enterprise customers. The AWS Inspector and Azure Update Management services aim to push this lower through automation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Chain of Trust Verification Probability

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

In a Secure Boot chain of $n = 5$ stages (BIOS → Bootloader → Kernel → Init → Service), suppose each stage has an **independent verification success probability** $p_i$, and a failure at any stage **halts the boot** (security event).

We want to find:
1. The probability that **the entire boot chain succeeds**.
2. The probability that a **trusted state is reached**.

### 3.3.2 Mathematical Model

Since verification stages are **independent** and **all must pass** for trusted boot:

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

Assume each stage has a verification success probability of $p_i = 0.99$ (i.e., a hardware root-of-trust is highly reliable).

**Step 1 — Compute for 5 stages:**

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = (0.99)^5
$$

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = 0.99^5 = 0.95099
$$

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) \approx \mathbf{95.1\%}
$$

**Step 2 — The probability of boot failure (a security event):**

$$
P(\text{Failure}) = 1 - P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = 1 - 0.95099
$$

$$
P(\text{Failure}) \approx \mathbf{4.9\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity Analysis — What if we have 10 stages?**

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot})_{10} = (0.99)^{10} = 0.9044
$$

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot})_{10} \approx \mathbf{90.44\%}
$$

### 3.3.3 Conclusion

> As the **chain of trust grows longer**, even a high per-stage reliability of 99% causes the overall trusted boot probability to **decline rapidly**. This is a key reason why modern systems use **hardware root-of-trust (TPM)** for the early stages, ensuring very high $p_i$ (often $p_i \geq 0.9999$).

This derivation is critical for **KTE 14-mark questions on Secure Boot and TPM**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Host Security Tools

| Tool | Type | Detection Method | Real-Time? | Open-Source? | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **OSSEC** | HIDS | Log + File Integrity | Yes | Yes | Multi-host centralized monitoring |
| **AIDE** | FIM | File hash comparison | No (scheduled) | Yes | File integrity assurance |
| **Tripwire** | FIM | File hash + policy | Yes (Commercial) | No (Commercial) | Compliance-driven environments |
| **ClamAV** | Anti-Malware | Signature-based | Yes (on-access scan) | Yes | Mail/file servers |
| **Windows Defender** | Anti-Malware | Signature + Heuristic + Behavior + Cloud | Yes | Bundled (Windows) | Windows hosts |
| **SELinux** | MAC | Policy-based access control | Yes (kernel-enforced) | Yes | RHEL/CentOS hosts |
| **AppArmor** | MAC | Path-based access control | Yes (kernel-enforced) | Yes | Ubuntu/SUSE hosts |
| **iptables/nftables** | Firewall | Rule-based packet filter | Yes | Yes | Linux host traffic filtering |
| **Wazuh** | HIDS + SIEM | Log + FIM + Vulnerability | Yes | Yes | Modern cloud SOC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Heuristic:** If a question mentions **"file integrity"**, the answer is **AIDE/Tripwire**. If it mentions **"centralized log monitoring with file integrity"**, the answer is **OSSEC/Wazuh**. If it mentions **"mandatory access control in Linux"**, the answer is **SELinux/AppArmor**.

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: HIDS vs NIDS

| Parameter | **HIDS (Host-based)** | **NIDS (Network-based)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Monitoring Scope** | Inside a single host (files, logs, syscalls) | Network traffic on a segment |
| **Visibility** | High (sees encrypted traffic after decryption, host logs) | Limited (cannot see inside encrypted payloads) |
| **Deployment** | Per host (agent-based) | Strategic network chokepoints |
| **Encrypted Traffic Analysis** | Yes (post-decryption) | No (unless using SSL inspection) |
| **Resource Overhead** | Higher (per host) | Lower (per network) |
| **Insider Threat Detection** | Strong | Weak |
| **Lateral Movement Detection** | Strong | Moderate |
| **Zero-Day Detection (Anomaly)** | Possible (behavioral baselines) | Possible (statistical traffic analysis) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Layered Host Security Architecture (Defense-in-Depth)

This Mermaid diagram shows the **defense-in-depth layers** of host level security, from hardware (bottom) to data (top). Each layer protects the one above it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    L7["L7 AUDIT AND MONITORING LAYER"]:::top
    L6["L6 DATA PROTECTION LAYER"]:::data
    L5["L5 APPLICATION SECURITY LAYER"]:::app
    L4["L4 HOST NETWORK FIREWALL LAYER"]:::net
    L3["L3 OPERATING SYSTEM HARDENING LAYER"]:::os
    L2["L2 HYPERVISOR AND VM ISOLATION LAYER"]:::hyper
    L1["L1 HARDWARE AND FIRMWARE ROOT OF TRUST LAYER"]:::hw

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7

    classDef hw fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hyper fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef os fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef net fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef app fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef data fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef top fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The arrows point *upward* in the sense of "protects the layer above." A failure at L1 compromises the entire chain, while a failure at L5 is contained within the application layer.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Secure Boot Chain of Trust (TPM + Measured Boot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["CRYPTO VERIFY 0: HARDWARE ROOT OF TRUST - TPM 2.0"]:::root
    B["CRYPTO VERIFY 1: UEFI FIRMWARE"]:::fw
    C["CRYPTO VERIFY 2: BOOTLOADER GRUB2"]:::bl
    D["CRYPTO VERIFY 3: KERNEL IMAGE VMLINUZ"]:::kern
    E["CRYPTO VERIFY 4: INIT SYSTEM SYSTEMD"]:::init
    F["CRYPTO VERIFY 5: USERSPACE SERVICES"]:::user

    A -->|"PCR0 measurement"| B
    B -->|"PCR1 measurement"| C
    C -->|"PCR2 measurement"| D
    D -->|"PCR3 measurement"| E
    E -->|"PCR4 measurement"| F

    classDef root fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef fw fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bl fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef kern fill:#92d050,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef init fill:#00b050,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef user fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Explanation:** Each stage is hashed and stored in a TPM Platform Configuration Register (PCR). If any stage is tampered with, the hash changes, the PCR value mismatches the expected value, and boot is halted.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: VM Isolation using sVirt/SELinux Labels (KVM/QEMU)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HostOS["HOST OPERATING SYSTEM - LINUX KVM"]
        HV["KVM HYPERVISOR - QEMU LIBVIRT"]:::hyper
        SEL["SELINUX MAC POLICY ENGINE"]:::policy
    end

    subgraph VM1["VM1 - TENANT A"]
        PROC1["PROCESS INSIDE VM1"]:::vma
    end

    subgraph VM2["VM2 - TENANT B"]
        PROC2["PROCESS INSIDE VM2"]:::vmb
    end

    subgraph VM3["VM3 - TENANT C"]
        PROC3["PROCESS INSIDE VM3"]:::vmc
    end

    HV -->|"launches with sVirt label s0 c1"| PROC1
    HV -->|"launches with sVirt label s0 c2"| PROC2
    HV -->|"launches with sVirt label s0 c3"| PROC3

    SEL -.->|"enforces MAC: c1 cannot access c2 or c3"| HV

    classDef hyper fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef policy fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef vma fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef vmb fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef vmc fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

**Explanation:** KVM uses **sVirt** (SELinux + virtualization) to assign **unique MCS labels** (`c1`, `c2`, `c3`) to each VM. The SELinux policy engine in the host **prevents any cross-VM access** — even if a hypervisor bug is exploited, the kernel's MAC layer blocks lateral movement.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: HIDS Detection Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S["DATA SOURCES: LOGS, FILES, SYSCALLS, REGISTRY"]:::src
    A["HIDS AGENT - OSSEC OR WAZUH"]:::agent
    P["PRE-PROCESSOR: NORMALIZE, PARSE, ENRICH"]:::pre
    D1["SIGNATURE MATCHER - KNOWN THREATS"]:::sig
    D2["ANOMALY DETECTOR - BEHAVIORAL ML"]:::ano
    C["CORRELATION ENGINE"]:::corr
    R1["RESPONSE: ALERT ONLY - HIDS MODE"]:::r1
    R2["RESPONSE: BLOCK AND ALERT - HIPS MODE"]:::r2
    F["FORWARDER TO SIEM - SPLUNK, ELK, QRADAR"]:::siem

    S --> A --> P --> D1
    P --> D2
    D1 --> C
    D2 --> C
    C -->|low severity| R1
    C -->|high severity active threat| R2
    C --> F

    classDef src fill:#deebf7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef agent fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef pre fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef sig fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef ano fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef corr fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef r1 fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef r2 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef siem fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

**Explanation:** HIDS collects data from multiple host sources, normalizes it, runs both signature and anomaly detection, correlates findings, and either alerts (HIDS mode) or actively blocks (HIPS mode) while forwarding to SIEM.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Patch Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["ASSESS - SCAN FOR VULNERABILITIES"]:::a
    P["PLAN - PRIORITIZE AND SCHEDULE"]:::b
    T["TEST - STAGING ENVIRONMENT"]:::c
    D["DEPLOY - PRODUCTION ROLLOUT"]:::d
    V["VERIFY - CONFIRM PATCH APPLIED"]:::e
    DOC["DOCUMENT - COMPLIANCE RECORDS"]:::f

    A --> P --> T --> D --> V --> DOC
    DOC -.->|"feedback loop continuous scanning"| A

    classDef a fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef b fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef c fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef d fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef e fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    classDef f fill:#7030a0,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

---
