# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**Define Host Level Security. List any four key components of host level security in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Host Level Security refers to the set of protective measures, controls, and tools implemented at the individual machine (host/VM) level to protect the operating system, applications, data, and virtualization layer from unauthorized access, malicious attacks, and misconfigurations.

**Four Key Components:**

1. **Operating System Hardening** — disabling unnecessary services, applying CIS benchmarks.
2. **Host-Based Firewall** — `iptables`, `nftables`, Windows Defender Firewall.
3. **Host-Based Intrusion Detection/Prevention System (HIDS/HIPS)** — OSSEC, AIDE, Wazuh.
4. **Anti-Malware Protection** — ClamAV, Windows Defender, heuristic + signature-based.

*(Any 4 of the 8 components from §1.5 may be given.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]

**What is a Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)? Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based detection.**

**Model Answer:**

**HIDS Definition:** A HIDS is a security software that monitors and analyzes the internals of a single computing system — including file system integrity, system calls, log files, and registry — to detect suspicious or malicious activity.

**Difference Table:**

| Parameter | **Signature-Based Detection** | **Anomaly-Based Detection** |
|---|---|---|
| **Method** | Pattern matching against a database of known attack signatures | Statistical/ML model of normal behavior; flags deviations |
| **Detects Known Threats** | Yes (very accurate) | Yes |
| **Detects Zero-Day Threats** | No (signature blind spot) | Yes (deviation from baseline) |
| **False Positive Rate** | Low | Higher (can flag legitimate unusual behavior) |
| **Resource Overhead** | Low | Higher (baseline training required) |
| **Example Tool** | ClamAV (signature) | OSSEC anomaly module, Wazuh ML |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, with internal choice between two sub-questions. Each sub-part typically carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain OS Hardening and Trusted Computing Base (TCB) with respect to Host Level Security.

**Model Answer:**

**Operating System Hardening:**

OS hardening is the process of reducing the **attack surface** of an operating system by applying secure configuration baselines, removing unnecessary components, and enforcing least privilege.

**Key Steps in OS Hardening:**

1. **Disable Unused Services:** Remove or disable daemons like `telnet`, `ftp`, `rlogin` that are unencrypted or unnecessary.
2. **Apply Security Baselines:** Use **CIS Benchmarks** or **DISA STIGs** for vendor-specific hardening.
3. **Mandatory Access Control (MAC):** Enable **SELinux** (Red Hat) or **AppArmor** (Ubuntu) to enforce fine-grained access control beyond standard Unix permissions.
4. **Patch Regularly:** Apply security updates through `yum`, `apt`, or `Windows Update`.
5. **Strong Authentication:** Disable root SSH login, enforce key-based authentication, set password policies (length, complexity, expiry).
6. **File System Permissions:** Audit with tools like `find / -perm -4000` for SUID binaries; restrict world-writable directories.
7. **Audit Logging:** Enable `auditd` (Linux) or Advanced Audit Policy (Windows).

**Trusted Computing Base (TCB):**

The TCB is the **set of all hardware, firmware, and software components** critical to enforcing the security policy of a system. A breach of *any* TCB component compromises the *entire* system's security.

**Mathematically:**

$$
\text{System is Trusted} \iff \forall c \in TCB, \; c \text{ is functioning as specified}
$$

**Examples of TCB Components:**

- Hardware: CPU, TPM chip, memory
- Firmware: UEFI/BIOS
- Hypervisor (in virtualized environments)
- OS Kernel
- Critical security daemons (e.g., `sshd`, `auditd`)

**Engineering Utility:** TCB is the foundation of systems designed under the **Orange Book (TCSEC)** and modern **Common Criteria (CC)** evaluation. Cloud providers like AWS, Azure, and GCP define their TCB in SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audit reports.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a neat diagram, explain the Secure Boot Chain of Trust using TPM. How does it ensure system integrity?

**Model Answer:**

**Secure Boot Definition:** Secure Boot is a security standard that ensures a device boots using only **software that is trusted by the OEM**. It establishes a cryptographic **chain of trust** from hardware to user applications.

**Trusted Platform Module (TPM):** A dedicated cryptographic chip that performs **Measured Boot** — each boot stage is hashed and the hash stored in TPM Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs).

**Chain of Trust Diagram (5 Stages):**

$$
\text{TPM} \rightarrow \text{UEFI} \rightarrow \text{Bootloader (GRUB2)} \rightarrow \text{Kernel} \rightarrow \text{Init} \rightarrow \text{Services}
$$

| Stage | Component | TPM PCR | Verification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | TPM (Root of Trust) | PCR0 | Hardware-anchored |
| 1 | UEFI Firmware | PCR0, PCR1 | Public key signed |
| 2 | Bootloader (GRUB2) | PCR2 | Kernel signature checked |
| 3 | Kernel (`vmlinuz`) | PCR3 | Modules verified |
| 4 | Init System (`systemd`) | PCR4 | Service signatures |
| 5 | User Services | PCR5..7 | Per-service policy |

**Mathematical Model of Trust Probability:**

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i
$$

For 5 stages with $p_i = 0.99$ each:

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = (0.99)^5 = 0.951 \approx 95.1\%
$$

**How Integrity is Ensured:**

1. **Each stage measures (hashes) the next** and stores the hash in a TPM PCR.
2. **TPM Sealing:** Disk encryption keys can be *sealed* to specific PCR values — they are released **only if** the boot state matches.
3. **Remote Attestation:** A remote server can challenge the TPM to report PCR values and verify the host's integrity.
4. **Boot Failure:** If any verification fails, the system halts or boots into recovery.

**Engineering Utility:** Secure Boot + TPM is the basis of **Microsoft Pluton, Intel Boot Guard, Apple Secure Enclave**, and **AWS Nitro System** root-of-trust.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain HIDS in detail. Compare signature-based and anomaly-based detection with examples.

**Model Answer:**

**HIDS — Detailed Explanation:**

A **Host-based Intrusion Detection System (HIDS)** is software that monitors the **internals of a single host** to detect malicious activity. Unlike a Network IDS (NIDS) that watches network packets, a HIDS has **deep visibility** into the host's:

- **File system** (integrity of `/etc/passwd`, binaries, configuration files)
- **System calls** (`execve`, `open`, `connect` patterns)
- **Log files** (`/var/log/auth.log`, Windows Security Log)
- **Registry** (Windows: `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`)
- **Running processes** (CPU/memory anomalies, unknown parent-child relationships)

**HIDS Operational Phases:**

1. **Data Collection** — agent-based (e.g., OSSEC agent) reads logs, files, syscalls.
2. **Preprocessing** — normalization, enrichment (e.g., geo-tagging IPs).
3. **Detection Engine** — runs rules (signatures) and statistical models.
4. **Alerting/Response** — generates alerts, optionally blocks (HIPS mode).
5. **Forwarding** — sends alerts to a central SIEM/manager.

**Signature-Based Detection (Misuse Detection):**

- **How it works:** Each known attack is encoded as a *signature* — a pattern (hash, regex, byte sequence) that uniquely identifies it.
- **Example:** A HIDS rule may flag a file change if its hash matches the **EICAR test string** (`X5O!P%@AP[4\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR-STANDARD-ANTIVIRUS-TEST-FILE!$H+H*`).
- **Strengths:** Very low false-positive rate, fast matching.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot detect **zero-day** attacks, requires constant signature updates.

**Anomaly-Based Detection:**

- **How it works:** The HIDS builds a **statistical profile** of "normal" host behavior (e.g., average CPU usage, number of login attempts per hour, file modifications per day). Any significant deviation triggers an alert.
- **Example:** A Linux server normally sees ~50 SSH login attempts per day; 5000 attempts in 1 hour = anomaly → possible brute-force.
- **Strengths:** Can detect novel/zero-day attacks.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher false-positive rate (legitimate but unusual behavior flagged).

**Hybrid Approach:** Modern HIDS like **OSSEC, Wazuh, and CrowdStrike Falcon** use **both** signature and anomaly detection in parallel for high accuracy and low false positives.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of TPM, Secure Boot, and Measured Boot in establishing a Trusted Computing Base for cloud hosts.

**Model Answer:**

**The Triad of Trust for Cloud Hosts:**

In cloud data centers, every physical host (and the VMs running on it) depends on a **chain of trust** anchored in hardware. Three components form this triad:

**1. Trusted Platform Module (TPM):**

- A **discrete cryptographic processor** (chip) compliant with the **TCG (Trusted Computing Group)** specification.
- TPM 2.0 (current standard) supports multiple cryptographic algorithms (RSA, ECC, SHA-256, AES).
- Key functions:
  - **Random Number Generation** (hardware entropy)
  - **Key Storage** (endorsement key, storage root key)
  - **Measured Boot** (hashes stored in PCRs)
  - **Sealing** (bind data to a specific platform state)
  - **Remote Attestation** (prove platform integrity to a remote verifier)

**2. Secure Boot:**

- A UEFI specification (since UEFI 2.3.1) that ensures **only signed bootloaders and kernels** are executed.
- Uses **public-key cryptography**: vendor's private key signs the firmware; platform stores the public key in hardware.
- If a rootkit modifies the bootloader, the signature check fails, and the system **refuses to boot**.

**3. Measured Boot:**

- A TPM-based mechanism that **records hashes of each boot stage** in PCRs but **does not stop boot** if values differ.
- The PCR values can be queried later via **TPM2_Quote** commands for **remote attestation**.
- Used by **BitLocker (Windows)**, **Linux IMA (Integrity Measurement Architecture)**, and **Azure Confidential Computing**.

**Establishing the TCB — The Unified Process:**

| Stage | Mechanism | Trust Anchor |
|---|---|---|
| Hardware Power-On | TPM measures ROM | **Hardware Root of Trust** |
| Firmware Load | UEFI Secure Boot signature check | Vendor public key |
| Bootloader Load | TPM PCR2 measurement | TPM |
| Kernel Load | Secure Boot + TPM PCR3 | TPM + UEFI DB |
| User Space | IMA / dm-verity measurements | TPM PCR10 |
| Runtime | Remote attestation quote to verifier | TPM Attestation Identity Key |

**Engineering Utility for Cloud:**

- **AWS Nitro System:** Uses custom hardware root-of-trust (Nitro Enclaves) for confidential computing.
- **Azure Confidential VMs:** Use AMD SEV-SNP with TPM 2.0 attestation.
- **Google Shielded VMs:** Use vTPM + Measured Boot + integrity monitoring.

**Mathematical Validation of the Chain of Trust:**

For a 5-stage chain with $p_i = 0.999$ per stage (high hardware reliability):

$$
P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = (0.999)^5 = 0.99501 \approx 99.5\%
$$

This is a 4-percentage-point improvement over $p_i = 0.99$ and demonstrates why **hardware root-of-trust** is non-negotiable for cloud TCB.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Host Level Security Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing HIDS with NIDS:** Students often write about network packet inspection in HIDS questions. **HIDS = file integrity + log analysis + syscall monitoring**, NOT packet capture.
> 
> 2. **Writing about Cloud Security in general instead of Host Security:** If the question specifies *Host Level*, you must discuss **OS hardening, host firewalls, HIDS, anti-malware, TPM** — not IAM, not DDoS at the network edge, not data center physical security.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the difference between HIDS and HIPS:** Many answers do not clarify that **HIPS = HIDS + active blocking**. Examiners allocate marks specifically for this distinction.
> 
> 4. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A neat diagram (chain of trust, HIDS architecture, layered defense) can fetch **2–3 extra marks**. Always include one.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning CIS Benchmarks / STIGs:** For OS hardening, name-dropping **CIS Benchmarks** and **DISA STIGs** shows examiner-level depth and earns bonus marks.
> 
> 6. **Omitting numerical examples:** For questions on detection rate, F1-score, or MTTP, a **worked numerical example** (as in §3.1) is worth 2–3 marks. Without it, the answer remains theoretical and loses marks.
> 
> 7. **Spelling "TPM" as "TMP" or "TPC":** Trivial but real — marks lost on technical accuracy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Host Level Security**

- [x] **Host Level Security = innermost defense ring** of cloud security (after network and IAM fail).
- [x] **8 Core Components:** OS Hardening, Host Firewall, HIDS/HIPS, Anti-Malware, Patch Mgmt, TCB (TPM/Secure Boot), VM Security, Logging.
- [x] **OS Hardening reduces attack surface** — use CIS Benchmarks, disable unused services, enable SELinux/AppArmor.
- [x] **Host Firewall examples:** `iptables` (Linux), `nftables`, Windows Defender Firewall.
- [x] **HIDS = passive detection; HIPS = active blocking** — both based on host internals (files, logs, syscalls).
- [x] **Signature-based = known threats only; Anomaly-based = can detect zero-day but higher FPR.**
- [x] **Common HIDS tools:** OSSEC, AIDE, Tripwire, Wazuh, Samhain.
- [x] **Anti-malware uses multi-layered detection:** Signature + Heuristic + Behavioral + Sandbox + Cloud reputation.
- [x] **Key metrics:** Detection Rate (Recall), FPR, Precision, F1-Score, Accuracy — know the formulas.
- [x] **MTTP ≤ 7 days** is the industry benchmark for critical CVE patching.
- [x] **TCB = the set of components whose failure compromises the entire system.**
- [x] **TPM 2.0** = hardware cryptographic chip; performs Measured Boot via PCRs.
- [x] **Secure Boot** = UEFI-based signature verification of bootloader/kernel.
- [x] **Chain of Trust equation:** $P(\text{Trusted Boot}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i$.
- [x] **VM Security relies on:** VM image hardening, hypervisor isolation, sVirt/SELinux labels (KVM).
- [x] **Logging destinations:** `/var/log/` (Linux), Event Viewer (Windows), SIEM (centralized).
- [x] **Defense-in-Depth = union of multiple diverse controls**, not just redundant layers.
- [x] **Most important distinction for exam:** HIDS ≠ NIDS; HIDS ≠ AV (anti-virus); Host Security ≠ Network Security.

---

**End of Module 3 — Host Level Security Notes**
**PEITT523 — Cloud Computing | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Application Level Security. Secure Distributed Data Storage in Cloud - Cryptography as a Primary Technique to Ensure Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Application Level Security. Secure Distributed Data Storage in Cloud - Cryptography as a Primary Technique to Ensure Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Application Level Security (ALS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Application Level Security* refers to the set of security controls, protocols, and mechanisms implemented at the **software application layer (Layer 7 of the OSI model)** to protect data, user sessions, APIs, and business logic from application-specific threats such as injection attacks, cross-site scripting (XSS), broken authentication, and data exfiltration within cloud-hosted services.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a high-rise apartment building (the cloud data center). The building has perimeter fences (network security), lobby guards (firewalls), and elevator access cards (transport security). *Application Level Security* is like the **door lock on your individual apartment door, the peephole, and the tenant verification system** — it protects *your* specific unit (the application and its data) from the inside, even when an intruder has somehow made it past the lobby. In cloud environments, since multiple tenants share the same physical infrastructure, the application becomes the last and most critical line of defense.

---

### 1.2 Secure Distributed Data Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Secure Distributed Data Storage* in the cloud refers to a storage architecture where data is **fragmented, encrypted, replicated, and dispersed across multiple geographically separated cloud nodes or data centers**, such that no single node holds complete plaintext data, thereby ensuring **Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability (CIA Triad)**, and resilience against node compromise, insider attacks, and physical theft.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a secret diary you want to hide from a nosy family. Instead of keeping the whole diary in one drawer (single server), you tear each page into 4 pieces and hide each piece in a different friend's house across different cities. Even if a burglar breaks into one friend's house, they only get a quarter of one page — useless by itself. To reconstruct the diary, you need all 4 pieces and a "decoder ring" (the cryptographic key). This is essentially what secure distributed storage does with your cloud data.

**Key Standard Metrics in Bold:**

- **CIA Triad:** **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability
- **AES Key Sizes:** **128 bits, 192 bits, 256 bits**
- **RSA Minimum Key Size:** **2048 bits** (as of 2024 NIST recommendations)
- **SHA-3 Output:** **224, 256, 384, or 512 bits**
- **Shamir's Secret Sharing Threshold:** *k-of-n* scheme
- **Storage Overhead Factor:** Typically **1.3x to 2.0x** of original data size

---

### 1.3 Cryptography as a Primary Security Technique

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:**
> *Cryptography* is the mathematical discipline of transforming readable plaintext into unintelligible ciphertext and back, using algorithms and keys, to provide **confidentiality, integrity, authentication, and non-repudiation** of data — both **at rest** (stored in cloud) and **in transit** (moving across networks).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, cryptography is positioned as the **primary (but not the only)** technique for cloud security because:
1. It is **mathematically provable** (unlike obfuscation or steganography).
2. It scales well to large cloud workloads.
3. It supports **fine-grained access control** through key management.
4. It is **algorithm-agnostic** — works across heterogeneous cloud providers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The examiner expects students to clearly differentiate between **symmetric, asymmetric, and hashing cryptography**, and to map each to a specific cloud security goal (e.g., AES → confidentiality, SHA-256 → integrity, RSA/ECC → key exchange & digital signatures).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillars of Cryptography in Cloud Security

The application of cryptography to secure distributed cloud storage rests on **three foundational pillars**:

1. **Symmetric Key Cryptography (SKC)**
   - Same key $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption.
   - Speed: **Very fast** (suitable for bulk data encryption).
   - Problem: **Key distribution** — how do two parties share $K$ securely?
   - Algorithms: **AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)**, 3DES, Blowfish, ChaCha20.

2. **Asymmetric Key Cryptography (AKC / Public Key Cryptography)**
   - Uses a mathematically linked **key pair**: Public key $K_{pub}$ and Private key $K_{priv}$.
   - $K_{pub}$ encrypts; $K_{priv}$ decrypts (or vice versa for digital signatures).
   - Speed: **Slower** (~1000x slower than symmetric); used for key exchange and signatures.
   - Algorithms: **RSA, ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography), Diffie-Hellman**.

3. **Cryptographic Hash Functions**
   - One-way function $H(M) = h$ that maps arbitrary-length input to fixed-length digest.
   - Properties: **Deterministic, Pre-image resistant, Collision resistant, Avalanche effect**.
   - Algorithms: **SHA-256, SHA-3, BLAKE2**.

---

### 2.2 Operational Workflow in Cloud Storage

The "Why" and "How" of each step:

- **Step 1: User Authentication** — *Why?* To verify identity. *How?* Using X.509 digital certificates + RSA/ECDSA signatures.
- **Step 2: Key Exchange** — *Why?* To establish a shared symmetric key. *How?* Using Diffie-Hellman or RSA-encrypted key transport over TLS.
- **Step 3: Data Encryption** — *Why?* Confidentiality at rest. *How?* AES-256 in GCM mode (provides both confidentiality and integrity).
- **Step 4: Data Fragmentation** — *Why?* To prevent single-node compromise. *How?* Using **Shamir's Secret Sharing** or **Information Dispersal Algorithm (IDA)**.
- **Step 5: Integrity Verification** — *Why?* To detect tampering. *How?* Storing SHA-256 hashes or HMAC values.
- **Step 6: Audit & Non-Repudiation** — *Why?* To track who did what. *How?* Digital signatures + immutable audit logs.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** mathematical foundation for cryptography questions. Note the use of `\mid` and `\vert` instead of the pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Entropy (information content) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)$ | $p(x_i)$ = probability of symbol | Measured in **bits per symbol** |
| RSA Key Generation Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ = large distinct primes | $n \ge 2048$ bits |
| RSA Public Exponent Condition | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $e$ = public exponent, $\phi$ = Euler totient | $e = 65537$ (standard) |
| Euler's Totient Function | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | For $n = p \cdot q$ with $p, q$ prime | Integer |
| RSA Private Exponent | $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Modular multiplicative inverse | $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| RSA Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $M$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext | $0 \le M < n$ |
| RSA Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Recovers plaintext | Unique per pair |
| AES Block Size | $B = 128$ bits | Fixed | **128 bits** |
| AES Key Schedule Rounds | $N_r = 10$ (128-bit), $12$ (192-bit), $14$ (256-bit) | Depends on key size | Rounds |
| Hash Output Length (SHA-256) | $\vert h \vert = 256$ bits | Fixed regardless of input | **256 bits** |
| Avalanche Effect Target | $\Delta H \ge 50\%$ for 1-bit input change | Output bits flipped | **At least half** |
| Shamir's Threshold Scheme | $k \le n$ (any $k$ of $n$ shares reconstruct) | $k$ = threshold, $n$ = total shares | $k, n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = (g^{b})^{a} \pmod{p} = (g^{a})^{b} \pmod{p}$ | $a, b$ = private keys of A, B | Modulo prime $p$ |
| Digital Signature Verification | $\text{Valid} \iff V(M, S, K_{pub}) = \text{True}$ | $S$ = signature, $V$ = verify algorithm | Boolean |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AWS S3 Server-Side Encryption (SSE-KMS):** Uses **AES-256** with per-object keys managed by AWS Key Management Service.
- **Google Cloud Storage CSEK:** Allows customers to supply their own **AES-256** keys; Google never stores them.
- **Microsoft Azure Confidential Computing:** Uses **Intel SGX enclaves** to decrypt and process data in encrypted memory — cryptography + hardware TEE.
- **Blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric):** Uses **ECDSA** for transaction signatures and **SHA-256** for Merkle tree integrity.
- **Healthcare (HIPAA-compliant clouds):** Field-level encryption of PHI (Protected Health Information) using envelope encryption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: RSA Key Generation, Encryption, and Decryption

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most frequently asked** numerical problem in KTU Cloud Computing / Network Security papers. Memorize the workflow.

**Scenario:** Alice wants to send an encrypted message $M = 7$ to Bob using RSA.

#### Step 1 — Choose Two Distinct Primes

Bob selects two large prime numbers. For illustration (small numbers), Bob picks:

$$p = 11, \quad q = 13$$

**Logic:** Primes are used because factorization is computationally hard (the basis of RSA security).

---

#### Step 2 — Compute the Modulus $n$

The modulus is the product of the two primes:

$$n = p \cdot q = 11 \cdot 13 = 143$$

**Logic:** $n$ becomes part of both the public and private keys. Its size (in bits) defines the security level.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

For $n = p \cdot q$ where $p$ and $q$ are distinct primes:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (11 - 1)(13 - 1) = 10 \cdot 12 = 120$$

**Logic:** $\phi(n)$ counts integers from $1$ to $n$ that are coprime to $n$. It governs the cyclic structure of modular exponentiation.

---

#### Step 4 — Choose the Public Exponent $e$

Choose $e$ such that $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ and $1 < e < \phi(n)$.

$$e = 7$$

**Verification:** $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ ✓ (7 and 120 share no common factors).

**Logic:** Standard practice uses $e = 65537$ for efficiency (only 2 bits set) and security.

---

#### Step 5 — Compute the Private Exponent $d$

Find $d$ such that $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $d \cdot 7 \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
120 &= 17 \cdot 7 + 1 \\
7 &= 7 \cdot 1 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 120 - 17 \cdot 7 \\
\Rightarrow d &\equiv -17 \pmod{120} \\
\Rightarrow d &\equiv 120 - 17 = 103
\end{aligned}$$

So, $d = 103$.

**Verification:** $7 \cdot 103 = 721 = 6 \cdot 120 + 1 = 720 + 1$ ✓

---

#### Step 6 — Form the Key Pair

$$\text{Public Key } (n, e) = (143, 7)$$
$$\text{Private Key } (n, d) = (143, 103)$$

**Logic:** Bob publishes $(143, 7)$ to the world. He keeps $(143, 103)$ secret.

---

#### Step 7 — Encrypt the Message

Alice, with message $M = 7$, computes:

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 7^{7} \pmod{143}$$

Compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
7^1 &= 7 \\
7^2 &= 49 \\
7^3 &= 49 \cdot 7 = 343 = 2 \cdot 143 + 57 \Rightarrow 57 \\
7^4 &= 57 \cdot 7 = 399 = 2 \cdot 143 + 113 \Rightarrow 113 \\
7^5 &= 113 \cdot 7 = 791 = 5 \cdot 143 + 76 \Rightarrow 76 \\
7^6 &= 76 \cdot 7 = 532 = 3 \cdot 143 + 103 \Rightarrow 103 \\
7^7 &= 103 \cdot 7 = 721 = 5 \cdot 143 + 6 \Rightarrow 6
\end{aligned}$$

So, $C = 6$. Alice transmits $C = 6$.

---

#### Step 8 — Decrypt the Message

Bob receives $C = 6$ and computes:

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 6^{103} \pmod{143}$$

By the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) optimization (split into mod 11 and mod 13):

$$\begin{aligned}
6^{103} \pmod{11} &: \text{ Fermat's little theorem: } 6^{10} \equiv 1 \pmod{11} \\
103 \bmod 10 &= 3 \\
6^{103} &\equiv 6^{3} \pmod{11} = 216 \pmod{11} = 216 - 19 \cdot 11 = 216 - 209 = 7 \\
6^{103} \pmod{13} &: 6^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13} \\
103 \bmod 12 &= 103 - 8 \cdot 12 = 103 - 96 = 7 \\
6^{103} &\equiv 6^{7} \pmod{13} = 279936 \pmod{13}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing $6^7 \pmod{13}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
6^2 &= 36 \equiv 10 \pmod{13} \\
6^4 &\equiv 10^2 = 100 \equiv 9 \pmod{13} \\
6^6 &\equiv 6^4 \cdot 6^2 = 9 \cdot 10 = 90 \equiv 12 \pmod{13} \\
6^7 &\equiv 12 \cdot 6 = 72 \equiv 7 \pmod{13}
\end{aligned}$$

So we have:
- $M \equiv 7 \pmod{11}$
- $M \equiv 7 \pmod{13}$

By CRT, the unique solution modulo $143$ is $M = 7$ ✓.

**Result:** The original message $M = 7$ is successfully recovered.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Cryptography Technique vs Cloud Security Goal

| Cryptography Technique | Primary Security Goal | Cloud Use Case | Vulnerable To |
|---|---|---|---|
| AES-256 (Symmetric) | **Confidentiality at rest** | S3 SSE, Azure Blob encryption | Brute force (mitigated by 256-bit key) |
| RSA-2048 (Asymmetric) | **Key exchange, signatures** | TLS handshake, code signing | Quantum algorithms (Shor's) |
| ECC (secp256r1) | **Lightweight key exchange** | Mobile cloud apps, IoT | Quantum algorithms |
| SHA-256 (Hashing) | **Integrity** | Merkle trees, deduplication | Collision attacks (computationally infeasible) |
| HMAC-SHA256 | **Authenticated integrity** | API request signing | Key compromise |
| Shamir's Secret Sharing | **Availability + Confidentiality** | Distributed key backup | Lost shares (if $k-1$ shares lost) |
| Paillier Cryptosystem | **Homomorphic addition** | Privacy-preserving analytics | Key compromise |
| Attribute-Based Encryption (ABE) | **Fine-grained access control** | Multi-tenant SaaS | Attribute revocation complexity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cryptographic Pipeline for Secure Distributed Cloud Storage

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow from a user's plaintext data to its secure distributed storage across multiple cloud nodes, with cryptographic operations at every stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Plaintext Data M] --> B[SHA-256 Hashing for Integrity H = SHA256 M]
    B --> C[AES-256 Symmetric Encryption C = AESK M]
    C --> D[RSA-OAEP Key Wrapping Wrap session key K with public key]
    D --> E[Shamir Secret Sharing Split C into n shares threshold k]
    E --> F1[Cloud Node 1 Store Share 1]
    E --> F2[Cloud Node 2 Store Share 2]
    E --> F3[Cloud Node 3 Store Share 3]
    E --> F4[Cloud Node k Store Share k]
    F1 --> G[Reconstruction Trigger k of n shares available]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Unwrap AES Key using RSA private key]
    H --> I[AES-256 Decryption M prime = AESinv Wrap C]
    I --> J[Integrity Check Compare SHA256 M prime vs H]
    J --> K{Integrity Valid}
    K -->|Yes| L[Deliver Plaintext to Authorized User]
    K -->|No| M[Reject and Trigger Security Alert]
```

---

### 4.2 Cryptographic Layer Architecture in a Cloud Application

This diagram maps the OSI/layered model to where each cryptographic primitive is applied in a typical cloud SaaS application.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer7 [Application Layer]
        L7A[End to End Encryption E2EE with user key]
        L7B[Attribute Based Encryption for RBAC]
    end

    subgraph Layer6 [Presentation Layer]
        L6A[TLS 1.3 Handshake with ECDHE]
        L6B[JWT Tokens signed with HMAC SHA256]
    end

    subgraph Layer5 [Session and Key Management]
        L5A[Key Management Service KMS or HSM]
        L5B[Key Rotation Policy every 90 days]
    end

    subgraph Layer4 [Storage Layer]
        L4A[AES 256 GCM encryption at rest]
        L4B[Shamir Secret Sharing for key fragmentation]
    end

    subgraph Layer3 [Integrity and Audit Layer]
        L3A[SHA 256 Merkle Tree for tamper detection]
        L3B[ECDSA digital signatures for non repudiation]
    end

    Layer7 --> Layer6
    Layer6 --> Layer5
    Layer5 --> Layer4
    Layer4 --> Layer3
```

---

### 4.3 Threat vs Defense Mapping Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Threats [Cloud Application Threats]
        T1[Data Breach at Rest]
        T2[Man in the Middle Attack]
        T3[Data Tampering]
        T4[Unauthorized Access]
        T5[Replay Attack]
    end

    subgraph Defenses [Cryptographic Defenses]
        D1[AES 256 Encryption]
        D2[TLS 1.3 with ECDHE]
        D3[HMAC SHA 256]
        D4[Attribute Based Encryption]
        D5[Timestamped Nonce in JWT]
    end

    T1 --> D1
    T2 --> D2
    T3 --> D3
    T4 --> D4
    T5 --> D5
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Application Level Security in cloud computing. List any four application-level threats.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Application Level Security (ALS) refers to security mechanisms implemented at the software application layer to protect data, sessions, APIs, and business logic from threats specific to cloud-hosted applications. It is the last line of defense after network and host security.
>
> **Four Application-Level Threats (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **SQL Injection** – Malicious SQL statements inserted into input fields.
> 2. **Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** – Injection of malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users.
> 3. **Broken Authentication** – Weak session management allowing session hijacking.
> 4. **Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)** – Unauthorized access to data by manipulating object identifiers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of cryptography in ensuring security of distributed data storage in cloud? Mention any two symmetric and two asymmetric algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Role of Cryptography (1.5 marks):** Cryptography provides **confidentiality** (via encryption), **integrity** (via hashing), **authentication** (via digital signatures), and **non-repudiation** (via signed audit trails). In distributed cloud storage, it ensures that even if a storage node is physically compromised or an insider snoops, the data remains unintelligible without the cryptographic key.
>
> **Algorithms (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> - **Symmetric:** AES (Advanced Encryption Standard), 3DES (Triple Data Encryption Standard).
> - **Asymmetric:** RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman), ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students often **skip the mathematical derivation steps** in RSA questions and jump to the answer → loses 2-3 marks. Always show modular exponentiation step-by-step.
> - In AES questions, students **forget the key schedule and round structure** → mark deduction. Always state the number of rounds (10/12/14 for AES-128/192/256).
> - For "explain cryptography in cloud" questions, students write a **generic answer without mapping algorithms to cloud security goals** → poor impression on the examiner.
> - **Never confuse hashing with encryption.** Hashing is one-way; encryption is reversible.
> - Always **state key sizes** explicitly (e.g., AES-256, RSA-2048) — vague answers lose marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Cryptography Fundamentals & RSA*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the three main categories of cryptographic techniques: symmetric key cryptography, asymmetric key cryptography, and hash functions. For each, give one algorithm and one cloud use case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform RSA encryption and decryption for the following parameters and message: $p = 5$, $q = 11$, $e = 7$, plaintext $M = 9$. Show all key generation, encryption, and decryption steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Categories of Cryptography (7 marks):**

**1. Symmetric Key Cryptography (2.5 marks)**
- Uses a **single shared key** $K$ for both encryption $E_K$ and decryption $D_K$.
- Speed: Very high throughput (suitable for bulk data).
- Key challenge: Secure key distribution.
- **Algorithm:** AES-256 (Advanced Encryption Standard with 256-bit key).
- **Cloud use case:** Server-side encryption of S3 buckets in AWS; database field-level encryption in Azure SQL.

**2. Asymmetric Key Cryptography (2.5 marks)**
- Uses a **key pair** $(K_{pub}, K_{priv})$ mathematically related via one-way functions.
- Enables key exchange, digital signatures, and authentication.
- **Algorithm:** RSA-2048 (or ECC secp256r1).
- **Cloud use case:** TLS 1.3 handshake for HTTPS connections; signing of OAuth/JWT tokens for API authentication.

**3. Cryptographic Hash Functions (2 marks)**
- One-way functions producing fixed-length digests.
- Properties: Pre-image resistance, collision resistance, avalanche effect.
- **Algorithm:** SHA-256.
- **Cloud use case:** Integrity verification of stored blobs; Merkle tree construction in cloud sync services like Dropbox.

---

**(b) RSA Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $n$:**
$$n = p \cdot q = 5 \cdot 11 = 55$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\phi(n)$:**
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$$

**Step 3 — Verify $e$:**
$$\gcd(7, 40) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$:**
Using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$\begin{aligned}
40 &= 5 \cdot 7 + 5 \\
7 &= 1 \cdot 5 + 2 \\
5 &= 2 \cdot 2 + 1
\end{aligned}$$

Back-substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 5 - 2 \cdot 2 \\
&= 5 - 2(7 - 1 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7 \\
&= 3(40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7
\end{aligned}$$

So $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$. Thus $d = 23$.

**Verification:** $7 \cdot 23 = 161 = 4 \cdot 40 + 1$ ✓

**Step 5 — Encrypt $M = 9$:**
$$C \equiv 9^{7} \pmod{55}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
9^2 &= 81 = 1 \cdot 55 + 26 \equiv 26 \pmod{55} \\
9^4 &\equiv 26^2 = 676 = 12 \cdot 55 + 16 \equiv 16 \pmod{55} \\
9^7 &= 9^4 \cdot 9^2 \cdot 9^1 \equiv 16 \cdot 26 \cdot 9 \pmod{55} \\
16 \cdot 26 &= 416 = 7 \cdot 55 + 31 \equiv 31 \pmod{55} \\
31 \cdot 9 &= 279 = 5 \cdot 55 + 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{55}
\end{aligned}$$

So $C = 4$.

**Step 6 — Decrypt $C = 4$:**
$$M \equiv 4^{23} \pmod{55}$$

By CRT split: $4^{23} \pmod{5}$ and $4^{23} \pmod{11}$.

Mod 5: $4 \equiv -1$, so $4^{23} \equiv (-1)^{23} = -1 \equiv 4 \pmod{5}$.

Mod 11: $4^{10} \equiv 1$ (Fermat), $23 \bmod 10 = 3$, so $4^{23} \equiv 4^3 = 64 \equiv 9 \pmod{11}$.

CRT for $M \equiv 4 \pmod{5}$ and $M \equiv 9 \pmod{11}$:

Let $M = 4 + 5k$. Then $4 + 5k \equiv 9 \pmod{11} \Rightarrow 5k \equiv 5 \pmod{11} \Rightarrow k \equiv 1 \pmod{11}$.

So $M = 4 + 5(1) = 9$ ✓

**Result:** Original message $M = 9$ recovered.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Distributed Storage & Hashing*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of secure distributed data storage in cloud computing. Discuss Shamir's Secret Sharing scheme with its mathematical foundation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the SHA-256 algorithm in detail. List its properties and explain its role in ensuring data integrity in cloud storage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Secure Distributed Data Storage & Shamir's Secret Sharing (7 marks):**

**Architecture (3.5 marks):**
Secure distributed data storage in cloud involves:
1. **Encryption:** Plaintext data is encrypted using AES-256 before distribution.
2. **Fragmentation:** Ciphertext is split into $n$ shares using a threshold scheme.
3. **Distribution:** Each share is stored on a different cloud node (possibly across regions/providers).
4. **Replication:** Shares are replicated for fault tolerance.
5. **Reconstruction:** Any $k$ of $n$ shares can reconstruct the original data; fewer than $k$ shares reveal nothing.

**Benefits:** Resilience against single-node compromise, insider attacks, and physical theft.

**Shamir's Secret Sharing (3.5 marks):**
- **Mathematical Foundation:** Based on the fact that a polynomial of degree $(k-1)$ is uniquely determined by $k$ points.
- A secret $S$ is encoded as the constant term $a_0$ of a random polynomial:

$$f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \ldots + a_{k-1} x^{k-1} \pmod{p}$$

where $p$ is a large prime and $a_1, \ldots, a_{k-1}$ are random coefficients.
- Each share is a point $(x_i, f(x_i))$. With $k$ shares, the polynomial is reconstructed using **Lagrange interpolation**; with fewer than $k$ shares, there are infinitely many possible polynomials → secret remains secure.

**Example:** For $k = 3, n = 5$ and $S = 1234$ with $p = 1613$ (large prime), a degree-2 polynomial $f(x) = 1234 + 166x + 94x^2 \pmod{1613}$ produces 5 shares given to 5 nodes; any 3 can recover $S$.

---

**(b) SHA-256 Algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Padding:** Append a '1' bit, then '0' bits, then the 64-bit message length, to make the total length a multiple of 512 bits.
2. **Parse:** Divide padded message into 512-bit blocks $M^{(1)}, M^{(2)}, \ldots, M^{(N)}$.
3. **Initialize Hash Values:** Set 8 working variables $H_0$ to $H_7$ to fixed initial constants (square roots of first 8 primes).
4. **Message Schedule:** For each block, create a 64-word schedule $W_t$:
   - $W_t = M_t^{(i)}$ for $t = 0, \ldots, 15$
   - $W_t = \sigma_1(W_{t-2}) + W_{t-7} + \sigma_0(W_{t-15}) + W_{t-16}$ for $t = 16, \ldots, 63$
5. **Compression:** Run 64 rounds using functions $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1$ and constants $K_t$ (cube roots of first 64 primes).
6. **Output:** Final hash $H = H_0 \vertvert H_1 \vertvert \ldots \vertvert H_7$ — a 256-bit digest.

**Properties (1.5 marks):**
- **Deterministic:** Same input → same output.
- **Pre-image resistance:** Infeasible to find $M$ given $H(M)$.
- **Collision resistance:** Infeasible to find $M_1 \ne M_2$ with $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$.
- **Avalanche effect:** 1-bit change in input → ~50% bits changed in output.
- **Fixed output:** Always 256 bits regardless of input size.

**Role in Cloud Data Integrity (1.5 marks):**
- Cloud providers (e.g., AWS S3) compute and store SHA-256 ETags for every object.
- When data is retrieved, the client recomputes the hash and compares → detects any tampering during transit or at rest.
- Forms the basis of **Merkle trees** for verifying large datasets and **proof of retrievability (PoR)** protocols.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Application Security & Cryptography**

- **CIA Triad** is the foundation: **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability.
- **Application Level Security** = Layer 7 protections (XSS, SQLi, broken auth, IDOR defenses).
- **Symmetric crypto** = same key, fast, used for bulk data → **AES-128/192/256**.
- **Asymmetric crypto** = key pair, slow, used for key exchange & signatures → **RSA-2048, ECC**.
- **Hashing** = one-way, fixed output, integrity → **SHA-256, SHA-3**.
- **RSA core formulas:** $n = pq$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, $C = M^e \bmod n$, $M = C^d \bmod n$.
- **Shamir's Secret Sharing** = $k$-of-$n$ threshold; based on polynomial interpolation; any $k$ shares recover secret.
- **TLS 1.3** uses **ECDHE** for key exchange + **AES-GCM** for bulk encryption + **HMAC-SHA256** for integrity.
- **AES rounds:** 10 (128-bit key), 12 (192-bit), 14 (256-bit); block size always 128 bits.
- **Cloud storage architecture:** Encrypt → Fragment → Distribute → Replicate → Audit.
- **Key management** is the *hardest problem* in cloud cryptography — use **KMS/HSM** services.
- **Post-quantum threat:** RSA and ECC are vulnerable to Shor's algorithm on a quantum computer; **NIST PQC standards** (CRYSTALS-Kyber, CRYSTALS-Dilithium) are emerging.
- **Common exam traps:** confusing encryption with hashing, skipping modular arithmetic steps, forgetting key sizes, not mapping algorithms to security goals.
- **2-mark favorite question:** "Differentiate symmetric and asymmetric cryptography" — prepare a 4-point comparison table.
- **7-mark favorite question:** RSA numerical (key generation + encrypt + decrypt) — practice at least 5 numericals.
- **14-mark favorite question:** Explain cryptography techniques + RSA derivation + cloud use case mapping.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gartner’s Seven Cloud Computing Security Risks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gartner’s Seven Cloud Computing Security Risks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gartner's Seven Cloud Computing Security Risks** refers to a foundational risk taxonomy published by the research firm **Gartner Inc.** in **2008** (analyst: **Jon Oltsik**). It enumerates the seven most critical security concerns an enterprise must evaluate before migrating core assets to a public **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)**. This framework predates modern frameworks like the **Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM)** and **CSA STAR**, but remains a syllabus-mandated baseline in the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEITT523** curriculum.

In rigorous academic terms, the taxonomy categorizes risks across three orthogonal dimensions:
1. **Data Governance Risks** (Risks 1, 2, 3)
2. **Operational/Architectural Risks** (Risks 4, 5)
3. **Legal & Continuity Risks** (Risks 6, 7)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Bank Locker Analogy"**
> Imagine you decide to store your family's most precious jewellery in a **bank locker** instead of your own house safe.
> - You no longer have **direct physical control** over the locker (Privileged User Access).
> - The bank must follow **RBI regulations**, but *you* are still legally responsible if your jewellery is misused (Regulatory Compliance).
> - You don't know **which branch vault** in which city your locker actually resides (Data Location).
> - Your jewellery sits in a vault **shared with thousands of other customers** — the bank must guarantee it never gets mixed up (Data Segregation).
> - If the bank floods, **how fast** will they recover your items? (Recovery).
> - If a crime occurs, can the **police investigate** effectively in a shared vault? (Investigative Support).
> - What if the **bank itself goes bankrupt**? Will you still get your jewellery back? (Long-term Viability).

Cloud computing security is conceptually **identical**. You are handing over control of your most sensitive digital assets (data, applications, identities) to a third party. The "Seven Risks" are the seven questions a smart customer asks the bank *before* signing the locker agreement.

---

## 1.3 The Seven Risks — Summary Snapshot

| # | Risk Name | Core Concern |
|---|-----------|--------------|
| **R1** | Privileged User Access | Insider abuse by CSP administrators |
| **R2** | Regulatory Compliance | Audit & legal accountability gaps |
| **R3** | Data Location | Jurisdictional uncertainty |
| **R4** | Data Segregation | Multi-tenancy isolation failures |
| **R5** | Recovery | Disaster recovery & SLA gaps |
| **R6** | Investigative Support | Forensic & logging limitations |
| **R7** | Long-term Viability | Provider business continuity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Expect direct 3-mark questions asking students to "List and briefly explain Gartner's seven security risks" and 7-mark analytical questions comparing two or more risks with modern equivalents (e.g., shared responsibility model).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Risk 1 — Privileged User Access

**Definition:** The risk that a cloud provider's internal administrators (who have elevated system privileges) could access, leak, or misuse sensitive customer data.

**Why it matters:**
- In an on-premise model, organizations control *who* has root/admin access.
- In the cloud, the CSP's staff inherently hold super-user privileges over your data.
- A single rogue administrator or compromised insider account can exfiltrate petabytes of data.

**How to mitigate (KTU Board-Expected Answers):**
- Strong **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** contracts
- **Encryption of data at rest and in transit** with customer-held keys (BYOK — Bring Your Own Key)
- **Background checks** mandated in the SLA
- **Audit logs** of privileged access
- **Separation of duties** within the CSP's internal teams

---

## 2.2 Risk 2 — Regulatory Compliance

**Definition:** The risk that the cloud deployment violates industry-specific regulations (e.g., **GDPR**, **HIPAA**, **PCI-DSS**, **IT Act 2000 (India)**).

**Why it matters:**
- Traditional auditors can physically inspect on-premise data centers.
- Cloud-hosted data is often **distributed across geographies**, making compliance attestation difficult.
- *The customer always remains the data controller*, even if the CSP is the data processor.

**How to mitigate:**
- Choose providers with **ISO 27001**, **SOC 2 Type II**, **CSA STAR** certifications.
- Implement **Data Processing Agreements (DPAs)**.
- Maintain an **internal compliance overlay** independent of the CSP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic (Board Favourite):** Gartner estimated that by **2020**, more than **50%** of enterprise data would be stored outside the enterprise firewall — amplifying regulatory exposure. *(Note: This was a Gartner 2010 projection cited in subsequent reports.)*

---

## 2.3 Risk 3 — Data Location

**Definition:** The uncertainty surrounding the **physical geographic location** where customer data is stored, processed, or replicated.

**Why it matters:**
- Different countries have **different data sovereignty laws** (e.g., Russia's Federal Law No. 242-FZ mandates citizen data stay in Russia).
- Cross-border data transfer may trigger **GDPR adequacy decisions**.
- Latency-sensitive workloads need specific regional placement.

**How to mitigate:**
- Contractual commitment to **data residency** (e.g., "EU-only" or "India-only" storage).
- Use of **regional cloud zones** (AWS Mumbai, Azure Central India, GCP Asia-South).
- Customer-driven **key management** to control access even if the data moves.

---

## 2.4 Risk 4 — Data Segregation

**Definition:** The risk that data from one tenant may be inadvertently exposed to another tenant in a shared multi-tenant cloud environment.

**Why it matters:**
- Public clouds use **multi-tenancy** — your data sits on the same physical disk as competitors'.
- Famous vulnerabilities: **Spectre/Meltdown (2018)** broke CPU-level isolation; **Hyperjacking attacks** break hypervisor isolation.

**How to mitigate:**
- **Encryption** per tenant (AES-256 minimum).
- **Logical isolation** via Virtual Private Clouds (VPCs).
- **Tenant-specific cryptographic keys**.
- **Homomorphic encryption** (emerging) for processing encrypted data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encryption is NOT a cure-all.** Gartner explicitly noted that encryption alone fails if keys are co-located with the data they protect.

---

## 2.5 Risk 5 — Recovery

**Definition:** The risk that the cloud provider cannot guarantee **disaster recovery (DR)** and **business continuity (BC)** at the contracted service level.

**Why it matters:**
- Cloud outages occur — **AWS S3 outage (Feb 2017)**, **Azure AD outage (Mar 2021)**.
- Many providers only guarantee **99.9%** (Three Nines) uptime = ~8.77 hours downtime/year.
- A customer cannot always perform their own DR drills on the provider's infrastructure.

**How to mitigate:**
- Insist on **RTO (Recovery Time Objective) ≤ 4 hours** and **RPO (Recovery Point Objective) ≤ 15 minutes** in SLAs.
- **Multi-region replication** with automated failover.
- Regular **DR drills** with provider cooperation.
- **Backup of backups** in an independent provider (multi-cloud).

---

## 2.6 Risk 6 — Investigative Support

**Definition:** The risk that forensic investigations (e.g., breach analysis, e-discovery for litigation) are **impossible or impaired** in a cloud environment.

**Why it matters:**
- Logs are **co-mingled** across multiple tenants.
- Data is spread across **dynamically provisioned** virtual hosts whose IP addresses change hourly.
- The CSP may **refuse to share logs** citing other tenants' privacy.
- **Chain of custody** for digital evidence breaks down.

**How to mitigate:**
- Contractual right to **audit logs**.
- Deploy customer-side **SIEM (Security Information & Event Management)** agents.
- **Timestamped, signed, immutable logs** (blockchain-anchored logs in modern systems).
- Pre-defined **e-discovery cooperation clauses**.

---

## 2.7 Risk 7 — Long-term Viability

**Definition:** The risk that the cloud provider goes bankrupt, gets acquired, or discontinues the service, leaving the customer with **orphaned data** or an unsupported workload.

**Why it matters:**
- Cloud markets consolidate: dozens of IaaS providers from 2010 have disappeared (e.g., **Nirvanix, 2013** — customers got 30 days to extract data).
- **Vendor lock-in** (proprietary APIs, formats) compounds the risk.

**How to mitigate:**
- Use **open standards** (OpenStack, TOSCA, Kubernetes).
- Maintain **portable data formats** (CSV, JSON, Parquet — not proprietary databases).
- Contractual **data escrow** clauses.
- Periodic **cloud exit rehearsals** ("un-cloud" drills).

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Risk # | Name | Primary Concern | Key Mitigation | Common Exam Keyword |
|--------|------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **R1** | Privileged User Access | Insider threat | BYOK, IAM, audit | "Super-user abuse" |
| **R2** | Regulatory Compliance | Legal accountability | ISO 27001, SOC 2, DPA | "Data controller" |
| **R3** | Data Location | Sovereignty | Regional zones, residency | "Jurisdiction" |
| **R4** | Data Segregation | Multi-tenancy breach | Encryption, VPC, keys | "Tenant isolation" |
| **R5** | Recovery | Disaster & continuity | RTO/RPO SLAs, multi-region | "Business continuity" |
| **R6** | Investigative Support | Forensics impossible | SIEM, log audit rights | "Chain of custody" |
| **R7** | Long-term Viability | Vendor failure | Portability, escrow | "Vendor lock-in" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Mnemonic:** "**P**lease **R**espect **D**ata **S**o **R**obustly — **I**nvestigate **L**ong-term" → **P-R-D-S-R-I-L**
> Maps to: **P**rivileged, **R**egulatory, **D**ata Location, **S**egregation, **R**ecovery, **I**nvestigative, **L**ong-term viability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Risk Categorization

## 3.1 Categorization Logic (Step-by-Step Derivation)

The seven risks can be derived from three fundamental **security pillars** of cloud architecture. Below is the structured analytical reasoning path.

### **Step 1 — Identify the Fundamental Trust Boundary**

In any cloud deployment, the trust boundary shifts from the enterprise's physical perimeter to the **logical boundary of the CSP's infrastructure**. This shift generates three primary categories of risk.

### **Step 2 — Map Each Risk to a Pillar**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Category} &\in \{\text{Governance}, \text{Operational}, \text{Continuity}\} \\[6pt]
\text{R1 (Privileged Access)} &\rightarrow \text{Governance (People)} \\[2pt]
\text{R2 (Compliance)} &\rightarrow \text{Governance (Process)} \\[2pt]
\text{R3 (Data Location)} &\rightarrow \text{Governance (Jurisdiction)} \\[2pt]
\text{R4 (Segregation)} &\rightarrow \text{Operational (Technology)} \\[2pt]
\text{R5 (Recovery)} &\rightarrow \text{Operational (Resilience)} \\[2pt]
\text{R6 (Investigative)} &\rightarrow \text{Operational (Forensics)} \\[2pt]
\text{R7 (Long-term Viability)} &\rightarrow \text{Continuity (Vendor)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 3 — Compute a Simplified Risk Score (Board-Friendly)**

For a KTU 14-mark question, examiners often expect a **risk-prioritization matrix**. The simplified scoring formula is:

$$
\text{Risk Priority Number (RPN)} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Detectability}
$$

Where:
- **Impact** ∈ {1, 2, 3} (Low, Medium, High)
- **Likelihood** ∈ {1, 2, 3} (Low, Medium, High)
- **Detectability** ∈ {1, 2, 3} (Easy, Moderate, Hard)

> [!NOTE]
> Higher RPN = Higher priority for mitigation investment. The **"harder to detect"** a risk is, the more dangerous it becomes.

### **Step 4 — Illustrative RPN Calculation for Each Risk**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RPN}_{R1} &= 3 \times 2 \times 3 = 18 \quad \text{(Insider abuse — high impact, hard to detect)} \\
\text{RPN}_{R2} &= 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12 \\
\text{RPN}_{R3} &= 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8 \\
\text{RPN}_{R4} &= 3 \times 2 \times 3 = 18 \quad \text{(Multi-tenancy breach — catastrophic)} \\
\text{RPN}_{R5} &= 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12 \\
\text{RPN}_{R6} &= 2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18 \quad \text{(Forensic gap — frequent, undetectable)} \\
\text{RPN}_{R7} &= 2 \times 1 \times 2 = 4 \quad \text{(Rare but catastrophic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **Step 5 — Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The top three RPN scores (**R1, R4, R6**) tie at **18**, indicating they are the **highest-priority** risks according to the simplified model. This aligns with modern industry consensus that **insider threat**, **multi-tenancy isolation**, and **forensic blind spots** are the most pressing concerns in cloud security.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Dimension | On-Premise Equivalent | Cloud-Specific Amplification | Modern Counterpart (Post-2020) |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **R1 Access Control** | Local admins, RBAC | Global super-user pool | Zero-Trust Architecture (ZTA) |
| **R2 Compliance** | Single-jurisdiction audit | Multi-jurisdictional | CSA STAR, ISO 27017 |
| **R3 Data Location** | Known, fixed | Dynamic, unknown | Sovereign Cloud regions |
| **R4 Segregation** | N/A (single tenant) | Multi-tenant by design | Confidential Computing (SGX, SEV) |
| **R5 Recovery** | Tape backups offsite | Provider-controlled RTO/RPO | Multi-cloud DR, Cloud-to-Cloud Backup |
| **R6 Investigation** | Full physical access | Log co-mingling | Cloud-native SIEM (Splunk, Chronicle) |
| **R7 Viability** | N/A | Vendor failure risk | Cloud Portability APIs, Terraform |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Recall)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three security risks identified by Gartner for cloud computing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three security risks identified by Gartner (2008) are:

1. **Privileged User Access** — Risk of CSP administrators misusing their elevated privileges to access customer data.
2. **Data Segregation** — Risk of one tenant's data being exposed to another in a shared multi-tenant environment.
3. **Long-term Viability** — Risk that the cloud provider may go bankrupt or discontinue services, leaving customer data orphaned.

> [!NOTE]
> Any three risks with a one-line explanation fetch full **3 marks** in KTU valuation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is the role of encryption in addressing Gartner's "Data Segregation" risk?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Encryption mitigates the Data Segregation risk by ensuring that even if multi-tenant data is co-located on shared physical media, **only authorized tenants with the correct cryptographic keys can decrypt and access their data**. Modern implementations use **AES-256** encryption with **tenant-specific keys** managed via Hardware Security Modules (HSMs). However, Gartner explicitly warned that **encryption is not a complete cure-all** — if the keys are stored by the same provider that hosts the data, the protection is compromised. Hence, **Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)** models are recommended.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students commonly lose marks by **listing risks without explaining mitigations** — always pair each risk with a concrete mitigation strategy.
> 2. Avoid using **generic terms** like "use security" or "use firewalls" — examiners expect **cloud-specific** answers (BYOK, VPC, SIEM, RTO/RPO).
> 3. For 14-mark questions, **headings + sub-points + examples** score more than running paragraphs.
> 4. Do **NOT confuse** Gartner's risks with the **OWASP Top 10** or **CSA TRE** — they are different frameworks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail **Gartner's seven cloud computing security risks**. For each risk, suggest at least one mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Risk 1 (Privileged User Access)** and **Risk 4 (Data Segregation)** in terms of threat actor, attack surface, and modern mitigation techniques. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

| # | Risk | Explanation (≈3 lines) | Mitigation |
|---|------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Privileged User Access** | CSP admins have super-user access. A rogue insider can leak data. | BYOK encryption, audit logs, strict IAM policies |
| 2 | **Regulatory Compliance** | Customer remains the data controller under GDPR/HIPAA. CSP is processor. | ISO 27001/SOC 2 certified providers, signed DPAs |
| 3 | **Data Location** | Data may be stored in unknown jurisdictions with conflicting laws. | Regional zones (e.g., AWS Mumbai), contractual residency |
| 4 | **Data Segregation** | Multi-tenancy creates co-location risk. | AES-256 encryption, VPC isolation, tenant-specific keys |
| 5 | **Recovery** | Provider outages can cause data unavailability. | RTO/RPO SLAs, multi-region replication, DR drills |
| 6 | **Investigative Support** | Logs are co-mingled; forensics is impaired. | Cloud SIEM, immutable logs, audit rights in SLA |
| 7 | **Long-term Viability** | Provider may go bankrupt (e.g., Nirvanix 2013). | Open standards, data escrow, portable formats |

*(Scoring: 1 mark for naming + 1 mark for explanation + 1 mark for mitigation → adjusted to 7 marks total)*

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Dimension | R1: Privileged User Access | R4: Data Segregation |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| **Threat Actor** | Authorized CSP insider (rogue admin) | External attacker exploiting multi-tenancy flaw |
| **Attack Surface** | Hypervisor admin console, IAM roles, key management | Shared physical disks, hypervisor, network planes |
| **Impact** | Mass data exfiltration by a single insider | Cross-tenant data leakage |
| **Modern Mitigation** | Zero-Trust Architecture, privileged access workstations, customer-held HSM keys | Confidential Computing (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), tenant-specific encryption, micro-segmentation |
| **Detection Difficulty** | Hard — looks like legitimate access | Moderate — anomalous cross-tenant traffic detectable via SIEM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Synthesis (1 mark for comparison):** Both risks stem from **shared infrastructure** but differ in *attacker profile* and *detection vector*. Modern **Zero-Trust** and **Confidential Computing** paradigms address both simultaneously.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **"Data Location"** and **"Long-term Viability"** risks in detail. How do they impact organizations operating in India under the **IT Act 2000** and **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA) 2023**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how a **multi-tenant cloud architecture** gives rise to **Data Segregation** risk. Suggest **three modern techniques** to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Data Location Risk (≈3.5 marks):**
- Data may be stored in jurisdictions with weaker data protection laws.
- Under **DPDPA 2023**, personal data of Indian citizens must be handled with consent and stored on systems meeting prescribed security standards. Cross-border transfer is restricted.
- Under **IT Act 2000 (Section 43A)**, a body corporate handling sensitive personal data is liable for damages if it is negligent in maintaining reasonable security practices.
- **Mitigation:** Choose a provider with an **Indian data center** (e.g., AWS Mumbai `ap-south-1`, Azure Central India, GCP `asia-south1`). Sign a **Data Residency Addendum** explicitly restricting storage to Indian soil.

**Long-term Viability Risk (≈3.5 marks):**
- If the CSP shuts down or is acquired, the customer may lose access to data.
- Real-world example: **Nirvanix (2013)** — customers had only **30 days** to extract their data before the company shut down.
- Under **DPDPA 2023**, the data fiduciary remains responsible for data even after contract termination — making vendor failure a compliance issue, not just an operational one.
- **Mitigation:** Use **open standards** (OpenStack, Kubernetes), maintain **portable data formats**, and insist on **data escrow** clauses in the MSA (Master Service Agreement).

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Diagram (≈3 marks):**
```mermaid
graph LR
    t1A[Tenant A Data] --> vm1A[VM A]
    t2A[Tenant B Data] --> vm2A[VM B]
    t3A[Tenant C Data] --> vm3A[VM C]
    vm1A --> hyperA[Hypervisor Layer]
    vm2A --> hyperA
    vm3A --> hyperA
    hyperA --> phyA[Physical Host Server]
    phyA --> diskA[Shared Physical Storage]
    diskA -.->|Risk| leakA[Cross Tenant Data Leak]
```

**Explanation (≈2 marks):**
In multi-tenant architecture, multiple customers share the same physical hardware (compute, storage, network) but are isolated via **virtualization**. A failure in the hypervisor, a misconfigured virtual switch, or a side-channel attack (e.g., Spectre) can break this isolation — leading to **Data Segregation risk**.

**Three Modern Mitigation Techniques (≈2 marks):**
1. **Tenant-specific encryption (AES-256)** with **BYOK** — even raw disk access yields ciphertext.
2. **Confidential Computing** — using **Intel SGX** or **AMD SEV-SNP** to encrypt data *in use* inside CPU enclaves.
3. **Micro-segmentation** — using **VPCs, security groups, and NSGs** (Network Security Groups) to enforce per-tenant traffic boundaries.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- **Gartner's report (2008)** identified exactly **7** security risks — not 5, not 10. Memory aid: **"P-R-D-S-R-I-L"**.
- **Risk Categories:** R1-R3 are **Governance**; R4-R6 are **Operational**; R7 is **Continuity**.
- **Top 3 High-Priority Risks (by RPN model):** R1 (Privileged Access), R4 (Segregation), R6 (Investigative) — all tie at RPN = 18.
- **Encryption is NOT a complete mitigation for R4** — keys must be customer-held (BYOK/HYOK).
- **Indian Context:** Always mention **IT Act 2000 (Section 43A)** and **DPDPA 2023** for India-specific exam answers.
- **Real-world case:** **Nirvanix (2013)** shutdown is the textbook example for **R7 Long-term Viability**.
- **Modern Parallels:** Gartner's risks map to **Zero-Trust (R1)**, **Confidential Computing (R4)**, **Cloud SIEM (R6)**, and **Multi-cloud DR (R5)**.
- **Examiner Traps:**
  - Do not confuse with **OWASP Top 10** (web app) or **CSA TRE** (Cloud Security Alliance — Top Threats to Enterprise).
  - Do not list risks without mitigations.
  - Always cite **at least one regulation or real-world incident** in 14-mark answers.
- **Gartner's RPN Formula:** $\text{RPN} = \text{Impact} \times \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Detectability}$ — handy for risk-prioritization sub-questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip:** For KTU 2024 ESE, expect a **direct 3-mark question** on listing the risks and a **7-mark analytical question** on a specific risk (most likely R4 or R5) with modern mitigations. Practice the **Nirvanix case study** and the **DPDPA 2023** angle for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
