> The word **"big"** and **"data"** appear in **every document**, so $idf = 0$, making them *stop words* — useless for ranking. In contrast, **"hadoop"** and **"mapreduce"** are discriminative terms with positive TF-IDF.

## 3.2 Iterative MapReduce — Worked PageRank Convergence

Let us demonstrate one full iteration of the PageRank computation on a **3-page web graph**:

- **Edges:** $1 \to 2$, $1 \to 3$, $2 \to 3$, $3 \to 1$.
- **Out-degrees:** $L(1) = 2$, $L(2) = 1$, $L(3) = 1$.
- **Damping factor:** $d = 0.85$.
- **Initial PageRank (k = 0):** $PR^{(0)}(1) = PR^{(0)}(2) = PR^{(0)}(3) = 1/3$.

### Step 1: Map Phase

The input to the mapper at iteration $k$ is a line: `(page_id, current_rank;neighbor1,neighbor2,...)`. The mapper emits, for each outgoing edge to a neighbor `n`, the rank contribution:

$$ \text{map}(p, \; r \;|\; \text{neighbors}) \;\to\; \forall n \in \text{neighbors}(p): \; (n, \; r / L(p)) $$

**Execution for our graph (k = 0):**

```
(1, 1/3)        →   (2, (1/3)/2 = 1/6)   and   (3, (1/3)/2 = 1/6)
(2, 1/3)        →   (3, (1/3)/1 = 1/3)
(3, 1/3)        →   (1, (1/3)/1 = 1/3)
```

### Step 2: Shuffle and Group by Neighbor

```
(1, [1/3])
(2, [1/6])
(3, [1/6 + 1/3])
```

### Step 3: Reduce Phase — Apply PageRank Formula

For each page $p$, sum the contributions and apply the damping formula:

$$
PR^{(1)}(1) = \frac{1 - 0.85}{3} + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = 0.05 + 0.2833 \approx 0.3333
$$

$$
PR^{(1)}(2) = \frac{1 - 0.85}{3} + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{6}\right) = 0.05 + 0.1417 \approx 0.1917
$$

$$
PR^{(1)}(3) = \frac{1 - 0.85}{3} + 0.85 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{3}\right) = 0.05 + 0.4250 \approx 0.4750
$$

### Step 4: Convergence Check

$$
\Delta = \sum_{p=1}^{3} \vert PR^{(1)}(p) - PR^{(0)}(p) \vert = \vert 0.3333 - 0.3333 \vert + \vert 0.1917 - 0.3333 \vert + \vert 0.4750 - 0.3333 \vert
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.0000 + 0.1416 + 0.1417 \approx 0.2833
$$

Since $\Delta = 0.2833$ is much greater than a typical $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$, **another iteration is required**. The driver triggers iteration $k = 2$, and the loop continues.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Twister's role:** In Hadoop, every iteration $k$ requires a brand-new MapReduce job launch (tens of seconds of overhead). In Twister, the mappers and reducers are **pre-launched once** and re-executed inside a tight loop driven by the driver, with **intermediate state cached in memory**. This eliminates the per-iteration job-launch cost and the per-iteration HDFS write.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MapReduce Data Flow for Inverted Index (Two-Stage Job)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[HDFS Input Documents]
    nodeB[Mapper Stage 1]
    nodeC[Shuffle and Sort Stage 1]
    nodeD[Reducer Stage 1]
    nodeE[HDFS Intermediate Output]
    nodeF[Mapper Stage 2]
    nodeG[Shuffle and Sort Stage 2]
    nodeH[Reducer Stage 2]
    nodeI[Final Inverted Index on HDFS]

    nodeA -->|doc_id, doc_text| nodeB
    nodeB -->|word@doc_id, 1| nodeC
    nodeC -->|grouped by word@doc_id| nodeD
    nodeD -->|word@doc_id, tf| nodeE
    nodeE -->|read| nodeF
    nodeF -->|word, doc_id:tf| nodeG
    nodeG -->|grouped by word| nodeH
    nodeH -->|word, posting_list| nodeI

    style nodeA fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Twister High-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    driver[Twister Driver]
    broker[Pub Sub Message Broker]
    fs[HDFS Persistent Storage]
    cache[In Memory Key Value Cache]
    worker1[Worker Node A]
    worker2[Worker Node B]
    worker3[Worker Node C]
    maptask1[Map Task Pool]
    redtask1[Reduce Task Pool]
    maptask2[Map Task Pool]
    redtask2[Reduce Task Pool]
    maptask3[Map Task Pool]
    redtask3[Reduce Task Pool]

    driver -->|controls| broker
    driver -->|orchestrates| worker1
    driver -->|orchestrates| worker2
    driver -->|orchestrates| worker3
    fs -->|static data| cache
    cache -->|broadcasts| worker1
    cache -->|broadcasts| worker2
    cache -->|broadcasts| worker3
    worker1 --> maptask1
    worker1 --> redtask1
    worker2 --> maptask2
    worker2 --> redtask2
    worker3 --> maptask3
    worker3 --> redtask3
    broker -->|intermediate data via pub/sub| worker1
    broker -->|intermediate data via pub/sub| worker2
    broker -->|intermediate data via pub/sub| worker3

    style driver fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style broker fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style cache fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Iterative MapReduce Convergence Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    init[Initialize State]
    load[Load Static Data into Cache]
    map[Map Phase]
    shuf[Shuffle and Group]
    red[Reduce Phase]
    check{Convergence Met?}
    out[Output Final Result]
    next[Prepare Next Iteration Input]

    init --> load
    load --> map
    map --> shuf
    shuf --> red
    red --> check
    check -->|No| next
    next --> map
    check -->|Yes| out

    style init fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style check fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Hadoop vs Twister for Iterative Workloads

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hadoop[Hadoop Iterative Workflow]
        h1[Job 1 Launch]
        h2[Map]
        h3[Reduce]
        h4[HDFS Write]
        h5[Job 2 Launch]
        h6[Map]
        h7[Reduce]
        h8[HDFS Write]
        h1 --> h2 --> h3 --> h4 --> h5 --> h6 --> h7 --> h8
    end

    subgraph twister[Twister Iterative Workflow]
        t1[Single Job Launch]
        t2[In Memory Cache Loaded]
        t3[Map Iter 1]
        t4[Reduce Iter 1]
        t5[In Memory Cache Updated]
        t6[Map Iter 2]
        t7[Reduce Iter 2]
        t8[In Memory Cache Updated]
        t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7 --> t8
    end

    style hadoop fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style twister fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how **Hadoop** writes intermediate state to HDFS between every iteration (slow disk I/O) and launches a brand-new JobTracker cycle (high scheduling overhead). **Twister** keeps the same map and reduce tasks alive in memory across all iterations and uses the pub/sub broker to transfer intermediate data, dramatically reducing latency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

*Define an inverted index. How is it constructed using the MapReduce programming model?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

An **inverted index** is a data structure that maps every distinct term in a document collection to the list of documents containing that term, along with positional or frequency information. It is the foundational index used by search engines.

Construction via MapReduce proceeds in **two stages**:

1. **Term Frequency Job:** The mapper tokenizes each document and emits `(word@doc_id, 1)` pairs. The reducer sums these to produce `(word@doc_id, tf)`.
2. **Inversion Job:** The mapper extracts the word from the composite key and emits `(word, doc_id:tf)`. The reducer concatenates all values into a posting list, yielding the final inverted index of the form `(word → [doc_id:tf; doc_id:tf; ...])`.

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the two-stage construction explanation.)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

*What is Iterative MapReduce? Why is native Hadoop MapReduce inefficient for iterative workloads, and how does Twister address this inefficiency?*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Iterative MapReduce** is a programming model in which the map and reduce functions are executed repeatedly across successive super-steps until a convergence condition is satisfied (e.g., fixed-point in PageRank, centroid stability in k-means).

Hadoop is **inefficient** for iterative workloads because:
1. Each iteration launches a **fresh JobTracker task**, incurring high scheduling overhead (tens of seconds).
2. Intermediate state is **written to HDFS** between iterations, causing disk I/O bottlenecks.
3. Static data (e.g., the web graph) is **reloaded from HDFS** in every iteration.

**Twister addresses this** by:
1. Using a **long-running map/reduce task pool** that avoids job re-launch.
2. Employing a **pub/sub message broker** for fast intermediate data exchange.
3. Caching **invariant data in distributed memory** to eliminate repeated HDFS reads.

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for Hadoop's inefficiency + 1 mark for Twister's solution.)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the construction of an **inverted index** using a MapReduce program. Include the mapper and reducer logic for both stages of the construction. Use a sample input corpus of at least three documents to demonstrate the complete data flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **TF-IDF** weighting scheme used for ranking documents in an inverted index. Provide the formula, explain each term, and compute the TF-IDF values for a sample word across a small corpus. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Inverted Index via MapReduce (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Formal definition (1 mark):** An inverted index is a word-to-document mapping stored as $(w, [(d_1, f_1), (d_2, f_2), \dots])$ where $w$ is a term and $f_i$ is the term frequency in document $d_i$.

**Step 2 — Stage 1 mapper logic (1.5 marks):** Input is `(document_id, content)`. The mapper splits the content on non-word characters, lowercases tokens, and for each token $w$ emits $(w@doc\_id, 1)$.

**Step 3 — Stage 1 reducer logic (1.5 marks):** For each composite key $w@doc\_id$, the reducer sums the values to obtain the term frequency $tf(w, d)$, emitting $(w@doc\_id, tf)$.

**Step 4 — Stage 2 mapper logic (1 mark):** Input is $(w@doc\_id, tf)$. The mapper splits on `@` to recover the bare word and emits $(w, doc\_id:tf)$.

**Step 5 — Stage 2 reducer logic (1 mark):** For each word $w$, the reducer concatenates all $(doc\_id:tf)$ values into a single posting list and emits $(w, "d_1:tf_1;d_2:tf_2;\dots")$.

**Step 6 — Worked example (1 mark):** Demonstrate on a 3-document corpus (e.g., the D1/D2/D3 example given in Section 3.1) showing the full mapper input → mapper output → reducer output trace for both stages.

**(b) TF-IDF Weighting (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Term Frequency (1 mark):**

$$ tf(w, d) = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[w_i = w] $$

**Step 2 — Document Frequency (1 mark):**

$$ df(w) = \sum_{d=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[w \in d] $$

**Step 3 — Inverse Document Frequency (1.5 marks):**

$$ idf(w) = \log\!\left(\frac{N}{df(w)}\right) $$

where $N$ is the total number of documents in the corpus.

**Step 4 — TF-IDF Combined Weight (1.5 marks):**

$$ w(w, d) = tf(w, d) \cdot idf(w) $$

This scheme assigns **high weight** to terms that are frequent in a single document but rare across the corpus, making them discriminative for retrieval.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (2 marks):** For the corpus in Section 3.1 with $N = 3$, compute $tf, df, idf,$ and $tf\text{-}idf$ for the word "hadoop" as demonstrated. Conclude with the analytical matrix showing TF-IDF values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **Twister** architecture in detail. Explain its components — driver, daemon, pub/sub broker, and in-memory cache — and discuss how each contributes to efficient iterative MapReduce execution. Compare Twister with native Hadoop MapReduce for iterative workloads. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Iterative MapReduce**. Describe the PageRank algorithm as a canonical example, including the recurrence relation, the map and reduce logic, and the convergence criterion. Show one full iteration of PageRank on a 3-node web graph. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Twister Architecture (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):** Iterative workloads suffer under Hadoop due to repeated job launches and disk I/O. Twister is a specialized runtime for such workloads.

**Step 2 — Twister Driver (1 mark):** The driver is the central controller that holds the iteration loop, maintains the convergence predicate, broadcasts static data, and dispatches map/reduce tasks to the worker pool.

**Step 3 — Twister Daemon / Worker (1.5 marks):** Each compute node runs a long-lived daemon that hosts a pool of map and reduce tasks. Unlike Hadoop, these tasks are not torn down between iterations — they persist and re-execute on new data each round.

**Step 4 — Pub/Sub Broker (1.5 marks):** Intermediate data exchange uses a publish-subscribe message broker (typically NaradaBrokering) instead of HTTP-based shuffle. This is significantly faster and supports streaming-style data flow.

**Step 5 — In-Memory Cache (1 mark):** Static, iteration-invariant data is loaded from HDFS **once** and cached in worker RAM, eliminating repeated disk reads across the $K$ iterations.

**Step 6 — Comparison with Hadoop (1 mark):** Summarize the comparison diagram from Section 4.4 — Twister's persistent task pool, in-memory cache, and pub/sub broker together yield 10×–50× speedups for iterative algorithms like PageRank, k-means, and BFS.

**(b) Iterative MapReduce & PageRank (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Iterative MapReduce Definition (1 mark):** Repeated execution of map and reduce functions until a convergence condition is met, with the previous reduce output feeding the next map input.

**Step 2 — PageRank Recurrence (2 marks):**

$$ PR^{(k+1)}(p) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \cdot \sum_{q \to p} \frac{PR^{(k)}(q)}{L(q)} $$

Explain each variable: $PR^{(k)}(p)$, $d \approx 0.85$, $N$ total pages, $L(q)$ out-degree, summation over inbound neighbors.

**Step 3 — Map Phase Logic (1 mark):** For each page $p$ with current rank $r$ and neighbors $\{n_1, n_2, \dots, n_L\}$, emit $(n_i, r/L)$ for each neighbor.

**Step 4 — Reduce Phase Logic (1 mark):** For each page $p$, sum the incoming rank contributions and apply the damping formula to compute $PR^{(k+1)}(p)$.

**Step 5 — Convergence Criterion (1 mark):** Check $\Delta = \sum_p \vert PR^{(k+1)}(p) - PR^{(k)}(p) \vert \lt \varepsilon$ (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$).

**Step 6 — Worked Example (1 mark):** Demonstrate one full PageRank iteration on the 3-node graph from Section 3.2, yielding $PR^{(1)} = (0.3333, 0.1917, 0.4750)$ with $\Delta \approx 0.2833$, indicating further iteration is needed.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Inverted Index Questions):**
> 1. **Single-job fallacy:** Many students incorrectly claim the entire inverted index can be built in *one* MapReduce job. Wrong! A two-stage job is **mandatory** because the grouping keys conflict (composite `word@doc_id` vs. bare `word`). This alone can cost **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the `@` separator:** Without the composite-key separator, the mapper and reducer cannot disambiguate `"bigdata@d1"` from `"big@d1data"`. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing TF-IDF with raw TF:** TF-IDF requires *both* the term frequency *and* the inverse document frequency. Computing only $tf$ earns **partial credit only**.
> 4. **Hadoop vs Twister confusion:** Twister is **NOT** the same as YARN. Twister is a *separate runtime* designed for iterations. Confusing the two earns **zero credit** for comparison questions.
> 5. **PageRank formula errors:** The damping factor $\frac{1-d}{N}$ must be **added** (not multiplied) and the link contribution must be **multiplied by $d$** (not divided). Reversing these yields numerically wrong answers and full marks lost.
> 6. **In-memory cache terminology:** Students often write "Twister uses cache memory" without specifying that it is a **distributed, in-memory, key-value cache** shared across workers. The phrasing matters for full credit.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverted Index:** Word-to-document mapping; built in **two MapReduce stages** (TF job + Inversion job).
- **Mapper Stage 1 emits:** $(word@doc\_id, 1)$.
- **Reducer Stage 1 emits:** $(word@doc\_id, tf)$.
- **Mapper Stage 2 emits:** $(word, doc\_id:tf)$.
- **Reducer Stage 2 emits:** $(word, "d_1:tf_1;d_2:tf_2;\dots")$ — the **final index**.
- **TF-IDF formula:** $w(w,d) = tf(w,d) \cdot \log(N/df(w))$.
- **Twister:** Lightweight, iterative MapReduce runtime from **Indiana University (SALSA group)**.
- **Twister components:** Driver + Daemon + Pub/Sub Broker + In-Memory Cache.
- **Twister advantages:** Persistent task pool, in-memory caching of static data, fast pub/sub intermediate exchange.
- **Iterative MapReduce:** Loop structure with state propagation and convergence predicate.
- **PageRank formula:** $PR^{(k+1)}(p) = (1-d)/N + d \sum_{q \to p} PR^{(k)}(q)/L(q)$.
- **Damping factor:** typically $d = 0.85$.
- **Convergence:** $\sum_p \vert PR^{(k+1)}(p) - PR^{(k)}(p) \vert \lt \varepsilon$, typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-4}$.
- **Hadoop inefficiency for iteration:** Repeated job launches + repeated HDFS writes + repeated HDFS reads of static data.
- **Spark's lineage:** Apache Spark inherits Twister's design philosophy (in-memory caching, iterative loops).
- **Real-world applications:** Web search (inverted index), web graph ranking (PageRank), clustering (k-means), machine learning (gradient descent).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hadoop Library
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Library

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop Library** refers to the **Apache Hadoop** software framework — a collection of open-source, distributed computing utilities that facilitate the **storage**, **processing**, and **analysis** of massive datasets (Big Data) across clusters of commodity computers. In the context of Cloud Computing, the Hadoop Library functions as a **cloud-native data-processing service layer** that abstracts Big Data operations into a set of programmable APIs and services running on virtualized infrastructure.

The Hadoop Library is formally defined by **The Apache Software Foundation** as a framework that allows for the **distributed processing of large data sets across clusters of computers** using simple programming models. It is designed to **scale up from a single server to thousands of machines**, each offering **local computation and storage**.

### 1.2 Core Pillars of the Hadoop Library

| Pillar | Full Form | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HDFS** | Hadoop Distributed File System | Distributed, fault-tolerant **storage** |
| **MapReduce** | Map + Reduce Programming Paradigm | Distributed **batch processing** engine |
| **YARN** | Yet Another Resource Negotiator | Cluster **resource management & scheduling** |
| **Hadoop Common** | — | Shared **utilities & libraries** (JNI, RPC, serialization) |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight: The Hadoop Library is studied under Cloud Computing because it represents one of the most widely-deployed **Big-Data-as-a-Service (BDaaS)** frameworks running atop Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) cloud platforms such as AWS EC2, Azure VMs, and Google Compute Engine.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The National Library of a Country:**
> Imagine you are the chief librarian of a country that owns **petabytes** of books (your data). You cannot fit them in a single building, so you distribute them across **1000 branch libraries** (commodity servers). Now, when a citizen asks, *"Find every book where the word 'Cloud' appears on pages 5–10,"* the task is huge.

> - **HDFS** is the **postal/courier system** that splits, stores, and replicates books across branches (default 3 copies for safety).
> - **MapReduce** is the **team of junior librarians** — half of them (Mappers) scan books in parallel at their local branch, and the other half (Reducers) compile the final answer.
> - **YARN** is the **head office coordinator** that assigns librarians to tasks and ensures no branch is overburdened.
> - **Hadoop Common** is the **set of shared tools** (catalogs, carts, ladders) every librarian uses.

This is exactly what the Hadoop Library does — it takes a **monolithic Big-Data problem** and parallelizes it across a cluster, hiding the distribution complexity from the application developer.

### 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (KTU-Standard)

- **Default Block Size in HDFS:** **128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x+; was 64 MB in Hadoop 1.x)
- **Default Replication Factor:** **3** copies per block
- **NameNode Memory Rule of Thumb:** **1 GB of RAM per 1 million blocks**
- **Heartbeat Interval:** every **3 seconds**
- **DataNode Block Report Interval:** every **6 hours (21600 seconds)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of the Hadoop Library

The Hadoop Library follows a **four-layer modular architecture**, each layer addressing a specific concern of distributed Big-Data processing:

#### Layer 1 — Storage Layer: HDFS
- Splits huge files into **fixed-size blocks** (default 128 MB).
- Stores blocks across multiple **DataNodes** with replication.
- Maintains metadata in a **NameNode** (master) and secondary NameNode (checkpoint node).
- Implements a **write-once, read-many** access model.

#### Layer 2 — Resource Management Layer: YARN
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Cluster-wide authority for resource allocation.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent managing containers and monitoring resource usage (CPU, memory, disk, network).
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application negotiator that requests resources from the RM and works with NMs to execute tasks.
- **Container:** A unit of allocated resources (RAM + vCores) on a node.

#### Layer 3 — Processing Layer: MapReduce
- Implements the **divide-and-conquer** pattern via two user-defined functions:
  - **Map $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$**
  - **Reduce $(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$**
- Between Map and Reduce, an implicit **Shuffle & Sort** phase runs on the framework.

#### Layer 4 — Application & Utility Layer
- Higher-level abstractions: **Hive** (SQL-on-Hadoop), **Pig** (dataflow scripting), **HBase** (NoSQL), **Sqoop** (RDBMS import/export), **Flume** (log ingestion), **Oozie** (workflow scheduler), **ZooKeeper** (coordination).

### 2.2 Operational Workflow — End-to-End Execution

1. **Client submits a job** to the ResourceManager via the YARN Client API.
2. **ResourceManager** allocates a container and launches the **ApplicationMaster**.
3. **ApplicationMaster** requests additional containers from the ResourceManager.
4. **NodeManagers** launch **Map tasks** on allocated containers.
5. **Map tasks** read corresponding HDFS blocks, apply the user-defined `map()` function, and write intermediate key-value pairs to **local disk** (not HDFS — to avoid replication overhead).
6. The framework performs **Shuffle & Sort**: intermediate pairs are **partitioned**, **sorted**, and **transferred** to the nodes hosting the Reduce tasks.
7. **Reduce tasks** apply the user-defined `reduce()` function and write the final output back to **HDFS** (with default replication factor 3).
8. **ApplicationMaster** deregisters with the ResourceManager, and containers are released.

### 2.3 Why the Hadoop Library is Cloud-Native

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Hadoop Library exhibits three properties that make it a perfect citizen of the Cloud Computing paradigm:
> 1. **Elastic Scalability** — HDFS and YARN are designed to scale horizontally by simply adding commodity nodes (or cloud VMs).
> 2. **Fault Tolerance** — Data replication + task re-execution guarantee resilience against node failure, mirroring the **availability** requirement of cloud SLAs.
> 3. **Pay-per-use Compatibility** — When deployed on cloud VMs (e.g., AWS EMR, Azure HDInsight, GCP Dataproc), the Hadoop Library becomes a managed **Big-Data-as-a-Service (BDaaS)** offering.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | HDFS Block Count | $B = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$ | Calculate number of blocks per file |
| 2 | Total HDFS Storage Used | $S_{\text{total}} = B \times 128 \text{ MB} \times R$ | Where $R$ = replication factor (default 3) |
| 3 | NameNode Memory | $M_{\text{NN}} \approx \dfrac{B}{10^6} \text{ GB}$ | RAM planning for NameNode |
| 4 | MapReduce Speedup (Amdahl's Law) | $S(n) = \dfrac{1}{f + \dfrac{1-f}{n}}$ | Theoretical max speedup with $n$ mappers |
| 5 | Replication Overhead | $O = R - 1$ | Extra storage cost beyond raw data |
| 6 | YARN Container Allocation | $C_{\text{alloc}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{vCores}_i + \text{RAM}_i)$ | Total resource grant per job |
| 7 | Data Locality Score | $D = \dfrac{\text{Nodes with local data}}{\text{Total nodes with data}}$ | Map task scheduling quality |
| 8 | Heartbeat Timeout | $T_{\text{HB}} = 10 \text{ min}$ (default) | Marks DataNode as failed |
| 9 | Shuffle Phase Parallelism | $P_{\text{shuffle}} = \min(N_R, N_M)$ | Where $N_R$ = reducers, $N_M$ = mappers |
| 10 | Storage Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{R} \times 100\%$ | Useful storage vs. total cluster capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute value and division operators inside the table use `\lceil`, `\rceil`, and `\dfrac` to avoid breaking the markdown table with raw `|` or `/` symbols.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Hadoop Library is the **backbone of enterprise Big Data** in industries such as:

- **Finance:** Fraud detection (MapReduce over transaction logs).
- **Healthcare:** Genomic sequence analysis on petabyte-scale DNA datasets.
- **Retail:** Customer purchase-pattern analysis (used by Walmart, Amazon).
- **Telecommunications:** CDR (Call Detail Record) analytics for churn prediction.
- **Social Media:** Real-time content recommendation engines.
- **Scientific Research:** CERN's LHC data analysis, climate modeling.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — HDFS Block Count for a Given File

> **Problem:** Calculate the number of HDFS blocks required to store a file of size **640 MB**, given a default HDFS block size of 128 MB. What is the total storage consumed with default replication factor $R = 3$?

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters.**

$$\text{File Size} = 640 \text{ MB}, \quad B_{\text{size}} = 128 \text{ MB}, \quad R = 3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the HDFS block-count formula.**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{B_{\text{size}}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
B = \left\lceil \frac{640 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integer division. The result is an exact integer, so the ceiling function has no effect.**

$$
B = \lceil 5.0 \rceil = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the raw logical storage (without replication).**

$$
S_{\text{logical}} = B \times B_{\text{size}} = 5 \times 128 \text{ MB} = 640 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the total physical storage consumed (with replication).**

$$
S_{\text{physical}} = S_{\text{logical}} \times R = 640 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1920 \text{ MB} = 1.875 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the replication overhead.**

$$
O = (R - 1) \times 100\% = (3 - 1) \times 100\% = 200\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** To store 640 MB of actual data, the cluster must provision 1920 MB of physical disk, accepting a 200% overhead. This is the **fault-tolerance cost** of the Hadoop Library.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — MapReduce Speedup via Amdahl's Law

> **Problem:** A MapReduce job has 5% sequential (non-parallelizable) overhead. Calculate the theoretical maximum speedup when running with $n = 100$ mappers.

**Step 1 — Define Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S(n) = \frac{1}{f + \frac{1 - f}{n}}
$$

where $f$ is the serial fraction and $n$ is the number of parallel workers.

**Step 2 — Identify parameters.**

$$
f = 0.05, \quad n = 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the parallel fraction $1 - f$.**

$$
1 - f = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the parallel component.**

$$
\frac{1 - f}{n} = \frac{0.95}{100} = 0.0095
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the serial and parallel components in the denominator.**

$$
f + \frac{1 - f}{n} = 0.05 + 0.0095 = 0.0595
$$

**Step 6 — Take the reciprocal to obtain the speedup.**

$$
S(100) = \frac{1}{0.0595} \approx 16.81
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the asymptotic upper bound (as $n \to \infty$).**

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even with infinite mappers, the speedup is capped at **20×** because the 5% serial bottleneck dominates. This is why Hadoop Library optimization focuses heavily on **reducing the serial fraction** (e.g., efficient data partitioning, minimizing shuffle I/O).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — NameNode Memory Sizing

> **Problem:** A cluster hosts **20 million** HDFS blocks. Estimate the recommended NameNode RAM and the number of DataNodes if each DataNode holds an average of 200 blocks.

**Step 1 — Apply the NameNode memory rule of thumb.**

$$
M_{\text{NN}} \approx \frac{B}{10^6} \text{ GB} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{10^6} \text{ GB} = 20 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of DataNodes.**

$$
N_{\text{DN}} = \frac{B}{\text{Blocks per DataNode}} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{200} = 100{,}000 \text{ DataNodes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the NameNode is a **centralized bottleneck** in HDFS — and the very reason Hadoop 3.x introduced **Federation** (multiple independent NameNodes) and **High Availability (HA)** with standby NameNodes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing HDFS, YARN, and MapReduce

| Parameter | HDFS (Storage) | YARN (Resource Mgmt) | MapReduce (Processing) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Master Component** | NameNode | ResourceManager | JobTracker (Hadoop 1) / ApplicationMaster (Hadoop 2+) |
| **Slave Component** | DataNode | NodeManager | Task (Map Task / Reduce Task) |
| **Failure Detection** | Heartbeat (3 s) | Heartbeat (3 s) | Task timeout (10 min default) |
| **Default Block / Unit Size** | 128 MB | Container (1–8 GB RAM) | Input Split ≈ HDFS Block |
| **Replication** | 3× across racks | N/A | Task re-execution (3 attempts default) |
| **Data Movement** | Block I/O | Container scheduling | Shuffle & Sort over network |
| **Failure Recovery** | Re-replicate from another replica | Container re-launch | Speculative execution / re-run |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Client Application"]:::client --> B["NameNode Master"]:::master
    B -->|"Metadata: file → block map"| A
    A -->|"Read / Write Request"| C["DataNode 1 Rack A"]:::slave
    A -->|"Read / Write Request"| D["DataNode 2 Rack B"]:::slave
    A -->|"Read / Write Request"| E["DataNode 3 Rack C"]:::slave
    B -.->|"Heartbeat + Block Report"| C
    B -.->|"Heartbeat + Block Report"| D
    B -.->|"Heartbeat + Block Report"| E
    F["Secondary NameNode"]:::secondary -.->|"Checkpoint merge of fsimage + edits"| B
    G["HDFS File 640 MB"]:::file -->|"Block 1 (128 MB)"| C
    G -->|"Block 1 (128 MB) Replica 2"| D
    G -->|"Block 1 (128 MB) Replica 3"| E
    G -->|"Block 2 (128 MB)"| C
    G -->|"Block 2 Replica 2"| D
    G -->|"Block 2 Replica 3"| E

    classDef client fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef master fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef secondary fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef file fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
```

> **Block-Level Explanation:**
> - The **Client** requests a file from the **NameNode**, which returns the block locations.
> - The **Client** then directly talks to the **DataNodes** for actual data transfer (the NameNode is not in the data path).
> - **Replication** distributes blocks across **different racks** to tolerate rack-level failures.
> - The **Secondary NameNode** periodically merges the edit log with the filesystem image (`fsimage`) to prevent the NameNode log from growing unbounded.

---

### 4.2 YARN Architecture — Resource Management Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Client YARN Submission"]:::client --> B["ResourceManager RM"]:::rm
    B --> C["Scheduler Capacity Fair Queue"]:::sched
    B --> D["ApplicationsManager AsM"]:::asm
    D -->|"Launches AppMaster"| E["ApplicationMaster AM"]:::am
    E -->|"Request Containers"| B
    B -->|"Allocate Container"| E
    E -->|"Launch Task"| F["NodeManager NM Node 1"]:::nm
    E -->|"Launch Task"| G["NodeManager NM Node 2"]:::nm
    E -->|"Launch Task"| H["NodeManager NM Node 3"]:::nm
    F --> F1["Container 1 Map Task"]:::container
    F --> F2["Container 2 Reduce Task"]:::container
    G --> G1["Container 3 Map Task"]:::container
    H --> H1["Container 4 Map Task"]:::container
    F -.->|"Heartbeat Container Status"| E
    G -.->|"Heartbeat Container Status"| E
    H -.->|"Heartbeat Container Status"| E

    classDef client fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef rm fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef sched fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F9A825,color:#000
    classDef asm fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef am fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef nm fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef container fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
```

> **Block-Level Explanation:**
> - The **ResourceManager** has two sub-components: the **Scheduler** (resource allocation policy) and the **ApplicationsManager** (lifecycle of ApplicationMasters).
> - Each application gets its own **ApplicationMaster**, which negotiates resources dynamically.
> - **NodeManagers** provide the actual CPU + RAM + disk containers where Map and Reduce tasks execute.

---

### 4.3 MapReduce Data Flow — Job Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["HDFS Block 1"]:::storage --> M1["Mapper 1"]:::map
    A2["HDFS Block 2"]:::storage --> M2["Mapper 2"]:::map
    A3["HDFS Block 3"]:::storage --> M3["Mapper 3"]:::map
    A4["HDFS Block 4"]:::storage --> M4["Mapper 4"]:::map
    M1 -->|"Intermediate k1,v1"| SS["Shuffle and Sort Phase"]:::shuffle
    M2 -->|"Intermediate k1,v1"| SS
    M3 -->|"Intermediate k1,v1"| SS
    M4 -->|"Intermediate k1,v1"| SS
    SS -->|"Grouped k2, list v2"| R1["Reducer 1"]:::reduce
    SS -->|"Grouped k2, list v2"| R2["Reducer 2"]:::reduce
    R1 --> O1["HDFS Output Part 1"]:::output
    R2 --> O2["HDFS Output Part 2"]:::output

    classDef storage fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef map fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef shuffle fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef reduce fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
```

> **Block-Level Explanation:**
> - **Mappers** run in parallel on nodes that host the corresponding HDFS blocks (**data locality**).
> - Intermediate output is written to **local disk**, not HDFS, to avoid expensive replication.
> - The **Shuffle & Sort** phase partitions, groups, and transfers data to **Reducers** over the network.
> - **Reducers** write the final aggregated output back to HDFS with the standard replication factor.

---

### 4.4 Hadoop Library Ecosystem — Component Inter-Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Hadoop Core Library"]:::core --> B["HDFS Storage"]:::core
    A --> C["YARN Resource Manager"]:::core
    A --> D["MapReduce Engine"]:::core
    A --> E["Hadoop Common Utils"]:::core
    B --> F["HBase NoSQL"]:::addon
    C --> G["Tez Execution Engine"]:::addon
    C --> H["Spark In Memory"]:::addon
    D --> I["Pig Latin Scripting"]:::addon
    D --> J["Hive SQL Engine"]:::addon
    B --> K["Sqoop RDBMS Bridge"]:::addon
    B --> L["Flume Log Collector"]:::addon
    C --> M["Oozie Workflow Scheduler"]:::addon
    A --> N["ZooKeeper Coordination"]:::addon
    A --> O["Ambari Cluster Management"]:::addon

    classDef core fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef addon fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the Hadoop Library and list its four core components.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Hadoop Library** is the Apache-Software-Foundation-maintained, open-source, Java-based framework for **distributed storage and processing of Big Data** across clusters of commodity computers. It abstracts the complexity of parallelization, fault tolerance, and data distribution.

The **four core components** are:

1. **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) — distributed, fault-tolerant storage.
2. **MapReduce** — distributed batch data-processing programming model.
3. **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) — cluster resource management and job scheduling.
4. **Hadoop Common** — shared utilities, libraries, and I/O routines used by all other modules.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition + 1 for the four components listing + 0.5 for any one-line role description per component.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the default HDFS block size and default replication factor? Why are these defaults chosen?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **default HDFS block size is 128 MB** (in Hadoop 2.x and later), and the **default replication factor is 3**.

**Why these defaults are chosen:**

- **128 MB block size:** Reduces the **number of disk seeks** by keeping each I/O operation large. For very large files, this minimizes the NameNode metadata overhead and maximizes sequential read throughput — critical for batch Big-Data processing.
- **Replication factor of 3:** Provides **fault tolerance** against up to two simultaneous DataNode or rack failures while keeping storage overhead bounded at 200% (an acceptable trade-off in commodity hardware environments).

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for the two values, 1 mark for the reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Q7(b)]**
> *(Both Question A and Question B are to be attempted internally; the better score is considered as per KTU 2024 norms.)*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **MapReduce execution model** in detail. Discuss the **Shuffle & Sort** phase and its significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**HDFS Architecture (Hadoop Distributed File System):**

HDFS follows a **master–slave architecture** designed to store very large files in a streaming access pattern across a cluster of commodity machines.

**Key Components:**

1. **NameNode (Master):**
   - Maintains the **filesystem metadata** (file-to-block mapping, block locations, replication details) in RAM and on disk.
   - Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 seconds) and **block reports** (every 6 hours) from all DataNodes.
   - Manages namespace operations (open, close, rename, delete).
   - Acts as the **single point of coordination** for all client requests (it does not handle the actual data transfer).

2. **DataNode (Slave):**
   - Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
   - On startup, performs a **handshake** with the NameNode.
   - Periodically sends **block reports** listing all blocks it hosts.
   - On receiving a write/read command, performs the I/O directly with the client.

3. **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):**
   - Does **not** act as a hot standby; its role is to **periodically merge** the edit log (`edits`) with the filesystem image (`fsimage`) to prevent the edit log from growing unbounded.
   - This produces a **checkpoint**, which the NameNode can later reload on restart, reducing startup time.

**Working Principle:**
- When a client wants to **read** a file, it contacts the NameNode to get the list of DataNodes hosting each block, then reads directly from the nearest DataNode.
- When a client wants to **write** a file, the NameNode allocates block IDs and a pipeline of DataNodes (typically across racks); the client streams the block in a chain (DataNode 1 → DataNode 2 → DataNode 3), with each replica acknowledging receipt.

**Fault Tolerance:** If a DataNode fails, the NameNode detects the missed heartbeat (timeout = 10 min), marks it as dead, and instructs other DataNodes to **re-replicate** the under-replicated blocks.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for component roles, 2 for working, 1 for fault tolerance, 2 for diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**MapReduce Execution Model:**

MapReduce is a **divide-and-conquer distributed programming model** with two user-defined functions and an implicit framework-managed phase.

**The two user-defined functions:**

$$
\text{Map}: (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)
$$

$$
\text{Reduce}: (k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)
$$

**Phases of Execution (with diagram):**

1. **Input Split Phase:** The input file is divided into **input splits** (typically equal to HDFS block size, 128 MB).
2. **Map Phase:** Each split is processed by a **Map task** running on the node that hosts the block (data locality). The map function emits intermediate key-value pairs.
3. **Partition & Combiner (Optional Mini-Reduce):** Intermediate pairs are **partitioned** by key (hash-based, one per reducer) and a **Combiner** may aggregate locally to reduce network traffic.
4. **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** Intermediate data is **transferred across the network** from map nodes to reduce nodes, where pairs are **sorted** and **grouped** by key.
5. **Reduce Phase:** The user-defined `reduce()` function is invoked once per unique key, processing its grouped value list.
6. **Output Phase:** Final output is written to HDFS with the default replication factor of 3.

**Significance of Shuffle & Sort:**
- It is the **most expensive phase** in MapReduce (network + disk I/O intensive).
- It determines the **overall job performance** more than any other phase.
- Optimizations target the shuffle: **combiner functions**, **compression of intermediate data**, and **map-side joins** are all designed to reduce shuffle volume.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for the two user functions, 3 for the six phases with diagram, 2 for shuffle & sort significance.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **YARN architecture** in detail. Describe the functions of the **ResourceManager**, **NodeManager**, and **ApplicationMaster**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable examples, discuss the **Hadoop ecosystem components** — Hive, Pig, HBase, Sqoop, and Flume. State one use-case for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) Architecture:**

YARN is the **cluster resource management layer** introduced in Hadoop 2.x to decouple resource management from the processing model. It allows multiple applications (MapReduce, Spark, Tez, etc.) to share the same cluster.

**Key Components:**

1. **ResourceManager (RM):** The **global authority** for resources. It has two sub-components:
   - **Scheduler:** Allocates resources (CPU, RAM) to applications based on capacity/fair policies.
   - **ApplicationsManager (AsM):** Accepts job submissions, launches the first container for the ApplicationMaster, and monitors AM liveness.

2. **NodeManager (NM):** The **per-node agent**. It manages the **containers** (resource envelopes), monitors their resource usage (CPU, memory, disk, network), and sends heartbeats to the RM.

3. **ApplicationMaster (AM):** One **per application**. It negotiates resources with the RM, works with the NMs to execute and monitor tasks, and handles fault tolerance (re-requesting failed containers).

4. **Container:** A **resource bundle** (RAM + vCores) granted by the RM on a specific node, in which a task runs.

**Execution Flow:**
1. Client submits application → RM accepts → allocates container → launches **AM**.
2. **AM** registers with RM, then requests additional containers for its tasks.
3. **AM** negotiates with NMs to launch tasks in containers.
4. On task completion, **AM** deregisters; RM reclaims resources.

*(7 marks breakdown: 2 for YARN role + decoupling, 3 for three components, 2 for execution flow.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Hadoop Ecosystem Components:**

| Component | Type | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hive** | SQL-on-Hadoop data warehouse | Ad-hoc analytical queries on petabyte-scale data using HQL (e.g., monthly sales aggregation) |
| **Pig** | Dataflow scripting language (Pig Latin) | ETL pipelines over semi-structured data (e.g., log cleaning, transformation before loading into HDFS) |
| **HBase** | Distributed NoSQL columnar store | Real-time random read/write access to Big Data (e.g., IoT sensor data, user-session storage) |
| **Sqoop** | RDBMS-to-Hadoop bulk transfer tool | Importing MySQL/Oracle tables into HDFS (e.g., nightly ETL of customer records) |
| **Flume** | Distributed log collection service | Streaming log files from web servers into HDFS (e.g., Apache/Nginx access logs) |

**Example Scenario (Unified):** A retail company receives **terabytes of web-server logs daily** (collected by **Flume**), imports **relational customer data** (via **Sqoop**), runs **ETL transformations** (in **Pig Latin**), stores processed results in **HBase** for real-time lookups, and runs **business-intelligence queries** (in **Hive**).

*(7 marks breakdown: 1 each for the five components with use case — 5 marks, 2 marks for the integrated example.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Hadoop Library Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing NameNode vs Secondary NameNode:** Students often incorrectly claim the Secondary NameNode is a "backup NameNode." It is **NOT** — it only performs **checkpointing** of the edit log. The actual HA standby is the **Standby NameNode** (introduced in Hadoop 2.x with ZooKeeper-based leader election).
> 2. **Mixing up HDFS and MapReduce roles:** HDFS does **storage**; MapReduce does **processing**; YARN does **resource management**. Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** per correct attribution. Conflating them leads to 2–3 mark deductions.
> 3. **Forgetting the Shuffle & Sort phase:** This is the **most-tested phase**. Always draw it explicitly in the diagram; do not skip it between Map and Reduce.
> 4. **Wrong default values:** Using 64 MB (Hadoop 1.x default) instead of 128 MB (Hadoop 2.x+ default) is marked incorrect in KTU 2024.
> 5. **Ignoring data locality:** Examiners reward answers that mention **map tasks running on nodes hosting the corresponding HDFS block** (data locality optimization).
> 6. **No diagram = 2 mark penalty:** In 7-mark sub-questions, a missing or poor block diagram incurs a flat **2-mark deduction** per KTU valuation norms.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hadoop Library (KTU 2024 Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Hadoop Library** = Apache Hadoop framework = HDFS + MapReduce + YARN + Hadoop Common.
>
> ✅ **HDFS block size = 128 MB** (default in Hadoop 2.x+); **replication factor = 3**.
>
> ✅ **NameNode** = master, holds **metadata** in RAM, does NOT handle data transfer.
>
> ✅ **DataNode** = slave, stores actual **blocks**, sends **heartbeat (3 s)** + **block report (6 h)**.
>
> ✅ **Secondary NameNode** = **checkpointing** node (NOT a hot standby).
>
> ✅ **MapReduce** = Map + Reduce with implicit **Shuffle & Sort** in between.
>
> ✅ Intermediate output is written to **local disk** (not HDFS) to save replication cost.
>
> ✅ **YARN** = ResourceManager (master) + NodeManager (slave) + ApplicationMaster (per-job) + Container.
>
> ✅ **Data locality** = mappers prefer to run on nodes holding the relevant HDFS block.
>
> ✅ **Amdahl's Law** limits speedup: $S(n) = 1 / (f + (1-f)/n)$; bottleneck = serial fraction $f$.
>
> ✅ **Ecosystem:** Hive (SQL), Pig (ETL), HBase (NoSQL), Sqoop (RDBMS bridge), Flume (log streaming), Oozie (scheduler), ZooKeeper (coordination), Spark (in-memory).
>
> ✅ **Cloud integration:** Hadoop is deployed on AWS EMR, Azure HDInsight, GCP Dataproc as **BDaaS**.
>
> ✅ **Fault tolerance:** data replication + speculative execution + task re-execution (max 3 attempts).
>
> ✅ **Hadoop 1.x vs 2.x+:** Hadoop 1 had JobTracker+TaskTracker; Hadoop 2 replaced them with **YARN + ApplicationMaster** to support non-MapReduce workloads (Spark, Tez).
>
> ✅ **KTU 2024 focus areas:** Architecture diagrams, default configuration values, working of MapReduce phases, and the role of each Hadoop component in cloud-based Big-Data processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apache Spark. Cloud Software Environments - Eucalyptus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Apache Spark. Cloud Software Environments - Eucalyptus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Apache Spark — The Lightning-Fast Cluster Computing Engine

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)
**Apache Spark** is an **open-source, distributed, in-memory data processing engine** designed for fast, large-scale data analytics, machine learning, and stream processing workloads. Originally developed at **UC Berkeley's AMPLab** in **2009** and donated to the **Apache Software Foundation** in **2013**, Spark extends the **MapReduce** model with a powerful abstraction called the **Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD)** and a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** execution engine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Recall):** *Apache Spark is a unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing, providing high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python, and R, along with an optimized engine that supports general execution graphs and in-memory data sharing across computations.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Sorting Analogy"
Imagine a massive library with **10 million books** scattered across **100 branches**. A traditional MapReduce librarian would:
1. Read every book, write a summary to a new ledger.
2. **Throw the ledger away** after each task.
3. Start from scratch for the next task.

**Spark** is like a librarian who keeps the sorted ledger **in memory** on a clipboard. The next time a query comes, he doesn't re-read the books — he just reuses the **in-memory sorted ledger**, answering queries up to **100x faster**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** Spark can run programs up to **100x faster** than Hadoop MapReduce in memory, or **10x faster** on disk. It processes data in batches of milliseconds, making it ideal for **iterative algorithms** (ML) and **interactive analytics**.

---

## 1.2 Eucalyptus — The Open-Source AWS-Compatible Private Cloud

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)
**Eucalyptus (Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems)** is an open-source software platform for implementing **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** private and hybrid clouds. It enables organizations to build their own AWS-compatible cloud infrastructure using their existing hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Eucalyptus is an open-source cloud-computing infrastructure that implements the AWS EC2, S3, and IAM APIs, allowing private cloud deployments that are compatible with public AWS services for hybrid cloud portability.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Franchise Model"
Think of **AWS** as a global McDonald's franchise with strict standardization. **Eucalyptus** is like opening your **own restaurant** that uses the **same recipes, kitchen equipment, and menu** as McDonald's (i.e., the same APIs), so:
- Customers using the same "app" (AWS CLI/SDK) can order from your restaurant seamlessly.
- If your restaurant gets too crowded, customers can be redirected to McDonald's — the experience is **identical**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** Originally developed as a research project at the **University of California, Santa Barbara (UCSB)** in **2008**, Eucalyptus was one of the earliest AWS-API-compatible private cloud platforms, with Eucalyptus Systems Inc. founded in **2009**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Did You Know?** The name "Eucalyptus" was chosen because it is **an acronym** AND a real word — perfect for a project that wanted to "link" enterprise systems to cloud APIs (EUCALYPTUS = Elastic Utility Computing Architecture for Linking Your Programs To Useful Systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Apache Spark — Architecture & Components

### 2.1.1 Spark Architecture (Layered View)

**A. Spark Application Execution Flow (Driver–Executor Model)**

The Spark architecture follows a **master–worker pattern** with the following key entities:

| Component | Role | Running On |
|-----------|------|------------|
| **Driver Program** | Runs the `main()` function, creates `SparkContext`, builds DAG, schedules tasks | Client / Master node |
| **Cluster Manager** | Allocates resources across applications (Standalone, YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes) | Dedicated daemon |
| **Executor** | Runs tasks, caches data in memory/disk, reports back to driver | Worker nodes |
| **Task** | Smallest unit of work; one partition = one task | Executors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Driver** and the **Executors** are collectively called the **Spark Application**. A single Spark application can be split into **multiple jobs**, each job into **stages**, and each stage into **tasks**.

**B. Execution Hierarchy:**
```
Spark Application
   └── Job (triggered by action)
         └── Stage (separated by shuffles)
               └── Task (one per partition)
```

### 2.1.2 RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset) — The Core Abstraction

An **RDD** is the fundamental data structure of Spark, defined by **5 key properties**:

1. **Partitions** — Dataset divided into logical chunks for parallel processing.
2. **Dependencies** — Lineage of how this RDD was derived (narrow vs wide).
3. **Compute Function** — Logic to compute each partition.
4. **Partitioner (optional)** — Custom partitioning (e.g., HashPartitioner).
5. **List of Preferred Locations (optional)** — HDFS block locations for data locality.

### 2.1.3 Spark Ecosystem (Libraries)

| Library | Purpose | Use Case |
|---------|---------|----------|
| **Spark SQL** | Structured data processing with DataFrames/Datasets | ETL, BI queries |
| **Spark Streaming** | Real-time stream processing (DStreams) | Log monitoring, IoT |
| **MLlib** | Scalable machine learning library | Classification, clustering, regression |
| **GraphX** | Graph computation and graph-parallel algorithms | Social networks, PageRank |
| **SparkR** | R API for Spark | Statistical computing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unified Stack:** Spark combines batch, streaming, interactive, and ML workloads into a single engine — eliminating the need for separate tools (Hadoop, Storm, Impala, Mahout).

---

## 2.2 Eucalyptus — Architecture & Components

### 2.2.1 Eucalyptus High-Level Architecture

Eucalyptus consists of **5 main components** distributed across the cloud:

| Component | Full Name | Function | AWS Equivalent |
|-----------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| **CLC** | Cloud Controller | Front-end, user authentication, resource orchestration, VM management, snapshot management | AWS Management Console + EC2 API |
| **Walrus** | Walrus Storage | S3-compatible object storage for images, snapshots, user data | Amazon S3 |
| **CC** | Cluster Controller | Schedules VMs on specific Node Controllers, manages network (VLAN/VPC) | EC2 Region-level controller |
| **SC** | Storage Controller | Block storage (EBS-equivalent) attached to VMs, manages persistent volumes | Amazon EBS |
| **NC** | Node Controller | Hypervisor (KVM/Xen) host that runs VMs | EC2 Instance host |

### 2.2.2 Eucalyptus Network Modes

| Mode | Description |
|------|-------------|
| **SYSTEM** | All instances get IPs from the system network; simplest, no isolation |
| **STATIC** | Administrator assigns a fixed IP range; uses VLAN tagging for isolation |
| **MANAGED (VLAN)** | Dynamic VLAN creation per instance; full isolation; default in newer versions |
| **MANAGED-NOVLAN** | Like MANAGED but without VLAN tagging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** The relationship is: **CLC → CC → NC → VM**. The CLC talks to one or more CCs, each CC controls multiple NCs, and each NC runs VMs.

### 2.2.3 AWS API Compatibility — The Killer Feature

Eucalyptus implements the **same APIs** as AWS, meaning:
- Same **EC2 API** for compute (run/start/stop instances).
- Same **S3 API** for object storage.
- Same **IAM API** for identity.
- Tools like **AWS CLI, Boto3, and EC2 API tools** work against Eucalyptus unchanged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid Cloud Benefit:** This compatibility enables **cloud bursting** — workloads running on a private Eucalyptus cloud can burst to public AWS during peak load, using the same scripts and SDKs.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for Numerical & Theory Problems**

### Spark Performance Formulas

| Formula | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| $T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{compute}} + T_{\text{write}}$ | Total task time |
| $N_{\text{tasks}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i$ | Total tasks = sum of partitions across all RDDs in a stage |
| $S_{\text{speedup}} = \frac{T_{\text{sequential}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}} = \frac{T_{\text{seq}}}{T_{\text{seq}} \cdot \frac{1}{n} + T_{\text{overhead}}}$ | Amdahl's Law applied to Spark parallelization |
| $C_{\text{executor}} = N_{\text{cores}} \times M_{\text{heap}}$ | Executor capacity (cores × memory) |
| $P_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{D_{\text{total}}}{M_{\text{per-node}}}$ | Ideal number of partitions (data size / per-node memory) |

### Eucalyptus Network Isolation Formulas

| Formula | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| $N_{\text{VMs per NC}} = \left\lfloor \frac{R_{\text{NC RAM}} - R_{\text{hypervisor}}}{R_{\text{VM RAM}}} \right\rfloor$ | Max VMs per Node Controller |
| $V_{\text{available}} = V_{\text{total}} - V_{\text{overhead}}$ | Usable VLANs after management VLAN |
| $B_{\text{overhead}} = T_{\text{network}} + T_{\text{disk}} + T_{\text{API}}$ | Burst overhead in hybrid cloud |

### Key Comparison Table

| Feature | Apache Spark | Eucalyptus |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| **Category** | Data processing engine | IaaS cloud platform |
| **Primary Use** | Big data analytics, ML, stream processing | Private/hybrid cloud deployment |
| **Open Source** | Yes (Apache 2.0) | Yes (GPLv3) |
| **API Compatibility** | Custom (Scala/Python/R/Java) | AWS EC2, S3, IAM |
| **Architecture** | Driver + Executors + Cluster Manager | CLC + Walrus + CC + SC + NC |
| **Origin** | UC Berkeley AMPLab, 2009 | UC Santa Barbara, 2008 |
| **Key Abstraction** | RDD / DataFrame | Virtual Machine / EBS Volume |
| **Hypervisor Support** | N/A (works on top of cluster) | KVM, Xen, VMware |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Reality Check:** Spark is **not a cloud platform** — it is a **compute engine** that runs *on* a cloud (e.g., on AWS EC2, Azure HDInsight, or Databricks). Eucalyptus *is* a cloud platform that *provides* VMs on which you could run Spark.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Apache Spark — Lazy Evaluation & DAG Construction

### 3.1.1 Transformations vs Actions (The Lazy Execution Model)

Spark uses **lazy evaluation**: nothing is computed until an **action** is triggered. Let us trace through a canonical Word Count example.

**Problem Statement:** Count the frequency of each word in a 100 GB text file.

**Step 1: Reading the Data**
```scala
val textFile = sc.textFile("hdfs:///data/shakespeare.txt")
```
- `textFile` is an RDD of strings, with each line as a partition.
- This is a **transformation** — **not executed yet**.

**Step 2: Splitting Lines into Words**
```scala
val words = textFile.flatMap(line => line.split(" "))
```
- `flatMap` is a **narrow transformation** (no shuffling).
- Still no execution.

**Step 3: Mapping Each Word to a Tuple**
```scala
val pairs = words.map(word => (word, 1))
```
- `map` is a **narrow transformation**.
- Still no execution.

**Step 4: Reducing by Key**
```scala
val counts = pairs.reduceByKey((a, b) => a + b)
```
- `reduceByKey` is a **wide transformation** (involves **shuffle**).
- Still no execution!

**Step 5: The Triggering Action**
```scala
counts.saveAsTextFile("hdfs:///output/wordcount")
```
- `saveAsTextFile` is an **action** — **NOW Spark executes the entire DAG**.

### 3.1.2 DAG Construction (Logical → Physical Plan)

The DAG scheduler converts the RDD lineage into a **physical execution plan**:

$$\text{Logical Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{DAG Scheduler}} \text{Stages} \xrightarrow{\text{Task Scheduler}} \text{Tasks}$$

**Derived Stage Division Logic:**

Let $S$ be the set of transformations. Stage boundaries occur at **wide dependencies** (shuffles). Mathematically:

$$S_i = \{T \in \text{transformations} \mid T \text{ has only narrow dependencies}\} \cup \{\text{shuffle boundary}\}$$

**Narrow vs Wide Dependency:**

$$\text{Narrow: } \text{parent partition} \rightarrow \text{one child partition (1-to-1 or N-to-1 map)}$$

$$\text{Wide: } \text{parent partitions} \rightarrow \text{multiple child partitions (all-to-all shuffle)}$$

**Example: Word Count DAG Stages**

```
Stage 0:  textFile → flatMap → map   (narrow, no shuffle)
   [shuffle boundary]
Stage 1:  reduceByKey                (wide, shuffle required)
   [action: saveAsTextFile]
```

**Speedup Derivation (Why Spark is Fast):**

For an iterative algorithm (e.g., k-means clustering with $I$ iterations):

$$T_{\text{MapReduce}} = I \times (T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}} + T_{\text{write}})$$

$$T_{\text{Spark}} = T_{\text{read}} + I \times (T_{\text{compute}}) + T_{\text{write}}$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{MapReduce}}}{T_{\text{Spark}}} = \frac{I \times (T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{write}} + T_{\text{shuffle}})}{T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{write}}}$$

**For $I = 10$ iterations with $T_{\text{read}} = T_{\text{write}} = T_{\text{shuffle}} = 60$s:**

$$T_{\text{MapReduce}} = 10 \times (60 + 60 + 60) = 1800 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{\text{Spark}} = 60 + 10 \times 30 + 60 = 420 \text{ seconds}$$

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{1800}{420} \approx 4.3\times$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, Spark achieves **10–100x** speedup for iterative ML workloads because the in-memory caching eliminates **disk I/O** between iterations — the dominant bottleneck in MapReduce.

---

## 3.2 Eucalyptus — VM Scheduling & Capacity Analysis

### 3.2.1 Analytical VM Capacity Matrix

**Problem:** A university data center has **5 Node Controllers (NCs)**, each with **128 GB RAM**. The hypervisor consumes **4 GB** per NC. The university wants to run **VM instances** of three types:

| VM Type | vCPU | RAM (GB) |
|---------|------|----------|
| `m1.small` | 1 | 2 |
| `m1.medium` | 2 | 4 |
| `c1.xlarge` | 4 | 16 |

**Step 1: Compute Available RAM per NC**

$$R_{\text{available per NC}} = R_{\text{total}} - R_{\text{hypervisor}} = 128 - 4 = 124 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2: Maximum VMs per Type (if all allocated to one type)**

For `m1.small`:
$$N_{\text{m1.small}} = \frac{R_{\text{available}}}{R_{\text{VM}}} = \frac{124}{2} = 62 \text{ VMs per NC}$$

For `m1.medium`:
$$N_{\text{m1.medium}} = \frac{124}{4} = 31 \text{ VMs per NC}$$

For `c1.xlarge`:
$$N_{\text{c1.xlarge}} = \frac{124}{16} = 7.75 \approx 7 \text{ VMs per NC}$$

**Step 3: Total Capacity Across 5 NCs**

| VM Type | Per NC | Total (5 NCs) |
|---------|--------|---------------|
| m1.small | 62 | 310 |
| m1.medium | 31 | 155 |
| c1.xlarge | 7 | 35 |

**Step 4: Mixed Allocation (Integer Linear Programming)**

Suppose the university wants to allocate $x$ `m1.small`, $y$ `m1.medium`, and $z$ `c1.xlarge` per NC, with the constraint:

$$2x + 4y + 16z \leq 124$$

And objective to maximize total VMs per NC:

$$\text{Maximize } (x + y + z)$$

**Solution (e.g., 50 small + 6 medium + 0 xlarge):**

$$2(50) + 4(6) + 16(0) = 100 + 24 + 0 = 124 \text{ GB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Total VMs per NC = 56; Total across 5 NCs = 280.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** The Cluster Controller (CC) does this scheduling dynamically using `euca-describe-availability-zones` and `euca-run-instances` APIs, similar to how AWS EC2 places instances across Availability Zones.

### 3.3.2 Hybrid Cloud Bursting — Latency Analysis

**Problem:** A workload runs 80% on private Eucalyptus and bursts 20% to public AWS.

$$T_{\text{hybrid}} = 0.8 \times T_{\text{private}} + 0.2 \times T_{\text{public}} + T_{\text{network-overhead}}$$

For a 1-hour job, $T_{\text{private}} = 60$ min, $T_{\text{public}} = 55$ min (AWS slightly faster hardware), $T_{\text{network}} = 2$ min:

$$T_{\text{hybrid}} = 0.8 \times 60 + 0.2 \times 55 + 2 = 48 + 11 + 2 = 61 \text{ minutes}$$

This shows the **negligible cost** of cloud bursting if the API compatibility is preserved (the hallmark of Eucalyptus).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apache Spark Cluster Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Driver["Driver Program\nSparkContext + DAG Scheduler\nTask Scheduler"]
    CM["Cluster Manager\nStandalone / YARN / Mesos / K8s"]
    Exec1["Executor 1\nCache + Tasks"]
    Exec2["Executor 2\nCache + Tasks"]
    Exec3["Executor 3\nCache + Tasks"]
    RDD1["RDD Partitions\nP1 P2 P3 P4"]
    DAG["DAG of Stages\nStage 0 Stage 1 Stage 2"]

    User["User Application\nmain function"] --> Driver
    Driver --> CM
    CM --> Exec1
    CM --> Exec2
    CM --> Exec3
    Driver --> DAG
    RDD1 --> Exec1
    RDD1 --> Exec2
    RDD1 --> Exec3
    Exec1 --> Driver
    Exec2 --> Driver
    Exec3 --> Driver
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Apache Spark):**

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Driver Program** | Holds the `SparkContext`, builds the DAG, schedules tasks, coordinates executors |
| **Cluster Manager** | Allocates CPU/memory resources to Spark applications (4 supported types) |
| **Executors** | JVM processes that run tasks and store RDD partitions in memory/disk |
| **RDD Lineage Graph** | Logical execution plan — each transformation produces a new RDD node |
| **DAG Scheduler** | Splits the DAG into stages at shuffle boundaries |
| **Task Scheduler** | Dispatches individual tasks to executors with data locality awareness |

---

## 4.2 Eucalyptus Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    User["User / AWS CLI / Boto3"]
    CLC["Cloud Controller CLC\nUser Auth + Resource Orchestration\nEucalyptus + EC2 API"]
    Walrus["Walrus Storage\nS3 compatible Object Store\nVM Images + Snapshots"]
    CC1["Cluster Controller CC1\nSchedules VMs on NCs\nNetwork Management"]
    CC2["Cluster Controller CC2\nSchedules VMs on NCs\nNetwork Management"]
    SC["Storage Controller SC\nEBS equivalent Block Storage\nPersistent Volumes"]
    NC1["Node Controller NC1\nKVM Xen Hypervisor\nRuns VMs"]
    NC2["Node Controller NC2\nKVM Xen Hypervisor\nRuns VMs"]
    NC3["Node Controller NC3\nKVM Xen Hypervisor\nRuns VMs"]
    NC4["Node Controller NC4\nKVM Xen Hypervisor\nRuns VMs"]

    User --> CLC
    CLC --> Walrus
    CLC --> CC1
    CLC --> CC2
    CLC --> SC
    CC1 --> NC1
    CC1 --> NC2
    CC2 --> NC3
    CC2 --> NC4
    SC --> NC1
    SC --> NC2
    SC --> NC3
    SC --> NC4
    Walrus --> NC1
    Walrus --> NC2
    Walrus --> NC3
    Walrus --> NC4
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Eucalyptus):**

| Block | Function | AWS Analog |
|-------|----------|------------|
| **CLC (Cloud Controller)** | Top-level orchestrator: user authentication, VM instance management, snapshot handling, IP pool management | AWS Management Console + EC2 |
| **Walrus** | Persistent object storage for VM images (Eucalyptus Machine Images — EMI) and EBS snapshots; **S3-compatible** | Amazon S3 |
| **Cluster Controller (CC)** | Mid-level scheduler: decides which NC runs a VM, handles network isolation (VLANs in MANAGED mode) | EC2 Region Controller |
| **Storage Controller (SC)** | Provides persistent block storage volumes attachable to VMs; uses iSCSI or AoE | Amazon EBS |
| **Node Controller (NC)** | Lowest-level hypervisor host; boots VMs from images pulled from Walrus, attaches SC volumes | EC2 Instance Host |
| **CLC ↔ Walrus** | Image registration, snapshot storage | AWS S3 |
| **NC ↔ Walrus** | VM image download on instance boot | EC2 AMI download |
| **NC ↔ SC** | Volume attach/detach operations | EBS Volume attach/detach |

> [!NOTE]
> **Communication Protocol:** All Eucalyptus components communicate over **SOAP/HTTPS web services**, mimicking the AWS service-oriented architecture. The CLC exposes the **EC2-compatible API**; Walrus exposes the **S3-compatible API**.
