topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OpenStack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — OPENSTACK: CORE CONCEPTS, ARCHITECTURE & SERVICES

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **OpenStack** is a free, open-source **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** cloud computing platform that orchestrates and manages large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources across a data center. It is governed by the **OpenStack Foundation** (now part of the Open Infrastructure Foundation) and is built on a modular architecture composed of several interrelated sub-projects (services) that communicate via RESTful APIs and a centralized message queue.

According to the official OpenStack documentation, the platform provides a **"Cloud Operating System"** that controls large collections of compute, storage, and networking resources throughout a data center. All management tasks are exposed through a **RESTful HTTP API**, allowing orchestration engines, dashboards, and command-line tools to interact uniformly with the cloud.

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Initial Release | **July 2010** (combination of NASA Nova + Rackspace Swift) |
| License | **Apache 2.0** (Open Source) |
| Language Stack | Primarily **Python** |
| API Standard | **REST + JSON over HTTP/HTTPS** |
| Message Broker | **RabbitMQ / AMQP** |
| Database Backend | **MySQL / MariaDB / PostgreSQL** |
| Hypervisor Support | KVM, XenServer, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V, LXC |
| Current Version Focus | Antelope, Bobcat, Caracal, Dalmatian, Epoxy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (PEITT523 Module 4):** OpenStack falls under the *"Cloud Platforms and Tools"* cluster. Students are expected to understand its architectural philosophy, core component services, and its role as the leading **open-source alternative to AWS, Azure, and GCP** in private/hybrid cloud deployments.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cloud Construction Company"

Imagine a massive **gigantic apartment-building construction company** that needs to manage many buildings:

- The **CEO's office (Horizon)** is the glass-walled reception where any tenant walks in and says, "I want a 3-room apartment, with parking, a high-speed internet cable, and a safe locker room."
- The **Building Engineer (Nova)** immediately dispatches workers to build the apartment, choosing the right materials and floor (hypervisor & flavor).
- The **Locksmith (Keystone)** verifies the tenant's ID card, checks permissions, and issues access keys.
- The **Interior Designer (Glance)** brings the pre-furnished apartment template (image) — furnished, unfurnished, or premium.
- The **Electrician & Cable Network Team (Neutron)** lays out the routers, switches, subnets, and floating IPs so apartments can communicate.
- The **Storage Warehouse (Cinder + Swift)** provides either personal lockers (block storage) or a giant shared warehouse (object storage) for tenant's goods.
- The **Accountant (Ceilometer)** tracks every watt of electricity and liter of water used by each apartment for billing.
- The **Project Manager (Heat)** reads the architectural blueprint (template) and orchestrates the entire construction in a single click.

> **That "construction company" is OpenStack.** It is not a single product — it is an **ecosystem of cooperating services** that together deliver a self-service, programmable cloud.

---

## 1.3 The Six Pillars of OpenStack Philosophy

1. **Open Source** — No vendor lock-in; community-driven.
2. **Modular** — Each service is a standalone project with its own release cycle.
3. **API-First** — Every service speaks **REST/JSON**; the GUI is just one of many frontends.
4. **Scalability** — Horizontally scalable — add more compute nodes as demand grows.
5. **Interoperability** — Compatible with AWS EC2/S3 APIs (via plugins like ElasticStack).
6. **Standardization** — Follows **DMTF standards (CIMI, OVF)** and **OASIS TOSCA** for templates.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Top OpenStack Services → "King Nova Eats Greasy Hamburgers, Then Sips Cold Juice"**
> **K**eystone • **N**ova • **G**lance • **H**orizon • **T**rove • **S**wift • **C**inder • **J**uJu (or **N**eutron, **H**eat, **C**eilometer)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — ARCHITECTURE, COMPONENTS & FORMULAS

## 2.1 The OpenStack Logical Architecture

OpenStack follows a **shared-nothing, message-driven, multi-tenant** architecture. Conceptually, it is divided into **regions → availability zones → host aggregates**, and every service is a stateless daemon (or set of daemons) communicating via AMQP and a central database.

### 2.1.1 The Tier-1 Conceptual Layers

| Logical Tier | Concern | Representative Services |
|---|---|---|
| **Identity & Authentication** | Who are you? | Keystone |
| **Resource Provisioning (Compute)** | Run my VM | Nova |
| **Resource Provisioning (Network)** | Connect my VM | Neutron |
| **Resource Provisioning (Storage — Block)** | Attach a hard disk | Cinder |
| **Resource Provisioning (Storage — Object)** | Store files | Swift |
| **Image / Template** | Pre-built OS | Glance |
| **Orchestration** | Multi-VM app templates | Heat |
| **Telemetry / Billing** | How much did I use? | Ceilometer / Gnocchi / Panko |
| **Dashboard** | Web UI | Horizon |
| **Database as a Service** | Managed DB | Trove |
| **Container** | Run Docker/Kubernetes | Magnum / Zun |
| **Shared File System** | NFS-like | Manila |
| **DNS** | Domain resolution | Designate |
| **Key Manager** | Encryption keys | Barbican |
| **Bare-Metal Provisioning** | Physical servers | Ironic |
| **Message Bus** | Internal communication | RabbitMQ |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Component-Level Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why each component exists:** Every OpenStack service solves a specific, well-defined problem in cloud data center management. KTU questions frequently ask *"Explain the role of `<service>`"*. Master these.

### 🟦 1. NOVA — The Compute Service (Project Name: *OpenStack Compute*)

**Purpose:** Launches, schedules, and supervises **virtual machines (instances)** on hypervisors.

**Sub-Processes (Daemons):**
- `nova-api` — REST API endpoint (accepts user requests).
- `nova-scheduler` — Decides *which* compute node will run the VM (using filters & weighers like `AvailabilityZoneFilter`, `RamFilter`, `ComputeFilter`).
- `nova-conductor` — Mediates DB access (compute nodes never touch DB directly — improves security).
- `nova-compute` — Daemon running on each compute node that talks to the hypervisor (e.g., libvirt for KVM).
- `nova-consoleauth` & `nova-novncproxy` — Provides browser-based VNC/console access.
- `nova-placement` — Tracks inventory of resources (since Queens release).

**Scheduler Decision Formula (Conceptual):**

$$
\text{Score}_{\text{host}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot f_i(\text{host}, \text{request})
$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of the $i$-th weigher, and $f_i$ is the value returned by the function. The host with the **highest positive weighted sum** wins.

---

### 🟩 2. NEUTRON — The Networking Service (Project Name: *OpenStack Networking*)

**Purpose:** Provides **Networking-as-a-Service (NaaS)** — manages networks, subnets, routers, ports, floating IPs, security groups, and load balancers.

**Core Objects:**
- **Network** — An isolated L2 broadcast domain.
- **Subnet** — A block of IPv4/IPv6 addresses within a network.
- **Router** — A virtual router that connects subnets (and to the external network).
- **Port** — A connection point attaching a NIC to a network.
- **Floating IP** — A **1-to-1 NAT** mapping a public IP to a private IP, enabling inbound traffic.
- **Security Group** — Stateful L4 firewall rules attached to VM NICs.

**Plugin Architecture:** Neutron is *plugin-driven*. ML2 (Modular Layer 2) is the standard core plugin, with mechanism drivers such as:
- **Open vSwitch (OVS)** — most common.
- **Linux Bridge** — simpler, common in devstack.
- **OVN (Open Virtual Network)** — newer, more scalable.

**Two Network Types:**
1. **Provider Networks** — Pre-created by the admin; flat, simple.
2. **Self-Service (Tenant) Networks** — Created by users; use overlay (VXLAN/GRE) for isolation.

---

### 🟧 3. CINDER — The Block Storage Service

**Purpose:** Provides **persistent block storage volumes** (akin to EBS in AWS) attachable to VMs.

- A Cinder volume survives VM termination (unlike ephemeral disk).
- Backed by LVM, Ceph RBD, NFS, NetApp, EMC, etc.
- Supports snapshots, clones, and volume types (e.g., *ssd-fast*, *hdd-cheap*).

**Volume Attachment Flow:** VM ←→ iSCSI / FC / RBD ←→ Cinder Volume ←→ Backend Storage

---

### 🟨 4. SWIFT — The Object Storage Service

**Purpose:** Highly available, distributed **object/blob storage** (akin to AWS S3) for unstructured data — images, videos, backups.

**Key Concepts:**
- **Ring** — A consistent-hash map of data across physical storage nodes.
- **Zone** — A physical/logical isolation unit (rack, disk, data center) for fault tolerance.
- **Replica** — Data is stored with **3x replication by default** to ensure durability.

**Storage Requirements (Durability Math):**

$$
P_{\text{data loss}} = \prod_{i=1}^{r} p_{i}
$$

where $r$ is the replication factor and $p_i$ is the probability of failure of replica $i$. With **3 replicas** and each disk having a 5% annual failure rate:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = (0.05)^{3} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.0125\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The probability of all three replicas failing simultaneously is roughly **1 in 8000** — giving the **eleven-nines (99.999999999%) durability** that object stores promise.

---

### 🟥 5. KEYSTONE — The Identity Service

**Purpose:** Single source of truth for **authentication, authorization, and service catalog**.

**Key Concepts:**
- **User** — A digital identity (person or service).
- **Project (Tenant)** — An isolation unit (organizational container for resources).
- **Role** — A set of privileges (e.g., `admin`, `member`, `reader`).
- **Token** — A time-limited credential (UUID, PKI, Fernet, JWT) issued after authentication.
- **Service Catalog** — A list of endpoints (URLs) for every service.
- **Domain** — A higher-level container for projects and users (added in Juno).

**Authentication Workflow:**

$$
\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{credentials}} \text{Keystone} \xrightarrow{\text{verifies}} \text{Token} \xrightarrow{\text{used in}} \text{Every Other Service}
$$

---

### 🟪 6. GLANCE — The Image Service

**Purpose:** Stores and retrieves **VM disk images** (templates).
- Supports formats: **qcow2, vhd, vmdk, raw, ami, aki, ari**.
- Image statuses: `queued → saving → active → killed/deactivated`.
- Image visibility: `public`, `private`, `shared`, `community`.
- Can store image data on filesystem, Swift, Ceph, S3, HTTP.

---

### 🟫 7. HORIZON — The Dashboard

**Purpose:** A **Django-based web UI** for managing the entire cloud via the browser.
- Provides read/write operations for most services.
- Customizable branding per cloud provider.
- Not required for API-driven automation.

---

### 🟦 8. HEAT — The Orchestration Service

**Purpose:** Implements a **declarative template-driven** orchestration engine using **HOT (Heat Orchestration Template)** files (YAML/JSON), inspired by AWS CloudFormation.

**HOT Template Skeleton:**

```yaml
HeatTemplateFormatVersion: '2012-12-12'
Description: My first OpenStack stack
Resources:
  my_instance:
    Type: OS::Nova::Server
    Properties:
      flavor: m1.small
      image: ubuntu-22.04
      networks:
        - network: my_net
```

---

### 🟩 9. CEILOMETER + GNOCCHI + PANKO — The Telemetry Stack

- **Ceilometer** — Collects metering/metrics from all services.
- **Gnocchi** — Time-series database for metrics storage.
- **Panko** — Stores event data.
- **Aodh** — Alarms engine (triggers on thresholds).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Quick Reference

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| VM Sizing | $\text{RAM}_{\text{flavor}} = \text{overcommit\_ratio} \times \text{RAM}_{\text{host}}$ | Ratio: typically **1.5 : 1** to **2 : 1** |
| vCPU Allocation | $\text{vCPU}_{\text{total}} = \text{pCPU} \times \text{CPU\_overcommit}$ | Common ratio: **16 : 1** |
| Storage Replication | $D = 1 - (1 - p_{\text{disk}})^{r}$ | $D$ = durability, $p_{\text{disk}}$ = disk failure rate, $r$ = replicas |
| Object Storage Capacity | $C_{\text{usable}} = \dfrac{N \times S}{r}$ | $N$ = nodes, $S$ = storage per node, $r$ = replica count |
| Floating IP NAT | $\text{PubIP} \xleftrightarrow{\text{1:1 NAT}} \text{PrivIP}$ | Stateless DNAT + SNAT |
| Scheduler Weight | $W = \sum w_{i} f_{i}$ | Highest $W$ wins the scheduling decision |
| Hypervisor CPU Pinning | $\text{VCPU}_{\text{pinned}} = \text{PCPU}_{\text{host}}$ | Used for NFV / real-time workloads |
| Keystone Token TTL | $\text{Token} = f(\text{user}, \text{creds}, \text{TTL})$ | Default TTL: **1 hour** (Fernet) |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The overcommit ratios and replication math are *not* academic — they are critical for **cloud capacity planning**. KTU frequently tests whether students can size an OpenStack cluster for $N$ tenants given these ratios.

---

## 2.4 OpenStack Deployment Topologies

| Topology | Components on a Single Node | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **All-in-One (DevStack)** | All services | Development, learning, POCs |
| **Two-Node** | Controller + Compute | Small labs |
| **Three-Node** | Controller + Compute + Storage | Small prod |
| **Multi-Node HA** | Separate Controller (3-node cluster), Compute (N), Storage (N) | Production |
| **Hyperconverged** | Compute + Storage on same node | Mid-scale private cloud |

**Production Reference Architecture (5+ nodes minimum):**

| Node Type | Services | Hardware Baseline |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller × 3** | Keystone, Glance, Nova (control plane), Neutron-server, Horizon, Heat, Ceilometer, MySQL, RabbitMQ | 8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 500 GB SSD |
| **Compute × N** | Nova-compute, Neutron-agent | 16+ vCPU, 64+ GB RAM, 1 TB SSD |
| **Block Storage × N** | Cinder-volume | 8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 12+ TB HDD/SSD |
| **Object Storage × N** | Swift-proxy + account/container/object | 8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 36+ TB HDD |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL DERIVATIONS & CASE STUDIES

## 3.1 Case Study 1 — Capacity Planning with Overcommit Ratios

> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> An organization plans to deploy OpenStack with 5 compute nodes, each having **128 GB RAM** and **32 physical cores**. The admin configures a **RAM overcommit ratio of 1.5 : 1** and a **CPU overcommit ratio of 4 : 1**. There are 3 flavors defined:
> - `flavor-tiny` → 2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM
> - `flavor-small` → 2 vCPU, 8 GB RAM
> - `flavor-medium` → 4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM
>
> **How many `flavor-medium` instances can the cluster host simultaneously, assuming they consume all available resources?**

### Step 1 — Compute Total Host Resources

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{total physical}} = 5 \text{ nodes} \times 128 \text{ GB} = 640 \text{ GB}
$$

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{total physical}} = 5 \text{ nodes} \times 32 \text{ cores} = 160 \text{ cores}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Overcommit Ratios

The overcommit ratio expresses how many virtual units can be allocated per physical unit. A 1.5:1 RAM ratio means:

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{sellable}} = \text{RAM}_{\text{physical}} \times 1.5
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{RAM}_{\text{sellable}} = 640 \text{ GB} \times 1.5 = 960 \text{ GB}
$$

For CPU with a 4:1 ratio:

$$
\text{CPU}_{\text{sellable}} = 160 \text{ cores} \times 4 = 640 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Bottleneck (Bounded by the Slower Resource)

For `flavor-medium` (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM):

$$
N_{\text{RAM}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{960 \text{ GB}}{16 \text{ GB}} \right\rfloor = 60 \text{ instances}
$$

$$
N_{\text{CPU}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{640 \text{ vCPUs}}{4 \text{ vCPUs}} \right\rfloor = 160 \text{ instances}
$$

### Step 4 — Take the Minimum (Hardest Constraint)

$$
N_{\text{max}} = \min(N_{\text{RAM}}, N_{\text{CPU}}) = \min(60, 160) = 60
$$

### ✅ Final Answer

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{max medium instances}} = 60}
$$

**Real-world insight:** Memory is typically the **first bottleneck** in OpenStack clouds because RAM overcommit ratios are kept conservative (1.5–2) while CPU ratios are aggressive (8–16). Hence, capacity planners always size for RAM first.

---

## 3.2 Case Study 2 — Object Storage Durability Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** A Swift cluster has **6 storage nodes**, each with 10 disks of **2 TB** capacity. Disk annual failure rate = **3 %**. Replication factor = **3**.
> Calculate: (a) Total usable capacity, (b) Probability of data loss for a single object.

### Step 1 — Raw Capacity

$$
C_{\text{raw}} = 6 \text{ nodes} \times 10 \text{ disks} \times 2 \text{ TB} = 120 \text{ TB}
$$

### Step 2 — Usable Capacity (after 3x replication)

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{raw}}}{\text{replicas}} = \dfrac{120 \text{ TB}}{3} = 40 \text{ TB}
$$

### Step 3 — Probability of a Disk Failing

$$
p_{\text{disk}} = 0.03
$$

### Step 4 — Probability That All 3 Replicas Fail

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = (p_{\text{disk}})^{3} = (0.03)^{3} = 2.7 \times 10^{-5}
$$

### Step 5 — Annual Durability

$$
D = 1 - P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - 2.7 \times 10^{-5} = 0.999973 = 99.9973\,\%
$$

> **Insight:** The standard SLA promised by cloud object stores is **eleven nines (99.999999999%)** — to achieve that in production, real clouds use **erasure coding** (e.g., Reed-Solomon 10+4) rather than simple replication, drastically reducing storage overhead while increasing resilience.

---

## 3.3 Case Study 3 — Floating IP and SNAT/DNAT Flow

> **Problem Statement:** A tenant VM has a fixed private IP `10.0.0.5` and is associated with floating IP `203.0.113.20`. A user from the public Internet sends an HTTP request to `203.0.113.20:80`. Trace the packet flow.

### Step 1 — Ingress (DNAT at Router)

The Neutron router (in the external network namespace `qrouter-xxx`) intercepts the packet destined for `203.0.113.20`. The DNAT rule rewrites the destination:

$$
\text{Dst IP}: 203.0.113.20 \rightarrow 10.0.0.5
$$

### Step 2 — Forwarding (L2 within Tenant Network)

The packet is forwarded via the **br-int** integration bridge and the **tap** interface of the VM, where the VM's iptables (managed by the security group driver) applies **ingress security-group rules**.

### Step 3 — Egress (Reverse SNAT)

When the VM replies, the reply hits the router. Since the connection was tracked in `conntrack`, SNAT is applied to keep the floating IP on egress (otherwise asymmetric routing breaks):

$$
\text{Src IP}: 10.0.0.5 \rightarrow 203.0.113.20
$$

### Step 4 — Connection Tracking State

The connection entry lives in `conntrack`:

$$
\text{State}: ASSURED, \text{Timeout}: 432000 \text{ s (default 5 days)}
$$

> **Key takeaway:** Floating IPs use **stateless NAT with connection tracking**, not stateful NAT in the classical sense. This is why horizontal scaling of Neutron routers needs DVR (Distributed Virtual Routing).

---

## 3.4 Case Study 4 — Scheduler Decision Walkthrough

> **Problem Statement:** A user requests a `m1.large` instance (8 vCPU, 16 GB RAM, 80 GB disk). Three compute hosts report available resources:
> - **Host A** → free 20 vCPU, 32 GB RAM, 200 GB disk; weigher `RamWeigher` returns +5.0.
> - **Host B** → free 16 vCPU, 24 GB RAM, 100 GB disk; weigher returns +2.0.
> - **Host C** → free 24 vCPU, 48 GB RAM, 400 GB disk; weigher returns -1.0.
>
> Default filter `RamFilter` passes all (each has ≥ 16 GB free). Compute weighted score.

### Step 1 — Apply Filters First

After `RamFilter` and `ComputeFilter`, all three hosts qualify (no negative filtering).

### Step 2 — Apply Weighters

$$
W_{A} = 5.0, \quad W_{B} = 2.0, \quad W_{C} = -1.0
$$

### Step 3 — Select the Winner

$$
\text{Winner} = \arg\max(W) = \text{Host A}
$$

> **Insight:** A *negative* weigher (like `DiskWeigher` when low disk exists) can **steer the scheduler away from a host** that otherwise seems ideal. This is how OpenStack balances heterogeneous hardware.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Services at a Glance

| Service | Scope | State? | Persistence | Failure Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nova | Compute | Stateful (DB) | Ephemeral by default | VM unavailable |
| Neutron | Network | Stateful (DB) | Config persists | Network outage |
| Cinder | Block storage | Stateful | Persistent (volume) | Data unavailability |
| Swift | Object storage | Eventually consistent | Highly persistent | Data unavailability |
| Keystone | Identity | Stateful (DB) | Persistent | Total cloud lockout |
| Glance | Images | Stateful | Persistent | Cannot launch new VMs |
| Heat | Orchestration | Stateless | Stacks persisted | Stacks fail |
| Ceilometer | Telemetry | Streaming | Metrics DB | Billing/auto-scaling break |
| Horizon | UI | Stateless | No state | UX degraded (CLI works) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS — OPENSTACK ARCHITECTURE & INTERACTION FLOWS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Production Multi-Node)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph clientLayer[Client Access Layer]
        cliUser[CLI Client openstack / nova]
        webUser[Web Browser Horizon Dashboard]
        apiClient[REST API Consumer]
    end

    subgraph ctrlLayer[Controller Node Cluster]
        keystoneSrv[Keystone Identity Service]
        glanceSrv[Glance Image Service]
        novaCtrl[Nova Control Plane API Scheduler Conductor Placement]
        neutronSrv[Neutron Server ML2 Plugin]
        cinderCtrl[Cinder API Scheduler]
        heatSrv[Heat Orchestration Engine]
        ceilSrv[Ceilometer Telemetry Collector]
        horizonSrv[Horizon Dashboard Django]
    end

    subgraph dataLayer[Shared Backend Services]
        rabbitMQ[RabbitMQ Message Broker AMQP]
        mysqlDB[(MySQL / MariaDB Central Database)]
    end

    subgraph compLayer[Compute Node Pool]
        computeNodeA[Compute Node 1 Nova Compute libvirt KVM]
        computeNodeB[Compute Node 2 Nova Compute libvirt KVM]
        computeNodeC[Compute Node 3 Nova Compute libvirt KVM]
    end

    subgraph netLayer[Network Node]
        neutronAgent[Neutron L3 Agent DHCP Agent OVS / OVN]
        externalNet[External Network Gateway Public Internet]
    end

    subgraph storLayer[Storage Nodes]
        cinderNode[Block Storage Node Cinder Volume LVM / Ceph]
        swiftProxy[Object Storage Proxy Node Swift]
        swiftNodeA[Swift Storage Node Zone 1]
        swiftNodeB[Swift Storage Node Zone 2]
    end

    cliUser --> horizonSrv
    webUser --> horizonSrv
    apiClient --> horizonSrv

    horizonSrv --> keystoneSrv
    horizonSrv --> novaCtrl
    horizonSrv --> glanceSrv
    horizonSrv --> neutronSrv
    horizonSrv --> cinderCtrl

    keystoneSrv --> mysqlDB
    glanceSrv --> mysqlDB
    novaCtrl --> mysqlDB
    neutronSrv --> mysqlDB
    cinderCtrl --> mysqlDB

    novaCtrl --> rabbitMQ
    neutronSrv --> rabbitMQ
    cinderCtrl --> rabbitMQ
    ceilSrv --> rabbitMQ

    novaCtrl --> computeNodeA
    novaCtrl --> computeNodeB
    novaCtrl --> computeNodeC

    computeNodeA --> neutronAgent
    computeNodeB --> neutronAgent
    computeNodeC --> neutronAgent

    neutronSrv --> neutronAgent
    neutronAgent --> externalNet

    cinderCtrl --> cinderNode
    computeNodeA --> cinderNode

    swiftProxy --> swiftNodeA
    swiftProxy --> swiftNodeB
    glanceSrv --> swiftProxy
```

---

## 4.2 VM Boot Lifecycle (End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant U as User / CLI
    participant H as Horizon Dashboard
    participant K as Keystone
    participant N as Nova API
    participant S as Nova Scheduler
    participant P as Nova Placement
    participant C as Nova Compute
    participant V as libvirt / KVM
    participant G as Glance
    participant NN as Neutron
    participant CI as Cinder
    participant MQ as RabbitMQ

    U->>H: Launch instance request flavor + image
    H->>K: Authenticate user request token
    K-->>H: Token issued
    H->>N: POST /servers with token
    N->>K: Validate token
    N->>P: Claim resources for instance
    P-->>N: Resource claim success
    N->>NN: Allocate network port subnet
    NN-->>N: Port created
    N->>CI: Attach block volume optional
    CI-->>N: Volume attached
    N->>MQ: Schedule instance task
    MQ->>S: Deliver to scheduler
    S->>S: Filter weigh hosts
    S-->>MQ: Selected host
    MQ->>C: Boot request to compute node
    C->>G: Download image
    G-->>C: Image stream
    C->>NN: Plug VIF into OVS bridge
    C->>V: Define and start libvirt domain
    V-->>C: VM running
    C-->>N: Status ACTIVE
    N-->>H: Return instance info
    H-->>U: Instance launched successfully
```

---

## 4.3 Request Path — "What Happens When You Click 'Launch'?"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User clicks Launch] --> B[Keystone verifies token]
    B --> C[Glance fetches image metadata]
    C --> D[Neutron allocates MAC IP Port]
    D --> E[Placement finds suitable host]
    E --> F[Nova Scheduler weighs candidates]
    F --> G[RabbitMQ delivers task]
    G --> H[Nova Compute on host]
    H --> I[libvirt creates VM]
    I --> J[Cinder attaches volume if any]
    J --> K[Neutron agent plugs VIF]
    K --> L[VM is ACTIVE]
```

---

## 4.4 Keystone Token Authentication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[User / Service] -->|1 Submit credentials| keystoneAuth[Keystone API]
    keystoneAuth -->|2 Validate against DB| keystoneDB[(Keystone Backend)]
    keystoneDB -->|3 User roles project| keystoneAuth
    keystoneAuth -->|4 Issue scoped token| clientA
    clientA -->|5 Present token X-Auth-Token| novaAPI[Nova / Neutron / Cinder / etc]
    novaAPI -->|6 Verify token validity| keystoneAuth
    keystoneAuth -->|7 Token valid + service catalog| novaAPI
    novaAPI -->|8 Process request| clientA
```

---

## 4.5 Storage Decision Tree — Block vs Object

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{Need to store data?} --> q1{Structured as files and objects?}
    q1 -->|Yes| objPath[Use Swift Object Storage]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Need a filesystem mount inside VM?}
    q2 -->|Yes| blkPath[Use Cinder Block Volume iSCSI RBD]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Shared across many VMs?}
    q3 -->|Yes| manilaPath[Use Manila Shared File System NFS CephFS]
    q3 -->|No| ephemeralPath[Use Nova Ephemeral Disk local SSD]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME — EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define OpenStack. List any four core services of OpenStack with one-line functionality.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **OpenStack** is an *open-source cloud operating system* that controls large pools of compute, storage, and networking resources through a **RESTful API**, providing **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** capabilities to public, private, and hybrid cloud deployments.

**Four core services:**
1. **Nova (Compute)** — Launches and manages virtual machine instances on hypervisors. *(1 mark)*
2. **Neutron (Networking)** — Provides network connectivity as a service (networks, subnets, routers, floating IPs). *(1 mark)*
3. **Keystone (Identity)** — Performs authentication, authorization, and provides a service catalog of all endpoints. *(½ mark)*
4. **Cinder (Block Storage)** — Provides persistent block-level storage volumes attachable to VMs. *(½ mark)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Cinder (Block Storage) and Swift (Object Storage) in OpenStack.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cinder | Swift |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Block storage (raw disk volumes) | Object storage (files/objects/binary blobs) |
| **Access** | Attached as a disk to a VM (iSCSI/RBD/FC) | Accessed via REST HTTP API |
| **Use case** | Database disks, OS root volumes | Backups, images, videos, archives |
| **Metadata** | Limited | Rich custom metadata |
| **Mutability** | Mutable (read/write) | Immutable (write once, read many) |
| **Analogy in AWS** | EBS | S3 |

*(2 marks for table; 1 mark for the concluding line that "Cinder is for performance-critical block I/O, Swift is for scale-out unstructured data".)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **OpenStack architecture** and the role of **any five core services**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Nova architecture** in detail, including its sub-components, scheduler, and the workflow for launching a virtual machine. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 marks**

**OpenStack Architecture (Block-level):** *(3 marks)*

OpenStack follows a **shared-nothing, modular, message-driven, multi-tenant** architecture. The platform is divided into logical services, each running as a set of daemons, communicating via:
- **REST/HTTP APIs** (synchronous, request-response)
- **AMQP message queue (RabbitMQ)** (asynchronous, fire-and-forget)
- **Central database (MySQL/MariaDB)** (shared state)

**Five Core Services and Their Roles:** *(4 marks, 0.8 each)*

1. **Keystone (Identity Service):** Centralized authentication & authorization. Issues tokens to users/services and provides a service catalog of endpoints.
2. **Nova (Compute Service):** Manages the lifecycle of virtual machine instances — scheduling, launching, pausing, terminating.
3. **Neutron (Networking Service):** Provides networking-as-a-service — networks, subnets, routers, ports, floating IPs, security groups, load balancers.
4. **Cinder (Block Storage):** Provides persistent block storage volumes attachable to VMs via iSCSI/RBD.
5. **Glance (Image Service):** Stores and retrieves VM disk images (templates) in various formats (qcow2, vmdk, raw).

*(Reference: Architecture diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn here.)*

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 marks**

**Nova Architecture:** *(3 marks)*

Nova consists of the following daemons:
- `nova-api` — REST API endpoint.
- `nova-scheduler` — Decides *which* host runs a VM.
- `nova-conductor` — Mediates DB access (compute nodes never connect to DB directly).
- `nova-compute` — Talks to the hypervisor (KVM/Xen/VMware) via `libvirt` or driver-specific code.
- `nova-placement` — Tracks resource inventory (PCI, RAM, disk).
- `nova-consoleauth` + `nova-novncproxy` — Browser-based console.

**VM Launch Workflow:** *(4 marks, step-by-step)*

1. User authenticates via **Keystone** → receives a token.
2. User sends `POST /servers` to `nova-api` with flavor, image, and network.
3. `nova-api` calls `nova-placement` to claim resources.
4. `nova-scheduler` runs **filters** (e.g., `RamFilter`, `ComputeFilter`) to shortlist hosts.
5. `nova-scheduler` runs **weighers** (e.g., `RamWeigher`) to pick the best host.
6. Message is published to **RabbitMQ** queue.
7. `nova-compute` on the chosen host picks up the message.
8. `nova-compute` calls **Glance** to download the image.
9. `nova-compute` calls **Neutron** to plug a VIF into the tenant's OVS bridge.
10. `nova-compute` instructs **libvirt** to start the KVM domain.
11. VM becomes **ACTIVE**; status returned to the user.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative to Q.A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **Keystone (Identity Service)** in detail — its components, token types, and authentication flow. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Neutron networking concepts** — networks, subnets, routers, floating IPs, and security groups. Show how a tenant creates a self-service network and assigns a floating IP to enable external access. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 marks**

**Keystone Components:** *(3 marks)*

- **User** — Digital identity of a person/service.
- **Project (Tenant)** — Isolation container for resources.
- **Role** — Set of privileges (`admin`, `member`, `reader`).
- **Domain** — Top-level container for users & projects (Juno+).
- **Service Catalog** — List of all service endpoints.
- **Endpoint** — URL for accessing a service.
- **Token** — Time-bound credential proving identity.
- **Policy** — JSON rules defining role-based access.

**Token Types:** *(2 marks)*

| Type | Format | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **UUID** | Random 32-char hex | Default in older versions; persists in DB. |
| **PKI / PKIZ** | X.509 certificate bundle | Big tokens, lower DB load. |
| **Fernet** | AES-encrypted symmetric | **Default in modern OpenStack**; no DB lookup required, fast. |
| **JWT** | JSON Web Token | Used by some Federation scenarios. |

**Authentication Flow:** *(2 marks, 4 steps)*

1. User submits credentials (username/password) to Keystone.
2. Keystone validates and issues a **scoped token** (binds user + project + roles).
3. User presents the token in `X-Auth-Token` header to other services.
4. Each service independently verifies the token with Keystone (cached for performance) and consults its **policy.json** to authorize the request.

---

#### **MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 marks**

**Core Neutron Objects:** *(3 marks)*

- **Network** — Isolated L2 broadcast domain (identified by `network_id`).
- **Subnet** — A CIDR block of IP addresses within a network.
- **Router** — Virtual router; connects subnets and provides external gateway.
- **Port** — Connection point attaching a VM's virtual NIC to a network.
- **Floating IP** — A 1:1 NAT mapping of a public IP to a private IP.
- **Security Group** — Stateful L4 firewall rules attached to VM ports.

**Self-Service Network + Floating IP Workflow:** *(4 marks)*

1. Tenant creates a **Network** (e.g., `my-net`).
2. Tenant creates a **Subnet** under the network (e.g., `10.0.0.0/24`) with DHCP enabled.
3. Tenant creates a **Router** and sets its gateway to the `external` provider network.
4. Tenant attaches the router interface to the subnet.
5. Tenant launches a VM — Neutron allocates a **Port** with a private IP (`10.0.0.5`).
6. Tenant allocates a **Floating IP** from the external pool and associates it with the VM's port.
7. Now the VM is reachable from the public Internet via the floating IP.

**Packet Flow:** *(bonus 1 mark)*
- Inbound: Public IP → DNAT at router → Private IP.
- Outbound: VM reply → SNAT at router → Public IP.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (drawn from KTU valuation reports):**
> 1. **Writing "OpenStack is AWS" or "OpenStack is a hypervisor."** It is **NOT** AWS, nor is it a hypervisor. It is an *IaaS platform that uses hypervisors* like KVM. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing Cinder and Swift.** Cinder = block, attached like a disk. Swift = object, accessed via HTTP. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the role of RabbitMQ.** Many students omit the message broker in their architecture diagram. Examiners **always** deduct for this. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Describing the scheduler vaguely.** Do NOT say "the scheduler picks a host." Always mention **filters + weighers + weighted score** for full credit. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Forgetting Keystone's role in the workflow.** Every single OpenStack request begins with Keystone authentication. Drawing a workflow *without* Keystone costs marks.
> 6. **Using wrong terminology for "Project vs Tenant."** In K2017+ OpenStack, the preferred term is **Project**; "Tenant" is the legacy term. Use *project* to be safe.
> 7. **Missing the difference between provider and self-service networks.** This is a **favourite 2-mark question**. Always state that provider networks are admin-created/flat, while self-service networks are tenant-created and use overlay (VXLAN/GRE) for isolation.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **OpenStack = open-source IaaS platform** governed by OpenInfra Foundation.
- ✅ Released in **2010** by **NASA + Rackspace**; written mostly in **Python**; licensed under **Apache 2.0**.
- ✅ All services expose **REST/JSON APIs**; message bus = **RabbitMQ**; shared DB = **MySQL**.
- ✅ **Nova** = compute, **Neutron** = network, **Cinder** = block, **Swift** = object, **Keystone** = identity, **Glance** = images, **Horizon** = UI, **Heat** = orchestration, **Ceilometer** = telemetry, **Trove** = DBaaS, **Magnum** = containers, **Manila** = shared FS, **Barbican** = key manager, **Ironic** = bare metal, **Designate** = DNS.
- ✅ A **Project (Tenant)** is the unit of isolation. Users get scoped tokens from Keystone.
- ✅ VM launch = Keystone → Nova API → Placement → Scheduler (filters + weighers) → RabbitMQ → Nova-compute → libvirt → VM ACTIVE.
- ✅ **Block storage (Cinder)** persists; **object storage (Swift)** is replicated (default 3x) → eleven nines durability.
- ✅ **Floating IP** = 1:1 NAT for inbound + outbound; uses connection tracking.
- ✅ **Self-service networks** use overlay (VXLAN/GRE); **provider networks** are flat/admin-owned.
- ✅ RAM overcommit ratio (1.5–2) governs max VM density; CPU ratio (8–16) is much higher.
- ✅ **OpenStack is NOT a hypervisor** — it *manages* hypervisors (KVM, Xen, VMware, Hyper-V).
- ✅ **OpenStack is NOT AWS** — but AWS APIs can be emulated via plugins (e.g., EC2 API compatibility).
- ✅ HOT templates (YAML) drive Heat orchestration; inspired by AWS CloudFormation.
- ✅ Production deployments require **at least 3 controller nodes** for HA (Galera + Pacemaker/RabbitMQ HA).
- ✅ Horizon is *optional* (CLI + API work without it).
- ✅ Default token format in modern releases = **Fernet** (AES-symmetric, no DB lookup).

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** Always **draw a labeled block diagram** of OpenStack architecture in your answer. KTU examiners award *automatic 1–2 marks* for a clean, well-labeled diagram, even if your text is incomplete. Always place **Keystone at the top** and **RabbitMQ + MySQL as the central backbone**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aneka
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Aneka

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Aneka** is an open-source **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** middleware framework developed by **Manjrasoft Pty Ltd** (originally conceived as the .NET My Services project at the University of Melbourne) that enables the rapid development, deployment, and management of distributed applications on top of heterogeneous, federated cloud infrastructures (Private, Public, or Hybrid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology Lock-in:** Aneka is *not* an Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) offering. It sits **above** the VM layer (Hypervisor) and **below** the end-user application, providing **runtime containers**, **storage services**, and **abstractions for parallelism**.

### Key Vocabulary for the Examiner

| Term | Board-Safe Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fabric** | The collection of physical/virtual compute nodes managed by Aneka |
| **Container** | The software layer hosting the Aneka runtime on each node |
| **Service** | A managed functionality exposed by Aneka (execution, storage, accounting) |
| **Worker** | A compute node that executes application tasks |
| **Master / Scheduler** | A specialized node responsible for job dispatching & load balancing |
| **Worker Node** | A generic node in the Aneka pool capable of executing tasks |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine Aneka as a **centralized cloud kitchen** (think Swiggy/CloudEats) where:

- **Dishes (Tasks)** are ordered by customers (developers/users) through a single **app (Aneka API)**.
- The **Head Chef (Master/Scheduler node)** receives orders and dispatches them to available **line cooks (Worker nodes)**.
- Each cook has a **uniform (Aneka Container)** giving them access to standardized tools — pans (execution service), refrigerators (storage service), and timers (accounting service).
- The cooks may be working in the main restaurant (**Private Cloud**), rented commercial kitchens (**Public Cloud — AWS/Azure**), or a mix (**Hybrid Cloud**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this analogy works:** Just as a kitchen doesn't own the farms supplying vegetables, Aneka doesn't own the underlying infrastructure — it **abstracts and orchestrates** compute resources provided by heterogeneous clouds into a unified programming surface.

---

## 1.3 High-Level Capabilities (What Aneka Offers)

Aneka provides three primary service categories to applications:

1. **Execution Service** — Parallel task execution via threading models.
2. **Storage Service** — Distributed data management across Aneka nodes.
3. **Accounting & Profiling Service** — Resource usage monitoring, billing, and fault tolerance.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Heuristic:** Whenever KTU asks *"List the services of Aneka"*, the trio is always **Execution + Storage + Accounting**. Memorize this trio — it appears in nearly every Dec/Jan paper.

---

## 1.4 Where Aneka Sits in the Cloud Stack

```
+------------------------------+
|      End-User Application    |  (Your .NET/Java code)
+------------------------------+
|       ANEKA (PaaS Layer)     |  ⬅ Topic of this module
+------------------------------+
|   Hypervisor / VMs (IaaS)    |  (Xen, Hyper-V, EC2)
+------------------------------+
|   Physical Infrastructure    |
+------------------------------+
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Aneka Architecture — The Three Logical Layers

The Aneka architecture is decomposed into three orthogonal layers. **This decomposition is the single most-tested diagram in the KTU 2024 Module 4 paper.**

### Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure Layer

- Represents the **bare-metal** or **virtualized hardware**.
- May span desktops, servers, clusters, data centers.
- Network interconnect: LAN (private), WAN (public), or VPN (hybrid).

### Layer 2 — Aneka Container Layer (The Heart of Aneka)

The **Aneka Container** is the runtime environment deployed on every participating node. It exposes:

- **Aneka Management Console (AMC)** — Web-based GUI for administrators.
- **Aneka SDK** — APIs for developers (C#, Java bindings historically).
- **Runtime services** listed below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's favourite question:** *"Explain the architecture of Aneka."*
> **Model answer must include:** Fabric + Container + Services (with sub-types of each service).

### Layer 3 — Application Layer

- Custom user applications built using the Aneka SDK.
- Applications select a **threading model** that defines how parallelism is expressed.

---

## 2.2 The Three Service Families

### 2.2.1 Execution Service

Responsible for **dispatching and running application tasks** on workers. Components:

- **Scheduler:** Implements a scheduling policy (FCFS, Priority, Deadline, Cost-aware).
- **Worker Threads:** Pre-allocated pool of threads waiting for tasks.
- **Task Dispatcher:** Marshals tasks over the network.

### 2.2.2 Storage Service

Manages **persistent and transient data** across nodes. Variants:

| Storage Type | Scope | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Storage** | Single node | Caching, intermediate results |
| **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)** | Fabric-wide | Key-value lookups |
| **Persistent Storage** | Backed by DB | Long-term state, results |

### 2.2.3 Accounting & Profiling Service

- Tracks **CPU**, **memory**, **network**, **wall-clock time** per task.
- Provides **billing** primitives for commercial deployment.
- Enables **fault detection** via heartbeats.

---

## 2.3 Aneka Threading Models (Board Hot-Spot ⭐⭐⭐)

Aneka exposes **three threading models** to application developers. Each model is suited to a different parallelism style.

| Threading Model | Parallelism Style | Best For | Code Construct (Conceptual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Model** | Task-parallelism | Independent units of work | `ITask` interface |
| **Thread Model** | Manual thread-management | Custom parallel loops | `Thread.Start()` style API |
| **Manual Model** | Direct worker control | MPI-style coordination | Custom worker logic |

### 2.3.1 Task Threading Model

- Developer defines a class implementing the **`ITask` interface**.
- Each **task** is an independent, atomic unit of work submitted to the fabric.
- Aneka handles distribution, scheduling, and result collection.
- **Most-used model in KTU exam answers** — show the `ITask` interface signature.

### 2.3.2 Thread Threading Model

- Developers manually create, start, and synchronize threads.
- Provides finer-grained control than the Task model.
- Suited to embarrassingly parallel loops where each iteration is a "thread."

### 2.3.3 Manual Threading Model

- **Lowest-level** abstraction — developer controls submission, polling, and completion manually.
- Used for advanced scenarios like **dynamic work-stealing** or **barrier synchronization**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student pitfall:** Confusing **Thread Model** with **Manual Model**. Remember: *Thread Model still uses an abstraction (Thread object); Manual Model gives you raw access to the worker.*

---

## 2.4 Aneka Deployment Topologies

Aneka supports three topologies, fully consistent with NIST cloud deployment models:

| Topology | Description | Infrastructure Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Private Cloud** | Aneka runs on organization's own data center | In-house servers |
| **Public Cloud** | Aneka nodes leased from AWS, Azure, etc. | Commercial IaaS |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Burst-out to public cloud from private base | Mixed |

---

## 2.5 Aneka Application Development Lifecycle (5-Step Board Answer)

1. **Design** — Identify parallelism style → pick threading model.
2. **Implement** — Write tasks using Aneka SDK (`ITask` etc.).
3. **Configure** — Define Aneka cloud (nodes, services, credentials).
4. **Submit & Execute** — Deploy via AMC or programmatically.
5. **Monitor & Scale** — Use accounting service to track and add nodes.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative Aspects of Aneka

Although Aneka is primarily architectural, the following quantitative metrics appear in KTU numerical/model questions:

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Total Compute Capacity | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i$ | CPU-cores |
| Average Task Completion Time | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} T_j$ | seconds |
| Speedup | $S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}}$ | dimensionless |
| Parallel Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{N}$ | dimensionless |
| Task Throughput | $\lambda = \dfrac{n_{tasks}}{T_{window}}$ | tasks/second |
| Resource Utilization | $U = \dfrac{BusyTime}{TotalTime}$ | ratio $\vert 0,1 \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` (or `\mid`) for absolute-value delimiters inside tables — **never** use the raw pipe `|` character, as it breaks markdown table parsing in the KTU e-content pipeline.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Parallel BLAST searches across Aneka workers.
- **Financial Monte-Carlo simulations** running overnight on burst-out public cloud capacity.
- **Engineering FEA (Finite Element Analysis):** embarrassingly parallel sub-domain solving.
- **Image rendering farms** for animation studios using idle desktop cycles (private Aneka deployment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Parallel Speedup for Aneka Task Model

> KTU 2024 Scheme loves a numerical on **Amdahl's Law applied to Aneka Task Model**. Below is the full exhaustive derivation.

**Scenario:** An application has a parallelizable fraction $P$ executed across $N$ Aneka workers. The serial fraction $(1-P)$ runs on the master.

**Step 1 — Define sequential execution time.**
$$T_{seq} = T_{parallelizable} + T_{serial}$$

**Step 2 — Define parallel execution time on Aneka.**
$$T_{par} = \dfrac{T_{parallelizable}}{N} + T_{serial}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of speedup $S$.**
$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute expressions.**
$$S = \dfrac{T_{parallelizable} + T_{serial}}{\dfrac{T_{parallelizable}}{N} + T_{serial}}$$

**Step 5 — Normalize by setting $T_{seq} = 1$.**
$$S = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{P}{N} + (1-P)}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into the classical Amdahl form.**
$$S = \dfrac{N}{P + N(1-P)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **Amdahl's Law** as applied to Aneka's task-distribution model. The examiner expects the same form. Memorize it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Aneka Cluster Throughput

**Given:**

- An Aneka fabric has $N = 8$ worker nodes.
- Each worker executes on average $C_i = 4$ tasks per second.
- There are $n_{tasks} = 10{,}000$ total Monte-Carlo simulation tasks.
- Sequential baseline: $T_{seq} = 1200$ seconds.
- Observed parallel time: $T_{par} = 210$ seconds.

**Find:** Speedup $S$, Efficiency $\eta$, and total wall-clock throughput $\lambda$.

### 3.2.1 Speedup Calculation

$$S = \dfrac{T_{seq}}{T_{par}} = \dfrac{1200}{210} = 5.714$$

### 3.2.2 Efficiency Calculation

$$\eta = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{5.714}{8} = 0.7143 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 71.43\%)$$

### 3.2.3 Throughput Calculation

$$\lambda = \dfrac{n_{tasks}}{T_{par}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{210} \approx 47.62 \;\; \text{tasks/second}$$

### 3.2.4 Total Fabric Capacity Check

$$C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i = 8 \times 4 = 32 \;\; \text{tasks/second (peak)}$$

The achieved $\lambda = 47.62 > 32$ is **higher** than per-worker aggregate because tasks **overlap** with I/O and dispatch — a key Aneka scheduling insight.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Threading Model Selection

| Application Characteristic | Recommended Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Independent identical tasks (embarrassingly parallel) | **Task Model** | Simplest API; Aneka handles distribution |
| Iterative loop with shared counter | **Thread Model** | Manual barrier/loop control needed |
| MPI-style rank-based communication | **Manual Model** | Full control over messaging |
| Map-Reduce style aggregation | **Task Model + Map/Reduce library** | Aneka supports pre-built abstractions |
| Real-time low-latency jobs | **Thread Model** | Lower overhead than task serialization |

---

## 3.4 Storage Service Resolution — DHT Lookup Walkthrough

Aneka uses a **Distributed Hash Table** to locate data across nodes. Walkthrough:

1. Client hashes the key $k$ to obtain $h(k) \in [0, 2^m)$.
2. DHT routing maps $h(k)$ to a specific worker $W_j$.
3. Lookup request forwarded hop-by-hop until $W_j$ is reached.
4. $W_j$ returns the value or a "not-found" marker.

**Average lookup hop count (uniform hashing):**
$$H_{avg} = \dfrac{\log_2 N}{2}$$

For $N = 64$ nodes:
$$H_{avg} = \dfrac{\log_2 64}{2} = \dfrac{6}{2} = 3 \;\; \text{hops}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aneka High-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
        AppA["User App 1"]
        AppB["User App 2"]
        AppC["User App 3"]
    end

    AppA --> SDK

    ExecSvc --> W3

    StorSvc --> W2
    StorSvc --> W3
    AcctSvc --> W1
    AcctSvc --> W2
    AcctSvc --> W5
    AMC -.manages.-> ExecSvc
    AMC -.manages.-> StorSvc
```

---

## 4.2 Aneka Threading Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Developer has Application"] --> B{"Parallelism Style?"}
    B -->|"Independent tasks"| C["Use Task Model - ITask"]
    B -->|"Parallel loops with sync"| D["Use Thread Model"]
    B -->|"Custom control or MPI style"| E["Use Manual Model"]
    C --> F["Submit via Aneka SDK"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Aneka Scheduler"]
    G --> H["Worker Node executes task"]
    H --> I["Result returned to master"]
```

---

## 4.3 Aneka Deployment Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Private["PRIVATE CLOUD ANEKA"]
        P1["Org Server 1"]
        P2["Org Server 2"]
        P3["Org Desktop Pool"]
    end
    subgraph Public["PUBLIC CLOUD ANEKA"]
        Q1["AWS EC2 Instance"]
        Q2["Azure VM"]
    end
    subgraph Hybrid["HYBRID CLOUD ANEKA"]
        R1["Master in Private"]
        R2["Burst workers in Public"]
    end
    Master1["Aneka Master"] --> P1
    Master1 --> P2
    Master1 --> P3
    Master2["Aneka Master Public"] --> Q1
    Master2 --> Q2
    Master3["Aneka Master Hybrid"] --> R1
    Master3 --> R2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Aneka. List its three primary services. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Aneka is a PaaS-oriented application platform for cloud computing developed by Manjrasoft that allows developers to build and deploy distributed applications on heterogeneous infrastructures.

The three primary services are:

1. **Execution Service** — for running parallel tasks on worker nodes.
2. **Storage Service** — for managing distributed data (DHT-based).
3. **Accounting & Profiling Service** — for monitoring resource usage and billing.

---

### Q2. Mention any two threading models supported by Aneka. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Task Threading Model** — Applications implement the `ITask` interface; Aneka handles task distribution and scheduling.
2. **Thread Threading Model** — Developers manually create and manage threads for finer control over parallel execution.

*(Manual Model is the third acceptable answer.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🎯 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### (a) Explain the architecture of Aneka with a neat diagram. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Aneka architecture is organized into three logical layers:

**Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure Layer:**
Contains the actual hardware resources — servers, desktops, VMs — connected via LAN/WAN. These can be located on-premise (private), rented from public providers (AWS/Azure), or a hybrid mix.

**Layer 2 — Aneka Container Layer:**
Installed on every node participating in the Aneka fabric. It exposes:
- Aneka Management Console (AMC) — GUI for admin
- Aneka SDK — APIs for developers
- The three core services: Execution, Storage, Accounting

**Layer 3 — Application Layer:**
Contains the user-developed distributed applications that leverage the Aneka container.

*(Insert the diagram from Section 4.1 — it scores full 7 marks.)*

---

#### (b) Describe the three threading models of Aneka in detail. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Model | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Task Model** | Application defines tasks via `ITask` interface; Aneka distributes them automatically | Embarrassingly parallel workloads |
| **Thread Model** | Developer explicitly creates and synchronizes threads | Parallel loops with shared state |
| **Manual Model** | Developer controls worker submission and polling directly | Advanced/MPI-style coordination |

**Task Model** is the highest-level abstraction; **Manual Model** is the lowest. Selection depends on the application's need for control versus ease of development.

---

### 🎯 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### (a) With a suitable example, explain how Aneka supports hybrid cloud deployment. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

In a **hybrid Aneka deployment**, the Aneka master node resides in the organization's **private data center**, while additional worker nodes can be **dynamically provisioned in a public cloud** (e.g., AWS EC2) when private capacity is saturated.

**Example — E-Commerce Application during Festival Sale:**
- Base load is handled by 4 private workers.
- When traffic spikes, Aneka's scheduler spins up 12 EC2 instances.
- Tasks are dispatched transparently — the application code is unaware of node location.
- After the sale, public instances are terminated; billing is computed by the **Accounting Service**.

This achieves **elastic scalability** while preserving data locality for sensitive workloads.

---

#### (b) Discuss the Execution Service and Storage Service of Aneka. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Execution Service:**
- Comprises the **Scheduler**, **Dispatcher**, and **Worker Thread Pool**.
- Implements policies such as FCFS, priority-based, deadline-aware, and cost-aware scheduling.
- Receives tasks via the API and dispatches to suitable workers based on availability and policy.

**Storage Service:**
- Provides **Local Storage** (per-node), **Distributed Hash Table Storage** (fabric-wide lookups), and **Persistent Storage** (DB-backed).
- Enables tasks running on different workers to share intermediate results efficiently.
- Average lookup hop count for $N$ nodes: $H_{avg} = \dfrac{\log_2 N}{2}$.

Together, these services allow Aneka applications to execute distributed computations while sharing state across the fabric.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diagram** in architecture questions → lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Conflating Aneka with AWS/Azure** — Aneka is the *middleware*, not a cloud provider.
> 3. **Omitting the third service** — students often list only Execution and Storage, forgetting Accounting.
> 4. **Wrong threading-model names** — saying "Threading Model 1, 2, 3" instead of Task / Thread / Manual.
> 5. **Numerical errors** — mixing up speedup and efficiency formulas (efficiency = speedup ÷ N, not the other way around).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Aneka:**

- ✅ Aneka is a **PaaS** layer, **not** IaaS.
- ✅ Developer: **Manjrasoft**; Origin: University of Melbourne.
- ✅ Three services: **Execution, Storage, Accounting** (memorize the trio).
- ✅ Three threading models: **Task, Thread, Manual** (Task = highest abstraction).
- ✅ Three deployment topologies: **Private, Public, Hybrid**.
- ✅ Master node hosts **Scheduler + AMC**; Worker nodes execute tasks.
- ✅ Storage uses **Distributed Hash Table (DHT)**; average hops = $\dfrac{\log_2 N}{2}$.
- ✅ Speedup formula: $S = \dfrac{N}{P + N(1-P)}$ — derived from Amdahl's Law.
- ✅ Efficiency formula: $\eta = \dfrac{S}{N}$.
- ✅ Aneka SDK originally targeted **.NET**; later bindings added for Java.
- ✅ **Exam code word:** "Aneka **orchestrates** heterogeneous clouds into a unified programming surface."
- ✅ Always draw the **three-layer architecture diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CloudSim. Popular Cloud Databases - MongoDB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CloudSim. Popular Cloud Databases - MongoDB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ☁️ CloudSim & MongoDB: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. CloudSim — The "Flight Simulator" for Cloud Environments

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition
**CloudSim** is an extensible, open-source simulation toolkit (framework) developed in Java by the **CLOUDS Laboratory** at the **University of Melbourne, Australia** (led by Prof. Rajkumar Buyya). It enables modeling, simulation, and experimentation of Cloud computing systems and application provisioning environments on a single physical machine. CloudSim provides a self-contained platform that can be used to model **data centers, hosts, virtual machines (VMs), service brokers, scheduling policies, and resource allocation strategies** without the need for a real physical infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** CloudSim is *the* standard simulation framework referenced in the KTU 2024 Scheme for cloud resource management studies. Expect definition-based 3-mark and architecture-based 14-mark questions.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Imagine you want to train as a pilot, but you cannot afford to crash a real Boeing 747 every time you make a mistake. So, you use a **flight simulator** — a virtual cockpit that mimics every knob, gauge, wind current, and engine of a real aircraft.

**CloudSim is the "flight simulator" of cloud computing.**

- Instead of buying **10,000 real servers** to test a load-balancing algorithm, you write a Java program that **simulates** those 10,000 servers on your laptop.
- You can press "fast-forward" and simulate 24 hours of data center operation in 10 seconds.
- You can crash, restart, and reconfigure virtual machines without spending a single rupee on hardware or electricity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** CloudSim is a *discrete-event simulator* — meaning it advances time in jumps when events occur, rather than second-by-second. This makes it extremely fast and memory-efficient for large-scale cloud experiments.

---

## 2. MongoDB — The "Flexible Filing Cabinet" of Modern Databases

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition
**MongoDB** is an open-source, document-oriented **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) database** that stores data in flexible, JSON-like documents called **BSON (Binary JSON)** documents. It is designed for scalability, high availability, and developer agility. MongoDB supports features such as **horizontal scaling via sharding, automatic failover via replica sets, and a flexible schema-less data model**, making it one of the most popular cloud-native databases deployed on platforms like **AWS, Azure, GCP, and MongoDB Atlas** (its own managed cloud database service).

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)
Think of a **traditional SQL database** as a rigid spreadsheet:
- Every row **must** have the exact same columns.
- If you want to add a new field, you must alter the entire table.

Now imagine a **MongoDB database** as a **flexible filing cabinet** where each folder (document) can contain different papers (fields):
- Folder #1 might have: Name, Age, Address.
- Folder #2 might have: Name, Age, Address, *Twitter Handle*, *Medical History*.
- You can add, remove, or modify fields **per folder** without affecting the others.

> [!NOTE]
> **The BSON Document Model:** A MongoDB document is essentially a JSON object stored in a binary-encoded format (BSON) for efficiency. Example:
> ```json
> {
>   "_id": ObjectId("507f1f77bcf86cd799439011"),
>   "student_name": "Aravind",
>   "course": "B.Tech CSE",
>   "ktu_register_no": "KTU2024CS101",
>   "marks": { "maths": 92, "cloud_computing": 88 }
> }
> ```

---

## 3. Why Study CloudSim & MongoDB Together?

| Aspect | CloudSim | MongoDB |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Simulation/Research | Production Database |
| **Language** | Java | C++ (core), with drivers in many languages |
| **Key Use** | Algorithm benchmarking | Real-time data storage at scale |
| **Cloud Relevance** | Mimics cloud behavior | Often deployed *in* the cloud |
| **KTU Module 4 Role** | Performance evaluation | Modern cloud data management |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** **"S-D-D-D-B"** — **S**imulator, **D**atacenter, **D**ocuments, **D**istributed, **B**SON.

---

## 4. Standards & Metrics to Memorize

- **CloudSim's latest stable version (as of 2024):** CloudSim 7.0+ (with CloudSim Plus as an active fork).
- **MongoDB's latest stable version (as of 2024):** MongoDB 7.0+ (released 2023).
- **Default port for MongoDB:** **27017**.
- **Default document size limit in MongoDB:** **16 MB** (single BSON document).
- **CloudSim's core package:** `org.cloudbus.cloudsim.*`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 1. CloudSim — Layered Architecture & Core Components

CloudSim's architecture is organized into **multiple logical layers**, each abstracting a specific concern of cloud simulation. Understanding these layers is **critical** for KTU 14-mark questions.

### 🏗️ Layered Architecture (Top to Bottom)

**Layer 1 — User Code (Simulation Script)**
- This is the Java program written by the researcher.
- It instantiates datacenters, hosts, VMs, and cloudlets.
- It defines the **scheduling policy** and **workload distribution**.

**Layer 2 — CloudSim Core API**
- Provides classes like `Datacenter`, `DatacenterBroker`, `Vm`, `Host`, `Cloudlet`.
- Manages the **event queue** and **discrete-event simulation engine**.

**Layer 3 — CloudSim Simulation Engine**
- The heart of CloudSim.
- Manages the simulation **clock**, **event scheduling**, and **entity lifecycle** (start, run, pause, stop).
- Uses a `SimEntity` base class for all simulated components.

**Layer 4 — Cloud Information Service (CIS) & DataCenter**
- CIS acts as a **global registry** that brokers query to find suitable data centers.
- DataCenter is a physical facility containing a collection of hosts.

**Layer 5 — Virtual Machine & Host Provisioning**
- Handles **VM-to-Host mapping** (VM allocation policy).
- Manages **PE (Processing Element)** allocation, RAM, and bandwidth.

**Layer 6 — Cloudlet (Task) Execution**
- Cloudlets represent **application tasks / jobs** that run inside VMs.
- Their execution time is calculated using MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second) ratings.

### 🧩 Core Classes & Their Roles

| Class | Full Name | Role | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Datacenter` | Cloud Datacenter | Physical building with servers | A factory |
| `Host` | Physical Server | Hardware with CPU/RAM | A workstation in the factory |
| `Vm` | Virtual Machine | Virtualized OS instance | A virtual office on the workstation |
| `Cloudlet` | Computational Task | Application job to be run | A project assigned to a worker |
| `DatacenterBroker` | Resource Broker | Mediates between users and DCs | A contractor/manager |
| `CIS` | Cloud Information Service | Global DC registry | A telephone directory |
| `CloudSim` | Simulation Engine | The simulator itself | The simulation clock |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind discrete-event simulation:** CloudSim doesn't iterate second-by-second. It jumps from one event to the next (e.g., "VM allocated at t=5s, task finished at t=12.4s"). This makes it **100–1000× faster** than real-time, allowing massive-scale experiments.

### 🧮 Key Formulas in CloudSim (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Execution Time} = \frac{\text{Cloudlet Length (MI)}}{\text{VM MIPS}}$ | Time taken by a cloudlet on a VM | seconds |
| $\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{VM}_i.\text{costPerSec} \times T_i$ | Aggregate cost of all VMs over their lifetimes | currency units |
| $\text{PE Utilization} = \frac{\text{Busy MIPS}}{\text{Total MIPS Capacity}}$ | Fraction of CPU used | dimensionless (%) |
| $\text{Energy (kWh)} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} P(t) \times \Delta t$ | Total energy consumed by hosts | kWh |
| $P(\text{util}) = P_{\text{idle}} + (P_{\text{max}} - P_{\text{idle}}) \times \text{util}$ | Power model (linear) | Watts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠️ MARKDOWN TABLE SAFETY:** All absolute-value bars have been written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the table syntax. The standard **vertical pipe `\|` character is reserved as a column separator.**

---

## 2. MongoDB — Architecture, Features & Data Model

### 📐 MongoDB's Logical Architecture

**1. Database** → Top-level container (like a schema in SQL).
**2. Collection** → A group of documents (like a table in SQL).
**3. Document** → A BSON object (like a row in SQL, but schema-less).
**4. Field** → A key-value pair within a document (like a column in SQL).

### 🗃️ Comparison: RDBMS vs MongoDB Terminology

| RDBMS Term | MongoDB Equivalent | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Database | Database | Same concept |
| Table | Collection | A group of documents |
| Row | Document | A single record |
| Column | Field | A key-value pair |
| Primary Key | `_id` field | Unique identifier (auto-generated ObjectId) |
| JOIN | Embedded documents / `$lookup` | Document-level reference |
| Index | Index | Same B-tree concept |

### 🌐 MongoDB's Distributed Architecture (Cloud Perspective)

MongoDB achieves cloud-grade scalability via **two key mechanisms**:

#### a) Replica Sets (High Availability)
- A **replica set** is a group of MongoDB instances that maintain the same data.
- **One Primary** node (handles all writes) + **multiple Secondary** nodes (replicate the primary's data).
- If the primary fails, an **automatic election** promotes a secondary to primary.
- This provides **fault tolerance** and **read scaling**.

#### b) Sharding (Horizontal Scalability)
- **Sharding** splits data across multiple machines (shards) based on a **shard key**.
- Each shard is itself a replica set.
- A **Mongos** (query router) directs client queries to the correct shard.
- A **Config Server** stores metadata about cluster topology.

### 🧮 MongoDB High-Yield Reference Sheet

| Feature | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Format** | BSON (Binary JSON) | Always asked in 3-mark Q |
| **Schema** | Schema-less / Dynamic | Compare with RDBMS |
| **Query Language** | MongoDB Query Language (MQL) | JSON-based queries |
| **Default Port** | **27017** | Frequently tested |
| **Document Size Limit** | **16 MB** | Important boundary |
| **Indexing** | B-tree, Geospatial, Text, Hashed | Performance topic |
| **Aggregation** | Aggregation Pipeline (`$match`, `$group`, `$project`) | Advanced queries |
| **Replication** | Replica Sets (Primary + Secondaries) | High availability |
| **Sharding** | Horizontal partitioning by shard key | Scalability |
| **CAP Theorem Position** | **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) | Cloud trade-offs |
| **ACID Compliance** | Yes, since MongoDB 4.0 (multi-doc transactions) | Modern feature |
| **License** | SSPL (Server Side Public License) since 2018 | Licensing nuance |

> [!TIP]
> **CAP Theorem Quick Recap:** MongoDB is **CP** — in case of a network partition, it prefers consistency over availability (the primary will not accept writes if it cannot replicate to a majority).

### 🌍 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **IoT Data Pipelines:** MongoDB stores unstructured sensor data from millions of devices.
- **E-Commerce Catalogs:** Product catalogs have varying attributes (shirts have size+color, laptops have RAM+CPU).
- **Content Management Systems:** Articles, user comments, and metadata are document-shaped.
- **CloudSim Research:** Researchers use MongoDB to **store simulation results** from CloudSim runs for post-experimental analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. CloudSim — Deriving the Simulation Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

The CloudSim simulation follows a strict **9-step pipeline**. We derive each step with full justification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 14-mark question may ask you to "explain the steps involved in creating a CloudSim simulation" or "write the Java code architecture (pseudocode)." Memorizing this pipeline is non-negotiable.

### Step 1 — Initialize the CloudSim Library
The very first call in any CloudSim program is:

```java
int numUsers = 1;
Calendar calendar = Calendar.getInstance();
boolean traceFlag = false;
CloudSim.init(numUsers, calendar, traceFlag);
```

- `numUsers` = number of cloud users in the simulation.
- `calendar` = simulation start time (used for reproducibility).
- `traceFlag` = whether to log events to a file (useful for debugging).
- This call **resets the event queue** and starts the simulation clock at time **0**.

### Step 2 — Create the DataCenter
A DataCenter represents a physical facility (building) hosting servers.

```java
DatacenterCharacteristics characteristics = new DatacenterCharacteristics(
    arch, os, vmm, hostList, timeZone, costPerSec, costPerMem, costPerStorage, costPerBw
);
Datacenter datacenter = new Datacenter(name, characteristics, vmAllocationPolicy, storageList, schedulingInterval);
```

- `arch` = architecture (e.g., `"x86"`).
- `os` = operating system (e.g., `"Linux"`).
- `vmm` = virtual machine monitor (e.g., `"Xen"`).
- `hostList` = list of physical hosts (servers).
- `costPerSec`, `costPerMem`, `costPerStorage`, `costPerBw` = pricing parameters.
- `vmAllocationPolicy` = strategy for placing VMs on hosts (e.g., `VmAllocationPolicySimple`).

### Step 3 — Create Hosts (Physical Servers)

A `Host` has a fixed number of **PEs (Processing Elements / CPU cores)**, RAM, and bandwidth.

```java
List<Pe> peList = new ArrayList<>();
peList.add(new Pe(0, new PeProvisionerSimple(mipsCapacity)));
Host host = new Host(ram, bw, storage, peList, vmScheduler);
```

### Step 4 — Create VMs
Each VM is characterized by MIPS demand, RAM, BW, and a scheduling policy.

```java
Vm vm = new Vm(vmid, brokerId, mips, pesNumber, ram, bw, size, vmm, cloudletScheduler);
```

### Step 5 — Create Cloudlets (Tasks)
A `Cloudlet` represents a unit of work, measured in **Million Instructions (MI)**.

```java
Cloudlet cloudlet = new Cloudlet(id, lengthMI, pesNumber, fileSize, outputSize, utilizationModel);
```

### Step 6 — Create the DatacenterBroker
The broker is the **"customer"** who submits VMs and cloudlets to the data center.

```java
DatacenterBroker broker = new DatacenterBroker("Broker");
broker.submitVmList(vmList);
broker.submitCloudletList(cloudletList);
```

### Step 7 — Start the Simulation
The `CloudSim.startSimulation()` call processes all queued events.

```java
CloudSim.startSimulation();
```

### Step 8 — Retrieve Results
After simulation completion, fetch the finished cloudlets.

```java
List<Cloudlet> newList = broker.getCloudletReceivedList();
CloudSim.stopSimulation();
```

### Step 9 — Print Statistics
Calculate and print metrics: total execution time, total cost, VM utilization, makespan.

---

## 2. Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (14-mark KTU style):** A cloudlet has a length of **10,000 MI**. It is executed on a VM with a processing speed of **2,000 MIPS**. Calculate the execution time. If the VM costs **₹0.05 per second** and the cloudlet takes the calculated time, what is the cost?

### 🔢 Solution with Full Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify Given Values**
- Cloudlet Length $L = 10{,}000$ MI
- VM Speed $S = 2{,}000$ MIPS
- Cost per second $C_s = 0.05$ ₹/s

**Step 2 — Apply the Execution Time Formula**

The execution time is the total number of instructions divided by the rate of execution:

$$
T = \frac{L}{S}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Values**

$$
T = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ MI}}{2{,}000 \text{ MIPS}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Result**

$$
T = 5 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Cost Formula**

The cost is the product of cost rate and execution time:

$$
\text{Cost} = C_s \times T
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and Compute**

$$
\text{Cost} = 0.05 \text{ ₹/s} \times 5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 0.25 \text{ ₹}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Execution Time = **5 seconds**, Cost = **₹0.25**.

---

## 3. MongoDB — Deriving the Sharding Mechanism

### 🔢 Conceptual Derivation: How Sharding Works

**Step 1 — The Problem**
A single MongoDB server can only handle so much data and traffic. Beyond a threshold, vertical scaling (bigger machine) hits hardware limits and is expensive.

**Step 2 — The Solution: Horizontal Partitioning**
Sharding splits the dataset across multiple servers (shards) based on a **shard key** (a chosen field, e.g., `student_id`).

**Step 3 — Choose a Shard Key**
The shard key determines which shard stores which document. For example, if `student_id` ranges from 1 to 10000:
- Shard A: `student_id` in [1, 3333]
- Shard B: `student_id` in [3334, 6666]
- Shard C: `student_id` in [6667, 10000]

**Step 4 — Chunk Splitting**
MongoDB internally splits data into **chunks** (default 64 MB each). When a chunk exceeds the size limit, it is automatically split and migrated to maintain balance.

**Step 5 — Query Routing via Mongos**
A client sends a query to **Mongos**, which:
1. Consults the **Config Server** to find which shard holds the relevant chunk.
2. Forwards the query to that specific shard.
3. Returns the result to the client.

**Step 6 — Mathematical Model of Load Distribution**

If there are $N$ shards and a uniform distribution of shard key values, the load on shard $i$ is:

$$
L_i \approx \frac{\text{Total Documents}}{N}
$$

The **query latency** $Q$ is reduced as:

$$
Q_{\text{sharded}} \approx \frac{Q_{\text{single}}}{N} + Q_{\text{router}}
$$

where $Q_{\text{router}}$ is the small overhead from Mongos routing.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A common 14-mark question asks: *"Explain the sharding architecture of MongoDB with a neat diagram."* This 6-step derivation, paired with the diagram in Section 4, will fetch full marks.

---

## 4. Analytical Matrix: CloudSim vs MongoDB Trade-offs

| Criterion | CloudSim | MongoDB |
|---|---|---|
| **Speed vs Accuracy** | Very fast, approximate | Real-time, exact |
| **Cost** | Free (open source) | Free (community edition) |
| **Use Case** | Research & benchmarking | Production storage |
| **Learning Curve** | Moderate (Java required) | Low (JSON-like syntax) |
| **Scalability** | Single-machine only | Horizontally scalable |
| **Cloud Deployment** | Local/cluster | Atlas, AWS, Azure, GCP |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
