Since `gp2` provides 3 IOPS/GB = $500 \times 3 = 1,500$ baseline IOPS, it cannot deliver 10,000 IOPS at this size. **Insufficient**. Skip.

**Step 2: gp3 cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GB cost} &= 0.08 \times 500 = \$40 \\
\text{Extra IOPS needed} &= 10000 - 3000 = 7000 \; \text{IOPS} \\
\text{IOPS cost} &= 0.005 \times 7000 = \$35 \\
\text{Total gp3 cost} &= 40 + 35 = \$75 \; \text{per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Throughput 500 MB/s requires provisioning above 125 MB/s, costing an additional \$0.04 × 375 = \$15, making the total **\$90** if throughput is billed.)*

**Step 3: io2 cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GB cost} &= 0.125 \times 500 = \$62.5 \\
\text{IOPS cost} &= 0.065 \times 10000 = \$650 \\
\text{Total io2 cost} &= 62.5 + 650 = \$712.5 \; \text{per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison Table**

| Volume Type | IOPS Capability | Monthly Cost | Recommendation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gp2` | 1,500 (INSUFFICIENT) | — | ❌ Cannot meet requirements |
| `gp3` | 10,000 ✓ | **\$90** | ✅ Best cost-performance ratio |
| `io2` | 10,000 ✓ | **\$712.5** | ❌ Overkill for non-critical DB |

### Final Answer
**`gp3` is the optimal choice** for this startup scenario: it meets the 10,000 IOPS requirement at **~12% the cost of `io2`**. The extra cost of `io2` is justified only for mission-critical databases requiring the lowest possible latency and highest durability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EBS Architecture Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UserA["Cloud User"]
    AWSConsole["AWS Management Console"]
    EC2Inst["EC2 Instance Nitro Hypervisor"]
    Vol1["EBS Volume Root gp3"]
    Vol2["EBS Volume Data io2"]
    Vol3["EBS Volume Log st1"]
    StorageSrv1["Storage Server AZ-1a"]
    StorageSrv2["Storage Server AZ-1a"]
    S3Bucket["S3 Snapshot Bucket"]
    KMSKey["KMS Encryption Key"]

    UserA -->|1 Provision| AWSConsole
    AWSConsole -->|2 Create Volume| StorageSrv1
    AWSConsole -->|2 Create Volume| StorageSrv2
    StorageSrv1 -->|3 Replicate| StorageSrv2
    AWSConsole -->|4 Attach| EC2Inst
    EC2Inst -->|5 Read Write IO| Vol1
    EC2Inst -->|5 Read Write IO| Vol2
    EC2Inst -->|5 Read Write IO| Vol3
    Vol1 -->|6 Backed By| StorageSrv1
    Vol2 -->|6 Backed By| StorageSrv2
    Vol3 -->|6 Backed By| StorageSrv1
    StorageSrv1 -->|7 Encrypted With| KMSKey
    StorageSrv2 -->|7 Encrypted With| KMSKey
    AWSConsole -->|8 Initiate Snapshot| S3Bucket
    S3Bucket -->|9 Cross AZ Replicated| UserA
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- Steps **1–3** = Provisioning and replication.
- Steps **4–6** = Attachment and I/O path.
- Step **7** = Encryption at rest using AWS KMS.
- Steps **8–9** = Snapshot creation in S3 for cross-AZ durability.

## 4.2 Snapshot Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start(["Live EBS Volume"]) --> S1["Create Snapshot Initial Full Copy to S3"]
    S1 --> S2["Modify Data on Volume"]
    S2 --> S3["Create Next Snapshot Incremental Saves Only Changed Blocks"]
    S3 --> S4["Modify Data Again"]
    S4 --> S5["Create Third Snapshot Incremental"]
    S5 --> Delete(["Delete Middle Snapshot"])
    S5 -.->|Roll Forward Merge| S1
    Delete -->|S1 Still Holds Original Blocks| S1
    S5 --> Restore["Restore Volume from Latest Snapshot"]
    Restore --> End(["New EBS Volume Populated"])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight on Snapshots**: Snapshots are **incremental**, but deleting an intermediate snapshot does not delete the underlying data blocks until **all** snapshots referencing them are removed. This is why snapshots are space-efficient and safe to manage.

## 4.3 RAID Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OS["Guest Operating System"]
    MBRaid["Linux mdadm or Windows Storage Spaces"]
    V1["EBS Volume 1 io2"]
    V2["EBS Volume 2 io2"]
    V3["EBS Volume 3 io2"]
    V4["EBS Volume 4 io2"]

    OS --> MBRaid
    MBRaid -->|Stripe| V1
    MBRaid -->|Stripe| V2
    MBRaid -->|Mirror| V3
    MBRaid -->|Mirror| V4
    V1 -.->|Aggregated IOPS| MBRad
    V2 -.->|Aggregated IOPS| MBRad
    V3 -.->|Redundancy| MBRaid
    V4 -.->|Redundancy| MBRaid
```

This shows **RAID 10** (striped mirrors) which provides:
- **Performance** = Sum of IOPS from one side of the mirror (e.g., V1 + V2).
- **Redundancy** = Tolerates failure of one volume in each mirror pair (V3 or V4).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define EBS and distinguish it from S3. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

**Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS)** is a high-performance **block-level storage service** designed for use with Amazon EC2 instances, providing persistent, low-latency storage volumes.

**Distinction from S3**:

| Feature | EBS | S3 |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Type | **Block storage** | Object storage |
| Access | Mounted as a raw disk device | HTTP/REST API |
| Use Case | Databases, boot volumes, file systems | Backups, media, static websites |
| Access Pattern | Random, low-latency I/O | Sequential, high-throughput reads |
| AZ Scope | Single AZ | Multi-AZ, multi-region |
| Max Object Size | Volume up to 64 TiB | Object up to 5 TiB |

---

### Question 2: List any three EBS volume types and their primary use cases. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

1. **gp3 (General Purpose SSD)** – Boot volumes, development environments, small to medium databases. Baseline 3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s free, scalable up to 80,000 IOPS.
2. **io2 (Provisioned IOPS SSD)** – Mission-critical relational databases like Oracle, SQL Server, PostgreSQL requiring predictable, high-IOPS performance up to 64,000 IOPS per volume.
3. **st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD)** – Big data workloads such as Hadoop, Kafka, and data warehousing where sequential read/write throughput (up to 500 MB/s) is more important than IOPS.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A(a)** [7 Marks]: Explain the architecture of Amazon EBS with a neat diagram. Discuss the various SSD-backed volume types in detail. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer**:

**EBS Architecture**:
Amazon EBS consists of **storage servers** within an Availability Zone that host volumes. When a user provisions a volume, AWS allocates physical storage on these servers. The volume is then **attached** to an EC2 instance over the AWS internal network using the Nitro System, presenting the volume to the guest OS as a raw block device (e.g., `/dev/nvme1n1`).

Key architectural points:
1. **AZ-Scoped**: All volumes reside within a single AZ for low latency.
2. **Replication**: Each volume is replicated within the AZ to protect against hardware failure.
3. **Snapshots in S3**: Backups are stored as incremental snapshots in S3 for cross-AZ durability.
4. **Encryption**: At-rest encryption uses AWS KMS keys; in-transit encryption uses the Nitro System.
5. **Detachability**: Volumes can be detached and re-attached to other instances in the same AZ.

**SSD-Backed Volume Types**:

- **gp3 (General Purpose SSD)**: Latest generation, **3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s baseline** included for free up to 1 TiB. Scalable to 80,000 IOPS and 4,000 MB/s independently. Recommended for most workloads. **20% cheaper** than gp2.
- **gp2 (Legacy General Purpose SSD)**: 3 IOPS/GB baseline, bursting to 3,000 IOPS for volumes <1 TiB. Max 16,000 IOPS, 250 MB/s. Used for boot volumes and dev/test environments.
- **io2 Block Express**: Premium tier for SAP HANA, Oracle RAC. Up to **256,000 IOPS** and **4,000 MB/s** with sub-millisecond latency. **99.999% durability** with 6 nines of availability.
- **io2 (Provisioned IOPS SSD)**: 64,000 IOPS, 1,000 MB/s, 99.999% durability, ideal for I/O-intensive databases.
- **io1 (Legacy Provisioned IOPS)**: Same as io2 but **99.9% durability** (only 3 nines) and older generation.

**Decision Criteria**:
- Boot volume → `gp3`.
- OLTP database → `io2`.
- Mission-critical SAP HANA → `io2 Block Express`.

**Question A(b)** [7 Marks]: With a suitable example, explain how snapshots are created and used for data backup in EBS. Differentiate between data lifecycle manager and manual snapshots. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:

**EBS Snapshots**:
A snapshot is a **point-in-time, incremental backup** of an EBS volume stored in Amazon S3. The first snapshot is a **full copy**; subsequent snapshots are **incremental**, storing only the blocks that have changed since the previous snapshot.

**Example Workflow**:
1. **Create Volume**: Provision a 100 GiB `gp3` volume.
2. **First Snapshot**: "Snapshot-1" copies all 100 GiB to S3.
3. **Modify Data**: User writes 10 GiB of new data on the volume.
4. **Second Snapshot**: "Snapshot-2" copies only the 10 GiB of new blocks to S3.
5. **Restore**: To recover, AWS uses Snapshot-1 + Snapshot-2 to reconstruct the volume.

Snapshots can be:
- **Copied across AZs/regions** for disaster recovery.
- **Used to create new volumes** (in any AZ from a cross-AZ copy).
- **Automated** using Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM).

**Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) vs Manual Snapshots**:

| Feature | Manual Snapshots | Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) |
|---|---|---|
| Automation | User must create/delete manually | Automates creation, retention, deletion |
| Frequency | As needed | Scheduled (e.g., every 12 hours, daily, weekly) |
| Retention Policy | User-managed | Configurable (e.g., retain last 7 daily, 4 weekly) |
| Cross-Region Copy | Manual | Automated as part of policy |
| Use Case | One-off backups, testing | Production backup policies, compliance |

DLM is implemented through **IAM roles** and **tag-based policies** that target volumes with specific tags.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Question B(a)** [7 Marks]: Compare EBS with Instance Store. Under what circumstances would you prefer Instance Store over EBS? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer**:

**Comparison Table**:

| Feature | EBS | Instance Store |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage Type** | Network-attached block storage | Local physical disk attached to host |
| **Persistence** | **Persistent** (data survives stop/start) | **Ephemeral** (data lost on stop/termination) |
| **Performance** | Consistent, provisioned IOPS | **Very high** (millions of IOPS, low latency) |
| **Cost** | Billed per GB-month and per IOPS provisioned | **Free** (included with instance) |
| **Backup** | Snapshots (S3) | No native backup; must be replicated externally |
| **Detachability** | Detachable, attachable to other instances | **Cannot be detached** |
| **Use Case** | Databases, boot volumes, file servers | Caches, buffers, scratch data, distributed stores (e.g., Redis replication) |

**When to prefer Instance Store**:
1. **Temporary data**: Cache layers, in-memory-like data structures that can be rebuilt.
2. **High-performance requirements**: When you need millions of IOPS and sub-100-microsecond latency (e.g., NoSQL databases like Redis with replication).
3. **Distributed storage systems**: HDFS, Cassandra, Kafka where data is already replicated across nodes.
4. **Cost-sensitive workloads**: When data persistence is not required, you save on EBS costs.

**Example**: A **Redis cluster** running on EC2 with 3 nodes — each node has an instance store volume for local AOF (Append-Only File) writes. Data is replicated across nodes, so instance store loss on one node is acceptable.

---

**Question B(b)** [7 Marks]: A company wants to host a 2 TiB MySQL database on AWS. The database requires 20,000 IOPS consistently and 750 MB/s throughput. The company wants cost optimization. Design a suitable EBS storage architecture. Calculate the monthly cost. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer**:

**Design Analysis**:

- **Size**: 2 TiB = 2 × 1024 = **2,048 GiB**.
- **Required IOPS**: 20,000 consistent.
- **Required Throughput**: 750 MB/s.
- **Constraint**: Cost-optimized.

**Volume Type Evaluation**:

- **`gp2`**: At 2,048 GiB, baseline = 2,048 × 3 = 6,144 IOPS. Cannot deliver 20,000 IOPS. **Insufficient**.
- **`gp3`**: Scalable to 80,000 IOPS and 4,000 MB/s independently. **Meets requirements**.
- **`io2`**: 64,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s available. **Overkill and expensive** for this use case.

**Recommended Architecture**: **A single `gp3` volume of 2,048 GiB provisioned with 20,000 IOPS and 750 MB/s throughput.**

**Cost Calculation** (illustrative pricing):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GB cost} &= 0.08 \times 2048 = \$163.84 \\
\text{Extra IOPS} &= 20000 - 3000 = 17000 \\
\text{IOPS cost} &= 0.005 \times 17000 = \$85.00 \\
\text{Extra Throughput} &= 750 - 125 = 625 \; \text{MB/s} \\
\text{Throughput cost} &= 0.04 \times 625 = \$25.00 \\
\text{Total Monthly Cost} &= 163.84 + 85.00 + 25.00 = \$273.84
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total ≈ \$274 per month**.

**Alternative (High Availability)**: For production, add a **standby replica in another AZ** with a synchronous copy (e.g., using **Amazon RDS Multi-AZ** which automatically provisions a standby `gp3` volume). The total cost would roughly double to **~\$548/month**, but the system can survive an AZ failure.

**Snapshots**: Schedule daily snapshots using **DLM**, retaining 7 daily + 4 weekly + 12 monthly. Snapshot storage cost is roughly \$0.05/GB-month, so 2 TiB × 4 full snapshots ≈ 8 TiB ≈ \$400/month in S3 (incremental snapshots reduce this significantly).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks**:
> 1. **Confusing AZ-scope with Region-scope**: EBS volumes are **AZ-scoped**, not region-scoped. A volume in `us-east-1a` **cannot** be attached to an instance in `us-east-1b`. To move data, create a snapshot, copy the snapshot cross-AZ, and create a new volume.
> 2. **Forgetting the IOPS/Throughput trade-off**: A `gp2` volume of 1,000 GiB has 3,000 IOPS baseline. With 16 KiB I/O, the IOPS-limited throughput is only **46.88 MB/s**, far below the 250 MB/s cap. Students often assume the cap applies.
> 3. **Boot volume restriction**: HDD-backed volumes (`st1`, `sc1`) **cannot be used as boot volumes**. Forgetting this loses 2 marks easily.
> 4. **Snapshot ≠ Backup of Running DB**: Creating a snapshot of a live database requires either a **filesystem freeze** (e.g., `fsfreeze`) or detaching the volume first, otherwise the snapshot may be inconsistent. Application-level snapshots (e.g., MySQL's `mysqldump`) are also needed.
> 5. **gp2 Burst Credit Misconception**: `gp2` burst credits are earned at 3 IOPS/GB and capped at a **bucket size of 5.4 million credits**. Volumes < 1,000 GiB burst to 3,000 IOPS, but large volumes cannot burst at all.
> 6. **Encryption is volume-level, not instance-level**: An encrypted volume's data is encrypted at rest using AWS KMS. The **same encryption status propagates to snapshots and re-created volumes** — you cannot create an unencrypted snapshot from an encrypted volume.
> 7. **Throughput/IOPS Decoupling in gp3**: Unlike `gp2`, in `gp3` you can **independently provision IOPS and throughput** without changing volume size. Many students wrongly assume size drives both.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ EBS = **Block-level, persistent, AZ-scoped** storage for EC2.
- ✅ **6 volume types**: `gp2`, `gp3`, `io1`, `io2`, `io2 Block Express`, `st1`, `sc1`.
- ✅ **gp3** = 3,000 IOPS + 125 MB/s baseline; scalable to 80,000 IOPS + 4,000 MB/s.
- ✅ **gp2** = 3 IOPS/GB, bursts to 3,000 IOPS (only if size < 1,000 GiB).
- ✅ **io2 Block Express** = top-tier, 256,000 IOPS, 4,000 MB/s, 99.999% durability.
- ✅ **HDD volumes (`st1`, `sc1`) CANNOT be boot volumes**.
- ✅ **Snapshots are incremental, stored in S3, and can be copied cross-region**.
- ✅ **Encryption uses AWS KMS**; propagates to snapshots and re-created volumes.
- ✅ **Snapshots of running DBs may be inconsistent** — use filesystem freeze or app-level snapshots.
- ✅ **RAID 0** (stripe) for higher IOPS, **RAID 1** (mirror) for redundancy, **RAID 10** for both.
- ✅ **EBS is NOT multi-AZ natively**; cross-AZ resilience requires snapshots or RDS Multi-AZ.
- ✅ **Max volume size = 64 TiB** (gp3, io1, io2). Max IOPS/volume = 256,000. Max throughput = 4,000 MB/s.
- ✅ **Cost formula**: $C = (\text{GB-rate} \times \text{GB}) + (\text{IOPS-rate} \times \text{IOPS}) + (\text{TP-rate} \times \text{MB/s})$.
- ✅ **Instance Store = ephemeral, local, high-performance, free**; not for persistent data.
- ✅ **Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) automates snapshot creation, retention, and cross-region copy**.
- ✅ **Throughput (MB/s) = min(Cap, IOPS × I/O size / 1024)** — this is the **MOST TESTED FORMULA** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amazon CDN Services. Google Cloud Platform (GCP) - Compute Engine (GCE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Amazon CDN Services. Google Cloud Platform (GCP) - Compute Engine (GCE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Amazon CloudFront (Amazon's CDN Service)

**Amazon CloudFront** is a **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** service offered by AWS that securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to users globally with **low latency** and **high transfer speeds**. It uses a global network of **Points of Presence (PoPs)** — including both **Edge Locations** and **Regional Edge Caches** — to cache content close to the end user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** *A CDN is a system of geographically distributed servers that work together to provide fast delivery of Internet content. CloudFront is AWS's global CDN, routing each user request to the edge location that provides the lowest latency.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Pizza Shop Chain"

Imagine a single pizza shop in Delhi trying to deliver pizzas all over India. By the time a pizza reaches Kerala, it's cold and late. Now imagine the pizza shop opens small **outlets (Edge Locations)** in every major city. When a customer in Kochi orders a Margherita, the **nearest outlet** (closest Edge Location) makes and delivers it. If a particular outlet is missing a topping, it calls the **main kitchen (Origin)** and temporarily stores the topping in a **regional warehouse (Regional Edge Cache)** for future orders. This is exactly how CloudFront works — your website, video, or API is the "pizza," and the Edge Locations are the local outlets.

| Key Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Edge Location** | The "local outlet" — a data center where content is cached close to users. |
| **Regional Edge Cache** | A larger "regional warehouse" sitting between Edge Locations and Origin, used to cache content that is not popular enough to be at every Edge Location. |
| **Origin** | The "main kitchen" — the original source of content (S3, EC2, ELB, Route 53, MediaStore, custom HTTP server). |
| **PoP (Point of Presence)** | The physical site that hosts Edge Locations and Regional Edge Caches. |
| **Distribution** | The CloudFront configuration object that defines origin, behavior, and caching rules. |

---

## 1.2 Google Compute Engine (GCE)

**Google Compute Engine (GCE)** is the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** component of Google Cloud Platform that delivers **scalable, high-performance virtual machines (VMs)** running in Google's data centers. It provides configurable machine types, persistent block storage, global load balancing, and integrates with the broader GCP ecosystem (BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Kubernetes Engine).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Concise):** *GCE is Google's secure, customizable compute service that lets users run virtual machines on Google's infrastructure. It is equivalent to AWS EC2 and Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "Renting an Apartment vs. Renting a Furnished Hall"

Think of GCE like renting a fully-furnished hall in Google's huge apartment complex:
- The **building (Project)** is your logical container.
- The **city (Region)** is where the building exists (e.g., Mumbai, Singapore).
- The **wing of the building (Zone)** is an isolated data center within that city.
- The **flat (VM Instance)** is what you actually rent — pre-defined sizes (1BHK, 2BHK, 3BHK) called *Machine Types*.
- You can also design a **custom flat layout (Custom Machine Type)** if predefined sizes don't fit.
- The **landlord (Google)** can perform **live maintenance (Live Migration)** without kicking you out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key GCE Terminology (bolded for exam priority):**
> - **Project** → Top-level organizing entity; quota & billing container.
> - **Region** → Independent geographic area (e.g., `asia-south1` – Mumbai).
> - **Zone** → Isolated location within a Region (e.g., `asia-south1-a`).
> - **Instance** → The actual Virtual Machine.
> - **Machine Type** → The hardware configuration (vCPUs + RAM) of an instance.
> - **Preemptible / Spot VM** → Discounted, short-lived VMs that can be terminated by Google.

---

### 1.3 Why These Services Matter (Big Picture)

| Feature | Amazon CloudFront | Google Compute Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Category** | CDN (Edge Service) | IaaS Compute |
| **Analogy** | Local pizza outlets | Rented furnished hall |
| **Primary Benefit** | Low-latency global content delivery | Scalable VMs on demand |
| **Origin / Source** | S3, EC2, ELB, etc. | Self-contained compute |
| **Global Reach** | 600+ PoPs across 90+ cities | 40+ regions, 120+ zones |
| **Pricing Unit** | Data transfer out + requests | vCPU-hour + GB-RAM-hour + storage |
| **SLA** | **99.9%** monthly uptime | **99.99%** multi-zone, **99.9%** single zone |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Amazon CloudFront — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 How a CloudFront Request Flows (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **User (Viewer)** requests a file, e.g., `https://d111.cloudfront.net/image.jpg`.
2. **DNS Routing** → CloudFront DNS routes the user to the **nearest Edge Location** based on network latency.
3. **Cache Check at Edge** → CloudFront checks if the object is in that Edge Location's cache.
   - ✅ **Cache HIT** → Object is served immediately from the Edge Location.
   - ❌ **Cache MISS** → Edge Location forwards the request to a **Regional Edge Cache**.
4. **Regional Edge Cache Check** → If HIT, returns the object to the Edge Location. If MISS, the request goes to the **Origin**.
5. **Origin Fetch** → Origin (e.g., S3) returns the object. The Regional Edge Cache **stores a copy** for future requests in that region. The Edge Location also caches a copy.
6. **Response to User** → The object is delivered to the viewer; CloudFront applies the **expiration (TTL)** policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caching Principle:** Popular content stays at Edge Locations (smaller, more numerous). Less-popular content stays at Regional Edge Caches. Rarely-accessed content is fetched from Origin.

### 2.1.2 CloudFront Distribution Types

| Type | Purpose | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Web Distribution** | HTTP/HTTPS delivery for static & dynamic content | Websites, APIs, video streaming |
| **RTMP Distribution** | Real-Time Messaging Protocol (Adobe Flash) | Legacy media streaming (now deprecated) |

### 2.1.3 Origins Supported by CloudFront

- **Amazon S3 Bucket** — Most common origin for static assets.
- **AWS MediaStore container** / **MediaPackage** — For live streaming.
- **AWS ELB (Elastic Load Balancer)** — For dynamic EC2-backed origins.
- **Custom Origin** — Any public HTTP server (on-prem, non-AWS).

### 2.1.4 Origin Access Identity (OAI)

> [!NOTE]
> An **Origin Access Identity (OAI)** is a special CloudFront user that you attach to an S3 bucket policy so that the bucket can be accessed **only** through CloudFront — never directly via S3 URL. This prevents bypass attacks.

### 2.1.5 CloudFront Price Classes (Cheat Sheet for Exams)

CloudFront lets you choose **how many edge locations** to use; the more edge locations, the higher the cost.

| Price Class | Edge Locations Used | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price Class 100** | Lowest cost locations only (e.g., US, EU) | **$** | Content needed only in select regions |
| **Price Class 200** | All locations **except** South America, Australia | **$$** | Slightly broader reach |
| **Price Class All** | All CloudFront edge locations worldwide | **$$$** | Global reach (default) |

### 2.1.6 Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions

- **Lambda@Edge** → Run Node.js / Python Lambda functions at CloudFront edge locations in response to CloudFront events (viewer request, viewer response, origin request, origin response).
- **CloudFront Functions** → Lightweight JavaScript functions for high-scale, low-latency request manipulation (e.g., URL rewrites, header injection).

### 2.1.7 Signed URLs and Signed Cookies

- **Signed URL** → Time-limited, authenticated URL for one file. Used for premium video, private downloads.
- **Signed Cookies** → Multiple files accessible with one authentication token. Used for streaming a private video playlist.

---

## 2.2 Google Compute Engine — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 The GCP Resource Hierarchy

GCP resources are organized in a strict hierarchy. **Permissions and policies cascade downward**.

$$\text{Organization Node} \rightarrow \text{Folders} \rightarrow \text{Projects} \rightarrow \text{Resources}$$

Within a **Project**, compute resources are structured as:

$$\text{Project} \rightarrow \text{Regions} \rightarrow \text{Zones} \rightarrow \text{Instance Groups} \rightarrow \text{VM Instances}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the hierarchy matters:** You cannot place a VM in a Zone — you must create it in a Zone, which lives in a Region, which lives in a Project. Each level enforces quotas and IAM policies.

### 2.2.2 GCE Machine Types (Exam-Critical)

| Family | Series | Use Case | vCPU : RAM Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|
| **General Purpose** | E2, N2, N2D, T2D | Web servers, dev environments, small DBs | 1 : 4 (N2) |
| **Compute-Optimized** | C2, C2D | Gaming servers, HPC, batch processing | 1 : 4 (high vCPU) |
| **Memory-Optimized** | M2, M3, M1 (Ultramem) | SAP HANA, in-memory databases, Redis | 1 : 8 or higher |
| **Accelerator-Optimized** | A2 | AI/ML training with GPUs (A100, T4) | GPU-attached |
| **Shared-Core** | E2 (shared) | Burstable micro-workloads | Fractional vCPU |

- **Predefined Machine Types** → Standard sizes like `n2-standard-4` (4 vCPU, 16 GB RAM).
- **Custom Machine Types** → You choose vCPUs and RAM independently (e.g., 6 vCPU + 22 GB RAM) for cost optimization.

### 2.2.3 Instance Lifecycle States

1. **Provisioning** → Being created.
2. **Staging** → Resources being prepared.
3. **Running** → Active, accruing charges.
4. **Stopping** → Soft stop, can be restarted.
5. **Stopped** → All charges except attached storage stop.
6. **Suspended** (only for **Preemptible** VMs) → Disk preserved, VM paused.
7. **Terminated** → Deleted; cannot be recovered.

### 2.2.4 GCE Pricing Models (Exam-Favorite)

| Pricing Model | Description | Discount vs. On-Demand |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Pay per second (minimum 1 min) for running VMs | 0 % |
| **Preemptible VM** | Cheap VMs (max 24 hrs) that can be reclaimed any time | Up to **80 %** |
| **Spot VM** | Newer version of preemptible; no max duration; better eviction handling | Up to **60–91 %** |
| **Committed Use Discount (CUD)** | 1- or 3-year commitment for vCPUs + RAM | Up to **57 %** for 3-year |
| **Sustained Use Discount (SUD)** | Automatic discount when running a VM for >25 % of a month | Up to **30 %** (auto) |
| **Custom + Committed** | Combine CUD with custom machine types for max savings | Up to **65 %** |

### 2.2.5 Live Migration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Live Migration** is a defining feature of GCE. Google can move a running VM from one host to another (for hardware maintenance, security, or system updates) **without downtime** or rebooting the VM. AWS EC2 also supports this, but Google pioneered it.

### 2.2.6 Sole-Tenant Nodes

- **Sole-Tenant Nodes** are physical servers dedicated to a single project.
- Used for **licensing compliance** (e.g., Windows Server, SQL Server) where software is licensed per physical host.

### 2.2.7 Persistent Disks & Local SSDs

- **Standard PD** → HDD-backed, cheap, general use.
- **Balanced PD** → SSD-backed, balance of price & performance.
- **SSD PD** → High-performance SSD, for databases.
- **Extreme PD** → Highest IOPS, with provisioned IOPS.
- **Local SSD** → Physically attached, highest performance, but **data lost on VM stop**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLA Annual Downtime** | $D = (1 - \text{SLA}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | $D$ in minutes | min/year |
| **SLA 99.9 %** | $D = 0.001 \times 525600$ | Downtime | **≈ 525.6 min (8.76 h)/yr** |
| **SLA 99.99 %** | $D = 0.0001 \times 525600$ | Downtime | **≈ 52.56 min/yr** |
| **SLA 99.999 % (Five Nines)** | $D = 0.00001 \times 525600$ | Downtime | **≈ 5.26 min/yr** |
| **CloudFront Cost** | $C = V \times P_V + R \times P_R$ | $V$ = data out (GB), $R$ = HTTP requests, $P$ = price | **USD** |
| **GCE On-Demand Cost (hourly)** | $C_h = \text{vCPU count} \times p_{\text{cpu}} + \text{RAM GB} \times p_{\text{ram}}$ | $p_{\text{cpu}}, p_{\text{ram}}$ from GCP price list | **USD/hr** |
| **GCE Preemptible Discount** | $C_{\text{preempt}} = C_{\text{on-demand}} \times (1 - d)$, $d \le 0.80$ | $d$ = discount factor | **USD** |
| **CUD Discount (3-yr)** | $C_{\text{CUD}} = C_{\text{on-demand}} \times (1 - 0.57)$ | Resource-based commitment | **USD** |
| **Caching Hit Ratio** | $H = \dfrac{\text{Cache Hits}}{\text{Cache Hits} + \text{Cache Misses}} \times 100$ | % | % |
| **Origin Offload (CloudFront)** | $O = H \times 100$ | Requests served by Edge | % |
| **Monthly Hours** | $H_m = 24 \times 30 = 720$ hrs (approx) | Used for cost conversion | hours |
| **Custom Machine Type vCPU Range** | $1 \le v \le \text{defined max}$, RAM $\in [v \times 0.9, v \times 6.5]$ GB (N2) | Per family rules | vCPU / GB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** CloudFront cost = **Data Transfer Out + HTTP/HTTPS Requests + (optional) Invalidation Requests**. **There is no charge for data transfer INTO CloudFront from Origin.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Downtime from SLA Percentage

The **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** is the fraction of time a service is expected to be available. Downtime is the complement.

**Step 1 — Convert the time period to a single unit (minutes/year):**

$$\text{Minutes in a year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

**Step 2 — Compute the complement (unavailable fraction):**

$$\text{Downtime Fraction} = 1 - \text{SLA}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to get the absolute downtime in minutes:**

$$D = (1 - \text{SLA}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours if required:**

$$D_{\text{hrs}} = \dfrac{D}{60}$$

### 3.1.1 Numerical Example — SLA of 99.9 %

**Given:** SLA = 99.9 % = 0.999

**Step A — Compute the unavailable fraction:**

$$1 - 0.999 = 0.001$$

**Step B — Apply the formula:**

$$D = 0.001 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

$$D = 0.001 \times 525600$$

$$D = 525.6 \text{ minutes per year}$$

**Step C — Convert to hours:**

$$D_{\text{hrs}} = \dfrac{525.6}{60} = 8.76 \text{ hours per year}$$

> **Result:** A **99.9 % SLA** allows approximately **8.76 hours** of downtime per year.

### 3.1.2 Numerical Example — SLA of 99.99 %

**Given:** SLA = 99.99 % = 0.9999

**Step A — Unavailable fraction:**

$$1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

**Step B — Downtime in minutes:**

$$D = 0.0001 \times 525600 = 52.56 \text{ minutes per year}$$

**Step C — Convert to seconds for clarity:**

$$D_{\text{sec}} = 52.56 \times 60 = 3153.6 \text{ seconds per year}$$

> **Result:** **99.99 % SLA** allows **52.56 minutes** of downtime per year.

### 3.1.3 Comparison Table — SLA vs. Downtime

| SLA | Downtime per Year | Downtime per Month (÷12) | Downtime per Week (÷52) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **99 % (Two Nines)** | 3.65 days (87.6 hrs) | 7.30 hrs | 1.68 hrs |
| **99.9 % (Three Nines)** | 8.76 hrs | 43.83 min | 10.10 min |
| **99.99 % (Four Nines)** | 52.56 min | 4.38 min | 1.01 min |
| **99.999 % (Five Nines)** | 5.26 min | 26.30 sec | 6.05 sec |

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example — CloudFront Monthly Cost

**Problem:** A website serves **5,000 GB** of data via CloudFront (Price Class All) and handles **20 million HTTP requests** in a month. CloudFront pricing (illustrative): **$0.085 per GB** data out + **$0.01 per 10,000 requests**.

**Given:**
- Data volume $V = 5000$ GB
- Request count $R = 20{,}000{,}000$
- Price per GB: $P_V = 0.085$ USD
- Price per 10k requests: $P_R = 0.01$ USD

**Step 1 — Cost of Data Transfer Out:**

$$C_V = V \times P_V = 5000 \times 0.085$$

$$C_V = 425 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Requests:**

$$\text{Units of 10,000 requests} = \dfrac{20{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000} = 2000$$

$$C_R = 2000 \times 0.01 = 20 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Total Cost:**

$$C_{\text{total}} = C_V + C_R = 425 + 20$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = 445 \text{ USD}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A higher cache hit ratio (H) reduces both $C_V$ (data from origin) and request charges to origin. Adding **Lambda@Edge** for image optimization can also reduce the bytes transferred.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — GCE Custom Machine Type Cost

**Problem:** You provision a **Custom N2 machine type** with **6 vCPUs** and **22 GB RAM** in the `asia-south1` (Mumbai) region. On-demand pricing (illustrative): **$0.0445 per vCPU-hour** + **$0.0060 per GB-RAM-hour**. The VM runs **24 × 7** for **30 days**.

**Given:**
- $v = 6$ vCPUs, $r = 22$ GB RAM
- $p_{\text{cpu}} = 0.0445$ USD/vCPU-hr
- $p_{\text{ram}} = 0.0060$ USD/GB-hr
- Runtime $H = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours

**Step 1 — Hourly cost:**

$$C_h = (6 \times 0.0445) + (22 \times 0.0060)$$

$$C_h = 0.2670 + 0.1320 = 0.3990 \text{ USD/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Monthly cost (on-demand):**

$$C_m = 0.3990 \times 720$$

$$C_m = 287.28 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3 — Apply a 3-year Committed Use Discount (57 % off):**

$$C_{\text{CUD}} = 287.28 \times (1 - 0.57)$$

$$C_{\text{CUD}} = 287.28 \times 0.43$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{CUD}} = 123.53 \text{ USD/month}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply a Preemptible Discount (additional 80 % off on-demand):**

$$C_{\text{preempt}} = 287.28 \times (1 - 0.80) = 287.28 \times 0.20$$

$$\boxed{C_{\text{preempt}} = 57.46 \text{ USD/month}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Discounts are applied to the **on-demand base price**, not stacked. You either use CUD **or** Preemptible discount, not both. Custom machine types are eligible for **CUD with custom discount**.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example — Cache Hit Ratio

**Problem:** In one hour, CloudFront records **9,500 cache hits** and **500 cache misses** for a popular image.

**Given:** Hits = 9,500, Misses = 500

**Step 1 — Total requests:**

$$T = 9500 + 500 = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Hit ratio:**

$$H = \dfrac{9500}{10{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{H = 95\%}$$

**Step 3 — Origin offload:**

$$O = 95\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Only 5\% of requests reach the Origin}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Industry targets for a healthy CDN are **H ≥ 90 %**. Below this, the origin server feels high load — fix with longer TTLs, cache-control headers, or price-class adjustments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CloudFront Request Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    userA[VIEWER USER A]
    userB[VIEWER USER B]
    edge1[EDGE LOCATION NORTH AMERICA]
    edge2[EDGE LOCATION ASIA]
    rec1[REGIONAL EDGE CACHE US EAST]
    rec2[REGIONAL EDGE CACHE ASIA]
    s3[ORIGIN S3 BUCKET]
    ec2[ORIGIN EC2 INSTANCE]
    userA --> edge1
    userB --> edge2
    edge1 --> rec1
    edge2 --> rec2
    rec1 --> s3
    rec2 --> ec2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A user in India is routed to the nearest Edge Location in Asia. If the content is missing, the Edge Location consults the Regional Edge Cache. If still missing, the request goes to the Origin (S3/EC2). The response path is reversed.

---

## 4.2 CloudFront Distribution with Cache Behaviors and Lambda@Edge

```mermaid
graph TB
    dist[CLOUDFRONT DISTRIBUTION]
    beh1[BEHAVIOR 1 STATIC CACHE TTL 86400]
    beh2[BEHAVIOR 2 DYNAMIC NO CACHE]
    beh3[BEHAVIOR 3 IMAGES LAMBDA EDGE]
    oai[ORIGIN ACCESS IDENTITY]
    s3b[S3 BUCKET PRIVATE]
    cf[CODE FUNCTION NODE JS]
    dist --> beh1
    dist --> beh2
    dist --> beh3
    beh1 --> oai
    beh3 --> cf
    oai --> s3b
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A single distribution can have **multiple cache behaviors**, each path-pattern-matched (`*.jpg`, `/api/*`, etc.). Static content has a 24-hour TTL. Dynamic content is not cached. Image requests are processed by a **Lambda@Edge** function (e.g., resizing) before being served.

---

## 4.3 GCP Resource Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    org[ORGANIZATION NODE]
    fol1[FOLDER PRODUCTION]
    fol2[FOLDER DEVELOPMENT]
    projA[PROJECT A BILLING 1]
    projB[PROJECT B BILLING 2]
    regA[REGION ASIA SOUTH 1 MUMBAI]
    regB[REGION US CENTRAL 1 IOWA]
    zoneA[ZONE ASIA SOUTH 1 A]
    zoneB[ZONE ASIA SOUTH 1 B]
    instA[INSTANCE N2 STANDARD 4]
    instB[INSTANCE C2 STANDARD 8]
    org --> fol1
    org --> fol2
    fol1 --> projA
    fol2 --> projB
    projA --> regA
    projA --> regB
    regA --> zoneA
    regA --> zoneB
    zoneA --> instA
    zoneB --> instB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The Organization node hosts Folders; Folders contain Projects; Projects contain Regions; Regions contain Zones; Zones contain VM Instances. IAM policies cascade top-down.

---

## 4.4 GCE Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    gce[GOOGLE COMPUTE ENGINE]
    mt[MACHINE TYPES]
    nw[NETWORKING]
    st[STORAGE]
    img[IMAGES AND SNAPSHOTS]
    sec[SECURITY IAM]
    prc[PRICING ENGINE]
    prc1[ON DEMAND]
    prc2[PREEMPTIBLE]
    prc3[SPOT VM]
    prc4[COMMITTED USE]
    prc5[SUSTAINED USE]
    mt1[PREDEFINED N2 E2 C2 M2]
    mt2[CUSTOM MACHINE TYPES]
    nw1[VPC NETWORK]
    nw2[LOAD BALANCING]
    st1[PERSISTENT DISK STANDARD]
    st2[PERSISTENT DISK SSD]
    st3[LOCAL SSD]
    img1[OS IMAGES LINUX WINDOWS]
    img2[CUSTOM IMAGES]
    sec1[FIREWALL RULES]
    sec2[SERVICE ACCOUNTS]
    gce --> mt
    gce --> nw
    gce --> st
    gce --> img
    gce --> sec
    gce --> prc
    mt --> mt1
    mt --> mt2
    nw --> nw1
    nw --> nw2
    st --> st1
    st --> st2
    st --> st3
    img --> img1
    img --> img2
    sec --> sec1
    sec --> sec2
    prc --> prc1
    prc --> prc2
    prc --> prc3
    prc --> prc4
    prc --> prc5
```

> **Reading the diagram:** GCE is the central service. It is composed of Machine Types (predefined and custom), Networking (VPC + load balancers), Storage (PD Standard, PD SSD, Local SSD), Images (OS and custom), Security (firewall + service accounts), and a Pricing engine offering five pricing models.

---

## 4.5 CloudFront Price Class vs. Reach (Comparative Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pc100[PRICE CLASS 100 LOW COST]
    pc200[PRICE CLASS 200 MID TIER]
    pcall[PRICE CLASS ALL GLOBAL]
    r1[US EU ONLY]
    r2[US EU ASIA]
    r3[ALL EDGE LOCATIONS 600 PLUS POPS]
    pc100 --> r1
    pc200 --> r2
    pcall --> r3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define Content Delivery Network (CDN). List any four benefits of Amazon CloudFront.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Content Delivery Network (CDN)** is a globally distributed network of proxy servers deployed in multiple data centers that deliver web content (HTML, images, videos, APIs) to users based on their **geographic proximity** to a server, thereby **reducing latency** and **offloading traffic** from the origin server.

**Four benefits of Amazon CloudFront:**

1. **Low Latency** — Content is served from edge locations near the user.
2. **High Transfer Speeds** — Optimized network paths between AWS regions and PoPs.
3. **Global Scalability** — 600+ PoPs handle sudden traffic spikes seamlessly.
4. **Security Integration** — Works with AWS Shield, WAF, and Lambda@Edge for DDoS protection and code-at-the-edge.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a Machine Type in Google Compute Engine? Name any four predefined machine type families.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Machine Type** in GCE is a pre-defined (or custom) hardware configuration that specifies the **number of virtual CPUs (vCPUs)** and the **amount of memory (RAM)** allocated to a VM instance. Each machine type family is optimized for a specific workload.

**Four predefined machine type families:**

1. **General Purpose (E2, N2, N2D, T2D)** — Balanced vCPU-to-memory ratio; for web servers and dev environments.
2. **Compute-Optimized (C2, C2D)** — High-performance computing, batch processing.
3. **Memory-Optimized (M1, M2, M3)** — In-memory databases, SAP HANA, Redis.
4. **Accelerator-Optimized (A2)** — AI/ML workloads using GPUs (e.g., NVIDIA A100, T4).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q A (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the architecture of Amazon CloudFront with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of Edge Locations and Regional Edge Caches.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:** Amazon CloudFront is AWS's global CDN. It uses a network of Points of Presence (PoPs) — comprising **Edge Locations** and **Regional Edge Caches** — to deliver content with low latency. (1 mark)

**Architecture Components:**

1. **Viewer (User)** — The end-user requesting content (browser, mobile app, smart TV). (0.5 mark)
2. **Edge Locations (600+)** — Small data centers that cache and deliver content close to users. They handle both **cache hits** and forward **cache misses** upward. (1.5 marks)
3. **Regional Edge Caches (13+)** — Larger data centers positioned between Edge Locations and the Origin. They hold **less-popular content** that doesn't justify a slot at every Edge Location. (1.5 marks)
4. **Origin** — The original source of content: S3, EC2, ELB, MediaStore, or a custom HTTP server. (0.5 mark)
5. **CloudFront Distribution** — A logical configuration object that defines origin, cache behaviors, price class, and SSL settings. (0.5 mark)

**Working (Request Flow):**

- Step 1: User requests a file via a CloudFront URL or via a website integrated with CloudFront.
- Step 2: DNS routes the user to the **nearest Edge Location** based on latency.
- Step 3: Edge Location checks its local cache.
  - **HIT** → Object is returned immediately to the user.
  - **MISS** → Edge Location requests the object from the Regional Edge Cache.
- Step 4: Regional Edge Cache checks its cache.
  - **HIT** → Returns object to Edge Location, which serves it to the user.
  - **MISS** → Fetches the object from the **Origin**.
- Step 5: The Origin returns the object, which is cached at the Regional Edge Cache **and** the Edge Location, then served to the user.
- Step 6: CloudFront applies the **TTL (Time to Live)** policy to determine when the cached object expires. (1.5 marks)

**Diagram (Recreated from Section 4.1):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    userA[VIEWER USER A]
    userB[VIEWER USER B]
    edge1[EDGE LOCATION NA]
    edge2[EDGE LOCATION ASIA]
    rec1[REGIONAL EDGE CACHE US EAST]
    rec2[REGIONAL EDGE CACHE ASIA]
    s3[ORIGIN S3 BUCKET]
    userA --> edge1
    userB --> edge2
    edge1 --> rec1
    edge2 --> rec2
    rec1 --> s3
    rec2 --> s3
```

(0.5 mark for diagram)

**Role Summary:**

- **Edge Locations** → Reduce latency by serving cached content close to users.
- **Regional Edge Caches** → Improve cache hit ratio for content that is not popular enough to live at every Edge Location, **offloading** traffic from the Origin. (1 mark)

---

**Q A (b) [7 Marks]: Discuss the various CloudFront pricing components. Explain the three price classes with their use cases.**

**Model Answer:**

**CloudFront Pricing Components:** (4 marks)

1. **Data Transfer Out (DTO)**
   - Charged per **GB of data** delivered from CloudFront edge locations to viewers.
   - Cost varies by region and price class; first 1 TB/month to internet is free (always free tier) under AWS's free tier (subject to change).
2. **HTTP / HTTPS Requests**
   - Charged per **10,000 requests**. HTTPS costs more than HTTP due to TLS handshake overhead.
3. **Origin Shield**
   - An additional caching layer in the AWS region closest to the Origin. Charged per request and per GB.
4. **Invalidation Requests**
   - Charged per path after the first 1,000 free paths per month. Used to force-expire content from caches.
5. **Field-Level Encryption / Dedicated IP Custom SSL**
   - Monthly hourly fee for dedicated SSL certificates (vs. the free shared SNI).
6. **Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions**
   - Charged per invocation and compute duration.

**Three Price Classes:** (3 marks)

| Price Class | Edge Locations Used | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Price Class 100** | Lowest-cost regions only (e.g., US, EU) | **Lowest** | Content targeted only at specific regions (e.g., US-only e-commerce). |
| **Price Class 200** | All locations **except** South America & Australia | **Medium** | Slightly broader global reach with controlled cost. |
| **Price Class All** | All 600+ CloudFront PoPs worldwide | **Highest** | Truly global content (streaming, news, global SaaS). **Default.** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cost Optimization Tip:** If your users are concentrated in North America and Europe, choosing **Price Class 100** can reduce DTO costs by up to 60 % without impacting user experience.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q B (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the hierarchy of resources in Google Compute Engine. With the help of a neat diagram, show the relationship between Project, Region, Zone, and VM Instance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:** Google Cloud Platform organizes resources in a strict hierarchy that determines **IAM permissions, quota allocation, and billing**. (0.5 mark)

**Hierarchy (Top to Bottom):** (3 marks)

1. **Organization Node** — The root of the GCP resource tree. Policies attached here cascade down to all child resources.
2. **Folders** — Optional grouping mechanism inside an Organization (e.g., `Production`, `Development`).
3. **Projects** — The **fundamental** organizing unit in GCP. Every resource, including VMs, must belong to a Project. A Project has:
   - A **name** and unique **Project ID**.
   - **Billing account** attachment.
   - **IAM policies** defining who can access resources.
   - **Quotas** on resource usage.
4. **Regions** — **Independent geographic locations** (e.g., `asia-south1` = Mumbai, `us-central1` = Iowa). A region typically contains **3 or more zones** for high availability.
5. **Zones** — **Isolated physical data centers** within a region. Zones within a region have **low-latency network links** but **independent power and cooling**. Example: `asia-south1-a`, `asia-south1-b`, `asia-south1-c`.
6. **VM Instances** — The **actual virtual machines** that are created within a **specific zone** of a project.

**Relationship:**
- A **Project** is the billing & IAM container.
- A **Project** spans **one or more Regions**.
- A **Region** contains **one or more Zones**.
- A **VM Instance** is created in **one specific Zone** of **one specific Region** of **one specific Project**.

> *If a Project is deleted, all its regions, zones, and instances are deleted.* This is why Project deletion is a destructive action guarded by IAM. (0.5 mark)

**Diagram (Reused from Section 4.3):**

```mermaid
graph TB
    proj[PROJECT BILLING QUOTA IAM]
    regA[REGION ASIA SOUTH 1 MUMBAI]
    regB[REGION US CENTRAL 1 IOWA]
    zoneA[ZONE ASIA SOUTH 1 A]
    zoneB[ZONE ASIA SOUTH 1 B]
    instA[INSTANCE N2 STANDARD 4]
    instB[INSTANCE C2 STANDARD 8]
    proj --> regA
    proj --> regB
    regA --> zoneA
    regA --> zoneB
    zoneA --> instA
    zoneB --> instB
```

(1 mark for diagram with explanation)

**Key Properties:** (2 marks)

- **Resources do not span zones** — A VM is in one zone; to survive a zone failure, you deploy in **multiple zones** using **MIG (Managed Instance Groups)**.
- **Resources do not span regions** — A VM in Mumbai cannot talk to a VM in Iowa via internal network without **VPC Peering or Cloud Interconnect**.
- **Quotas** are per-project, per-region, and per-zone (e.g., 200 vCPUs per region).

---

**Q B (b) [7 Marks]: Discuss the various machine type families in GCE and explain the different pricing options available.**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction:** GCE offers both **predefined** and **custom** machine types, classified into **families** based on workload suitability. The pricing options allow trade-offs between **cost, availability, and commitment**. (0.5 mark)

**Machine Type Families:** (3.5 marks)

1. **General Purpose (E2, N2, N2D, T2D)**
   - Balanced vCPU-to-memory ratio (N2: 1:4).
   - Use: Web servers, small/medium databases, dev environments.
   - **E2** offers cost-optimized shared-core instances.
   - **T2D** uses **AMD EPYC** processors.
2. **Compute-Optimized (C2, C2D)**
   - High vCPU count, lower memory ratio.
   - Use: Gaming servers, HPC, scientific computing, batch processing.
   - **C2D** uses 3rd Gen AMD EPYC.
3. **Memory-Optimized (M1, M2, M3, Ultra Memory)**
   - High RAM-to-vCPU ratio (1:8 or higher).
   - Use: SAP HANA, in-memory databases (Redis, Memcached), big-data analytics.
   - **Ultra Memory** configurations offer up to **12 TB RAM** in a single VM.
4. **Accelerator-Optimized (A2)**
   - GPU-attached instances for AI/ML training and inference.
   - **A100 GPUs** for deep learning, **T4 GPUs** for inference.
5. **Shared-Core (E2 shared, f1-micro)**
   - Burstable fractional-vCPU instances for low-traffic workloads.

**Custom Machine Types:** Let you independently choose vCPUs (1 to a max per family) and memory (within an allowed range), enabling cost savings when the workload doesn't fit a predefined size.

**Pricing Options:** (3 marks)

| Pricing Model | Description | Max Discount |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Pay per second (min 1 min) for running VMs | 0 % (baseline) |
| **Preemptible VM** | Cheap VMs (max 24 hrs) that Google can reclaim at any time | **Up to 80 %** |
| **Spot VM** | Modern version of Preemptible — no max duration, better eviction handling | **Up to 60–91 %** |
| **Committed Use Discount (CUD)** | 1-year or 3-year commitment for vCPUs + RAM | **Up to 57 %** (3-yr) |
| **Sustained Use Discount (SUD)** | Automatic discount when a VM runs for >25 % of a month | **Up to 30 %** (auto-applied) |
| **Custom + Committed** | Combine custom machine types with CUD for further savings | **Up to 65 %** |

> **Exam Insight:** CUD and Preemptible discounts **cannot be stacked** on the same VM. A VM is either on-demand with SUD, or on-demand with CUD, or preemptible — not a combination.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Google App Engine (GAE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Google App Engine (GAE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Google App Engine (GAE)** is a fully managed **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** offering within Google Cloud Platform (GCP) that enables developers to build, deploy, run, and scale web applications and mobile back-ends on Google's massive global infrastructure without needing to provision, manage, or maintain the underlying servers, storage, networks, or databases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** GAE is a cloud computing platform for developing and hosting web applications in Google-managed data centers. It provides automatic scaling, built-in security, load balancing, and supports multiple language runtimes including Java, Python, PHP, Go, Node.js, Ruby, and .NET.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Electricity Grid vs. Power Plant**

Imagine you want to power a house:

- **Traditional Approach (IaaS):** You buy a generator (virtual machine), install fuel tanks, maintain the engine, and fix it when it breaks. You control everything but are responsible for everything.
- **GAE Approach (PaaS):** You simply plug an appliance into the wall socket. The electricity company (Google) generates the power, scales the grid during peak hours, and handles all maintenance. You just pay for what you consume.

> [!NOTE]
> In GAE, your **"appliance" is your code**, the **"wall socket" is the deployment endpoint**, and Google handles the **"electricity grid"** (servers, load balancers, scaling, patching) behind the scenes.

### 1.3 Core Characteristics of GAE

| Characteristic | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Fully Managed** | Google manages infrastructure | High-yield exam topic |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Scales from 0 to millions of users | Core PaaS feature |
| **Pay-per-Use** | Pay only for consumed resources | Economic model |
| **Multi-Language** | Supports Java, Python, Go, Node.js, PHP, Ruby, .NET | Runtime comparison |
| **Built-in Services** | Datastore, Memcache, Task Queues, User Auth | Platform capabilities |
| **Sandbox Security** | Applications run in isolated environments | Security model |

### 1.4 Historical Context

> [!NOTE]
> **GAE was launched in April 2008**, originally supporting only **Python**. Java support was added in 2009. The platform was the first major PaaS offering and set the industry standard for managed application hosting. It pioneered concepts like **automatic scaling** and **datastore abstraction** that are now standard across all PaaS platforms.

### 1.5 Position in Cloud Service Models

$$
\text{IaaS} \subset \text{PaaS} \subset \text{SaaS}
$$

- **IaaS (e.g., AWS EC2):** You manage OS, runtime, middleware, application, data
- **PaaS (e.g., GAE):** You manage only application and data
- **SaaS (e.g., Gmail):** You only use the software

GAE sits firmly in the PaaS layer, abstracting away the entire infrastructure stack.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GAE Architecture — Layered Breakdown

The GAE architecture is divided into **four primary layers**, each with specific responsibilities:

#### Layer 1: Application Layer (User-Developed)
- Application code written in supported runtimes
- Configuration via `app.yaml` (Python/PHP) or `appengine-web.xml` (Java)
- Static files (HTML, CSS, JS, images)
- Application ID, version, service routing rules

#### Layer 2: Runtime Layer (Google-Managed)
- **Standard Environment:** Lightweight sandbox containers with specific language runtimes
- **Flexible Environment:** Docker containers running on Google Compute Engine VMs
- Automatic runtime updates and security patches

#### Layer 3: Platform Services Layer (Google-Managed)
- **Datastore (NoSQL)** — schemaless, horizontally scalable
- **Memcache** — in-memory key-value cache
- **Task Queues** — asynchronous work scheduling
- **Cloud SQL** — relational database (managed MySQL/PostgreSQL)
- **User Authentication** — Google Sign-In integration
- **Blobstore / Cloud Storage** — large file storage
- **Mail, Push, Search APIs** — additional managed services

#### Layer 4: Infrastructure Layer (Google-Managed)
- Physical servers across Google's global data centers
- Networking fabric with edge caching
- Load balancers, fault tolerance, replication

### 2.2 Application Lifecycle in GAE

The complete lifecycle consists of **five stages**:

1. **Development** — Code is written locally or in Cloud Shell
2. **Testing** — Local emulator (`gcloud beta emulators`) simulates production
3. **Deployment** — `gcloud app deploy` uploads code to Google
4. **Scaling** — GAE automatically provisions instances based on load
5. **Monitoring & Logging** — Stackdriver (now Cloud Operations) tracks health, errors, latency

### 2.3 Two Runtime Environments: Standard vs. Flexible

| Feature | Standard Environment | Flexible Environment |
|---|---|---|
| **Instance Type** | Sandboxed containers | Docker containers on GCE VMs |
| **Startup Time** | Milliseconds (fast scale-out) | Minutes (slower) |
| **Scaling to Zero** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (minimum 1 instance) |
| **Custom Runtimes** | ❌ Limited to supported languages | ✅ Any language via Dockerfile |
| **SSH Access** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (debug via SSH) |
| **Pricing Model** | Per-instance-hour | Per-vCPU, memory, disk |
| **Network Access** | Restricted (sandbox) | Full VPC access |
| **Local Disk** | ❌ Ephemeral only | ✅ Persistent disk support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The Standard Environment is the **"classic GAE"** and is the most commonly tested. The Flexible Environment was introduced in 2016 to address limitations of the sandbox (e.g., need for native binaries, SSH debugging).

### 2.4 Scaling Types in GAE

GAE supports **three automatic scaling modes**:

#### a) Automatic Scaling (Default)
- Scales based on request rate, CPU, memory, latency
- Can scale to **zero instances** when no traffic
- Configured via `automatic_scaling` block in `app.yaml`

#### b) Manual Scaling
- Fixed number of instances regardless of load
- Suitable for steady, predictable workloads
- Cannot scale to zero (minimum 1 instance)

#### c) Basic Scaling
- Instances created when requests arrive, terminated when idle
- Max 10 minutes per request (longer than standard)
- Suitable for ad-hoc workloads

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Variables & Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Instance Hours** | $\text{Cost} = N_i \times T_h \times P_h$ | $N_i$ = instances, $T_h$ = hours, $P_h$ = hourly price | Billing calculation |
| **Concurrent Requests per Instance** | $\text{MaxConcurrent} = 10$ (default) | Hard limit for Standard env | Throughput planning |
| **Instance Memory** | $M_{\text{inst}} \in \{128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048\}$ MB | Memory class F1–F4 | Capacity planning |
| **Scaling Threshold (Latency)** | $L_{\text{target}} \in \{10, 15, 30, 60, 100\}$ ms | Default 1000ms? Adjustable | Auto-scaling trigger |
| **Task Queue Throughput** | $R_q \le 500$ tasks/sec/queue | Default queue limit | Async processing |
| **Datastore Read Cost** | $C_r = N_r \times P_r$ | $N_r$ = reads, $P_r$ = price/read | Cost estimation |
| **App Version Limit** | $V_{\max} = 1000$ versions/app | Storage cost consideration | Deployment management |
| **Request Timeout (Standard)** | $T_{\text{timeout}} = 60$ seconds | Hard limit | Long-task handling |
| **Cold Start Time** | $T_{\text{cold}} \approx 1\text{–}5$ s | First request latency | User experience |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** Always express cloud resource units in **bold** when writing exam answers. Use **GB**, **vCPU**, **ms**, **requests/sec** as standard metrics.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

GAAE is widely used in:

- **Web Application Hosting:** E-commerce sites, content portals
- **Mobile Backends:** RESTful APIs for iOS/Android apps
- **Microservices:** Lightweight, independently scalable services
- **IoT Data Ingestion:** High-volume sensor data pipelines
- **Enterprise Internal Tools:** HR portals, dashboards
- **Startups:** Rapid MVP deployment without infrastructure investment
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: GAE Cost Calculation for Auto-Scaling

**Problem:** A web application deployed on GAE Standard Environment runs with the following configuration:
- **Instance class:** F2 (256 MB RAM)
- **Average instances active:** 3
- **Daily active hours:** 18 hours
- **Outbound bandwidth:** 5 GB/day
- **Datastore reads:** 50,000/day
- **Datastore writes:** 5,000/day
- **Instance hour rate:** $0.05/hour

**Step 1: Calculate daily instance hour cost**

We need to determine the total instance-hours consumed per day.

$$
\text{InstanceHours}_{\text{day}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times T_{\text{active}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{InstanceHours}_{\text{day}} = 3 \times 18 = 54 \text{ instance-hours}
$$

This means 54 instance-hours are billed per day.

**Step 2: Calculate the daily compute cost**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{compute}} = \text{InstanceHours}_{\text{day}} \times P_{\text{hour}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{compute}} = 54 \times 0.05 = \$2.70 \text{ per day}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate monthly compute cost (30 days)**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{compute,month}} = 2.70 \times 30 = \$81.00
$$

**Step 4: Add Datastore Costs (assuming $0.06 per 100K reads, $0.18 per 100K writes)**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{reads}} = \frac{50{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 0.06 = 0.03
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{writes}} = \frac{5{,}000}{100{,}000} \times 0.18 = 0.009
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{storage,day}} = 0.03 + 0.009 = \$0.039 \text{ per day}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate monthly total**

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{total,month}} = 81.00 + (0.039 \times 30) = 81.00 + 1.17 = \$82.17
$$

**Final Result:** The approximate monthly cost is **$82.17**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other PaaS Offerings

| Parameter | Google App Engine | AWS Elastic Beanstalk | Azure App Service | Heroku |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Provider** | Google | Amazon | Microsoft | Salesforce |
| **Launch Year** | 2008 | 2011 | 2010 | 2007 |
| **Supported Languages** | 7+ | 7+ | .NET, Java, Node, Python, PHP, Ruby | 8+ |
| **Auto Scaling** | ✅ Native | ✅ Via CloudWatch | ✅ Built-in | ✅ Manual/Scaled |
| **Scale to Zero** | ✅ Yes (Standard) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (Eco dynos) |
| **Pricing Model** | Pay-per-use | Pay-per-resource | App Service Plan | Dyno-hour |
| **Custom Domain SSL** | ✅ Free | ✅ Via ACM | ✅ Free | ✅ Paid |
| **Database** | Datastore (NoSQL) | RDS, DynamoDB | Azure SQL, CosmosDB | Heroku Postgres |
| **Market Position** | Developer-friendly | AWS-integrated | Microsoft ecosystem | Startup-friendly |

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Scaling Decision Threshold

**Problem:** Determine when to trigger a new instance in GAE's auto-scaler given:
- Target CPU utilization: 60%
- Current instance CPU: 85%
- Target latency: 200 ms
- Current p95 latency: 350 ms

**Step 1: Apply the CPU threshold rule**

$$
\text{Trigger}_{\text{CPU}} = \left( \text{CPU}_{\text{current}} > \text{CPU}_{\text{target}} \right)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Trigger}_{\text{CPU}} = (85\% > 60\%) = \text{TRUE}
$$

The CPU threshold is exceeded, so this is a scaling trigger condition.

**Step 2: Apply the latency threshold rule**

$$
\text{Trigger}_{\text{latency}} = \left( L_{95} > L_{\text{target}} \right)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{Trigger}_{\text{latency}} = (350\text{ ms} > 200\text{ ms}) = \text{TRUE}
$$

The latency threshold is also exceeded, confirming a scaling trigger.

**Step 3: Combined decision logic (OR condition)**

$$
\text{Spawn Instance} = \text{Trigger}_{\text{CPU}} \lor \text{Trigger}_{\text{latency}}
$$

$$
\text{Spawn Instance} = \text{TRUE} \lor \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Conclusion:** GAE will spawn a new instance to distribute the load.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Configuration File (`app.yaml`)

```yaml
runtime: python39
service: backend-api
instance_class: F2

automatic_scaling:
  target_cpu_utilization: 0.65
  min_instances: 1
  max_instances: 10
  min_idle_instances: 1
  max_idle_instances: 3
  min_pending_latency: 30ms
  max_pending_latency: automatic
  max_concurrent_requests: 50

env_variables:
  DATABASE_URL: "postgresql://user:pass@host/db"
  DEBUG_MODE: "false"

handlers:
  - url: /.*
    script: auto
```

**Explanation of each block:**

- `runtime: python39` → Specifies Python 3.9 runtime in Standard Environment
- `instance_class: F2` → 256 MB RAM, 600 MHz CPU
- `min_instances: 1` → Always at least 1 instance warm
- `max_instances: 10` → Cap to prevent runaway costs
- `target_cpu_utilization: 0.65` → Scale out when CPU exceeds 65%
- `handlers` → URL routing rules
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level GAE Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client Browser]
    clientB[Mobile App]
    clientC[API Consumer]
    lb[Google Load Balancer]
    fe[Front End Instance Pool]
    inst1[App Instance 1]
    inst2[App Instance 2]
    inst3[App Instance N]
    ds[Datastore NoSQL]
    mc[Memcache]
    tq[Task Queues]
    cs[Cloud Storage]
    csql[Cloud SQL]
    log[Cloud Logging]
    mon[Cloud Monitoring]

    clientA --> lb
    clientB --> lb
    clientC --> lb
    lb --> fe
    fe --> inst1
    fe --> inst2
    fe --> inst3
    inst1 --> ds
    inst2 --> ds
    inst3 --> ds
    inst1 --> mc
    inst2 --> tq
    inst3 --> cs
    inst1 --> csql
    inst1 --> log
    inst2 --> mon
    inst3 --> mon
```

### 4.2 Application Deployment Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev[Developer Writes Code]
    local[Local Testing gcloud Emulator]
    deploy[gcloud App Deploy]
    upload[Code Uploaded to GAE]
    build[GAE Builds Container]
    version[New Version Created]
    traffic[Traffic Routing Split]
    monitor[Monitor via Cloud Operations]
    scale[Auto Scaler Adjusts Instances]
    update[Update or Rollback]

    dev --> local
    local --> deploy
    deploy --> upload
    upload --> build
    build --> version
    version --> traffic
    traffic --> monitor
    monitor --> scale
    scale --> update
    update --> dev
```

### 4.3 Standard vs. Flexible Environment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STANDARD["STANDARD ENVIRONMENT"]
        sApp[Application Code]
        sRun[Language Runtime Java Python Go Node]
        sSand[Sandbox Container]
        sNet[Restricted Network]
    end

    subgraph FLEXIBLE["FLEXIBLE ENVIRONMENT"]
        fApp[Application Code]
        fDocker[Dockerfile]
        fRun[Custom Runtime]
        fVM[Compute Engine VM]
        fNet[Full VPC Network]
    end

    sSand --> sNet
    fVM --> fNet
```

### 4.4 Auto-Scaling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[Incoming Request]
    check1{Instances at Minimum?}
    spawn[Spawn New Instance]
    route1[Route to Existing Instance]
    check2{CPU above threshold?}
    check3{Latency above target?}
    scaleout[Scale Out More Instances]
    route2[Route to Instance]
    idle{No Requests for 15 min?}
    terminate[Terminate Instance]
    keep[Keep Warm Min Idle]

    start --> check1
    check1 -->|No| spawn
    check1 -->|Yes| route1
    route1 --> check2
    check2 -->|Yes| scaleout
    check2 -->|No| check3
    check3 -->|Yes| scaleout
    check3 -->|No| route2
    route2 --> idle
    idle -->|Yes| terminate
    idle -->|No| keep
```

### 4.5 GAE Service Components Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application Instance]
    subgraph PLATFORM["GAE Platform Services"]
        ds[Datastore]
        cache[Memcache]
        queue[Task Queues]
        search[Search API]
        mail[Mail API]
        url[URL Fetch Service]
        users[Users API Auth]
        blob[Blobstore]
        log[Log Service]
        cron[Cron Service]
    end

    subgraph INFRA["Google Infrastructure"]
        gce[Compute Engine]
        gcs[Cloud Storage]
        big[Bigtable]
        net[Global Network]
    end

    app --> ds
    app --> cache
    app --> queue
    app --> search
    app --> mail
    app --> url
    app --> users
    app --> blob
    app --> log
    app --> cron
    ds --> big
    blob --> gcs
    queue --> gce
    log --> net
```

### 4.6 Request Handling Flow (Detailed)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User Client
    participant DNS as Google DNS
    participant LB as Load Balancer
    participant FE as Frontend Server
    participant BE as Backend Instance
    participant DS as Datastore
    participant MC as Memcache

    U->>DNS: Resolve appspot.com
    DNS->>U: Return IP address
    U->>LB: HTTPS Request GET /api/data
    LB->>FE: Forward Request
    FE->>BE: Route to Available Instance
    BE->>MC: Check Cache
    MC-->>BE: Cache Miss
    BE->>DS: Query Datastore
    DS-->>BE: Return Data
    BE->>MC: Store in Cache
    BE-->>FE: Response Payload
    FE-->>LB: HTTP Response
    LB-->>U: Return JSON Data
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Google App Engine (GAE). List any four features of GAE.**

**Model Answer:**

Google App Engine (GAE) is a fully managed **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** cloud computing platform provided by Google that allows developers to build and host web applications on Google's infrastructure without managing the underlying hardware and software layers.

**Four key features of GAE:**

1. **Automatic Scaling:** GAE automatically provisions or terminates instances based on traffic load, supporting scaling from zero to millions of users.

2. **Multiple Language Support:** Supports Java, Python, PHP, Go, Node.js, Ruby, and .NET runtimes in the Standard Environment.

3. **Built-in Services:** Provides managed services like Datastore (NoSQL), Memcache, Task Queues, User Authentication, and Cloud Storage integration.

4. **Pay-per-Use Pricing:** Users pay only for the resources (instance hours, storage, API calls) they actually consume, making it cost-efficient for variable workloads.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Standard Environment and Flexible Environment in Google App Engine.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Standard Environment | Flexible Environment |
|---|---|---|
| **Instance Type** | Sandboxed containers | Docker containers on GCE VMs |
| **Scaling to Zero** | Supported | Not supported |
| **Custom Runtimes** | Not allowed | Allowed via Dockerfile |
| **Startup Time** | Milliseconds (fast) | Minutes (slower) |
| **SSH Access** | Not available | Available for debugging |
| **Network Access** | Restricted sandbox | Full VPC access |
| **Best Use Case** | Stateless web apps, APIs | Long-running processes, custom binaries |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Google App Engine with a neat diagram. List the various platform services provided by GAE. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**GAE Architecture Layers:**

The GAE architecture consists of **four distinct layers** stacked on top of each other:

1. **Application Layer:** Contains the developer's code, static files, and configuration files (`app.yaml` or `appengine-web.xml`).

2. **Runtime Layer:** The execution environment (Standard or Flexible) that interprets and runs the application code.

3. **Platform Services Layer:** A set of managed APIs and services that applications can invoke without external dependencies.

4. **Infrastructure Layer:** Google's physical data centers, networking, and storage systems.

**Working Principle:**

When a user sends an HTTP request to a GAE application:

- The request first hits the **Load Balancer** at Google's edge.
- The load balancer routes it to a healthy **Front-End Instance** running in the runtime layer.
- The application code handles the request, optionally calling **Platform Services** like Datastore or Memcache.
- The response travels back through the same path to the user.

**Platform Services provided by GAE:**

1. **Datastore:** Schemaless, highly scalable NoSQL database.
2. **Memcache:** Distributed in-memory caching service.
3. **Task Queues:** Asynchronous work execution.
4. **Cloud SQL:** Managed relational database.
5. **User Authentication:** Google Sign-In integration.
6. **Blobstore:** Storage for large binary files.
7. **Mail API:** Send emails from the application.
8. **URL Fetch Service:** Make outbound HTTP/HTTPS requests.
9. **Search API:** Full-text search capability.
10. **Cron Service:** Schedule jobs at fixed intervals.

---

**(b) Discuss the various scaling options available in Google App Engine. How does auto-scaling work? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Scaling Options in GAE:**

1. **Automatic Scaling (Default):** GAE dynamically creates and terminates instances based on real-time traffic patterns. Configuration parameters include:
   - `min_instances` and `max_instances` boundaries
   - `target_cpu_utilization` threshold (e.g., 0.65 = 65%)
   - `min_idle_instances` to keep warm pools
   - `max_pending_latency` to trigger scaling on queue delay

2. **Manual Scaling:** The developer specifies a fixed number of instances. GAE does not auto-scale. Suitable for predictable, steady workloads.

3. **Basic Scaling:** Instances are created on-demand when requests arrive and shut down when idle. Maximum request duration is **10 minutes** (vs. 60 seconds for Standard Auto-scaling). Useful for intermittent traffic.

**How Auto-Scaling Works:**

Auto-scaling operates on a **continuous feedback loop**:

- **Step 1:** The scheduler monitors instance metrics (CPU, memory, p95 latency, pending request queue length).
- **Step 2:** If any metric exceeds the configured threshold, a new instance is spawned (subject to `max_instances` cap).
- **Step 3:** When load drops, idle instances are terminated down to `min_idle_instances`.
- **Step 4:** If no requests arrive for an extended period, instances may scale to **zero** (Standard Environment only).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Auto-scaling is **reactive**, not predictive. It responds to current load, not forecasted load. For predictive scaling, you would need to combine GAE with Cloud Scheduler or external orchestration.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) What are the advantages and limitations of Google App Engine? Compare GAE with AWS Elastic Beanstalk. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of GAE:**

1. **Zero Server Management:** No OS patching, security updates, or hardware maintenance.
2. **Automatic Scaling:** Handles traffic spikes seamlessly from 0 to millions.
3. **Pay-per-Use Billing:** No upfront costs; pay only for consumed resources.
4. **Built-in Security:** Sandboxed execution, automatic HTTPS, and DDoS protection.
5. **Multi-language Support:** Seven major runtimes supported.
6. **Integrated Services:** Native integration with Datastore, BigQuery, Pub/Sub, etc.
7. **High Availability:** Multi-zone replication is automatic.

**Limitations of GAE:**

1. **Vendor Lock-in:** Tightly coupled with Google's ecosystem.
2. **Sandbox Restrictions:** Standard Environment restricts certain system calls and local disk writes.
3. **Cold Start Latency:** First request after idle period can be slow.
4. **Limited Customization:** No control over underlying infrastructure.
5. **Pricing Complexity:** Costs can grow unpredictably at high scale.
6. **Migration Difficulty:** Moving from GAE Datastore to other databases is non-trivial.

**Comparison: GAE vs. AWS Elastic Beanstalk**

| Feature | Google App Engine | AWS Elastic Beanstalk |
|---|---|---|
| **Provider** | Google Cloud Platform | Amazon Web Services |
| **Service Type** | Pure PaaS | PaaS with IaaS flexibility |
| **Scaling** | Fully automatic | Automatic with manual configuration |
| **Customization** | Limited (sandbox) | High (full EC2 access) |
| **Database** | Datastore (NoSQL) | RDS, DynamoDB, Aurora |
| **Pricing** | Pay-per-instance-hour | Pay for underlying AWS resources |
| **Ecosystem** | GCP (BigQuery, Pub/Sub) | AWS (S3, Lambda, SQS) |

---

**(b) Describe the application deployment process in Google App Engine using `gcloud` CLI. Explain the role of `app.yaml`. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Application Deployment Process:**

**Step 1: Prerequisites**
- Install the `gcloud` CLI tool
- Authenticate: `gcloud auth login`
- Set the project: `gcloud config set project PROJECT_ID`

**Step 2: Application Structure**

```
my-gae-app/
├── app.yaml
├── main.py
├── requirements.txt
└── static/
    ├── style.css
    └── script.js
```

**Step 3: Write the `app.yaml` Configuration File**

```yaml
runtime: python39
service: default
instance_class: F2
automatic_scaling:
  min_instances: 1
  max_instances: 5
  target_cpu_utilization: 0.6
```

**Step 4: Deploy the Application**

The deployment command uploads the application code to Google Cloud and triggers a build:

```
gcloud app deploy app.yaml --project=PROJECT_ID
```

**Step 5: Verify Deployment**

```
gcloud app browse
```

**Step 6: View Logs**

```
gcloud app logs tail -s default
```

**Role of `app.yaml`:**

The `app.yaml` file is the **central configuration manifest** for a GAE application. It defines:

- `runtime:` — Which language runtime to use (e.g., `python39`, `java11`, `go119`).
- `service:` — The service name (used for microservices splitting).
- `instance_class:` — Memory and CPU allocation (F1–F4 for Standard).
- `automatic_scaling:` — Auto-scaling parameters (min/max instances, CPU targets).
- `handlers:` — URL routing rules and static file mappings.
- `env_variables:` — Environment variables passed to the application.
- `entrypoint:` — Command to start the application (Flexible Environment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `app.yaml` file is **mandatory** for Python, PHP, and Go applications. For Java applications, the equivalent is `appengine-web.xml` in the `WEB-INF/` directory.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse GAE with **Google Compute Engine (GCE)**. Remember: GCE is **IaaS**, GAE is **PaaS**.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Writing "GAE provides virtual machines" — **INCORRECT**. GAE abstracts VMs entirely; you cannot SSH into Standard Environment instances.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting to mention **scale-to-zero** as a unique feature of GAE Standard. This is a frequently-asked 2-mark question.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing **Datastore** with a relational database. Datastore is a **NoSQL, schemaless, document-style** database.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Writing the wrong unit for instance hours. Always specify **"instance-hours"** not just "hours" in cost calculations.
> 6. **Mark Distribution:** In 7-mark sub-questions, expect at least **2-3 marks for a diagram**. Always include a labeled architecture diagram.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GAE is PaaS**, not IaaS — Google manages servers, OS, middleware, and runtime.
- **Standard Environment** = Sandbox, fast, scales to zero, limited runtimes.
- **Flexible Environment** = Docker-based, slower, no scale-to-zero, any runtime.
- **Auto-scaling triggers:** CPU utilization, p95 latency, pending request queue.
- **Three scaling modes:** Automatic, Manual, Basic.
- **Key configuration file:** `app.yaml` (Python/PHP/Go) or `appengine-web.xml` (Java).
- **Deployment command:** `gcloud app deploy`
- **Core services:** Datastore, Memcache, Task Queues, Cloud SQL, User Auth.
- **Datastore** is a **schemaless, NoSQL, highly consistent** database.
- **Cold start** can cause latency spikes — mitigate with `min_idle_instances`.
- **Pay-per-use pricing** — costs scale with traffic; monitor with Cloud Billing.
- **Vendor lock-in** is a major drawback; consider Cloud Run or containers for portability.
