- **Multi-language support:** Java, Python, PHP, Go, Node.js, Ruby, .NET.
- **GAE was launched in 2008**, making it one of the oldest PaaS platforms.
- **Use case focus:** Web apps, mobile backends, APIs, microservices, MVP development.
- **Always draw a diagram** for 7-mark questions on GAE architecture — it secures easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📦 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Storage** is a service model in which data is maintained, managed, backed up, and made accessible to users over a network (typically the public Internet or a private network connection) through the coordinated use of distributed, virtualized, and highly redundant hardware and software infrastructure provided by a Cloud Service Provider (CSP).

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, Cloud Storage is formally categorized as a sub-service of **Storage-as-a-Service (STaaS)** within the broader **SPI (SaaS, PaaS, IaaS)** cloud service stack. It abstracts the physical location, hardware type, capacity, and management overhead of data from the end user, exposing only logical, programmable interfaces (REST APIs, SDKs, and console dashboards).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Cloud Storage as a "Self-Expanding Safety Deposit Box Network"** 🏦
>
> Imagine you own a small business that needs to store thousands of important documents. Instead of buying a giant physical safe (which is expensive, needs maintenance, and is at risk of fire/theft), you walk into a bank.
>
> - You don't know *where* exactly the bank keeps your box (abstraction of physical location).
> - You can store *any amount* — the bank just keeps allocating more boxes as you grow (elasticity).
> - Even if one branch catches fire, your documents are still safe because the bank automatically keeps copies in other branches (redundancy).
> - You access your box using a key (API) and a slip (credentials) — no need to carry the box yourself.
>
> That bank is your **Cloud Storage Provider** (AWS S3, Azure Blob, Google Cloud Storage, etc.).

### 1.3 Core Characteristics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **four pillars** of cloud storage as per KTU Module 4 syllabus:
>
> 1. **Elasticity & Scalability** — Storage capacity scales from **gigabytes to exabytes** on-demand, automatically, without downtime.
> 2. **Durability & Availability** — Object storage classes like **Amazon S3 Standard** offer **99.999999999% (eleven 9's) durability** and **99.99% availability SLA**.
> 3. **Multi-Tenancy & Virtualization** — Physical disks are partitioned logically and shared safely across thousands of customers using hypervisor-based isolation or container-level cgroups.
> 4. **Pay-Per-Use Metering** — Cost is billed per **GB stored/month** + **per-GB transfer** + **per-API request** (e.g., GET, PUT, LIST).

### 1.4 The Three Fundamental Cloud Storage Models

| Storage Type | Data Granularity | Access Pattern | Typical Latency | KTU Example Provider |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Object Storage** | Whole objects (files + metadata + unique ID) | RESTful HTTP (PUT, GET, DELETE) over HTTPS | **10–100 ms** | AWS S3, Azure Blob, MinIO |
| **Block Storage** | Fixed-size blocks (typically **4 KB – 256 KB**) | iSCSI, Fibre Channel, NVMe-oF (mounted as raw disk) | **< 1 ms (sub-millisecond)** | AWS EBS, Azure Disk, Google Persistent Disk |
| **File Storage** | Hierarchical files in folders | NFS, SMB/CIFS (POSIX-compliant) | **1–10 ms** | AWS EFS, Azure Files, Google Filestore |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember the analogy**: *Object* = sealed parcel in a postal warehouse. *Block* = raw hard drive plugged into a server. *File* = shared network drive (like Google Drive/OneDrive).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cloud Storage Architecture — The Layered Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cloud storage is **not a single product**; it is a **stack of 5 cooperating layers** as specified in KTU Module 4 (Storage Architecture).

### Layer 1: Physical Storage Tier (Hardware)
- Composed of commodity **HDDs (Hard Disk Drives)**, **SSDs (Solid State Drives)**, and **NVMe flash arrays**.
- Arranged in **JBODs (Just a Bunch Of Disks)** or hardware RAID enclosures.
- Connected via high-speed fabrics: **SATA, SAS, NVMe, InfiniBand**.

### Layer 2: Storage Virtualization Layer
- Aggregates physical disks into a single logical pool.
- Implements **Logical Volume Management (LVM)** or **Software-Defined Storage (SDS)** controllers (e.g., Ceph, VMware vSAN).
- Enables **thin provisioning** — allocating virtual capacity larger than physical capacity, growing on demand.

### Layer 3: Distributed File / Object System
- Coordinates thousands of nodes using consensus protocols (**Paxos**, **Raft**).
- Examples: **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**, **Ceph RADOS**, **Google File System (GFS)**.
- Implements **data placement algorithms**: consistent hashing, CRUSH (Controlled Replication Under Scalable Hashing).

### Layer 4: API / Interface Layer
- Exposes storage through:
  - **REST APIs** (HTTP/HTTPS with JSON/XML payloads) — for object storage.
  - **POSIX file APIs** (open, read, write, close) — for file storage.
  - **Block device interfaces** (mount /dev/xvdf /mnt/data) — for block storage.

### Layer 5: Service & Management Plane
- Handles authentication (IAM), billing, monitoring, lifecycle policies, encryption key management (KMS), and compliance logging.

## 2.2 Data Redundancy Strategies (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cloud storage guarantees durability through **three primary redundancy strategies**, each with different trade-offs.

### Strategy A: Full Replication (Mirroring)
- Each data object is copied **N times** across N geographically distinct nodes/data centers.
- **Use case**: Hot data requiring very fast read and zero data loss.

### Strategy B: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks)
- Combines multiple physical disks into one logical unit with parity or mirroring.
- Common levels: **RAID 0 (striping)**, **RAID 1 (mirroring)**, **RAID 5 (striping + single parity)**, **RAID 6 (striping + double parity)**, **RAID 10 (1+0)**.

### Strategy C: Erasure Coding (Reed-Solomon Codes)
- Splits data into **k data chunks** and computes **m parity chunks**, totaling **n = k + m** chunks.
- Storage can tolerate loss of **any m chunks** and still reconstruct original data.
- Used by: **Microsoft Azure**, **HDFS RAID**, **Ceph**, **Google Colossus**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly testable** in KTU University Exams (Part A & Part B numericals).

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Typical Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Replication Storage Overhead** | $S_{total} = D \times R$ | $D$ = data size, $R$ = replication factor | GB | 3 marks |
| 2 | **Replication Factor** | $R = \dfrac{S_{total}}{D}$ | copies per data unit | dimensionless | 3 marks |
| 3 | **RAID 0 Capacity (Striping)** | $C_{RAID0} = N \times d_{min}$ | $N$ = disks, $d_{min}$ = smallest disk | GB | 7 marks |
| 4 | **RAID 1 Capacity (Mirroring)** | $C_{RAID1} = \dfrac{N \times d_{min}}{2}$ | mirrored pairs | GB | 7 marks |
| 5 | **RAID 5 Capacity** | $C_{RAID5} = (N - 1) \times d_{min}$ | single parity disk worth | GB | 7 marks |
| 6 | **RAID 6 Capacity** | $C_{RAID6} = (N - 2) \times d_{min}$ | dual parity | GB | 7 marks |
| 7 | **RAID 10 Capacity** | $C_{RAID10} = \dfrac{N}{2} \times d_{min}$ | stripe of mirrors | GB | 7 marks |
| 8 | **Erasure Coding Usable Capacity** | $C_{usable} = \dfrac{k}{n} \times S_{raw}$ | $k$ = data chunks, $n = k+m$ | GB | 14 marks |
| 9 | **Erasure Coding Overhead** | $Overhead\% = \dfrac{m}{k} \times 100$ | extra storage cost | % | 7 marks |
| 10 | **Storage Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{usable}}{S_{raw}} \times 100$ | usable / total provisioned | % | 7 marks |
| 11 | **Durability Probability** | $P_{data\ loss} = (p_f)^{R}$ | $p_f$ = single-disk annual failure prob | probability | 7 marks |
| 12 | **Effective MTBF with Replication** | $MTBF_{R} = \dfrac{MTBF_{single}}{R}$ | mean time between failures | hours | 7 marks |
| 13 | **Storage Cost (Pay-per-Use)** | $C_{monthly} = (S_{GB} \times P_{GB}) + (T_{GB} \times P_{transfer})$ | size + egress cost | USD | 3 marks |
| 14 | **IOPS Aggregation (RAID 0)** | $IOPS_{array} = N \times IOPS_{disk}$ | additive across striped disks | ops/sec | 7 marks |
| 15 | **Stripe Unit Size** | $SU = \dfrac{Block_{size}}{N_{disks}}$ | data chunk per disk in stripe | KB/MB | 3 marks |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Video Streaming (Netflix, Hotstar)**: Uses object storage + CDN to deliver petabytes of video with sub-second startup.
> - **Big Data Analytics (Hadoop/Spark)**: Uses HDFS erasure coding to store **zettabytes** cost-effectively across commodity hardware.
> - **Backup & Disaster Recovery (DR)**: Cross-region replication of object storage enables **RPO = 0** (Recovery Point Objective) and **RTO < 1 minute**.
> - **IoT & Time-Series Data**: Tiered storage — hot SSD for last 7 days, cold S3 Glacier for older data — cuts costs by **up to 95%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1: Storage Capacity in RAID 5 (Full 7-Mark Solution)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark sub-question)**: A RAID 5 array consists of **5 disks**, each of size **2 TB**. Calculate the total usable storage capacity, storage efficiency, and the maximum number of disks that can fail without data loss.

### Step 1: Identify RAID 5 Architecture
- RAID 5 uses **block-level striping** with **single distributed parity**.
- Out of $N$ disks, **1 disk's worth of capacity is reserved for parity**.
- Parity is rotated across all disks (no dedicated parity disk) to avoid bottleneck.

### Step 2: Apply the RAID 5 Capacity Formula

$$C_{RAID5} = (N - 1) \times d_{min}$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Given Values
- Number of disks: $N = 5$
- Smallest disk size: $d_{min} = 2\ \text{TB}$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{RAID5} &= (5 - 1) \times 2\ \text{TB} \\
&= 4 \times 2\ \text{TB} \\
&= 8\ \text{TB}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Usable Capacity = 8 TB**

### Step 4: Calculate Storage Efficiency
The **raw physical capacity** provisioned is:

$$S_{raw} = N \times d_{min} = 5 \times 2\ \text{TB} = 10\ \text{TB}$$

Storage efficiency formula:

$$\eta = \dfrac{C_{usable}}{S_{raw}} \times 100$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{8\ \text{TB}}{10\ \text{TB}} \times 100 \\
&= 0.8 \times 100 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Storage Efficiency = 80%** (i.e., 20% overhead is the parity cost)

### Step 5: Maximum Tolerable Disk Failures
- RAID 5 tolerates **exactly 1 disk failure**.
- If 2 disks fail simultaneously → data loss (parity insufficient for double reconstruction).

✅ **Max tolerable failures = 1 disk**

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2: Erasure Coding (k=6, m=3) — Full 14-Mark Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**: A cloud object store uses **Reed-Solomon erasure coding (6, 3)**. A user uploads a **120 GB video file**.
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Total number of chunks generated.
> (b) Size of each data and parity chunk.
> (c) Total raw storage consumed.
> (d) Storage overhead percentage.
> (e) Maximum number of chunks that can be lost without data unavailability.
> (f) Compare with 3x replication in terms of storage cost.

### Step 1: Identify the (k, m) Parameters
- Data chunks: $k = 6$
- Parity chunks: $m = 3$
- Total chunks: $n = k + m = 6 + 3 = 9$

✅ **(a) Total chunks = 9**

### Step 2: Compute Chunk Size

$$\text{Chunk size} = \dfrac{S_{file}}{k}$$

Substituting $S_{file} = 120\ \text{GB}$ and $k = 6$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Data chunk size} &= \dfrac{120\ \text{GB}}{6} \\
&= 20\ \text{GB (each of 6 chunks)}
\end{aligned}$$

The 3 parity chunks are computed via **Reed-Solomon polynomial interpolation** and are also 20 GB each (since parity dimensions match data dimensions in RS codes).

✅ **(b) Each chunk = 20 GB**

### Step 3: Total Raw Storage Consumed

$$S_{raw} = n \times \text{chunk size} = 9 \times 20\ \text{GB} = 180\ \text{GB}$$

✅ **(c) Total raw storage = 180 GB**

### Step 4: Storage Overhead

$$\text{Overhead\%} = \dfrac{m}{k} \times 100 = \dfrac{3}{6} \times 100 = 50\%$$

✅ **(d) Overhead = 50%**

### Step 5: Maximum Tolerable Chunk Losses
- Erasure coding with $m$ parity chunks tolerates loss of **any $m$ chunks** (out of $n$).
- Tolerable losses = $m = 3$ chunks.

✅ **(e) Max losses = 3 chunks**

### Step 6: Comparison with 3x Replication

For 3x replication of the same 120 GB file:

$$S_{replicated} = 3 \times 120\ \text{GB} = 360\ \text{GB}$$

Comparison table:

| Metric | Erasure Coding (6,3) | 3x Replication |
|---|---|---|
| Raw Storage | **180 GB** | 360 GB |
| Overhead | **50%** | 200% |
| Tolerable Losses | **3 of 9 chunks** | 2 of 3 copies |
| Reconstruction | CPU-intensive (RS decode) | Simple copy from replica |
| Best For | Cold/Archival data | Hot, frequently-accessed data |

✅ **(f) EC saves 50% storage vs 3x replication**

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3: S3 Durability Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**: A single S3 Standard storage device has an **annual failure probability of 0.1%** (i.e., $p_f = 0.001$). S3 stores every object with **replication factor R = 3** across different availability zones. Calculate the **probability of permanent data loss** for an object.

### Step 1: Define Single-Disk Failure Probability
$$p_f = 0.001 = 10^{-3}$$

### Step 2: Probability that ALL 3 Replicas Fail Simultaneously
Data loss occurs only if **all 3 replicas fail in the same year**. Assuming independent failures:

$$P_{data\ loss} = (p_f)^{R} = (0.001)^{3}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{data\ loss} &= (10^{-3})^{3} \\
&= 10^{-9}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the famous **"eleven 9's" durability**:
$$1 - 10^{-9} = 0.999999999 = 99.9999999\%$$

✅ **Data loss probability = 1 in 1 billion per object per year**

```mermaid
    r0 -->|No redundancy, max speed| p0[Use: Scratch Temp Workloads]

```

```mermaid
    data -->|Option B: EC k=10 m=4| ec[14 Chunks of ~10 GB Each = 140 GB Storage]

    rep -->|Pros: Fast read, simple recovery| proA[Pro A]
    rep -->|Cons: 200% overhead| conA[Con A]
    ec -->|Pros: 40% overhead, high durability| proB[Pro B]
    ec -->|Cons: CPU intensive reconstruction| conB[Con B]
```

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q: Differentiate between Object Storage and Block Storage with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (Key Points for 3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Object Storage | Block Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Unit** | Whole object (file + metadata + ID) | Fixed-size blocks (4–256 KB) |
| **Access Method** | REST API (HTTP PUT/GET) | Mounted as raw disk (iSCSI/NVMe) |
| **Metadata** | Rich, custom, queryable | Limited (size, timestamp) |
| **Performance** | Higher latency (10–100 ms) | Very low latency (< 1 ms) |
| **Use Case** | Backups, media files, big data | Databases, OS boot volumes |
| **Example** | AWS S3, Azure Blob | AWS EBS, Azure Disk |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited (exabytes) | Limited by single volume size |

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
> **Q: What is the significance of the Eleven 9's (99.999999999%) durability guarantee in Amazon S3?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark)**: Eleven 9's = 99.999999999% means the probability of losing an object is **1 in 10¹¹** (one in 100 billion) per year.
2. **Mechanism (1 mark)**: Achieved via **cross-AZ replication** and continuous integrity checksumming.
3. **Practical implication (1 mark)**: If you store **10 million objects**, statistically you can expect to lose **0.0001 objects per year** — virtually zero data loss.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 1

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model | Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the **5-layer architecture of cloud storage** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cloud data center uses **RAID 6** configuration with **8 disks of 4 TB each**. Calculate:
> - (i) Total usable capacity
> - (ii) Storage efficiency
> - (iii) Maximum number of simultaneous disk failures tolerated
> - (iv) Total raw storage provisioned **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — 5-Layer Cloud Storage Architecture (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer 1: Physical Storage Hardware (1 mark)**
> - Comprises HDDs, SSDs, NVMe drives arranged in **JBOD** enclosures.
> - Connected via **SATA, SAS, NVMe, InfiniBand** fabrics.

> **Layer 2: Storage Virtualization (1 mark)**
> - Abstracts physical disks into logical pools using **LVM, Ceph, vSAN**.
> - Enables **thin provisioning** and **snapshots**.

> **Layer 3: Distributed Storage System (2 marks)**
> - Coordinates thousands of OSDs (Object Storage Daemons).
> - Uses **consensus algorithms (Paxos, Raft)** and **data placement (CRUSH, consistent hashing)**.
> - Examples: HDFS, Ceph RADOS, Google GFS.

> **Layer 4: API / Interface Layer (1 mark)**
> - REST APIs for object (S3-compatible).
> - POSIX for file (NFS/SMB).
> - Block device interface (iSCSI, NVMe-oF).

> **Layer 5: Service & Management Plane (2 marks)**
> - IAM, KMS encryption, lifecycle policies, monitoring, billing.
> - Provides **multi-tenancy, SLAs, and compliance** (GDPR, HIPAA).

*(Include a block diagram as shown in SECTION 4.1 — 1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — RAID 6 Numerical (7 marks)

**Given**:
- $N = 8$ disks
- $d_{min} = 4\ \text{TB}$

**(i) Usable Capacity** (1 mark):
RAID 6 reserves 2 disks' worth for **double parity**:

$$C_{RAID6} = (N - 2) \times d_{min} = (8 - 2) \times 4\ \text{TB} = 24\ \text{TB}$$

**(ii) Storage Efficiency** (2 marks):
$$S_{raw} = 8 \times 4\ \text{TB} = 32\ \text{TB}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{24}{32} \times 100 = 75\%$$

**(iii) Max Tolerable Failures** (1 mark):
RAID 6 tolerates **2 simultaneous disk failures**.

**(iv) Raw Storage** (1 mark):
$$S_{raw} = 32\ \text{TB}$$

✅ **Answers**: (i) 24 TB, (ii) 75%, (iii) 2 disks, (iv) 32 TB

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option 2

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model | Module 4]**
> **(a)** What is **Erasure Coding**? Explain **Reed-Solomon (k, m) codes** with a suitable example. Compare erasure coding with **full replication** in terms of storage overhead. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A video streaming company stores **4 TB of video data per hour of content** using **Erasure Coding (10, 4)**.
> - (i) Calculate total raw storage and overhead percentage.
> - (ii) How much storage would **3x replication** consume for the same data?
> - (iii) Justify which is more cost-effective for **archival** storage. **(7 marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Erasure Coding Theory (7 marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (2 marks)**: Erasure Coding is a data protection method that splits data into **k data chunks** and computes **m parity chunks** using mathematical codes (Reed-Solomon), allowing reconstruction of original data from **any k of the n = k+m chunks**.

**Reed-Solomon (k, m) Example** (3 marks):
- For **RS(6, 3)**: 6 data chunks + 3 parity chunks = 9 total.
- Any 6 of 9 chunks can reconstruct the original file.
- Used in: HDFS RAID, Azure, Ceph, Google Colossus.

**Comparison Table** (2 marks):

| Property | Replication (R=3) | Erasure Coding (6,3) |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Overhead | 200% | 50% |
| Fault Tolerance | 2 of 3 replicas | 3 of 9 chunks |
| Reconstruction | Simple copy | CPU-intensive RS decode |
| Best For | Hot data | Cold/archival data |

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given**: $S = 4\ \text{TB}$, $k = 10$, $m = 4$, so $n = 14$.

**(i) Erasure Coding Storage** (3 marks):
$$S_{raw} = \dfrac{n}{k} \times S = \dfrac{14}{10} \times 4\ \text{TB} = 5.6\ \text{TB}$$

$$\text{Overhead\%} = \dfrac{4}{10} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**(ii) 3x Replication Storage** (2 marks):
$$S_{rep} = 3 \times 4\ \text{TB} = 12\ \text{TB}$$

**(iii) Cost-Effectiveness Justification** (2 marks):
For **archival data** (rarely accessed, cost-sensitive):
- ✅ **Erasure Coding wins** because it consumes only **5.6 TB vs 12 TB** → **saves 53.3% storage cost**.
- The CPU cost of reconstruction is acceptable because archival reads are infrequent.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Common Marks-Deduction Traps in KTU Cloud Storage Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RAID levels**: Students often write $C_{RAID5} = N \times d_{min}$ forgetting to subtract the parity disk. **Always check**: RAID 0 = $N \times d$, RAID 1 = $N/2 \times d$, RAID 5 = $(N-1) \times d$, RAID 6 = $(N-2) \times d$.
>
> 2. **Misinterpreting k and m in Erasure Coding**: $k$ = data chunks, $m$ = parity chunks. **Total = k + m**, not $k \times m$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units**: Always write TB/GB explicitly. Writing just "8" instead of "8 TB" costs **0.5 mark**.
>
> 4. **Block diagrams without arrows or labels**: KTU evaluators deduct **1–2 marks** for unstructured diagrams. Always label **every box and arrow**.
>
> 5. **Missing examples in definition questions**: Definitions of object/block/file storage **must include at least one provider example** (S3, EBS, EFS).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist for Cloud Storage (Module 4)**
>
> ☐ **Three storage models**: Object (S3), Block (EBS), File (EFS) — know latency, access, use case.
>
> ☐ **Five-layer architecture**: Physical → Virtualization → Distributed System → API → Management.
>
> ☐ **RAID formulas** (memorize cold!):
> - RAID 0: $N \times d$
> - RAID 1: $(N/2) \times d$
> - RAID 5: $(N-1) \times d$
> - RAID 6: $(N-2) \times d$
> - RAID 10: $(N/2) \times d$
>
> ☐ **Erasure Coding formula**: $n = k + m$, overhead $= (m/k) \times 100\%$.
>
> ☐ **Replication formula**: $S_{total} = D \times R$.
>
> ☐ **Durability**: 11 nines = $(1 - 10^{-11}) \times 100\%$ durability in S3.
>
> ☐ **Storage efficiency**: $\eta = (C_{usable}/S_{raw}) \times 100$.
>
> ☐ **S3 Storage Classes**: Standard, Intelligent-Tiering, Glacier (Instant/ Flexible/ Deep Archive).
>
> ☐ **CAP Theorem** in storage context: Choose **2 of 3** — Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance.
>
> ☐ **Consistency models**: Strong, Eventual, Causal (know trade-offs).
>
> ☐ **CDN + Object Storage** = standard pattern for global content delivery.
>
> ☐ **Always cite provider examples** in definition answers (S3, EBS, EFS, Azure Blob, GCS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gmail
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gmail ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition **Gmail (Google Mail)** is a free, web-based **Software as a Service (SaaS)** email service provided by Google LLC, launched on **April 1, 2004**. It operates entirely on Google's distributed cloud infrastructure, leveraging the company's global network of data centers to deliver email, contact management, calendaring, and integrated productivity services to over **1.8 billion active users** worldwide. In the context of **Cloud Computing (PEITT523 - Module 4)**, Gmail is treated as a canonical real-world case study of a **public cloud SaaS application**, demonstrating principles of: - **Multi-tenancy** (millions of users on shared infrastructure) - **Elastic scalability** (storage grows from 15 GB to 30 GB on shared plans) - **High availability** (**99.9% uptime SLA**) - **Virtualization** (running on Google Compute Engine VMs) - **Pay-as-you-go** (free tier funded by advertising) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gmail is studied in Module 4 under the broader theme of *"Cloud Applications and Case Studies."* Students must understand how Gmail maps onto the **NIST Cloud Deployment Model (Public Cloud)** and the **Cloud Service Model (SaaS)**. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of Gmail like a **post office box in the sky**. Imagine you have moved to a new city. Instead of buying land, building a house, setting up a mailbox, hiring a postman, and managing a sorting room, you simply visit the **central post office** (Google's data centers). You fill out a one-minute form, and instantly you receive: - A **mailbox** (your email inbox) that never runs out of space - A **personal assistant** (Gmail's AI) that sorts your letters into Primary, Social, and Promotions - A **24/7 security guard** (2-Step Verification + Google's spam filters) that checks every visitor - A **photocopier in the cloud** (Google Drive integration) so you never lose a document You pay **nothing extra** for the building, electricity, or staff — Google charges advertisers instead. This is the essence of **SaaS**: you consume the software; Google owns and manages everything underneath. > [!NOTE] > **Key Term — "Cloud-Native":** Gmail was designed from day one to live in the browser, not on a local machine. This is fundamentally different from older email clients (like Microsoft Outlook) that were first built as desktop software and later ported to the cloud. ### Standard Metrics & Specifications (Gmail Free Tier, 2024) | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | **Initial Storage Quota** | **15 GB shared with Google Drive & Photos** | | **Maximum Attachment Size** | **25 MB per email** | | **Daily Sending Limit (Free)** | **500 recipients** | | **SMTP Server** | `smtp.gmail.com` (Port 465/587) | | **POP3 Server** | `pop.gmail.com` (Port 995) | | **IMAP Server** | `imap.gmail.com` (Port 993) | | **Uptime SLA (Google Workspace)** | **99.9%** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ### Architectural Breakdown of Gmail as a Cloud Service Gmail's architecture can be dissected into **six logical tiers**, each of which represents a specific cloud computing concept: 1. **Presentation Layer (The Thin Client)** - The user accesses Gmail through a browser (Chrome, Firefox, Edge). - This embodies the cloud principle of **"anywhere, anytime, any device"** access. - No data is stored locally except **HTTP cookies** and an offline cache (via Service Workers). 2. **Front-End Layer (Google Front End — GFE)** - Terminates the user's HTTPS connection at one of Google's **150+ edge Points of Presence (PoPs)**. - Performs **TLS/SSL decryption** and routes the request to the nearest healthy data center using **Anycast BGP routing**. - Implements **load balancing** (L4 + L7) to distribute user sessions. 3. **Application Logic Layer (Gmail Web Servers)** - Hosts the JavaScript-heavy Gmail frontend (built using the **Closure Compiler** and **Google Web Toolkit**). - Backend services run on **Borg/Kubernetes** clusters managed by Google's **SRE (Site Reliability Engineering)** teams. - Uses **Protocol Buffers** for fast internal RPC calls. 4. **Storage Layer (Google File System — GFS / Colossus)** - Email bodies, attachments, and metadata are stored in **Colossus** (the successor to GFS). - Uses **Bigtable** (a NoSQL wide-column store) for indexing emails for sub-second search. - Data is **replicated across at least 3 geographically distinct data centers** to ensure durability (**11 nines of durability: 99.999999999%**). 5. **Intelligence Layer (Machine Learning & AI)** - **Spam filtering** uses TensorFlow-based neural networks trained on billions of messages. - **Smart Compose / Smart Reply** uses large language models running on Google's **TPUs (Tensor Processing Units)**. - **Phishing detection** analyzes URL patterns, sender reputation, and content signatures. 6. **Integration Layer (APIs & Protocols)** - **Gmail API (RESTful)** — modern JSON-based access for third-party apps. - **IMAP** (Internet Message Access Protocol) — sync protocol for desktop clients. - **SMTP** (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) — for outgoing mail. - **OAuth 2.0** — for delegated, token-based third-party access. ### The "Why" Behind Each Layer - **Why a thin client?** → Zero installation, automatic updates, cross-platform consistency. - **Why GFE + Anycast?** → Reduces latency by serving users from the **nearest** edge location (often < 20 ms). - **Why Bigtable + Colossus?** → Email retrieval must be **eventually consistent but highly available**; traditional RDBMS would not scale to billions of mailboxes. - **Why ML-based spam filters?** → Rule-based filters (like the old SpamAssassin) cannot keep up with novel spam campaigns; ML adapts in real time. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Gmail's storage layer is one of the largest single NoSQL deployments in the world. As of recent disclosures, Google stores **hundreds of petabytes** of email data — a number that grows by **several terabytes per minute**. ### KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Gmail ↔ Cloud Computing Mapping) | Cloud Computing Concept | How Gmail Implements It | Exam-Relevance | |---|---|---| | **SaaS (Software as a Service)** | Gmail is delivered as a service over HTTP/HTTPS; the user does not install or maintain the email server. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Public Cloud Deployment** | Hosted on Google's global infrastructure; anyone with a Google account can sign up. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Elastic Scalability** | Storage grows automatically from **15 GB → 30 GB**; compute scales during peak (e.g., New Year's Day) using Borg. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Multi-Tenancy** | A single physical server may host mailboxes for thousands of users in isolated containers/VMs. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **High Availability & Redundancy** | Data replicated across **3+ data centers** in different geographic regions. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Pay-as-You-Go** | Free tier is ad-funded; Google Workspace charges **$6–$18/user/month** as OPEX. | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Virtualization** | Each Gmail backend service runs in lightweight VMs (gVisor) or containers on Borg. | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **API-Based Access** | Gmail REST API allows programmatic access for 3rd-party apps. | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **Security as a Service** | 2FA, OAuth 2.0, end-to-end encryption (S/MIME in Workspace), and AI-based phishing detection. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Disaster Recovery** | Cross-region replication means a single data center failure is **transparent to the user**. | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ### Real-World Utility Gmail is not just an email client — it is the **onboarding gateway** to Google's entire cloud ecosystem. Over **90% of Google Workspace** customers started with a personal Gmail account. This "freemium funnel" is a textbook example of how cloud providers reduce customer acquisition costs and demonstrate cloud principles to the mass market. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough: Inside a Gmail Request > Since Gmail is not a "derivable" engineering topic, this section provides an **exhaustive step-by-step trace** of what happens when a user clicks "Send" on an email. This is a common 14-mark analytical question in KTU examinations. ### Scenario > A user named **Arjun (arjun@ktu.ac.in)** types a message and clicks **Send** to **priya@gmail.com** at **10:00:00 AM IST**. ### Step 1: Client-Side Composition (Browser) The email is composed in the browser using JavaScript. Attachments are read using the **File API** and held in browser memory as `Blob` objects. A unique **message ID** is generated locally. ### Step 2: HTTPS Request Initiation The browser issues an **HTTP POST** request to `https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?ui=2&ik=...&at=...&view=cm&fs=1&tf=1`. This is encrypted with **TLS 1.3** and routed via Anycast to the nearest **Google Front End (GFE)**. ### Step 3: GFE Termination The GFE: - Decrypts the TLS session. - Verifies the user's **OAuth bearer token** (cached in the browser). - Performs **L7 load balancing** to pick a healthy Gmail web server. ### Step 4: Authentication & Authorization The web server validates the token against **Google's Central Authentication Service (CAS)**. The token contains: - User ID - Scopes (e.g., `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.send`) - Expiry timestamp If the token is valid, the request is **authorized**; otherwise, a **401 Unauthorized** is returned. ### Step 5: Anti-Spam & Malware Scanning Before acceptance, the message is passed through: - **VirusTotal-style attachment scanning** (clamAV + proprietary YARA rules) - **Spam classifier** (TensorFlow model, returns a score between 0 and 1) - **Phishing URL detector** (checks against Google's **Safe Browsing API**) ### Step 6: Storage Write to Colossus If the message passes all checks, it is written to **Colossus** in **at least 3 replicas** across different zones. A write-ahead log ensures **durability**. The message is then indexed in **Bigtable** using the recipient's user ID as the partition key. ### Step 7: SMTP Relay (Outbound) For external recipients (e.g., `@gmail.com` from `@ktu.ac.in`), Gmail acts as an **SMTP relay**: - The message is queued in **Pub/Sub** (Google's globally distributed message queue). - A worker process picks it up and resolves the MX record of `gmail.com` → `gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com`. - It then performs **SMTP** handshake (HELO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA) on **port 25**. ### Step 8: Receipt at Destination `gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com` receives the message, writes it to Priya's mailbox in Colossus, and triggers a **push notification** to any device Priya has signed in on (via **Firebase Cloud Messaging — FCM**). ### Step 9: Delivery to Priya's Browser Priya's browser, which has an open **WebSocket / EventSource** connection to Gmail's long-polling endpoint, receives a real-time notification. Within **~1–2 seconds**, the new email appears with a subtle animation. ### Quantitative Summary Table (Typical Latency Budget) | Step | Typical Latency | Cumulative Latency | |---|---|---| | Browser → GFE (TLS handshake + RTT) | 50 ms | 50 ms | | GFE → Web Server (internal RPC) | 5 ms | 55 ms | | Auth + Spam Scan (TensorFlow inference) | 30 ms | 85 ms | | Colossus Write (3 replicas in parallel) | 40 ms | 125 ms | | SMTP Relay to `gmail-smtp-in.l.google.com` | 60 ms | 185 ms | | Push Notification to Priya's Device | 100 ms | 285 ms | | **End-to-End (Send → Inbox)** | — | **~300 ms** | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The **300 ms** figure is often cited in Google's SRE literature. Any p99 latency above **1 second** triggers a page to the on-call SRE, per the **Error Budget** policy. ### Derivation: Why 3 Replicas? Google's storage policy is derived from the **Durability Equation**: $$ D = 1 - (1 - p)^{n} $$ where $p$ is the probability of a single disk failure per unit time and $n$ is the number of replicas. For a single disk with **Annualized Failure Rate (AFR) ≈ 0.8%**, the probability of a single replica loss in a year is: $$ p = 0.008 $$ With $n = 3$ independent replicas stored in **different physical racks** (so a single rack failure cannot take out more than one replica): $$ D = 1 - (1 - 0.008)^{3} = 1 - (0.992)^{3} = 1 - 0.97619 = 0.0238 $$ But this assumes **correlated** failures. With **independent** failure domains (different data centers), $p$ drops to roughly **0.0001**, yielding: $$ D = 1 - (1 - 0.0001)^{3} \approx 0.0003 \text{ (i.e., 0.03% annual loss risk)} $$ This is why Google advertises **"11 nines of durability"** for Gmail storage. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### Diagram 1: End-to-End Gmail Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["User Browser
Chrome or Firefox"] -->|HTTPS POST TLS 1.3| nodeB["Google Front End
GFE with Anycast Routing"] nodeB -->|Decrypt and L7 Load Balance| nodeC["Gmail Web Servers
Running on Borg Cluster"] nodeC -->|Verify OAuth Token| nodeD["Central Auth Service
CAS Layer"] nodeC -->|Spam and Malware Scan| nodeE["ML Inference Layer
TensorFlow + VirusTotal"] nodeE -->|Accept| nodeF["Colossus Storage
3 Replicas across Zones"] nodeE -->|Reject| nodeG["Quarantine Bucket
Spam Folder"] nodeF -->|Index in Bigtable| nodeH["Bigtable NoSQL Index
Fast Retrieval Layer"] nodeF -->|SMTP Relay via Pub Sub| nodeI["SMTP Gateway
Outbound Email Service"] nodeI -->|DNS MX Lookup| nodeJ["Receiving MTA
gmail smtp in"] nodeJ -->|Store Mailbox| nodeF nodeH -->|Push Notification FCM| nodeK["Recipient Device
WebSocket Connection"] nodeA -.->|Long Polling WebSocket| nodeC ``` ### Diagram 2: Gmail in the Cloud Service Model (NIST Reference Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SaaS["SaaS Layer"] nodeSaaS1["Gmail Web UI"] nodeSaaS2["Gmail Mobile App"] nodeSaaS3["Gmail API REST"] end subgraph PaaS["PaaS Layer"] nodePaaS1["App Engine Hosting"] nodePaaS2["Bigtable and Colossus APIs"] nodePaaS3["TensorFlow Serving"] end subgraph IaaS["IaaS Layer"] nodeIaaS1["Compute Engine VMs"] nodeIaaS2["Virtual Private Cloud"] nodeIaaS3["Block and Object Storage"] end nodeSaaS1 --> nodePaaS1 nodeSaaS2 --> nodePaaS1 nodeSaaS3 --> nodePaaS2 nodePaaS1 --> nodeIaaS1 nodePaaS2 --> nodeIaaS2 nodePaaS3 --> nodeIaaS3 nodeIaaS1 --> nodeIaaS2 ``` ### Diagram 3: Mail Flow with Protocols (POP3 / IMAP / SMTP) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeSender["Sender Client
Browser or Outlook"] -->|SMTP Port 587| nodeOutMTA["Outbound SMTP Server
smtp.gmail.com"] nodeOutMTA -->|SMTP Port 25| nodeInMTA["Inbound MTA
gmail smtp in"] nodeInMTA -->|Write to Colossus| nodeStore["Mailbox Storage"] nodeStore -->|Push via FCM| nodeReceiverApp["Receiver Mobile App"] nodeReceiverApp -->|User Opens App| nodeReceiverApp subgraph DesktopClients["Desktop Email Clients"] nodePOP["POP3 Client
pop.gmail.com Port 995
Downloads and Deletes"] nodeIMAP["IMAP Client
imap.gmail.com Port 993
Syncs and Keeps on Server"] end nodeStore --> nodePOP nodeStore --> nodeIMAP ``` ### Diagram 4: Gmail Security Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeUser["User Login Attempt"] --> nodeCheck1{"Has 2FA Enabled?"} nodeCheck1 -->|Yes| nodeCheck2["Send OTP via SMS or Authenticator"] nodeCheck1 -->|No| nodeCheck3["Password Hash Verify bcrypt"] nodeCheck2 --> nodeCheck4{"OTP Valid?"} nodeCheck3 --> nodeCheck5{"Password Match?"} nodeCheck4 -->|Yes| nodeAllow["Issue OAuth 2.0 Token"] nodeCheck4 -->|No| nodeDeny["Block and Alert User"] nodeCheck5 -->|Yes| nodeAllow nodeCheck5 -->|No| nodeDeny nodeAllow --> nodeSession["Session Active
All API Calls Authorized"] nodeSession --> nodeMonitor["Continuous Risk Analysis
Unusual Location and Device"] nodeMonitor -->|Suspicious| nodeDeny ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define SaaS. Explain how Gmail is an example of SaaS in cloud computing.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** SaaS (Software as a Service) is a cloud service model in which software applications are hosted and managed by a provider and delivered to users over the internet on a pay-per-use basis. The user does not install or maintain the software locally. - **Gmail as SaaS (2 marks):** Gmail is hosted entirely on Google's cloud infrastructure and accessed via a web browser. Users do not need to install any email server; Google manages storage, security, updates, and availability. Gmail's free tier exemplifies the pay-as-you-go model (ad-supported), while Google Workspace is a paid SaaS subscription. Thus, Gmail is a textbook SaaS application operating on a public cloud. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *List the key differences between POP3 and IMAP protocols used in Gmail.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3):** Downloads emails from the server to the local device and (by default) deletes them from the server. It is a "store-and-forward" protocol suited for single-device access. Gmail's POP3 server: `pop.gmail.com` on **port 995** (SSL). - **IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol):** Synchronizes emails between the server and multiple devices. Messages remain on the server and are flagged (read/unread) across all clients. Gmail's IMAP server: `imap.gmail.com` on **port 993** (SSL). - **Key Difference (1 mark):** POP3 is device-centric (download), while IMAP is server-centric (sync) — making IMAP preferable in a cloud environment. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE rules, students must answer **one full question from a pair**. Both alternatives are given below. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Explain the **client-server architecture of Gmail** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of SMTP, IMAP, and POP3 in email communication. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **security features** implemented in Gmail to protect user data. How does Gmail exemplify the **shared security responsibility model** of cloud computing? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A **Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** Gmail follows a **multi-tier client-server architecture** deployed on Google's public cloud: 1. **Client Layer (1 mark):** The user accesses Gmail through a browser (thin client) or mobile app. The client is responsible only for rendering the UI and managing local state (e.g., drafts). 2. **Front-End Layer (1.5 marks):** The Google Front End (GFE) terminates HTTPS connections at edge points-of-presence using Anycast. It performs TLS decryption, load balancing, and DDoS protection. 3. **Application Layer (1.5 marks):** Gmail's web servers run as containers on Google's Borg cluster (predecessor of Kubernetes). They handle business logic, search, and integration with other Google services. 4. **Storage Layer (1.5 marks):** Email bodies and attachments are stored in **Colossus** (Google's distributed file system), while metadata and indices are in **Bigtable** (NoSQL). Replicas span multiple geographic regions. 5. **Protocol Roles (1.5 marks):** - **SMTP** (`smtp.gmail.com`, port 587): Used for *sending* outgoing emails to other mail servers via the SMTP relay. - **IMAP** (`imap.gmail.com`, port 993): Used by desktop/mobile clients to *sync* emails across multiple devices while keeping them on the server. - **POP3** (`pop.gmail.com`, port 995): An older protocol that *downloads* emails to a single device; rarely used in modern cloud workflows. **Solution to (b) — 7 marks:** Gmail implements **defense-in-depth** security: 1. **Authentication Security (1.5 marks):** - Passwords hashed with **bcrypt** and salted. - **2-Step Verification (2FA)** via SMS, Google Authenticator, or security keys (FIDO2/WebAuthn). - **OAuth 2.0** for delegated third-party access (no password sharing). 2. **Data Security (1.5 marks):** - All data encrypted **in transit** (TLS 1.3) and **at rest** (AES-256). - S/MIME support in Google Workspace for end-to-end encryption. 3. **AI-Based Threat Detection (1.5 marks):** - TensorFlow-based spam filter with > **99.9%** block rate. - Phishing detection using URL reputation and content analysis. - Malware scanning of attachments via proprietary YARA rules. 4. **Operational Security (1 mark):** - Continuous session risk analysis; suspicious logins trigger additional challenges. - **Security Sandbox** detonates unknown attachments in isolated VMs. 5. **Shared Responsibility Model (1.5 marks):** - **Google's responsibility:** Securing the underlying infrastructure (data centers, hypervisors, storage, network) and the Gmail application code. - **User's responsibility:** Choosing a strong password, enabling 2FA, not sharing credentials, and being vigilant about phishing. - This division is the **shared security responsibility model** — a core tenet of public cloud computing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write vague answers like *"Gmail uses encryption."* That scores **zero**. You must specify **what** is encrypted (data at rest / in transit), **how** (TLS 1.3 / AES-256), and **which layer** of the shared responsibility model it falls under. Also, never claim Gmail uses "end-to-end encryption" by default — it does **not**; that requires S/MIME setup in Workspace. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **cloud computing architecture of Gmail**. Map each component to the relevant **NIST cloud service model** (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss how Gmail demonstrates the **characteristics of cloud computing**: on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, rapid elasticity, and measured service. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B **Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** (Refer to **Diagram 2** in Section 4 for the block diagram.) | Architecture Layer | Gmail Component | NIST Mapping | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | Web UI (browser, mobile) | Gmail front-end, Gmail API | **SaaS** | 1.5 | | Application logic (search, spam filter, ML) | App Engine, TensorFlow Serving | **PaaS** | 1.5 | | Storage and indexing | Bigtable, Colossus, Cloud SQL | **PaaS** (managed DB) | 1.5 | | Compute and networking | Compute Engine VMs, VPC, Load Balancers | **IaaS** | 1.5 | | Physical data centers | Google data centers (The Dalles, Council Bluffs, etc.) | Underlying infra | 1 | Each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer below it, allowing Google to innovate rapidly — a key cloud principle. **Solution to (b) — 7 marks (1.4 marks each):** 1. **On-Demand Self-Service (1.4 marks):** A user can sign up for Gmail and obtain a **15 GB mailbox** instantly, with no human interaction from Google. Compute and storage resources are provisioned automatically by Borg/Kubernetes. 2. **Broad Network Access (1.4 marks):** Gmail is accessible from any device with a browser — laptops, tablets, smartphones, smartwatches — over standard protocols (HTTPS, IMAP, SMTP). 3. **Resource Pooling (1.4 marks):** A single Gmail cluster serves millions of users. Physical resources (CPU, RAM, disk) are dynamically partitioned via virtualization and shared across "tenants." 4. **Rapid Elasticness (1.4 marks):** During peak events (e.g., New Year's Eve traffic spikes by **~50%**), Google autoscales Borg workers to handle the load, then scales down to save energy. Storage capacity also grows elastically. 5. **Measured Service (1.4 marks):** Google monitors per-user metrics (storage used, emails sent, spam blocked) and exposes dashboards in Google Workspace Admin Console. The free tier is ad-funded based on engagement metrics. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **measured service** with **pay-per-use**. Measured service is about *metering and reporting* usage, which is what enables pay-per-use. In Gmail's free tier, the service is *measured* (Google knows exactly how much you use) even though the user does not pay directly. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist — Gmail & Cloud Computing** - ☐ **Gmail = SaaS on Public Cloud** (most important one-line answer). - ☐ **Launch Date:** April 1, 2004 | **Owner:** Google LLC. - ☐ **Free storage:** 15 GB shared across Gmail + Drive + Photos. - ☐ **SMTP server:** `smtp.gmail.com` (port 587 for TLS, 465 for SSL). - ☐ **IMAP server:** `imap.gmail.com` (port 993). - ☐ **POP3 server:** `pop.gmail.com` (port 995). - ☐ **SMTP = send**, **IMAP = sync**, **POP3 = download**. - ☐ **Underlying tech:** Colossus (storage) + Bigtable (index) + Borg (orchestration) + TensorFlow (ML). - ☐ **11 nines of durability** because of **3-replica cross-zone replication**. - ☐ **End-to-end latency** for sending an email ≈ **300 ms**. - ☐ **5 NIST characteristics of cloud computing** — Gmail demonstrates **all five**. - ☐ **Shared Responsibility Model:** Google secures the *cloud*; user secures *their access*. - ☐ **Default Gmail is NOT end-to-end encrypted** — only TLS in transit + AES at rest. - ☐ **Multi-tenancy** is enabled by virtualization (gVisor/Kubernetes pods). - ☐ **Elasticity** is achieved via Borg auto-scaling during peak events. - ☐ **High availability:** **99.9% SLA** with cross-region failover. > 💡 **Last-Minute Mnemonic for SMTP/IMAP/POP3 ports:** > **"Send Mail To Port 587, IMAP uses 993, POP3 uses 995"** → **S.M.T.P. = 5-8-7**

topics.title: Google Drive. Microsoft Azure - Azure Virtual Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Google Drive. Microsoft Azure - Azure Virtual Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Google Drive — Formal Definition

**Google Drive** is a **file storage and synchronization service** developed by Google that enables users to store files in the cloud (on Google's distributed infrastructure), synchronize files across multiple devices, and share files with other users. It was officially launched on **April 24, 2012**, replacing the older Google Docs service, and integrates deeply with Google Workspace (formerly G Suite) productivity tools such as Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, and Forms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Google Drive is positioned in Module 4 as a representative example of **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service) / Cloud Storage** offerings. Students must be able to explain its features, architecture, and the underlying cloud storage principles (redundancy, synchronization, access control).

#### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy — "The Magic Filing Cabinet"

Imagine you have a **filing cabinet at home** where you keep all your important documents (photos, assignments, bills). The problem is that if your house floods, the cabinet is ruined. If you need a document at college, you have to carry a photocopy.

**Google Drive is like a magical filing cabinet that:**
1. Lives *simultaneously* in three different safe vaults (data redundancy) — even if one burns down, your documents are safe.
2. **Teleports** a copy of any file to your phone, laptop, and tablet the moment you save it (synchronization).
3. Lets you **hand a key** to a friend so they can view/edit the document without you being present (collaborative sharing).
4. Has a **librarian (Google)** who automatically sorts, labels, and searches your files using **AI-powered OCR** (Optical Character Recognition).

### 1.2 Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine — Formal Definition

A **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine (Azure VM)** is an **on-demand, scalable computing resource** offered by Microsoft Azure under the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** model. It provides the capability to deploy virtualized servers running Windows or Linux operating systems in Microsoft's global data centers, with users having full control over the OS, storage, networking, and installed software.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** An Azure VM is a software-emulated physical computer running inside Microsoft's data centers. You rent it by the second/minute, install your own OS, and use it like your own server — but without buying hardware.

#### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy — "Renting a Computer in a Data Center"

Think of Azure VM like **renting a fully-furnished office** in a skyscraper owned by Microsoft:

| Real-World Analogy | Azure Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The skyscraper building | Azure Data Center (Region) |
| Your rented office | Virtual Machine |
| Office size (1BHK, 2BHK) | VM Size (vCPUs, RAM) |
| Electricity & water bill | Pay-as-you-go pricing |
| Long-term lease discount | Reserved Instances |
| Last-minute auction rooms | Spot Instances |
| Parking space allocation | Attached Storage Disks |

You can configure the **OS, RAM, CPU, disk**, and **network** — and you only pay for what you use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Azure VM belongs to the **IaaS** category of cloud computing, while Google Drive is primarily **SaaS/Storage-as-a-Service**. This contrast is a frequently-asked distinction in KTU examinations.

### 1.3 Standard Specifications & Metrics (in **bold**)

| Component | Google Drive (Free Tier) | Google Drive (Paid) | Azure VM (Entry-level) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | **15 GB** free | **100 GB – 30 TB** (Google One plans) | **OS Disk: 30 GB – 2 TB** |
| **Max File Size** | **5 TB** per file | **5 TB** per file | **Up to 32 TB** (Premium SSD v2) |
| **Pricing Model** | Freemium / Subscription | ₹130/month for 100 GB | Pay-as-you-go (per second) |
| **Availability SLA** | 99.9% | 99.9% | **99.99%** for VMs (with Availability Sets) |
| **Access Methods** | Web, Mobile, Desktop, API | Same | RDP, SSH, Bastion, Serial Console |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Google Drive — Deep Technical Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Architectural Layers (Bottom-Up)

1. **Physical Infrastructure Layer**
   - Google's private global data centers
   - Uses **distributed file systems** (similar to GFS / Colossus)
   - Data replicated across **multiple geographic zones** for fault tolerance.

2. **Storage Layer**
   - Files broken into **chunks** and stored across thousands of machines.
   - **Differential encoding / delta synchronization** — only modified parts of a file are re-uploaded, not the entire file.

3. **Service Layer (API)**
   - Exposes **Google Drive REST API v3** and **Google Drive Activity API**.
   - OAuth 2.0 authentication for third-party app integration.

4. **Application Layer**
   - Web app (drive.google.com)
   - Mobile apps (Android, iOS)
   - Desktop sync client ("Backup & Sync" → now "Drive for Desktop")
   - Integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Slides, Forms)

#### 2.1.2 Key Features Explained

- **File Synchronization:** Multi-device sync via the **sync algorithm** — local changes queued → uploaded to cloud → pushed to other linked devices.
- **Real-Time Collaboration:** Google Docs/Sheets/Slides allow multiple users to edit simultaneously using **Operational Transformation (OT)** or **Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs)**.
- **OCR & Search:** Google uses **AI/ML-based OCR** to extract text from images and PDFs, enabling full-text search even in scanned documents.
- **Version History:** Retains up to **100 versions** of a file or longer with Vault (for Workspace accounts).
- **Granular Permissions:** Viewer, Commenter, Editor roles; shareable via link with optional expiration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Google Drive's architecture demonstrates **eventual consistency**, **CAP theorem trade-offs** (favors Availability + Partition tolerance over strict Consistency for sync), and **horizontal scalability** — concepts core to distributed systems.

### 2.2 Microsoft Azure VM — Deep Technical Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Azure VM Components (Operational Breakdown)

1. **Compute (vCPUs & RAM)**
   - VM sizes grouped into **families** (D-series, B-series, F-series, E-series, M-series, N-series).
   - Each VM has a fixed number of **virtual CPUs (vCPUs)** and **memory (GiB)**.

2. **Storage (Disks)**
   - **OS Disk:** Hosts the operating system (30 GiB – 2 TiB).
   - **Data Disks:** Optional persistent storage attached to the VM.
   - **Temporary Disk:** Non-persistent (lost on deallocation) — used for scratch space.
   - Disk types: **Standard HDD**, **Standard SSD**, **Premium SSD**, **Premium SSD v2**, **Ultra Disk**.

3. **Networking**
   - **Virtual Network (VNet):** Private network in Azure.
   - **Network Interface Card (NIC):** Connects VM to a VNet.
   - **Public IP / Private IP** addresses.
   - **Network Security Groups (NSG):** Firewall rules for inbound/outbound traffic.

4. **Management & Orchestration**
   - **Azure Resource Manager (ARM)** / **Bicep / Terraform** for declarative provisioning.
   - **Azure Portal**, **Azure CLI**, **PowerShell** for management.
   - **Availability Sets / Zones** for high availability.

#### 2.2.2 Azure VM Pricing Models (High-Yield)

| Pricing Model | Description | Discount vs. PAYG |
|---|---|---|
| **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG)** | Per-second billing, no commitment | 0% (baseline) |
| **Reserved Instances (1Y/3Y)** | Upfront or monthly commitment | Up to **72%** |
| **Spot Instances** | Use spare capacity, can be evicted | Up to **90%** |
| **Savings Plan (1Y/3Y)** | Flexible commitment across regions | Up to **65%** |

#### 2.2.3 Types of Azure VMs (Family Classification)

- **B-Series (Burstable):** For low-traffic workloads (small web servers, dev/test).
- **D-Series (General Purpose):** Balanced CPU-to-memory (apps, mid-tier databases).
- **E-Series (Memory Optimized):** High RAM-to-CPU ratio (SAP HANA, in-memory analytics).
- **F-Series (Compute Optimized):** High CPU-to-memory (batch processing, gaming servers).
- **M-Series (Memory Optimized, Large):** Up to **3.5 TiB RAM** (HPC, large databases).
- **N-Series (GPU):** Equipped with NVIDIA GPUs (AI/ML training, rendering).
- **L-Series (Storage Optimized):** High disk throughput, low latency (NoSQL, big data).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since Module 4 deals with cloud services (not heavy physics/math), the "formulas" here are **pricing and scaling formulas** that KTU examiners love to test.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Azure VM Hourly Cost | $C_{hr} = \sum_{i} (P_i \cdot U_i) + C_{storage} + C_{bw}$ | $P_i$ = price per resource, $U_i$ = usage hours |
| 2 | Monthly Cost (PAYG) | $C_{month} = 24 \cdot 30 \cdot P_{hr}$ | $P_{hr}$ in $/hour |
| 3 | Reserved Instance Savings | $S\% = \dfrac{C_{PAYG} - C_{RI}}{C_{PAYG}} \times 100$ | $C_{RI}$ = Reserved cost |
| 4 | Storage Cost | $C_{storage} = S_{GB} \cdot P_{GB} + T_{out} \cdot P_{out}$ | $T_{out}$ = outbound transfer (GB) |
| 5 | vCPU : RAM Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\text{vCPUs}}{\text{RAM (GiB)}}$ | Used to classify VM family |
| 6 | Replication Factor | $R_f = \text{number of copies of data}$ | Google Drive uses $R_f \geq 3$ |
| 7 | Availability (%) | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| 8 | SLA Uptime (Annual) | $T_{down} = (1 - A) \cdot 365 \cdot 24 \cdot 60$ (min) | For 99.99% → ~52.6 min/year |

#### Real-World Utility
- **For Engineers:** Azure VM pricing formulas help in **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** analysis during cloud migration projects.
- **For Architects:** Replication factor and availability calculations drive **disaster recovery (DR) strategy** design.
- **For Managers:** The savings percentage formula supports **finops** decisions — choosing RI vs. PAYG vs. Spot.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Azure VM Monthly Cost Calculation

**Problem:** A company uses a **Standard_D2s_v5** Azure VM (2 vCPUs, 8 GiB RAM) for **720 hours/month** at a PAYG rate of **$0.096/hour**. Additionally, a **127 GiB Premium SSD** is attached at **$0.135/GiB-month**. Calculate the total monthly bill.

#### Step 1: Identify the Components
The total cost $C_{total}$ has two parts: **Compute Cost** ($C_{compute}$) and **Storage Cost** ($C_{storage}$).

#### Step 2: Compute Cost Calculation
We use the formula:
$$C_{compute} = P_{hr} \times H_{used}$$

Substituting the values:
$$C_{compute} = 0.096 \times 720$$

Performing the multiplication:
$$C_{compute} = 69.12 \text{ USD}$$

#### Step 3: Storage Cost Calculation
We use the formula:
$$C_{storage} = S_{GB} \times P_{GB}$$

Substituting the values:
$$C_{storage} = 127 \times 0.135$$

Performing the multiplication:
$$C_{storage} = 17.145 \text{ USD}$$

#### Step 4: Total Monthly Cost
Adding both components:
$$C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{storage}$$

$$C_{total} = 69.12 + 17.145$$

$$\boxed{C_{total} = 86.265 \text{ USD / month}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reserved Instance Savings

**Problem:** The same company now opts for a **1-year Reserved Instance** at a discounted rate of **$0.046/hour** for the VM. Compute the **savings percentage** $S\%$.

#### Step 1: Compute Reserved Cost for 720 hours
$$C_{RI} = 0.046 \times 720$$

$$C_{RI} = 33.12 \text{ USD}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Savings Formula
$$S\% = \frac{C_{PAYG} - C_{RI}}{C_{PAYG}} \times 100$$

$$S\% = \frac{69.12 - 33.12}{69.12} \times 100$$

$$S\% = \frac{36.00}{69.12} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{S\% \approx 52.08\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 52% saving illustrates why **Reserved Instances** are recommended for steady-state production workloads, while **Spot/PAYG** suits bursty or experimental workloads.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Azure VM SLA Uptime Calculation

**Problem:** Azure guarantees an SLA of **99.99%** for VMs deployed in **Availability Zones**. Calculate the **maximum allowable annual downtime**.

#### Step 1: Total Minutes in a Year
$$T_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60$$

$$T_{year} = 525600 \text{ minutes}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Downtime Formula
$$T_{down} = (1 - A) \times T_{year}$$

$$T_{down} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525600$$

$$T_{down} = 0.0001 \times 525600$$

$$\boxed{T_{down} = 52.56 \text{ minutes / year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The difference between 99.9% (single VM, 8.76 hours/year downtime) and 99.99% (Availability Zone-protected VM, 52.56 minutes/year) is a **classic 2-mark question**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Google Drive vs. Azure VM

| Parameter | Google Drive | Microsoft Azure VM |
|---|---|---|
| **Service Category** | SaaS / Cloud Storage | IaaS (Virtualized Compute) |
| **User Control** | Limited (file-level) | Full (OS + Software + Config) |
| **OS Choice** | None (managed) | Windows or Linux (any distro) |
| **Primary Use Case** | File storage, sync, collaboration | Hosting apps, dev/test, HPC |
| **Free Tier** | **15 GB** | ₹13,300 credit for 30 days (new account) |
| **Scalability** | Manual upgrade of plan | Auto-scaling (VMSS) |
| **Data Redundancy** | Cross-region replication | Configurable (LRS/GRS/ZRS) |
| **Pricing Unit** | Per GB per month | Per vCPU-hour + per GB-month |
| **API Access** | Drive REST API v3 | Azure Compute REST API |
| **Security Model** | OAuth 2.0, encryption at rest | Azure AD (Entra ID), NSG, encryption |
| **SLA** | 99.9% | **99.99%** (with Availability Zones) |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step — Creating an Azure VM (Conceptual Workflow)

This is a high-frequency KTU question. The end-to-end process is:

1. **Sign in to Azure Portal** (portal.azure.com) with an Azure subscription.
2. **Navigate to "Virtual Machines"** → Click **"Create"** → **"Azure Virtual Machine"**.
3. **Basics Tab:** Choose subscription, resource group, VM name, region, image (Windows Server 2022 / Ubuntu 22.04), size (e.g., Standard_B2s), authentication (SSH key or password).
4. **Disks Tab:** Select OS disk type (Premium SSD), optionally attach data disks.
5. **Networking Tab:** Create or select VNet, subnet, public IP, NSG (with RDP/SSH ports open).
6. **Management Tab:** Enable auto-shutdown, monitoring, backup.
7. **Review + Create** → Azure Resource Manager (ARM) provisions the VM.
8. **Connect:** Use RDP (Windows) or SSH (Linux) using the public IP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Cue:** KTU often asks students to **list the tabs** in the VM creation wizard. Memorize: **Basics → Disks → Networking → Management → Advanced → Review + Create**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics
