<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**List any three features of Google Drive. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three salient features of Google Drive are:

1. **Cloud Storage & Synchronization:** Provides **15 GB of free storage** that synchronizes seamlessly across devices (mobile, laptop, desktop) using delta-sync technology — only modified portions of files are re-uploaded.

2. **Real-Time Collaboration:** Deep integration with Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Slides) allows multiple users to simultaneously edit a document, with changes propagated in real time using Operational Transformation.

3. **Powerful Search & OCR:** Google Drive uses **AI-based Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** to extract text from images and scanned PDFs, enabling full-text search across all stored content — a feature not present in traditional file systems.

*(Examiner's Note: Mentioning "15 GB free storage" specifically scores an extra mark. Diagrams earn bonus.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**What is a Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine? Name its any two components. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine (Azure VM)** is an on-demand, scalable computing resource offered under the **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** model. It allows users to deploy and run virtualized Windows or Linux servers in Microsoft's data centers, with full control over the operating system, storage, and networking configurations.

The two main components of an Azure VM are:

1. **Compute (vCPUs & RAM):** Determines the processing power and memory of the VM. Example: Standard_D2s_v5 has **2 vCPUs and 8 GiB RAM**.

2. **Storage (Disks):** Includes the **OS Disk** (hosting the operating system), **Data Disks** (optional persistent storage), and a **Temporary Disk** (non-persistent scratch space).

*(Other valid components: Virtual Network, Network Interface Card, Public IP, NSG — any two accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Pattern, Module 4 Choice-1]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of Google Drive with a neat diagram. List its key features and explain how synchronization is achieved. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
Google Drive is a cloud-based file storage and synchronization service launched by Google in 2012. It uses Google's distributed file systems (Colossus-based) and provides cross-device synchronization, file sharing, and collaboration features.

**Architecture (3 Marks):**
The architecture of Google Drive consists of the following layers:

- **Client Layer:** Web browsers, mobile apps (Android/iOS), and the desktop sync client ("Drive for Desktop") act as entry points.
- **API & Authentication Layer:** Uses the **Google Drive REST API v3** with **OAuth 2.0** for secure access.
- **Sync Engine:** Handles delta synchronization — only the modified parts of a file (chunks) are uploaded, reducing bandwidth consumption.
- **Storage Layer:** Files are broken into chunks and stored across Google's distributed infrastructure (**Colossus** file system), replicated across multiple geographic zones for fault tolerance.
- **Service Layer:** Integrates with Google Workspace apps (Docs, Sheets, Slides, Forms) and provides AI/ML-based OCR and search.

**Key Features (2 Marks):**
1. **15 GB free storage**, expandable via Google One subscription.
2. **Real-time collaboration** in Docs/Sheets/Slides.
3. **Version history** — up to 100 revisions per file.
4. **Granular sharing** — Viewer/Commenter/Editor roles with expiring links.
5. **AI-powered OCR** for searching inside images and PDFs.

**Synchronization Mechanism (1 Mark):**
When a file is modified locally, the **Sync Engine** detects the change, computes a **delta** (the difference from the server version), and uploads only the modified chunks. The server then pushes these changes to all other devices linked to the same account, ensuring eventual consistency across all endpoints.

**Diagram (Mandatory — 1 Mark):**
A block diagram showing *Client → API → Sync Engine → Storage → Data Centers → Workspace Integration* is expected.

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the components of a Microsoft Azure Virtual Machine. Differentiate between PAYG and Reserved Instance pricing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
An Azure VM is a virtualized server instance in Microsoft's cloud, providing IaaS capabilities. It comprises several integrated components that work together to deliver compute, storage, and networking.

**Components of Azure VM (3 Marks):**
1. **Compute:** vCPUs and RAM, classified into families (B, D, E, F, M, N, L).
2. **Storage:** OS Disk, Data Disks, and Temporary Disk.
3. **Networking:** Virtual Network (VNet), Network Interface Card (NIC), Public/Private IP, Network Security Group (NSG).
4. **Management:** Azure Resource Manager (ARM), Azure Monitor, Azure Backup.

**Diagram (1 Mark):**
A block diagram showing VM at the center with Compute, Storage, and Networking as connected blocks is expected.

**PAYG vs. Reserved Instance (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Pay-As-You-Go | Reserved Instance |
|---|---|---|
| **Commitment** | None | 1-year or 3-year |
| **Billing** | Per second | Upfront or monthly |
| **Discount** | 0% (baseline) | Up to 72% |
| **Use Case** | Short-term, unpredictable workloads | Steady-state, predictable workloads |
| **Flexibility** | High | Lower (region/size lock-in) |

**Conclusion:** PAYG suits dev/test and bursty workloads, while Reserved Instances offer cost optimization for long-running production VMs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Pattern, Module 4 Choice-2]**

**(a) Compare Google Drive and Microsoft Azure VM based on: (i) Service Model, (ii) User Control, (iii) Use Case, (iv) Pricing. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Google Drive | Microsoft Azure VM |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Service Model** | SaaS / Cloud Storage (Storage-as-a-Service) | IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service) |
| **(ii) User Control** | Limited — user manages files only | Full — user controls OS, software, network, storage |
| **(iii) Use Case** | File storage, sync, document collaboration, backup | Hosting applications, dev/test environments, HPC, ML training |
| **(iv) Pricing** | Freemium (15 GB free) + Google One subscriptions (₹130/month for 100 GB) | Pay-as-you-go (per vCPU-hour, per GB-month) + Reserved/Spot discounts |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
While Google Drive is a ready-to-use SaaS solution best for end-users, Azure VM is a flexible IaaS platform best for developers and IT administrators requiring full infrastructure control.

---

**(b) A company deploys an Azure VM (Standard_B2s, 2 vCPUs, 4 GiB RAM) for 30 days continuously. The PAYG rate is ₹8.50/hour. Calculate the monthly bill. If the company switches to a 1-year Reserved Instance at ₹4.00/hour, compute the savings percentage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $H_{used} = 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours
- $P_{PAYG} = ₹8.50/\text{hour}$
- $P_{RI} = ₹4.00/\text{hour}$

**Step 1: Compute PAYG Monthly Cost (3 Marks)**
$$C_{PAYG} = P_{PAYG} \times H_{used} = 8.50 \times 720$$

$$C_{PAYG} = ₹6120$$

**Step 2: Compute Reserved Instance Cost (2 Marks)**
$$C_{RI} = P_{RI} \times H_{used} = 4.00 \times 720$$

$$C_{RI} = ₹2880$$

**Step 3: Apply Savings Formula (2 Marks)**
$$S\% = \frac{C_{PAYG} - C_{RI}}{C_{PAYG}} \times 100$$

$$S\% = \frac{6120 - 2880}{6120} \times 100$$

$$S\% = \frac{3240}{6120} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{S\% \approx 52.94\%}$$

**Conclusion:** Switching to a 1-year Reserved Instance yields a **~52.94% cost saving**, amounting to ₹3,240 per month per VM — a substantial optimization for steady-state workloads.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SaaS with IaaS:** Many students wrongly classify Google Drive as IaaS. *Remember: Drive is SaaS, Azure VM is IaaS.*
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours to days/months:** When calculating Azure VM cost, the most common mistake is using 30 instead of 720 hours. Always show the conversion step.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram:** A 7-mark question *without* a diagram typically caps your score at 5–6 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 4. **Miscounting SLA digits:** 99.9% ≠ 99.99%. The downtime difference is 8.76 hours/year vs. 52.56 minutes/year — write out the calculation.
> 5. **Not specifying the VM family:** When discussing Azure VMs, always mention the family (B, D, E, F, M, N, L) — this shows depth.
> 6. **Mixing Google Drive with Google Docs:** These are *different* — Drive is the storage platform; Docs is one of the apps running *on* Drive.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Google Drive** = **SaaS** cloud storage service launched in **2012**; **15 GB free tier**; supports files up to **5 TB**; uses **OAuth 2.0** + **REST API v3**; **delta sync**; **AI-based OCR**; built on **Colossus** distributed file system.
- [ ] **Azure VM** = **IaaS** virtualized server; supports **Windows + Linux**; key components = **Compute (vCPUs/RAM), Storage (Disks), Networking (VNet/NSG), Management (ARM)**.
- [ ] **Azure VM Families:** **B** (burstable), **D** (general), **E** (memory), **F** (compute), **M** (memory-large), **N** (GPU), **L** (storage).
- [ ] **Disk Types:** Standard HDD → Standard SSD → Premium SSD → Premium SSD v2 → Ultra Disk.
- [ ] **Pricing Models:** **PAYG** (baseline), **Reserved (up to 72% off)**, **Spot (up to 90% off)**, **Savings Plan (up to 65% off)**.
- [ ] **Azure VM SLA:** **99.99%** with Availability Zones (≈52.56 min downtime/year); **99.95%** with Availability Sets; **99.9%** for a single VM.
- [ ] **Key Cost Formula:** $C_{compute} = P_{hr} \times H_{used}$; $S\% = \frac{C_{PAYG} - C_{RI}}{C_{PAYG}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Google Drive vs. OneDrive vs. Dropbox:** All are SaaS storage, but Drive integrates natively with **Google Workspace** (Docs, Sheets, Gmail).
- [ ] **Provisioning Workflow Tabs:** **Basics → Disks → Networking → Management → Advanced → Review + Create**.
- [ ] **Connectivity:** **RDP (port 3389)** for Windows VMs; **SSH (port 22)** for Linux VMs; **Azure Bastion** for secure browser-based access.
- [ ] **CAP Theorem Application:** Google Drive favors **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) — local edits succeed even when offline, syncing later (eventual consistency).
- [ ] **High Availability Tools:** **Availability Sets** (fault/update domains within a DC), **Availability Zones** (physically separate DCs in a region), **VM Scale Sets** (auto-scaling).
- [ ] **Disaster Recovery:** Use **Azure Site Recovery** + **Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS)** for cross-region DR; Google Drive inherently replicates across regions.
- [ ] **Exam Mantra:** Always write **formula → substitution → arithmetic → boxed answer** for any numerical problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hyper-V
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyper-V

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Hyper-V

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hyper-V** is Microsoft's native **Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** virtualization platform that enables the creation and management of **virtual machines (VMs)** on x86-64 Windows server and client operating systems. It partitions a single physical server into multiple isolated, secure virtual environments — each capable of independently running its own operating system and applications.

Hyper-V was first released alongside **Windows Server 2008** and has since evolved into a foundational component of Microsoft's hybrid cloud strategy (Azure Stack HCI, Azure Local). It is the **hypervisor layer** of the Windows Server stack and a core building block for **private cloud infrastructures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Hyper-V belongs to the unit on *Server Virtualization & Hypervisors*. Examiners expect you to clearly distinguish between **Type-1** (Hyper-V, ESXi, Xen) and **Type-2** (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) hypervisors and explain the **parent partition / child partition architecture**.

### 1.1.1 Standard KTU-Defined Metrics (must memorize)

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum vCPUs per VM** | **1,024 vCPUs** (Windows Server 2022+) | Logical processors assignable to a single VM |
| **Maximum RAM per VM** | **12 TB** (Gen 2) | Virtual memory ceiling per partition |
| **Maximum Active VMs per Host** | **1,024 VMs** | Theoretical scalability of a single Hyper-V host |
| **VHDX Max Capacity** | **64 TB** | Native virtual disk size (vs 2 TB for legacy VHD) |
| **Hyper-V Architecture** | **Microkernelized / Type-1** | Runs directly on hardware |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine a **large piece of land (the physical server)**. On this land, you want to build:

- **Multiple independent houses (Virtual Machines)**
- Each house must have its **own electricity, water, plumbing, doors, and locks**
- The houses cannot interfere with each other
- A **building manager (the Hyper-V Hypervisor)** oversees the entire structure, allocating resources fairly

In this analogy:

| Real-World Element | Hyper-V Counterpart |
|---|---|
| The land | Physical hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC) |
| Building manager | **Hyper-V Hypervisor** (VTL/VMS root partition control) |
| Each apartment | A **Virtual Machine (Child Partition)** |
| Building security desk | **Virtualization Service Provider (VSP)** |
| Apartment intercom | **Virtualization Service Client (VSC)** |
| Apartment keys / mailbox | **Virtual Devices** (synthetic) |
| The architect's office | **Parent Partition (Host OS)** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works:** Just as apartments share infrastructure (water, electricity) but remain isolated and private, Hyper-V VMs share physical hardware but appear as completely independent computers to their guest operating systems.

---

## 1.3 KTU One-Line Exam Definitions (memorize verbatim)

> **Hyper-V:** *"A native hypervisor-based virtualization technology from Microsoft that creates and runs virtual machines on Windows operating systems."*

> **Hypervisor:** *"A thin software layer that virtualizes the hardware resources of a single physical machine to enable multiple operating systems to run concurrently and in isolation."*

> **Type-1 Hypervisor:** *"A bare-metal hypervisor that runs directly on the host's hardware to control it and manage guest operating systems, with no host OS between the hypervisor and hardware."*

> **Type-2 Hypervisor:** *"A hosted hypervisor that runs as an application within a conventional host operating system, with the host OS mediating access to hardware."*

---

## 1.4 Position of Hyper-V in the Cloud Stack

Hyper-V serves as the **virtualization substrate** for Microsoft's **private cloud** offering. In the cloud computing stack:

1. **Hardware** → physical server
2. **Hypervisor (Hyper-V)** → creates VMs
3. **Virtualization Platform** → Windows Server with Hyper-V role
4. **Cloud Orchestration Layer** → System Center VMM, Azure Local
5. **Cloud Services** → IaaS, PaaS delivered to tenants

> [!NOTE]
> Hyper-V **by itself is NOT a cloud**. It is the **virtualization engine** that *enables* building a private cloud. Cloud characteristics (self-service, elasticity, measured service) must be added by higher layers (e.g., System Center, Azure).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" — Why Hyper-V Was Built

Before the advent of hardware-assisted virtualization, x86 CPUs were not virtualizable. Intel VT-x (2005) and AMD-V (2006) introduced **ring de-privileging** and **shadow page tables**, which made efficient Type-1 hypervisors possible. Hyper-V was Microsoft's strategic response to:

1. **Server consolidation** (reducing physical server sprawl, energy costs)
2. **Workload isolation** (fault containment, security)
3. **Enabling hybrid cloud** (lift-and-shift to Azure)
4. **Dev/Test agility** (rapid VM provisioning)
5. **High availability** (Live Migration, Failover Clustering)

---

## 2.2 Architecture of Hyper-V — The Partition Model

Hyper-V uses a **partition-based microkernel architecture**. A partition is an isolated logical unit with guaranteed resources.

### 2.2.1 Logical Architecture (Layered View)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CHILD PARTITION (Guest VM 1)            │ ← runs Guest OS
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  Guest OS  │  VSC (Virtualization Svc Client) │   │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
│   Synthetic / Enlightened I/O  ↕  Enlightened I/O     │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              CHILD PARTITION (Guest VM 2)            │
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  Guest OS  │  VSC                             │   │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│              PARENT PARTITION (Host)                 │ ← management
│   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│   │  Host OS (Windows Server)                    │   │
│   │  VSP (Virtualization Service Provider)       │   │
│   │  WMI / Hyper-V Manager / PowerShell          │   │
│   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         HYPER-V HYPERVISOR (Ring -1, microkernel)    │ ← controls HW
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         PHYSICAL HARDWARE (CPU, RAM, NIC, Disk)      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 6 Logical Steps a Hyper-V VM goes through to access hardware:**

1. **Guest Application** issues a system call (e.g., open a file).
2. **Guest OS** receives the request and forwards it to the **VSC (Virtualization Service Client)** in the child partition.
3. **VSC** sends the request across the **VMBus** (a high-speed logical channel).
4. **VSP (Virtualization Service Provider)** in the parent partition receives the request.
5. **VSP** translates the virtual request into a physical I/O call and executes it via the host OS.
6. The result travels back through VMBus to the **Guest OS** and finally to the **Application**.

> **Key Insight:** In *enlightened* guests (Windows, Linux with Hyper-V tools), the VSC/VSP path is used → **fast synthetic I/O**. In *non-enlightened* guests, emulation occurs → **slower emulated I/O**.

---

## 2.4 Key Architectural Models — Type-1 vs Type-2

| Feature | **Type-1 (Hyper-V, ESXi)** | **Type-2 (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation)** |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Bare-metal, on hardware | As application on host OS |
| Performance | **Near-native** | Lower (host OS overhead) |
| Use case | Servers, datacenters, cloud | Desktops, dev/test |
| Resource scheduling | Direct hardware access | Indirect via host OS |
| Examples | Hyper-V, VMware ESXi, Citrix XenServer | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation, Parallels |
| **Hyper-V is which?** | ✅ **Type-1 (bare-metal)** | ❌ |

---

## 2.5 Storage Architecture in Hyper-V

> [!NOTE]
> **VHD vs VHDX** is a frequently-asked comparison question.

| Feature | **VHD** | **VHDX** |
|---|---|---|
| Max size | **2 TB** | **64 TB** |
| Format | Sparse, monolithic | Sparse, block-aligned |
| Resilience | Vulnerable to power loss | **Resilient** (journaled log) |
| Performance | Standard | Better (4 KB logical sectors) |
| Recommended | Legacy | **Production** |

### 2.5.1 Disk Types in Hyper-V

1. **Dynamically Expanding** — grows as data is written; uses only what is needed.
2. **Fixed Size** — pre-allocates the full virtual disk size; best performance.
3. **Differencing** — child disk storing changes relative to a parent; used for checkpoints.

---

## 2.6 Networking Architecture in Hyper-V

Hyper-V uses an **Extensible Virtual Switch (vSwitch)** — a Layer-2 software switch. There are three types:

| Switch Type | Connectivity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **External** | Bound to a physical NIC → VM ↔ External network | Production VMs needing internet/LAN |
| **Internal** | Between VMs and the host (no external) | Management, isolated lab |
| **Private** | Only between VMs (host excluded) | Isolated test networks |

> [!TIP]
> The **virtual switch** is the most commonly tested networking concept for Hyper-V in KTU exams. Always draw/describe the three types.

---

## 2.7 Hyper-V High Availability Features

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Live Migration** vs **Failover Clustering** is a classic KTU 14-mark question.

| Feature | Live Migration | Failover Clustering |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Move a running VM with zero downtime | Continuous VM availability if host fails |
| Trigger | Manual / scheduled | Automatic (host failure detection) |
| Downtime | **Zero seconds** | Seconds (depends on cluster health detection) |
| Storage | Shared (SMB / Cluster Shared Volume) | **Required: shared storage** |
| Scale | Two nodes typically | Up to 64 nodes |

### 2.7.1 Live Migration — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pre-migration:** Source host transfers VM memory pages in iterative rounds.
2. **Memory copy:** Active memory is copied while VM keeps running.
3. **State transfer:** VM state (CPU registers, devices) transferred.
4. **Takeover:** Source pauses VM, destination resumes it — typically within **sub-second** blackout.
5. **Cleanup:** Source releases the VM.

---

|---|---|

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Hyper-V is a *system* topic, not a numerical one. This section therefore provides **structured derivations of architectural mechanisms, comparison matrices, and decision logic** — the kind of step-by-step reasoning KTU 14-mark questions demand.

---

## 3.1 DERIVATION: How Hyper-V Creates Isolation Between VMs

**Premise:** Two VMs (VM-A and VM-B) must be completely isolated even though they share the same physical hardware.

**Step 1 — CPU Isolation**
Hyper-V uses **Intel VT-x / AMD-V** hardware virtualization extensions. Each VM is allocated a set of virtual CPUs (vCPUs). The hypervisor traps all privileged instructions using the **ring-(-1) execution mode**, preventing one VM from directly controlling physical CPU state used by another.

**Step 2 — Memory Isolation**
Hyper-V employs **Extended Page Tables (EPT) on Intel** and **Nested Page Tables (NPT) on AMD**. Each VM is given a unique physical address space; the hardware's MMU walks the EPT/NPT on every memory access.

**Step 3 — Device Isolation**
Each VM gets a unique set of **synthetic virtual devices** (network adapter, storage controller, etc.). The **VMBus** ensures I/O channels between VSC (in VM) and VSP (in parent) are not visible to other VMs.

**Step 4 — Network Isolation**
A virtual switch segregates traffic at **Layer 2**. VLAN tagging, port ACLs, and bandwidth management enforce isolation at the network layer.

**Step 5 — State Isolation**
Each VM has its own **VM configuration file (.vmcx)**, **virtual hard disk (.vhdx)**, and **saved state (.bin)**. No shared mutable state exists between VMs.

> **Result:** Even if VM-A crashes due to a misbehaving application, VM-B continues unaffected. This is the *fault-isolation* guarantee of Type-1 virtualization.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION: How Live Migration Achieves Zero Downtime

**Step-by-step logic of the Live Migration process:**

1. **Initialize Migration** — Administrator triggers Move on a running VM from Host A (source) to Host B (destination).

2. **Pre-Copy Iteration #1** — All memory pages of the VM are transferred from A to B. The VM continues running on A.

3. **Dirty Page Tracking** — While the bulk copy occurs, the VM keeps running. The hypervisor tracks pages modified during transfer (*dirty pages*).

4. **Pre-Copy Iterations #2…N** — Only the *dirty pages* are resent. With each iteration, the dirty set shrinks (typically geometric decay).

5. **Stop-and-Copy Phase** — When the dirty set is below a threshold (a few hundred pages), VM on A is **paused**.

6. **Final State Transfer** — CPU registers, device state, and the small remaining dirty set are sent to B.

7. **Resume on Destination** — VM resumes on B. Total blackout time = **typically 100 ms to 2 seconds** for most workloads.

8. **Source Cleanup** — Host A releases the VM, frees memory, and updates the cluster's resource ownership.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is "zero downtime":** From the application's perspective, the blackout window is short enough that TCP sessions, in-flight transactions, and most user interactions survive intact. KTU exams often ask: *"How does Hyper-V achieve near-zero downtime during VM migration?"* — Answer: *iterative pre-copy of memory + sub-second blackout + state transfer.*

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION: VSC–VMBus–VSP Communication Path

The synthetic I/O data path is critical for understanding enlightened I/O:

$$
\text{Request Path: App} \rightarrow \text{GuestOS} \rightarrow \text{VSC} \rightarrow \text{VMBus} \rightarrow \text{VSP} \rightarrow \text{HostOS} \rightarrow \text{Physical HW}
$$

$$
\text{Response Path: Physical HW} \rightarrow \text{HostOS} \rightarrow \text{VSP} \rightarrow \text{VMBus} \rightarrow \text{VSC} \rightarrow \text{GuestOS} \rightarrow \text{App}
$$

**Component roles:**

$$
\text{VSC}_{\text{role}} = \text{Forward guest I/O requests to VSP via VMBus}
$$

$$
\text{VMBus}_{\text{role}} = \text{Logically secure channel; not a network protocol}
$$

$$
\text{VSP}_{\text{role}} = \text{Translate virtual I/O to physical I/O and respond}
$$

> **Why synthetic is faster than emulated:** Emulated I/O traps and translates **every instruction** through the hypervisor (CPU cycles wasted). Synthetic I/O uses **VMBus** for bulk data movement with **DMA-like** efficiency, leaving the hypervisor out of the data path for payloads.

---

## 3.4 COMPARISON MATRIX: Generation 1 vs Generation 2 VMs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always asked in KTU Module 4.**

| Feature | **Generation 1** | **Generation 2** |
|---|---|---|
| Firmware | Legacy **BIOS** | **UEFI** |
| Boot controller | IDE (emulated) | **SCSI (synthetic)** |
| Max vCPUs | 64 (older Windows) | **Up to 1,024** |
| Max RAM | Lower (e.g., 1 TB) | **Up to 12 TB** |
| Secure Boot | ❌ Not supported | ✅ Supported |
| Boot from SCSI | ❌ | ✅ |
| Boot from VHDX | ✅ | ✅ (native) |
| Network boot (PXE) | Legacy | ✅ UEFI-based |
| Recommended for | Legacy OS (Win XP, 2003) | **All modern workloads** |

---

## 3.5 COMPARISON MATRIX: Checkpoints vs Backups

| Feature | Checkpoint (Snapshot) | Backup |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Short-term rollback during testing | Long-term recovery |
| Captures | VM state + memory (optional) | VM state, app-consistent or crash-consistent |
| Storage | Same volume as VM (default) | Dedicated backup storage |
| Frequency | On-demand (manual) | Scheduled |
| Recovery time | Seconds (in-place revert) | Minutes to hours |
| Use case | Patch testing, dev/test | **DR, compliance, archival** |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU exam trap: *"Is a checkpoint the same as a backup?"* — **NO.** Checkpoints are NOT a substitute for backups. Production VMs should rely on real backup solutions (e.g., Veeam, Azure Backup Server).

```
                ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                │ What is the requirement?     │
                └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                               │
       ┌───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┐
       ▼                       ▼                       ▼
 [Isolate workloads]    [Move VM across hosts]    [Protect from disaster]
       │                       │                       │
       ▼                       ▼                       ▼
  Use Virtual Switch     Use Live Migration       Use Hyper-V Replica
  (Ext/Int/Private)     (zero-downtime)           (async, 5-min RPO)
       │                       │                       │
       │              ┌────────┴────────┐              │
       │              ▼                 ▼              │
       │       [Continuous HA]    [Planned moves]      │
       │              │                 │              │
       │              ▼                 ▼              │
       │       Failover Cluster    Live Migration      │
       │       (auto-failover)    (manual/scheduled)   │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## 3.7 DERIVATION: Virtual Switch Port Modes (Exam Favorite)

Hyper-V vSwitch supports three port modes:

| Port Mode | Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Access** | Carries traffic for a single VLAN (untagged or tagged) |
| **Trunk** | Carries traffic for multiple VLANs (tagged) |
| **Uplink** | Connects to a physical NIC; aggregates multiple physical links |

The bandwidth management model:

$$
\text{Total NIC Bandwidth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{BW}_{i}^{\text{VM}} \leq \text{Physical NIC Capacity}
$$

If a VM's traffic exceeds its **minimum bandwidth guarantee**, it may use *unused* capacity from other VMs up to a configured **maximum bandwidth cap**.

> **KTU exam answer template:** *"Hyper-V virtual switch supports bandwidth management where each VM can be assigned a minimum guaranteed bandwidth and a maximum allowed bandwidth, with the switch using quality-of-service (QoS) scheduling."*

---

## 3.8 EXAMINATION-ORIENTED REASONING: Why Hyper-V is a Type-1 Hypervisor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Define the hypervisor class.**
> A Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor installs **directly on physical hardware** without a host operating system mediating hardware access.

> **Step 2 — Identify Hyper-V's installation mode.**
> When the Hyper-V role is enabled on Windows Server, the **hypervisor is installed beneath** the Windows OS. The Windows OS itself is moved into a special privileged partition called the *parent partition*.

> **Step 3 — Note the parent partition is not a "host OS in the Type-2 sense."**
> In Type-2, the host OS is a *peer* to the hypervisor and a *mediator* for all hardware calls. In Hyper-V, the hypervisor loads first, then the parent partition boots on top. Hardware control belongs to the hypervisor, not Windows.

> **Step 4 — Conclude.**
> Hence, Hyper-V is a **Type-1, microkernelized, partitioning hypervisor** — confirmed by Microsoft's own architecture documentation.

---

## 3.9 KTU-FAVORITE 14-MARK STRUCTURE: "Explain Hyper-V Architecture"

> Use this **6-point skeleton** for full marks:

1. **Introduction** — define Hyper-V as a Type-1 hypervisor by Microsoft.
2. **Architecture layers** — Hardware → Hypervisor → Parent Partition → Child Partitions.
3. **Parent Partition** — purpose, components (VSP, WMI, Hyper-V Manager).
4. **Child Partitions** — guest OS, VSC, enlightened I/O.
5. **VMBus** — communication mechanism, why it matters.
6. **Key features** — Live Migration, Replica, Failover Clustering, vSwitch, VHDX.

> **Bonus marks:** Mention **Generation 1 vs Generation 2**, **virtual switch types**, and **integration services** for enlightened I/O.

---

## 3.10 KTU Numerical Estimate Problem (Practice)

**Question:** A company is consolidating 10 physical servers, each running at 15% average CPU utilization, onto a single Hyper-V host with 32 physical cores. Assuming an over-commitment ratio of 4:1, calculate the total vCPUs that can be assigned.

**Solution:**

$$
\text{Total Physical Cores} = 32
$$

$$
\text{Over-commitment Ratio} = \frac{\text{vCPUs Assigned}}{\text{Physical Cores}} = 4
$$

$$
\text{Total vCPUs Assignable} = 32 \times 4 = 128 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

$$
\text{vCPUs per Consolidated VM} = \frac{128}{10} = 12.8 \approx 12 \text{ vCPUs per VM}
$$

> **Answer:** A single 32-core host can host 10 VMs with ~12 vCPUs each, given a 4:1 over-commitment ratio.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a hypervisor. Distinguish between Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors with examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **hypervisor** is a thin software layer that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines by virtualizing the physical hardware resources of a single host, allowing multiple operating systems to share the same hardware in isolation.

| Feature | Type-1 (Bare-Metal) | Type-2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on hardware | As application over host OS |
| Performance | Near-native | Lower (host OS overhead) |
| Examples | **Hyper-V, VMware ESXi, XenServer** | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |

**Hyper-V is a Type-1 hypervisor.** *(1 mark for definition, 1.5 for table, 0.5 for identifying Hyper-V's type)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is a virtual switch in Hyper-V? List and briefly explain its types.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **virtual switch (vSwitch)** in Hyper-V is a software-based Layer-2 network switch that allows virtual machines to communicate with each other, with the host, and with external networks.

1. **External Virtual Switch** — bound to a physical NIC, connects VMs to the external network. *(1 mark)*
2. **Internal Virtual Switch** — allows communication between VMs and the host OS but no external connectivity. *(1 mark)*
3. **Private Virtual Switch** — allows communication only between VMs; host cannot communicate. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q.A (a)** Explain the architecture of Microsoft Hyper-V with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of the parent partition and child partitions. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A (b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the working of Live Migration in Hyper-V. List any two advantages of Live Migration. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Hyper-V is a Type-1, hardware-assisted hypervisor developed by Microsoft. It enables the creation and management of virtual machines on x86-64 systems running Windows.

**Architectural Components (5 marks):**

The Hyper-V architecture is a **partition-based microkernel model** with the following layers (bottom to top):

1. **Physical Hardware Layer** — CPU (with VT-x/AMD-V), RAM, disk, NIC. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Hyper-V Hypervisor Layer** — A thin microkernel that loads directly on hardware at Ring -1. It is responsible for CPU scheduling, memory management, and partition isolation. *(1 mark)*
3. **Parent Partition** — The first partition created; runs the Windows Server host OS. It contains the **Virtualization Service Providers (VSPs)**, the **WMI provider**, **Hyper-V Manager**, and **PowerShell management interfaces**. It is responsible for managing child partitions but does not mediate hardware access (the hypervisor does). *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Child Partitions** — Each child partition hosts a guest OS. It contains the **Virtualization Service Clients (VSCs)** that forward I/O requests to the VSPs of the parent partition via the **VMBus** (a logical high-speed channel). *(1.5 marks)*
5. **VMBus** — A logical communication channel enabling enlightened I/O. Synthetic devices use VMBus for high-performance data transfer. *(0.5 mark)*

**Role Distinction (1 mark):**

- The **parent partition** is privileged and has direct access to hardware resources for management purposes; it controls VM lifecycle (create, start, stop, snapshot).
- The **child partition** is isolated; it sees only the virtual resources assigned to it and cannot access the hardware directly.

> *[Always include a labelled diagram in your answer — at least a block diagram showing HW → Hypervisor → Parent → Children.]*

---

#### Model Answer to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Live Migration is a Hyper-V feature that allows the transfer of a running virtual machine from one physical host to another **with zero or sub-second downtime**, while preserving the VM's running state, network identity, and connected users.

**Working Steps with Diagram (5 marks):**

1. **Initialization:** Administrator triggers the Live Migration wizard from Hyper-V Manager, Failover Cluster Manager, or PowerShell.
2. **Pre-Copy Phase (Round 1):** The destination host establishes a connection, and **all memory pages** of the VM are copied from the source to the destination. The VM continues to run on the source.
3. **Dirty Page Tracking:** The hypervisor on the source host tracks pages modified during the transfer (dirty pages).
4. **Iterative Pre-Copy:** Successive rounds transfer **only the dirty pages**. The dirty set shrinks geometrically with each round.
5. **Stop-and-Copy:** When the dirty set is below a threshold (typically a few hundred pages), the VM is **paused** on the source.
6. **State Transfer:** CPU registers, device state, and the residual dirty pages are sent to the destination.
7. **Resume:** The destination host resumes the VM. Total blackout is typically **100 ms to 2 seconds**.
8. **Cleanup:** The source releases the VM, and cluster resources are updated.

**Advantages (1 mark — list any 2):**

- **Zero/near-zero downtime** for users and applications.
- **No disruption of network identity** (TCP sessions, IP address, and SMB connections survive).
- Enables **proactive maintenance** (hardware upgrades, patches) without scheduling outages.
- Supports **load balancing** of VMs across cluster nodes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q.B (a)** Compare Generation 1 and Generation 2 virtual machines in Hyper-V. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B (b)** Explain any four high-availability features of Hyper-V with examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

| Feature | **Generation 1 VM** | **Generation 2 VM** |
|---|---|---|
| **Firmware** | Legacy BIOS (1 mark) | UEFI (1 mark) |
| **Boot Controller** | IDE (emulated) (1 mark) | SCSI (synthetic) (1 mark) |
| **Max vCPUs** | 64 (older Windows) (0.5 mark) | 1,024 (Windows Server 2022) (0.5 mark) |
| **Max RAM** | Limited (~1 TB older OS) (0.5 mark) | 12 TB (modern Windows) (0.5 mark) |
| **Secure Boot** | Not supported (0.5 mark) | **Supported** (0.5 mark) |
| **Network Boot (PXE)** | Legacy PXE (0.25 mark) | UEFI-based PXE (0.25 mark) |
| **Boot from SCSI** | No (0.25 mark) | **Yes** (0.25 mark) |
| **Recommended for** | Legacy OS, Windows XP/2003 (0.5 mark) | **All modern workloads** (0.5 mark) |

> **Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Generation 2 VMs are the recommended choice for modern workloads due to better performance, security (Secure Boot), and scalability.

---

#### Model Answer to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

> [!NOTE]
> **Each feature = ~1.75 marks.** Select any four from the list below.

### 1. **Live Migration** *(1.75 marks)*
Allows movement of a running VM from one Hyper-V host to another with zero or sub-second downtime. Useful for planned maintenance and load balancing.

### 2. **Failover Clustering** *(1.75 marks)*
Combines multiple Hyper-V hosts into a cluster so that if one host fails, the VMs automatically restart on another healthy host. Requires **shared storage** (CSV / SMB 3.0).

### 3. **Hyper-V Replica** *(1.75 marks)*
Asynchronous replication of VMs to a **secondary site** for disaster recovery. Typical RPO (Recovery Point Objective) ranges from **30 seconds to 15 minutes**.

### 4. **Checkpoint (Snapshot)** *(1.75 marks)*
Captures the state of a VM (including memory optionally) at a point in time, allowing it to be reverted later. Primarily used for **dev/test environments** — not a substitute for backup.

### 5. **Shielded VMs** *(Bonus 0.5 mark)*
Generation 2 VMs with encryption and BitLocker; protect against insider attacks and unauthorized host tampering.

### 6. **Quality of Service (QoS) in vSwitch** *(Bonus 0.5 mark)*
Allows minimum and maximum bandwidth guarantees per VM, ensuring critical workloads get priority.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that KTU examiners penalize heavily:**

1. **Calling Hyper-V a Type-2 hypervisor** — this is the **#1 blunder** in KTU valuation. Hyper-V is a **Type-1, microkernelized, bare-metal hypervisor**. The Windows OS in the parent partition is *not* a mediator in the Type-2 sense. **Lose 2 full marks** for this mistake.

2. **Confusing checkpoints with backups** — checkpoints are for short-term dev/test, not production recovery. Examiners deduct marks if you claim otherwise.

3. **Omitting the VMBus mechanism** in architecture questions — VMBus is the *enlightened I/O backbone*. Always mention VSC ↔ VMBus ↔ VSP in your architecture answer.

4. **Forgetting the three virtual switch types** — External, Internal, Private. Drawing only one in a diagram loses at least 1.5 marks.

5. **Wrong VHDX capacity** — Examiners check the **64 TB** value. Writing **2 TB** (which is VHD) is a common confusion and costs marks.

6. **Skipping numerical details** in comparison questions — e.g., Generation 2 = UEFI, **1024 vCPUs**, **12 TB RAM**, **Secure Boot**.

7. **Not drawing a diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions — KTU valuation key allocates **1–2 marks** for a labelled diagram.

8. **Confusing Live Migration with Storage Migration** — Live Migration moves the **VM** (memory + state). Storage Migration moves **storage** of a VM between locations (e.g., SMB to CSV). They are different features.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision points — read these 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Hyper-V is a Type-1 (bare-metal) hypervisor** by Microsoft — *not Type-2*.
- ✅ **Architecture has 4 layers:** Hardware → Hypervisor (Ring -1) → Parent Partition (Windows) → Child Partitions (Guest VMs).
- ✅ **Parent partition** runs VSP, WMI, Hyper-V Manager, Failover Clustering.
- ✅ **Child partition** runs VSC + Guest OS; communicates via **VMBus**.
- ✅ **VMBus** = logical channel for synthetic (enlightened) I/O — *fast*.
- ✅ **Three virtual switches:** **External** (to LAN/internet), **Internal** (VMs + host), **Private** (VMs only).
- ✅ **VHDX = 64 TB max**; **VHD = 2 TB max** (legacy). **VHDX is production-grade**.
- ✅ **Disk types:** Dynamic Expanding, Fixed Size, Differencing.
- ✅ **Generation 1 = BIOS + IDE**; **Generation 2 = UEFI + SCSI + Secure Boot**.
- ✅ **Live Migration = zero/near-zero downtime**; uses iterative **pre-copy of memory**.
- ✅ **Failover Clustering = automatic restart** of VMs on a healthy host upon failure.
- ✅ **Hyper-V Replica = async DR replication** to a secondary site (RPO: 30 sec – 15 min).
- ✅ **Checkpoint = short-term snapshot** (not a backup).
- ✅ **Max vCPUs (Gen 2, Win Server 2022) = 1,024**; **Max RAM = 12 TB**.
- ✅ **Hardware-assisted virtualization required:** Intel **VT-x** or AMD **AMD-V**; **EPT/NPT** for memory.
- ✅ **Hyper-V role exists in:** Windows Server (Standard/Datacenter) and Windows 10/11 Pro/Enterprise/Education.
- ✅ **Hyper-V is the substrate of Azure Local / Azure Stack HCI** for Microsoft's **hybrid cloud**.
- ✅ **Integration Services** (now built-in in modern Windows) enable enlightened I/O on Hyper-V.
- ✅ **Shielded VMs** = Gen 2 VMs with encryption + BitLocker for **host-tampering protection**.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** (a) Hyper-V is NOT Type-2; (b) Checkpoint is NOT backup; (c) VHDX ≠ VHD size.

> **Final mnemonic — "HVP-CS":**
> **H**yper-V = **T**ype-1 **H**ypervisor; **V**MBus; **P**arent + Child **P**artitions; **C**luster + **S**witch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 — Hyper-V Notes. All the best for your KTU examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azure Storage Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT523 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Azure Storage Services # Azure Storage Services – Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition **Microsoft Azure Storage** is a highly available, massively scalable, durable, and secure cloud storage service offered by Microsoft Azure. It provides a suite of cloud-based storage services for structured and unstructured data, supporting both REST-based programmatic access and SDK-based client interaction. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective, Azure Storage Services encompass a portfolio of purpose-built data services — namely **Azure Blob Storage**, **Azure File Storage**, **Azure Queue Storage**, **Azure Table Storage**, and **Azure Disk Storage** — all unified under a **Storage Account** abstraction layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 4 focus is on *cloud service providers' storage offerings*. Azure is studied as a representative IaaS/PaaS provider. Students must be able to compare Blob vs File vs Queue vs Table vs Disk, explain storage tiers, and discuss redundancy models. ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you own a large **multi-story warehouse complex** (your Azure Storage Account) for your business: - **One wing stores huge unlabeled boxes** (videos, images, backups) → **Blob Storage** 🗃️ - **Another wing has office filing cabinets accessible via a shared network drive** → **File Storage** 🗂️ - **A bulletin board section holds short messages between workers** → **Queue Storage** 📨 - **A small ledger room catalogs items by a simple key (like an SKU code)** → **Table Storage** 📋 - **A secure vault contains SSDs/HDDs directly bolted to your rented servers** → **Disk Storage** 💽 The warehouse manager (Azure platform) handles replication, security, backups, scaling, and disaster recovery automatically — you only pay for what you use. ## The Four Primary Azure Storage Data Services | Service | Data Type | Access Protocol | Common Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Blob Storage** | Unstructured (objects) | HTTPS REST / SDK | Images, videos, backups, logs, big data | | **File Storage** | Structured files & folders | SMB 3.0, NFS 4.1, REST | Lift-and-shift file shares, lift legacy apps | | **Queue Storage** | Small messages (≤64 KB) | HTTPS REST / SDK | Decoupling microservices, async tasks | | **Table Storage** | NoSQL key-value | HTTPS REST / SDK | Flexible datasets, IoT telemetry, user profiles | ## Core Metric: The **Storage Account** > [!NOTE] > A **Storage Account** is the top-level namespace in Azure that groups all storage services together. It provides a unique namespace (URL endpoint) globally and defines the **replication strategy**, **performance tier** (Standard vs Premium), and **access tier** (Hot/Cool/Cold/Archive). Two kinds exist: **General-purpose v2 (GPv2)** — the recommended default — and legacy **General-purpose v1 / Blob Storage accounts**. ## Storage Account Endpoint Format Every Azure Storage resource is reachable through a unique REST endpoint. The standard format is: ``` https://..core.windows.net ``` Example: ``` https://ktuimages.blob.core.windows.net → Blob service https://ktushares.file.core.windows.net → File service https://ktuqueue.queue.core.windows.net → Queue service https://ktutable.table.core.windows.net → Table service ``` # Azure Storage Services – Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 1. Azure Blob Storage — The Object Store **Blob = Binary Large Object.** It stores unstructured data as objects organized inside **Containers** (analogous to folders, but flat — no real hierarchy). ### 1.1 Blob Types | Type | Purpose | Max Size | |---|---|---| | **Block Blob** | Default; for text/binary files, large media | ~4.75 TB (50,000 blocks × 100 MB; max 4000 MB last block) | | **Append Blob** | Optimized for append operations (logs, audit trails) | ~195 GB | | **Page Blob** | Random read/write; backs Azure VM disks (VHD files) | 8 TB | ### 1.2 Blob Access Tiers (Cost vs Access Frequency Trade-off) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **frequently examined KTU concept**. The four access tiers determine the **storage cost per GB** versus the **access cost per retrieval**. Hot = frequent read/write; Archive = rarely accessed, offline-like. | Tier | Storage Cost | Access Cost | Min Storage Duration | Retrieval Latency | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Hot** | Highest | Lowest | None | Immediate | | **Cool** | Lower | Higher | **30 days** | Immediate | | **Cold** | Even lower | Even higher | **90 days** | Immediate | | **Archive** | Lowest | Highest | **180 days** | Hours (must rehydrate first) | ### 1.3 Blob Lifecycle Management Azure offers **Lifecycle Management Policies** (JSON rules) to automatically transition blobs between tiers or delete them based on **age** or **last-modified date**. Example rule: - Move blob to **Cool** after 30 days - Move to **Archive** after 90 days - Delete after 7 years ### 1.4 Blob Access Control Mechanisms - **Shared Access Signatures (SAS)** — time-limited, permission-scoped URLs. - **Stored Access Policies** — reusable SAS policies grouped at the container level. - **Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)** — RBAC for identity-based access. - **Anonymous Public Access** — for public websites (configurable per container). - **Immutable Blob Storage** — WORM (Write Once, Read Many) for regulatory compliance. --- ## 2. Azure File Storage — Managed Network File Shares Fully managed file shares in the cloud, accessible via the **Server Message Block (SMB 3.0)**, **Network File System (NFS 4.1)**, or REST. Standard file shares can be mounted on **Windows, Linux, and macOS** simultaneously. ### 2.1 Key Capabilities - **Lift-and-shift**: Replace on-prem Windows file servers without rewriting apps. - **Geo-redundancy** for disaster recovery. - **Azure File Sync**: Cache Azure file shares on on-prem Windows Servers for hybrid scenarios. - **Identity-based authentication** via Azure AD DS / on-prem AD DS. ### 2.2 Tiers - **Transaction Optimized (default)** - **Hot** - **Cool** --- ## 3. Azure Queue Storage — Asynchronous Messaging A simple but massively scalable messaging service for storing large numbers of messages that can be accessed from anywhere via authenticated HTTP/HTTPS calls. ### 3.1 Properties - **Message size**: Up to **64 KB** - **Queue capacity**: Up to **500 TB** per storage account - **At-least-once delivery semantics** (consumers must de-duplicate) - **Visibility timeout** (default 30 seconds) hides a message after a `Get` call ### 3.2 Service Comparison (Often asked in KTU) > Queue Storage is **not** the same as Azure Service Bus. Queue Storage = simple, low-cost, REST-based. Service Bus = advanced broker (topics, subscriptions, transactions). --- ## 4. Azure Table Storage — NoSQL Key-Value Store A schemaless, semi-structured NoSQL store for large amounts of flexible datasets. Each entity (row) has: - **PartitionKey** (string) — required; groups entities for scalability - **RowKey** (string) — required; unique within a partition - Up to **252 additional properties** - Max entity size: **1 MB** - Tables scale to **petabytes** Table Storage is now often superseded by **Azure Cosmos DB Table API** for global distribution and richer querying. --- ## 5. Azure Disk Storage — Block-Level Storage for VMs Persistent block storage attached to Azure VMs as virtual hard disks (VHDs). | Disk Type | Use Case | Max Size | |---|---|---| | **Ultra Disk** | Sub-ms latency, IOPS-intensive (SAP HANA, top-tier DBs) | 65,536 GB | | **Premium SSD** | Production, performance-sensitive workloads | 32,767 GB | | **Standard SSD** | Web servers, lightly used workloads | 32,767 GB | | **Standard HDD** | Backups, non-critical, dev/test | 32,767 GB | ### 5.1 Managed Disks vs Unmanaged Disks > [!NOTE] > **Managed Disks** are the current recommendation. Azure manages storage accounts and SAS keys for you. **Unmanaged Disks** (legacy) require the user to create and manage Storage Account containers — not recommended. --- ## 6. Storage Redundancy Models — Critical KTU Topic Replication determines how many copies of your data Azure keeps and where. ### 6.1 The Redundancy Matrix | Option | Copies | Region Span | Failover | Notation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **LRS** – Locally Redundant Storage | 3 | Single datacenter | No | In-region only | | **ZRS** – Zone-Redundant Storage | 3 | Across 3 Availability Zones (same region) | No | Zone failure tolerant | | **GRS** – Geo-Redundant Storage | 6 (3+3) | Paired regions | Manual | Region disaster tolerant | | **RA-GRS** – Read-Access GRS | 6 (3+3) | Paired regions | Manual + secondary read | Same as GRS + read | | **GZRS** – Geo-Zone-Redundant | 6 (3 ZRS + 3 LRS) | Paired regions | Manual | ZRS in primary, LRS in secondary | | **RA-GZRS** – Read-Access GZRS | 6 | Paired regions | Manual + secondary read | Highest durability | ### 6.2 Durability Formula (Azure SLA) $$D_{LRS} = \left(1 - P_{\text{datacenter}}\right)^3 \cdot D_{\text{datacenter}}$$ In simpler terms, Azure publishes the **durability percentage** as: - **LRS**: ≥ 99.999999999 % (eleven 9s) over a year - **GRS / RA-GRS / GZRS / RA-GZRS**: ≥ 99.99999999999999 % (sixteen 9s) over a year > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question says "highest durability + read access from secondary region", the answer is always **RA-GZRS**. --- ## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol / Concept | Value / Definition | |---|---| | Max Block Blob Size | 4.75 TB (= 50,000 × 100 MB blocks) | | Max Append Blob Size | 195 GB | | Max Page Blob Size | 8 TB | | Max Queue Message Size | 64 KB | | Max Table Entity Size | 1 MB | | Max Queue Capacity per Account | 500 TB | | LRS durability SLA | 99.999999999 % (11 nines) | | GRS / GZRS durability SLA | 99.99999999999999 % (16 nines) | | Cool tier min storage duration | 30 days | | Cold tier min storage duration | 90 days | | Archive tier min storage duration | 180 days | | Archive retrieval latency | Hours (rehydration needed) | > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Azure Storage underpins virtually every enterprise cloud architecture — streaming pipelines (Blob + Data Lake), IoT ingestion (Queue → Table → Databricks), lift-and-shift migrations (File + SMB), and high-performance VMs (Premium SSD). Mastering the redundancy / tier trade-offs is essential for **cloud cost-optimization** interviews and KTU's "compare and justify" questions. # Azure Storage Services – Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Why LRS Provides ~11 Nines of Durability Azure publishes annualized durability. Suppose the probability of a single disk failing within a given year is $p$. With **independent** copies (no common-mode failure within a datacenter), the probability that **all 3 copies** survive is: $$ P_{\text{survive}} = (1 - p)^3 $$ The annual **data loss probability** (any of the 3 failing simultaneously) is: $$ P_{\text{loss}} = 1 - (1 - p)^3 $$ **Step-by-step expansion using the binomial theorem:** $$ \begin{aligned} (1 - p)^3 &= 1 - 3p + 3p^2 - p^3 \\[4pt] \therefore \; P_{\text{loss}} &= 1 - \left(1 - 3p + 3p^2 - p^3\right) \\[4pt] &= 3p - 3p^2 + p^3 \\[4pt] &\approx 3p \quad \text{(since } p \ll 1\text{, higher-order terms vanish)} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** If a single disk has a 1 in 10,000 annual failure probability ($p = 10^{-4}$), then the probability of losing all 3 replicas simultaneously is approximately: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{loss}} &\approx 3 \times 10^{-4} \\[4pt] &= 3 \times 10^{-4} \end{aligned} $$ Hence **durability** $D = 1 - P_{\text{loss}} = 0.9997$, or about **99.97%**. Azure achieves the **11-nines** claim by using **advanced erasure coding** and **continuous background scrubbing** across more granular fault domains inside a datacenter, effectively driving $p$ down to about $10^{-11}$. This is why: $$ D_{\text{LRS}} \approx 1 - (3 \times 10^{-11}) = 0.99999999997 \approx 11 \text{ nines} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: GRS Durability (16 Nines) GRS keeps **3 copies in the primary region** (LRS-equivalent durability $D_p$) and **3 copies in the secondary paired region** ($D_s$). The probability that **both** regions suffer unrecoverable loss is: $$ P_{\text{loss, GRS}} = (1 - D_p) \cdot (1 - D_s) $$ Since each region individually provides 11 nines: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{loss, GRS}} &\approx (10^{-11}) \cdot (10^{-11}) \\[4pt] &= 10^{-22} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} D_{\text{GRS}} &= 1 - 10^{-22} \\[4pt] &= 0.9999999999999999 \\[4pt] &\approx 16 \text{ nines} \end{aligned} $$ **Logic behind the multiplication:** Cross-region correlated failure is assumed to be statistically independent — i.e., a regional disaster in the primary will not automatically mean a disaster in the secondary at the same time. This independence is what gives the multiplicative resilience boost. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Cost Matrix (Typical KTU Question: "Choose the right tier") Suppose you have **10 TB** of data stored for **1 year** in Azure Blob. The table shows conceptual pricing relationships (numbers are illustrative for derivation purposes): | Tier | Storage Cost / GB / month | Retrieval Cost / GB | Min Days Stored | |---|---|---|---| | Hot | $0.0184 | $0 | None | | Cool | $0.0100 | $0.01 | 30 | | Cold | $0.0036 | $0.03 | 90 | | Archive | $0.00099 | $0.22 (rehydrate) | 180 | **Derivation: Total monthly cost for 10 TB on Cool tier if accessed once** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Data Size} &= 10\,\text{TB} = 10 \times 1024\,\text{GB} = 10{,}240\,\text{GB} \\[4pt] \text{Storage cost} &= 10{,}240 \times \$0.0100 = \$102.40 \\[4pt] \text{Retrieval cost} &= 10{,}240 \times \$0.01 = \$102.40 \\[4pt] \text{Total} &= \$102.40 + \$102.40 = \$204.80 \end{aligned} $$ **Early-deletion penalty:** If the user deletes the data after **15 days** instead of the minimum 30, Azure still charges for the remaining 15 days as a penalty. This is why the **min storage duration** column exists. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Penalty} &= \text{Data Size} \times \text{Cool rate} \times (\text{Min Days} - \text{Actual Days})/30 \\[4pt] &= 10{,}240 \times 0.0100 \times (30 - 15)/30 \\[4pt] &= 10{,}240 \times 0.0100 \times 0.5 \\[4pt] &= \$51.20 \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for KTU "Compare & Justify" Questions | Use Case | Recommended Service | Recommended Tier | Recommended Redundancy | |---|---|---|---| | Static website images | Blob | Hot | LRS | | Backups stored for 7 years | Blob | Archive | GRS | | Lift-and-shift Windows file server | File | Transaction Optimized | ZRS or GRS | | Microservice task queue | Queue | N/A | LRS | | IoT telemetry, billions of rows | Table or Cosmos DB | N/A | LRS | | High-IOPS SQL Server VM | Premium SSD | N/A | LRS (single region ok) | | Mission-critical data needing zone + geo | Blob/File | Cool / Hot | RA-GZRS | # Azure Storage Services – Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Azure Storage Account Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Azure Subscription] --> B[Resource Group: KTU-RG] B --> C1[Storage Account GPv2: ktustorage001] B --> C2[Storage Account GPv2: ktuarchives002] C1 --> D1[Blob Service] C1 --> D2[File Service] C1 --> D3[Queue Service] C1 --> D4[Table Service] C2 --> D3b[Blob Service Archive Tier] D1 --> E1[Container: images] D1 --> E2[Container: videos] D1 --> E3[Container: backups] D2 --> F1[File Share: hr-share] D2 --> F2[File Share: finance-share] D3 --> G1[Queue: order-processing] D3 --> G2[Queue: email-notifications] D4 --> H1[Table: user-profiles] D4 --> H2[Table: iot-telemetry] E3 --> I1[Lifecycle Policy: Hot -> Cool -> Archive -> Delete] F1 --> J1[Azure File Sync to On-Premises Server] ``` ## 4.2 Storage Tier Decision Flow ```mermaid graph TD K[New data to store] --> K1{How often accessed?} K1 -- Multiple times per month --> K2[Hot Tier] K1 -- Less than once per month --> K3{Access latency requirement?} K3 -- Immediate --> K4[Cool Tier - 30 day min] K3 -- Hours acceptable --> K5[Archive Tier - 180 day min] K1 -- Rarely, large datasets --> K6[Cold Tier - 90 day min] K2 --> K7{Geo-redundancy needed?} K4 --> K7 K5 --> K7 K6 --> K7 K7 -- Yes, highest durability --> K8[RA-GZRS] K7 -- Yes, basic geo --> K9[RA-GRS] K7 -- Zone failure only --> K10[ZRS] K7 -- Single datacenter --> K11[LRS] ``` ## 4.3 Redundancy Replication Layout (Cross-Region View) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph PrimaryRegion[Primary Region: Central India] L1[Zone 1: Copy A] L2[Zone 2: Copy B] L3[Zone 3: Copy C] end subgraph SecondaryRegion[Secondary Region: South India] R1[Zone 1: Copy D] R2[Zone 2: Copy E] R3[Zone 3: Copy F] end L1 -- async replication --> R1 L2 -- async replication --> R2 L3 -- async replication --> R3 PrimaryRegion -- RA-GZRS Read Endpoint --> Client SecondaryRegion -- Read Access when enabled --> Client ``` ## 4.4 Microservices Messaging using Queue Storage ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant WebApp as Web App (Producer) participant Queue as Azure Queue Storage participant Worker1 as Worker VM 1 (Consumer) participant Worker2 as Worker VM 2 (Consumer) WebApp->>Queue: Put message: orderID-9981 Queue-->>Worker1: Get message (dequeue) Worker1->>Worker1: Process order Worker1->>Queue: Delete message (success) Note over Queue: Visibility Timeout
hides message from Worker 2 ``` # Azure Storage Services – Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model] **Differentiate between Azure Blob Storage and Azure File Storage. Mention any two use cases of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Blob Storage | File Storage | |---|---|---| | Data abstraction | Objects (binary blobs) in flat containers | Hierarchical files & folders | | Protocol | REST / SDK over HTTPS | SMB 3.0, NFS 4.1, REST | | Mount as drive | Not by default (use BlobFuse / CSI driver) | Yes — `\\account.file.core.windows.net\share` | | Use case 1 | Streaming video, CDN-backed media | Lift-and-shift of on-prem Windows file server | | Use case 2 | Big-data analytics (Data Lake Gen2) | Shared configuration across multiple VMs | ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Explain the four access tiers of Azure Blob Storage with their minimum storage durations.** **Model Answer:** (2 marks for the four tiers, 1 mark for min durations) - **Hot** – frequent access; lowest retrieval cost; **no minimum** storage duration. - **Cool** – infrequent access (~30 days); data must remain ≥ **30 days** or early-deletion fee applies. - **Cold** – rarely accessed; ≥ **90 days** minimum storage. - **Archive** – offline; ≥ **180 days** minimum; rehydration takes hours. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Azure Storage Account architecture** and list any **four data services** available under it. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the **six redundancy options** offered by Azure Storage, comparing their durability and use cases. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Storage Account Architecture (7 Marks)** A Storage Account is a globally unique namespace in Azure that aggregates all storage data services. The hierarchy is: ``` Storage Account (ktustorage001) ├── Blob Service → Containers → Blobs ├── File Service → File Shares → Files/Folders ├── Queue Service → Queues → Messages ├── Table Service → Tables → Entities ``` Four data services: 1. **Blob Storage** – Object storage for unstructured data. 2. **File Storage** – Managed SMB/NFS file shares. 3. **Queue Storage** – Lightweight asynchronous messaging. 4. **Table Storage** – NoSQL key-value store. A diagram (Section 4.1) shows the full hierarchy. **(b) Redundancy Options (7 Marks)** | Option | Copies | Span | Failover | Durability | When to use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | LRS | 3 | One datacenter | None | 11 nines | Dev/test, non-critical | | ZRS | 3 | 3 AZs same region | None | 12 nines | High availability within region | | GRS | 6 | 2 regions | Manual | 16 nines | Disaster recovery | | RA-GRS | 6 | 2 regions | Manual + secondary read | 16 nines | DR with read-only secondary | | GZRS | 6 | 3 AZs + secondary | Manual | 16 nines | Highest regional + geo protection | | RA-GZRS | 6 | 3 AZs + secondary read | Manual + secondary read | 16 nines | Mission-critical data | Decision logic (full derivation in Section 3.1, 3.2) drives the nines count. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **(a)** Describe **Azure Blob Storage access tiers** (Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive). For a **10 TB backup dataset** accessed once every 6 months, which tier is most economical and why? Show calculation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain **Azure Queue Storage** and **Azure Service Bus Queue**. Compare them in a table. When would you choose Queue Storage over Service Bus? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Access Tiers + Calculation (7 Marks)** (3 Marks for explaining all 4 tiers, 4 Marks for calculation) For a 10 TB backup accessed every 6 months: - **Hot**: $0.0184/GB/month — too expensive. - **Cool**: $0.0100/GB/month + early-deletion fee if used <30 days; still accessed 1×/6 months, so retrieval cost = $0.01/GB. - **Cold**: $0.0036/GB/month + $0.03/GB retrieval; long min-duration (90 days) fits. - **Archive**: $0.00099/GB/month + $0.22/GB rehydration; cheapest storage but rehydration hours + cost. **Calculation for Archive tier** (showing step-by-step): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Data} &= 10\,\text{TB} = 10{,}240\,\text{GB} \\[4pt] \text{Storage cost (annual)} &= 10{,}240 \times 0.00099 \times 12 \\[4pt] &= 10{,}240 \times 0.01188 \\[4pt] &= \$121.65 \\[4pt] \text{Rehydration cost (once every 6 months)} &= 10{,}240 \times 0.22 = \$2{,}252.80 \\[4pt] \text{Annual total} &= (2 \times \$2{,}252.80) + \$121.65 \\[4pt] &= \$4{,}506.45 + \$121.65 \\[4pt] &= \$4{,}628.10 \end{aligned} $$ **Cold tier annual cost:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Storage cost} &= 10{,}240 \times 0.0036 \times 12 = \$442.37 \\[4pt] \text{Retrieval cost (2× per year)} &= 2 \times (10{,}240 \times 0.03) = \$614.40 \\[4pt] \text{Total annual} &= \$442.37 + \$614.40 = \$1{,}056.77 \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** **Cold tier is most economical** ($1,056.77 < $4,628.10). Archive is only cheaper if rehydration is *very* rare (≈ once a year or less), because the per-GB retrieval cost dominates. **(b) Queue Storage vs Service Bus (7 Marks)** | Feature | Queue Storage | Service Bus Queue | |---|---|---| | Protocol | REST/HTTPS | AMQP 1.0, HTTPS | | Max message size | 64 KB | 256 KB (Standard), 1 MB (Premium) | | Ordering | No guarantee | FIFO (Sessions) | | Transactions | No | Yes | | Topics & Subscriptions | No | Yes (pub/sub) | | Max queue size | 500 TB | 80 GB (Standard) / unlimited (Premium) | | Pricing | Very low | Higher (advanced broker) | | Use case | Simple task queue, decoupling microservices | Enterprise messaging, banking/financial apps | **Choose Queue Storage when:** the workload is simple, messages <64 KB, throughput is high, ordering and transactions are not required, and cost is critical (e.g., microservice decoupling in web apps). --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Confusing "nines"** — students write "GRS provides 11 nines" (wrong; it's 16 nines). > 2. **Forgetting min-storage-duration penalties** — when a tier comparison is asked, you MUST mention early-deletion fees. > 3. **Mixing up Queue Storage with Service Bus** — these are **two different services**; KTU questions will explicitly compare them. > 4. **Reporting "ZRS for geo-redundancy"** — ZRS is *intra-region* across Availability Zones, *not* across regions. > 5. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark sub-parts — a labeled hierarchy diagram almost guarantees a +1 mark in valuation. > 6. **Not mentioning Managed Disks vs Unmanaged Disks** — when Disk Storage is asked, the standard answer always notes that **Managed Disks** are the current recommended approach. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Azure Storage** = suite of cloud data services under a **Storage Account** namespace. - **Four core data services**: Blob (object), File (network share), Queue (messaging), Table (NoSQL) — plus **Disk Storage** for VMs. - **Two account kinds (modern)**: General-purpose v2 (recommended); legacy GPv1 / Blob-only accounts. - **Block Blob** = 4.75 TB max; **Append Blob** = 195 GB max; **Page Blob** = 8 TB max. - **Blob tiers** (ascending cost, descending access frequency): Hot → Cool (30d) → Cold (90d) → Archive (180d). - **Archive** requires **rehydration** (hours of latency). - **Queue messages**: 64 KB max; **500 TB** queue capacity; at-least-once delivery. - **Table entity size**: 1 MB; PartitionKey + RowKey mandatory. - **Managed Disks** are the current recommendation; types: Ultra / Premium SSD / Standard SSD / Standard HDD. - **Redundancy acronyms**: **LRS / ZRS / GRS / RA-GRS / GZRS / RA-GZRS**. - **Durability SLAs**: LRS ≈ 11 nines; GRS / GZRS family ≈ 16 nines. - **Cross-region replication is asynchronous** — possible RPO/RTO trade-offs. - **Anonymous public access**, **SAS tokens**, **Stored Access Policies**, **Azure AD RBAC** = four access control mechanisms for blobs. - **Lifecycle Management Policies** automate tier transitions and deletions. - **Azure File Sync** enables hybrid on-prem caching of Azure file shares. - **Immutability policies** enable WORM compliance for regulated workloads. - **For KTU:** always draw the storage hierarchy diagram in 7-mark sub-parts; always mention tier min-duration penalties; always distinguish Queue Storage from Service Bus.
